# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> An **Algorithm** is a finite, well-defined sequence of unambiguous, executable instructions formulated in a formal mathematical or computational language that transforms a given set of input data into a desired output, terminating after a finite number of steps, and serving as the procedural backbone for solving a specific class of problems or performing a definite computational task.

In the context of **Heuristics Solution Techniques (OEIET614)**, an algorithm represents the deterministic computational recipe against which *approximate* or *heuristic* methods are benchmarked. While classical algorithms guarantee optimal or exact solutions (often at the cost of exponential time), heuristic algorithms trade the guarantee of optimality for computational tractability on large-scale instances.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cooking Recipe Analogy 🍳**
> Imagine you want to prepare a dish (output) using ingredients (input data). A **recipe** is the perfect analogy for an algorithm:
> - **Finite steps** → You cannot cook forever; the recipe ends.
> - **Unambiguous instructions** → "Add salt" is vague; "Add 5 grams of NaCl" is unambiguous.
> - **Well-defined operations** → You can actually perform each step (chop, boil, fry).
> - **Same input → Same output** → Using the same recipe twice gives the same dish (deterministic).
>
> A **heuristic**, on the other hand, is like a *"grandmother's intuitive cooking trick"* — she adjusts spices "by eye," which works well (good quality solution) most of the time but is not strictly reproducible or guaranteed to be optimal (could be better with scientific measurement).

## 1.3 Fundamental Characteristics of an Algorithm (The 5 Pillars)

| # | Characteristic | Description | KTU Examiner's Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Finiteness** | Must terminate after a bounded number of steps. | *Termination* |
| 2 | **Definiteness** | Every instruction must be clear, precise, and non-ambiguous. | *Precision* |
| 3 | **Input** | Zero or more well-specified inputs are supplied. | *Domain* |
| 4 | **Output** | At least one output bearing a defined relation to the input. | *Range* |
| 5 | **Effectiveness** | Every operation must be basic enough to be carried out, in principle, by a person using paper and pencil. | *Feasibility* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction Highlight for Board Exams:**
> A **program** is NOT an algorithm. A program is the *implementation* of an algorithm in a specific programming language on a specific hardware. The algorithm is the *abstract logical procedure*; the program is its *concrete realization*.

## 1.4 Classification of Algorithms (Foundational Taxonomy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Heuristics (OEIET614):**
> The entire motivation behind heuristic methods stems from the limitations of **exact algorithms** on **NP-Hard** problems. The classification below is the *entry point* for Module 2.

1. **Deterministic Algorithms** → Same input always produces the same sequence of operations and output (e.g., Bubble Sort, Dijkstra's Algorithm).
2. **Non-Deterministic Algorithms** → Operations may have multiple valid choices; parallel/guessing execution model. Theoretically produces a result if a path exists (used as a benchmark for P, NP, NP-Complete classes).
3. **Exact Algorithms** → Guarantee optimal solution. Often exponential time-complexity (e.g., Brute Force, Branch & Bound, Dynamic Programming for TSP).
4. **Approximation Algorithms** → Guarantee a solution within a proven multiplicative factor of the optimum (e.g., Christofides' Algorithm for TSP gives $\frac{3}{2}$-approximation).
5. **Heuristic Algorithms** → No guarantee on solution quality, but practically fast and "good enough" (e.g., Genetic Algorithms, Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search).
6. **Metaheuristics** → Higher-level frameworks guiding subordinate heuristics (e.g., Ant Colony Optimization framework applied to TSP).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an Algorithm — Operational Stages

Every algorithmic procedure, whether exact or heuristic, can be decomposed into the following operational phases:

1. **Problem Definition Phase** → Identify inputs, outputs, constraints, and objective function. In heuristics, this is where the *search space* $\mathcal{S}$ is formally defined.
2. **Initialization Phase** → Set initial data structures, counters, and start states. For heuristics: generate the initial solution (random, greedy, or seeded).
3. **Processing / Iteration Phase** → Repeated execution of the core logic. For exact algorithms: systematic exploration. For heuristics: iterative improvement, neighborhood search, or population evolution.
4. **Decision / Termination Phase** → Check stopping criteria: (a) reached optimality, (b) exhausted time budget, (c) convergence plateau, (d) solution quality threshold met.
5. **Output / Reporting Phase** → Return the best-found solution, trace path, or statistical summary.

## 2.2 Asymptotic Complexity — The Core Mathematical Engine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Asymptotic Analysis?**
> We need a *machine-independent*, *input-size-scalable* measure of an algorithm's resource consumption. Exact wall-clock time depends on hardware; instead, we count **primitive operations** as a function of input size $n$.

### 2.2.1 Big-O Notation (Upper Bound — Worst Case)

$$f(n) = O(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\; n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)\;\; \forall\, n \ge n_0$$

- **Meaning:** For sufficiently large $n$, $f(n)$ grows no faster than a constant multiple of $g(n)$.
- **Engineering Use:** Guarantees the *maximum* resource the algorithm will ever need in the worst-case scenario.

### 2.2.2 Big-Ω Notation (Lower Bound — Best Case)

$$f(n) = \Omega(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\; n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le f(n)\;\; \forall\, n \ge n_0$$

- **Meaning:** $f(n)$ grows at least as fast as $g(n)$.

### 2.2.3 Big-Θ Notation (Tight Bound — Average Order)

$$f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff f(n) = O(g(n)) \text{ AND } f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$$

- **Meaning:** $f(n)$ and $g(n)$ grow at the same asymptotic rate.

### 2.2.4 Little-o and Little-ω (Strict Bounds)

- $f(n) = o(g(n))$ → $f$ grows *strictly slower* than $g$.
- $f(n) = \omega(g(n))$ → $f$ grows *strictly faster* than $g$.

## 2.3 Standard Complexity Classes (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE — Memorize for KTU Board Exams:**
> The vertical pipe symbol `|` has been replaced with `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| Complexity Class | Name | Example Algorithm | Growth Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| $O(1)$ | **Constant** | Array index access, hash lookup | Flat — best possible |
| $O(\log n)$ | **Logarithmic** | Binary Search | Extremely slow growth |
| $O(n)$ | **Linear** | Linear Search, array traversal | Direct proportion |
| $O(n \log n)$ | **Log-Linear** | Merge Sort, Quick Sort (avg), FFT | Efficient for sorting |
| $O(n^2)$ | **Quadratic** | Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort | Doubles work on $\sqrt{2}\times$ input |
| $O(n^3)$ | **Cubic** | Naive matrix multiplication, Floyd-Warshall | Polynomial — manageable for small $n$ |
| $O(2^n)$ | **Exponential** | Brute-force TSP, recursive Fibonacci | Infeasible beyond $n \approx 30$ |
| $O(n!)$ | **Factorial** | Permutation enumeration (naive TSP) | Catastrophic growth |

## 2.4 Time Complexity vs. Space Complexity

- **Time Complexity $T(n)$** → Number of primitive operations executed as a function of input size $n$.
- **Space Complexity $S(n)$** → Total memory (auxiliary + input) consumed as a function of $n$.

$$S_{\text{total}}(n) = S_{\text{input}}(n) + S_{\text{auxiliary}}(n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Principle:** Often you can reduce time by *spending* more memory (e.g., memoization in dynamic programming) or reduce memory by *spending* more time (e.g., in-place sorting). This is the **Time-Space Trade-off** — a guaranteed 14-mark question topic in KTU ESE.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Algorithm Analysis |
|---|---|
| **VLSI Design** | Heuristic placement & routing algorithms (SA, GA) for millions of gates |
| **Logistics & Supply Chain** | VRP (Vehicle Routing) solved via Tabu Search / ACO |
| **Bioinformatics** | Sequence alignment (Smith-Waterman, BLAST) — exact vs. heuristic |
| **Network Routing** | Dijkstra (exact) vs. QoS-aware heuristic protocols |
| **Machine Learning** | Gradient descent time-complexity dictates training feasibility |
| **Compiler Design** | Lexical analysis uses finite automata; parsing uses $O(n^3)$ algorithms in worst case |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Standalone Revision Block)

$$\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Performance Hierarchy (best to worst):} \\
&O(1) \;<\; O(\log n) \;<\; O(n) \;<\; O(n \log n) \;<\; O(n^2) \;<\; O(n^3) \;<\; O(2^n) \;<\; O(n!) \\
\\
&\textbf{Recurrence Master Theorem (Divide \& Conquer):} \\
&\text{If } T(n) = a\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + O(n^d), \text{ then:} \\
&T(n) = 
\begin{cases}
O(n^d) & \text{if } \log_b a < d \\
O(n^d \log n) & \text{if } \log_b a = d \\
O(n^{\log_b a}) & \text{if } \log_b a > d
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students confuse $O(n \log n)$ with $O(\log n)$. They are **different by a factor of $n$** — never interchangeable in asymptotic bounds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Asymptotic Bound Proofs

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 1 — Proving $3n^2 + 5n + 2 = O(n^2)$**
> This is the standard KTU 2024 short-answer type derivation.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of Big-O.**
We must find constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \le 3n^2 + 5n + 2 \le c \cdot n^2$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

**Step 2 — Upper-bound each positive term by $n^2$.**
For $n \ge 1$, observe that:
- $5n \le 5n^2$ (since $n \le n^2$ when $n \ge 1$)
- $2 \le 2n^2$ (since $1 \le n^2$ when $n \ge 1$)

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3n^2 + 5n + 2 &\le 3n^2 + 5n^2 + 2n^2 \\
&= (3 + 5 + 2)\,n^2 \\
&= 10\,n^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the constants.**
Choose $c = 10$ and $n_0 = 1$. Then for all $n \ge 1$:
$$3n^2 + 5n + 2 \le 10\,n^2$$
Therefore, by definition, $3n^2 + 5n + 2 = O(n^2)$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Solving a Recurrence (Master Theorem Application)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 2 — Time Complexity of Merge Sort**
> Merge Sort recursively splits the array into two halves and merges them in linear time:
> $$T(n) = 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(n)$$

**Step 1 — Identify Master Theorem parameters.**
- $a = 2$ (number of subproblems)
- $b = 2$ (subproblem size division factor)
- $d = 1$ (exponent in the non-recursive cost term $O(n^1)$)

**Step 2 — Compute the critical exponent $\log_b a$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_b a &= \log_2 2 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare $\log_b a$ with $d$.**
$$\log_b a = 1 = d$$
This is the **balanced case (Case 2)** of the Master Theorem.

**Step 4 — Apply Case 2 result.**
$$T(n) = O(n^d \log n) = O(n^1 \log n) = O(n \log n)$$
This matches the established empirical complexity of Merge Sort. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Best, Worst, and Average Case (Linear Search)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 3 — Classical 14-Mark Examination Problem**
> Consider the **Linear Search** algorithm on an array of $n$ elements.

**Pseudo-code for reference (descriptive only):**

```
LINEAR_SEARCH(A[0..n-1], key):
    for i ← 0 to n-1:
        if A[i] == key:
            return i
    return -1
```

**Step 1 — Best Case Analysis.**
The key is found at the first position $A[0]$.
$$T_{\text{best}}(n) = O(1)$$

**Step 2 — Worst Case Analysis.**
The key is either at the last position or absent from the array; the loop executes $n$ times.
$$T_{\text{worst}}(n) = O(n)$$

**Step 3 — Average Case Analysis.**
Assume uniform probability $p = \frac{1}{n}$ that the key is at any position. Expected number of comparisons:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} i \cdot \frac{1}{n} \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{n+1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dropping the lower-order constant:
$$T_{\text{avg}}(n) = O(n)$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
Linear Search has $O(1)$ best case, $O(n)$ worst case, and $O(n)$ average case — it is an $O(n)$ algorithm. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Design Paradigms vs. Heuristic Suitability

| Design Paradigm | Core Idea | Solution Guarantee | Typical Complexity | Heuristic Alternative? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Brute Force** | Try all possibilities | Optimal (exact) | $O(2^n)$ or $O(n!)$ | Yes — replaced by SA, GA |
| **Divide & Conquer** | Split, solve, merge | Optimal (exact) | $O(n \log n)$ typical | Rarely needed |
| **Dynamic Programming** | Memoize overlapping subproblems | Optimal (exact) | Polynomial in $n$ & state | Sometimes — for large state spaces |
| **Greedy** | Locally optimal choices | Often optimal, sometimes approximate | $O(n \log n)$ to $O(n^2)$ | Yes — when greedy fails |
| **Backtracking** | DFS with pruning | Optimal (exact) | Exponential worst case | Yes — beam search, tabu |
| **Branch & Bound** | BFS/DFS + bounds for pruning | Optimal (exact) | Exponential worst case | Yes — anytime B&B variants |
| **Heuristic / Metaheuristic** | Intelligent exploration via rules or meta-strategies | **No guarantee** (practical) | Often polynomial | This IS the method |

## 3.5 Numerical Evaluation: Complexity Comparison for Heuristic Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 4 — When do we *need* heuristics?**
> Compare $T_1(n) = 2^n$ vs. $T_2(n) = n^2$ for $n = 10, 20, 30, 40$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n = 10: \quad & 2^{10} = 1{,}024 \quad\text{vs.}\quad 10^2 = 100 \\
n = 20: \quad & 2^{20} \approx 1.05 \times 10^6 \quad\text{vs.}\quad 400 \\
n = 30: \quad & 2^{30} \approx 1.07 \times 10^9 \quad\text{vs.}\quad 900 \\
n = 40: \quad & 2^{40} \approx 1.10 \times 10^{12} \quad\text{vs.}\quad 1{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Inference:** At $n = 40$, the exponential algorithm requires over **1 trillion operations** — infeasible on any modern computer in reasonable time. Heuristics are not a luxury; they are a **necessity** for such problem sizes. This is the fundamental motivation behind Module 2 of OEIET614.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Execution Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram presents the canonical control-flow structure of an algorithm — initialization, iterative processing, decision gates, and termination pathways.

```mermaid

    B --> C[Initialize Data Structures]
    C --> D{Decision: Termination Criterion Met?}
    D -- No --> E[Execute Core Operation]

    F --> G[Increment Iteration Counter]
    G --> D
    D -- Yes --> H[Validate Output]
    H --> I{Validation Successful?}

    J --> L([End])
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an algorithm. List any four important characteristics of an algorithm.**

**Model Answer (Memorize):**

> An algorithm is a finite, well-defined sequence of unambiguous instructions for solving a class of problems or performing a computation.
>
> **Four Key Characteristics:**
> 1. **Finiteness** — terminates after a finite number of steps.
> 2. **Definiteness** — every instruction is precisely defined.
> 3. **Input** — zero or more inputs are well-specified.
> 4. **Output** — at least one output is produced with a defined relation to the input.
> *(Effectiveness is the 5th optional characteristic.)*

---

### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between an algorithm and a program.**

**Model Answer (3-mark table-format):**

| Criterion | Algorithm | Program |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Abstract, language-independent | Concrete, language-specific |
| **Execution** | Not directly executable | Directly executable on hardware |
| **Hardware Dependency** | None | Depends on compiler/OS/machine |
| **Debugging** | Logical analysis only | Requires runtime debugging |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain asymptotic notations Big-O, Big-Ω, and Big-Θ with mathematical definitions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Asymptotic Notations** are mathematical tools used to describe the *limiting behavior* of an algorithm's complexity function as the input size $n \to \infty$.

**1. Big-O Notation — $O$ (Upper Bound / Worst Case):**
$$f(n) = O(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\; n_0 > 0 \text{ s.t. } 0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)\;\; \forall\, n \ge n_0$$
*Interpretation:* $f(n)$ does not grow asymptotically faster than $g(n)$. Used to specify the **maximum** time an algorithm might take.

**2. Big-Ω Notation — $\Omega$ (Lower Bound / Best Case):**
$$f(n) = \Omega(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\; n_0 > 0 \text{ s.t. } 0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le f(n)\;\; \forall\, n \ge n_0$$
*Interpretation:* $f(n)$ grows at least as fast as $g(n)$. Specifies the **minimum** time required.

**3. Big-Θ Notation — $\Theta$ (Tight Bound):**
$$f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff c_1 \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n)\;\; \forall\, n \ge n_0$$
*Interpretation:* $f(n)$ is bounded both above and below by constant multiples of $g(n)$. Specifies the **exact order of growth**.

*Engineering Example:* Linear Search has $T(n) = O(n)$ (worst), $\Omega(1)$ (best), and average case $\Theta(n)$ for uniformly distributed keys.

---

**(b) Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 3\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n^2$ using the Master Theorem. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify Master Theorem parameters.**
Here $a = 3$ (subproblems), $b = 2$ (division factor), and $d = 2$ (cost of combine step).

**Step 2 — Compute the critical exponent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_b a &= \log_2 3 \\
&\approx 1.585
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare $\log_b a$ with $d$.**
$$\log_2 3 \approx 1.585 < 2 = d$$
This matches **Case 1** of the Master Theorem (recursion tree dominated by the root cost).

**Step 4 — Apply Case 1.**
$$T(n) = O(n^d) = O(n^2)$$

**Conclusion:** The algorithm runs in $O(n^2)$ time. The work done at each level of the recursion tree decreases geometrically, so the dominant cost is at the root.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) With suitable examples, classify algorithms based on their problem-solving approach. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Algorithms can be classified into the following design paradigms:

**1. Brute Force:**
Direct, exhaustive approach without special optimizations.
*Example:* Linear Search, naive string matching — $O(n \cdot m)$ for pattern of length $m$ in text of length $n$.
*Pros:* Guaranteed optimal, simple. *Cons:* Slow for large inputs.

**2. Divide and Conquer:**
Recursively breaks problem into independent subproblems, solves them, and combines.
*Example:* Merge Sort — $O(n \log n)$, Binary Search — $O(\log n)$.

**3. Greedy Algorithms:**
Builds solution incrementally, making the *locally optimal* choice at each step.
*Example:* Dijkstra's Shortest Path, Kruskal's MST, Huffman Coding.

**4. Dynamic Programming:**
Solves overlapping subproblems once and stores results in a table.
*Example:* Floyd-Warshall $O(n^3)$, Knapsack $O(n \cdot W)$.

**5. Backtracking:**
DFS-based exhaustive search with constraint-based pruning.
*Example:* N-Queens, Subset Sum.

**6. Branch and Bound:**
BFS/DFS with bounding functions to prune infeasible branches.
*Example:* TSP via BnB, Integer Programming solvers.

**7. Heuristic / Metaheuristic:**
Tractable, approximate methods trading optimality for speed.
*Example:* Genetic Algorithms, Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search — all covered later in this course.

---

**(b) Analyze the time complexity of Binary Search in best, worst, and average cases. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Binary Search repeatedly halves a *sorted* array to locate the key.

**Step 1 — Best Case.**
The key is at the middle index on the first comparison.
$$T_{\text{best}}(n) = O(1)$$

**Step 2 — Worst Case.**
The key is either not present or at an extreme end, requiring the search to continue until the subarray size becomes 1.
The recurrence for worst-case is:
$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(1)$$
Solving via Master Theorem: $a=1, b=2, d=0$; $\log_b a = 0 = d$ → Case 2:
$$T_{\text{worst}}(n) = O(\log n)$$

**Step 3 — Average Case.**
Assuming the key is equally likely to be at any position, expected number of comparisons is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}}(n) &\approx \log_2 n - 1 + 1 \\
&= \log_2 n
\end{aligned}
$$

(More rigorously: $T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \log_2 n + O(1)$.)

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
Binary Search achieves $O(\log n)$ in worst and average cases and $O(1)$ in the best case — confirming it is a **logarithmic algorithm**, vastly superior to linear search for large $n$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Evaluations:**
> 1. **Confusing $O$, $\Omega$, $\Theta$** — Many students write $f(n) = O(g(n))$ when they mean $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$. Always *state* whether you mean upper, lower, or tight bound. *Penalty: 1–2 marks per occurrence.*
> 2. **Forgetting the constants in Big-O definition** — You must exhibit specific $c$ and $n_0$ values when *proving* an asymptotic bound. A vague "dominates for large $n$" loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong Master Theorem Case** — Re-derive $\log_b a$ explicitly; do not skip. Misidentifying the case costs 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Mixing Best/Worst/Average** — In complexity questions, *all three* cases must be addressed and clearly labeled.
> 5. **Treating "Algorithm" and "Program" as synonyms** — They are *not*. Always highlight the abstraction vs. implementation distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Basic Algorithm:**

- ✅ An **algorithm** is a finite, definite, effective procedure with input(s) and output(s).
- ✅ The **5 mandatory characteristics**: Finiteness, Definiteness, Input, Output, Effectiveness.
- ✅ **Algorithm ≠ Program** — algorithm is abstract logic; program is its concrete implementation.
- ✅ **Big-O** gives the *upper (worst-case)* bound; **Big-Ω** gives the *lower (best-case)* bound; **Big-Θ** gives the *tight* bound.
- ✅ Master Theorem solves recurrences of the form $T(n) = a\,T(n/b) + O(n^d)$ via comparison of $\log_b a$ with $d$.
- ✅ **Complexity hierarchy**: $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$.
- ✅ **Time-Space Trade-off**: reducing one usually increases the other — a fundamental engineering principle.
- ✅ **Heuristics are needed** when exact algorithms become infeasible (typically $n \ge 30$–$40$ for exponential algorithms).
- ✅ For Linear Search: $T_{\text{best}} = O(1)$, $T_{\text{worst}} = T_{\text{avg}} = O(n)$.
- ✅ For Binary Search: $T_{\text{best}} = O(1)$, $T_{\text{worst}} = T_{\text{avg}} = O(\log n)$.
- ✅ For Merge Sort: $T(n) = O(n \log n)$ via Master Theorem Case 2.
- ✅ Algorithm design paradigms: Brute Force, D&C, Greedy, DP, Backtracking, B&B, Heuristic/Metaheuristic.
- ✅ Always *prove* asymptotic bounds by exhibiting explicit $c$ and $n_0$ values.
- ✅ Asymptotic analysis is *machine-independent* and *scalable* — the gold standard for algorithm comparison.
