## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Before Writing Your Exam):**
>
> 1. **Parameter Range Errors:** Students often write $c_1, c_2 \in [0, 1]$ or $\omega \in [0, 1]$. Correct ranges are $c_1, c_2 \in [0, 2]$ and $\omega \in [0.4, 0.9]$. Loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Cognitive and Social Components:** Writing only the inertia part of PSO loses **3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing $\eta_{ij}$:** Visibility is the **inverse** of distance, $\eta_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$, not the distance itself. Loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\beta$:** $\alpha$ controls pheromone weight, $\beta$ controls heuristic weight. Reversing them is a **2-mark** loss.
> 5. **Skipping the Position Update Equation:** Always write $\vec{x}_i^{t+1} = \vec{x}_i^t + \vec{v}_i^{t+1}$ after deriving velocity. Missing it costs **1 mark**.
> 6. **Not defining variables:** Examiners deduct marks for unexplained symbols like $\tau_{ij}$, $\rho$, $\eta_{ij}$, $\omega$, $c_1$. Always declare them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** = Collective intelligence from simple local agents — **decentralized, emergent, self-organized**.
- ✅ **Stigmergy** = Indirect communication through environment modification (pheromones).
- ✅ **PSO Velocity Equation:** $\vec{v}_i^{t+1} = \omega \vec{v}_i^t + c_1 r_1(\vec{P}_{best,i} - \vec{x}_i^t) + c_2 r_2(\vec{G}_{best} - \vec{x}_i^t)$.
- ✅ **PSO Position Equation:** $\vec{x}_i^{t+1} = \vec{x}_i^t + \vec{v}_i^{t+1}$.
- ✅ **Inertia weight $\omega$:** typical range **$0.4$ to $0.9$**, high $\omega$ = exploration, low $\omega$ = exploitation.
- ✅ **Acceleration coefficients $c_1, c_2$:** typical range **$0$ to $2$**, often equal to **$1.5$**.
- ✅ **ACO Pheromone Update:** $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^k$, with $\rho \in [0, 1]$.
- ✅ **ACO Transition Probability:** $P_{ij}^k(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in N_k}[\tau_{il}]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$.
- ✅ **Visibility:** $\eta_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$.
- ✅ **Communication Topologies:** Star, Ring, Von Neumann — affects convergence speed.
- ✅ **Convergence Property:** SI algorithms give **probabilistic** (not deterministic) guarantees of convergence.
- ✅ **ABC has 3 bee roles:** Employed, Onlooker, Scout.
- ✅ **No Free Lunch Theorem:** No single SI algorithm is universally best.
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** Ant shortest-path (ACO), Bird flocking (PSO), Bee waggle dance (ABC), Firefly flashing (FA).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computational Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computational Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Computational Characteristics of Heuristic Algorithms

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Computational Characteristics* refer to the measurable attributes that describe how a heuristic (or meta-heuristic) algorithm behaves on a computer in terms of resource consumption, runtime behavior, solution quality, and convergence behavior. They form the **analytical backbone** for comparing heuristic algorithms against each other and against exact (deterministic) optimization methods.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) framework, the course outcome for Module 2 typically evaluates the student's ability to **characterize, compare, and benchmark** heuristic techniques using standardized computational metrics.

### 1.1 Why Heuristics Need "Computational Characterization"

Exact algorithms (Branch & Bound, Dynamic Programming, Simplex) guarantee optimality but suffer from **exponential worst-case complexity** for NP-Hard problems. Heuristic methods trade optimality guarantees for **polynomial-time tractability**. To rigorously evaluate this trade-off, we need a structured set of "computational characteristics."

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** A heuristic is not "good" or "bad" in absolute terms — it is good *relative* to a specific **problem class**, **instance size**, **computational budget**, and **solution quality tolerance**. Computational characteristics provide the quantitative language for this comparison.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Navigation" Metaphor

Imagine you need to travel from Kochi to Delhi:

| Travel Approach | Algorithmic Counterpart | Computational Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| Reading a paper map (long, but correct) | Exact Algorithm (e.g., exhaustive search) | Optimal solution, exponential time |
| Using Google Maps with live traffic (fast, near-optimal) | Heuristic Algorithm | Polynomial time, near-optimal solution |
| Asking a local for shortcuts | Problem-specific heuristic | Very fast, but not generalizable |
| Trying random roads until you reach | Pure random search (RRT) | Fast but unreliable |

The **"computational characteristics"** of Google Maps would be: *average route time (fitness)*, *memory used (space complexity)*, *how often it re-routes (convergence rate)*, *how it performs during rush hour vs. night (robustness)*.

### 1.3 The Five Pillars of Computational Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Pillars (KTU Board-Favorite Framework):**
> 1. **Time Complexity** — how runtime scales with problem size
> 2. **Space Complexity** — how memory scales with problem size
> 3. **Convergence Behavior** — does the algorithm approach a quality solution?
> 4. **Solution Quality / Fitness** — how close to optimum?
> 5. **Robustness / Reliability** — consistent performance across runs/instances

### 1.4 Classification of Heuristics (Context for Characteristics)

Before diving deep, recall that heuristics are classified as:

- **Problem-Specific Heuristics** — tailored to one problem (e.g., nearest-neighbor for TSP)
- **Meta-heuristics** — general-purpose frameworks (GA, SA, TS, ACO, PSO)
- **Constructive Heuristics** — build solutions from scratch
- **Improvement (Local Search) Heuristics** — refine existing solutions
- **Population-based** (GA, PSO) vs. **Single-solution-based** (SA, TS)

The computational characteristics differ significantly across these classes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time Complexity Analysis

### 2.1.1 Formal Definition

**Time Complexity** of a heuristic is the asymptotic function $T(n)$ that bounds the number of elementary operations (or function evaluations) as a function of input problem size $n$.

For exact algorithms on NP-Hard problems:

$$T_{exact}(n) = O(2^n) \quad \text{or} \quad O(n!) \quad \text{(exponential)}$$

For heuristic algorithms:

$$T_{heuristic}(n) = O(n^k) \quad \text{or} \quad O(n^2 \log n) \quad \text{(polynomial)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite:** Heuristics convert **exponential** worst-case complexity into **polynomial** average-case complexity, sacrificing the *guarantee of optimality* for *practical tractability*.

### 2.1.2 Asymptotic Notation Hierarchy (Must-Know)

$$O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$$

This ordering is the **single most-tested concept** in KTU theory papers on computational characteristics.

### 2.1.3 Worst-Case vs. Average-Case vs. Best-Case

For heuristics, **average-case** is what matters practically, because worst-case is rarely encountered in well-designed stochastic heuristics.

| Case | Notation | Meaning | Heuristic Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Best-Case | $\Omega(\cdot)$ | Lower bound (lucky run) | Rarely reported |
| Average-Case | $\Theta(\cdot)$ | Expected over random instances/runs | **Most meaningful** |
| Worst-Case | $O(\cdot)$ | Upper bound (unlucky run) | Useful for guarantees |

## 2.2 Space Complexity

Memory footprint as a function of input size $n$ and (for population-based methods) population size $N_p$.

$$S(n) = O(n \cdot N_p) \quad \text{(population-based, e.g., GA, PSO)}$$

$$S(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(single-solution, e.g., SA, TS)}$$

## 2.3 Convergence Characteristics

### 2.3.1 Convergence in Probability (Most Common Definition)

A heuristic converges **in probability** to the global optimum $x^*$ if:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} P(|f(x_t) - f(x^*)| \leq \varepsilon) = 1 \quad \forall \varepsilon > 0$$

where $t$ is the iteration counter and $f$ is the objective function.

### 2.3.2 Convergence Rate

Measures how *fast* the algorithm approaches a quality solution:

$$\text{Convergence Rate} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \left| \frac{f(x_{t+1}) - f(x^*)}{f(x_t) - f(x^*)} \right|$$

A rate $< 1$ indicates convergence; closer to $0$ is faster.

### 2.3.3 Convergence vs. Diversity Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Premature Convergence Problem:** Population-based heuristics often lose **diversity** too quickly, converging to a local optimum. Computational characterization must report *both* the **quality trajectory** *and* the **diversity trajectory** over iterations.

## 2.4 Solution Quality Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimality Gap (%)** | $\frac{\vert f_{heuristic} - f_{opt} \vert}{\vert f_{opt} \vert} \times 100$ | When optimum is known |
| **Relative Percentage Deviation (RPD)** | $\frac{f_{heuristic} - f_{best\_known}}{f_{best\_known}} \times 100$ | Benchmarking |
| **Approximation Ratio** | $\rho = \frac{f_{heuristic}}{f_{optimal}}$ | Theoretical analysis |
| **Best / Worst / Mean / Std-Dev** | Over $N$ independent runs | Statistical reliability |

## 2.5 Robustness and Reliability

**Robustness** = consistency of performance across:
- Different problem instances of the same class
- Different random seeds
- Different parameter settings

Quantified by **Coefficient of Variation (CV)**:

$$CV = \frac{\sigma_f}{\mu_f} \times 100\%$$

where $\sigma_f$ is the standard deviation of solution quality across runs and $\mu_f$ is the mean.

Lower CV ⇒ more robust heuristic.

## 2.6 The No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wolpert & Macready's No Free Lunch Theorem (1997):**
> *Averaged over all possible problem instances, every heuristic algorithm performs identically.* No single algorithm is universally superior. The choice must be matched to the problem structure.

This is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU ESE.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Characteristic | Symbol / Formula | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time Complexity (Heuristic) | $T(n) = O(n^k)$ | Polynomial, problem-dependent |
| 2 | Time Complexity (Exact) | $T(n) = O(2^n)$ | Exponential, NP-Hard |
| 3 | Space Complexity (Population) | $S(n) = O(n \cdot N_p)$ | $N_p$ = population size |
| 4 | Space Complexity (Single) | $S(n) = O(n)$ | TS, SA |
| 5 | Convergence (in prob.) | $\lim_{t\to\infty} P(\vert f_t - f^* \vert \leq \varepsilon)=1$ | Asymptotic guarantee |
| 6 | Convergence Rate | $\vert \frac{f_{t+1}-f^*}{f_t-f^*} \vert$ | Rate $<1$ ⇒ convergent |
| 7 | Optimality Gap (%) | $\frac{\vert f_H - f^* \vert}{\vert f^* \vert} \times 100$ | Lower is better |
| 8 | Approximation Ratio | $\rho = f_H / f^*$ | Bounded $\rho$ ⇒ approx. scheme |
| 9 | Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \sigma_f / \mu_f$ | Robustness measure |
| 10 | Big-O Notation | $f(n) = O(g(n))$ | Upper asymptotic bound |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Computational characteristics are critical in:
- **VLSI Design** — placement/routing heuristics must terminate within hours, not weeks
- **Logistics (VRP)** — delivery routing for Amazon/Flipkart must run in minutes per re-route
- **Machine Learning Hyperparameter Tuning** — heuristics like Bayesian Optimization characterize themselves via regret bounds
- **Structural Optimization** — FEA coupled with heuristics needs tractable evaluation budgets
- **Telecom Network Design** — frequency assignment heuristics must scale to thousands of variables

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Exact to Heuristic Complexity — TSP Example

Consider the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** with $n$ cities.

### 3.1.1 Exact (Brute-Force) Time Complexity

The number of Hamiltonian cycles in a complete graph $K_n$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{cycles} &= \frac{(n-1)!}{2} \\
T_{exact}(n) &= O(n!) \\
T_{exact}(20) &\approx 6 \times 10^{16} \text{ evaluations} \\
\text{At } 10^9 \text{ eval/sec} &\Rightarrow \approx 19 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

This demonstrates the **intractability** of exact methods for moderate $n$.

### 3.1.2 Heuristic (Nearest Neighbor) Time Complexity

The **Nearest Neighbor (NN)** heuristic constructs a tour greedily:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For each of the } n \text{ cities:} \\
\text{Scan all } (n-1) \text{ remaining cities to find minimum distance} \\
T_{NN}(n) = n \times (n-1) = O(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{NN}(20) &= 400 \text{ operations} \\
\text{At } 10^9 \text{ ops/sec} &\Rightarrow \text{nanoseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 The Trade-off Ratio

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{exact}(20)}{T_{NN}(20)} = \frac{6 \times 10^{16}}{400} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{14}
$$

$$
\text{Quality Loss} = \frac{f_{NN} - f_{opt}}{f_{opt}} \times 100\% \approx 20\text{–}30\% \text{ on average}
$$

**Conclusion:** NN heuristic is $10^{14}\times$ faster but $\sim 25\%$ worse in quality. This *exact-speed-quality trade-off* is the central computational characteristic of heuristics.

## 3.2 Derivation: Convergence Rate of Simulated Annealing

The acceptance probability of a worse move in SA at iteration $t$ with temperature $T_t$:

$$
P_{accept}(t) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T_t}\right)
$$

Using a **logarithmic cooling schedule**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t &= \frac{T_0}{\log(1 + t)} \\
\Rightarrow \quad P_{accept}(t) &= \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta f \cdot \log(1+t)}{T_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

As $t \to \infty$, $T_t \to 0$, so $P_{accept} \to 0$ for $\Delta f > 0$. This guarantees convergence in probability to a local (often global, in practice) optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geman & Geman (1984) Theorem:** With the schedule $T_t \geq \frac{c}{\log(t+1)}$ for sufficiently large constant $c$, SA converges in probability to the global optimum. The computational cost of this guarantee, however, is **infinite runtime** — hence practical SA uses faster cooling.

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimality Gap Computation

Given benchmark results on a set of $K$ instances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{opt}^{(k)} &= \text{known optimal for instance } k \\
f_{H}^{(k)} &= \text{heuristic result for instance } k \\
\text{Gap}_k &= \frac{\vert f_H^{(k)} - f_{opt}^{(k)} \vert}{\vert f_{opt}^{(k)} \vert} \times 100\% \\
\overline{\text{Gap}} &= \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Gap}_k
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose for 5 TSP instances we have:

| Instance $k$ | $f_{opt}$ | $f_H$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 108 |
| 2 | 250 | 245 |
| 3 | 400 | 450 |
| 4 | 180 | 190 |
| 5 | 320 | 312 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gap}_1 &= \frac{\vert 108 - 100 \vert}{100} \times 100 = 8.00\% \\
\text{Gap}_2 &= \frac{\vert 245 - 250 \vert}{250} \times 100 = 2.00\% \\
\text{Gap}_3 &= \frac{\vert 450 - 400 \vert}{400} \times 100 = 12.50\% \\
\text{Gap}_4 &= \frac{\vert 190 - 180 \vert}{180} \times 100 = 5.56\% \\
\text{Gap}_5 &= \frac{\vert 312 - 320 \vert}{320} \times 100 = 2.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\overline{\text{Gap}} = \frac{8.00 + 2.00 + 12.50 + 5.56 + 2.50}{5} = \frac{30.56}{5} = 6.11\%
$$

This $\overline{\text{Gap}} = 6.11\%$ is the **computational signature** of this heuristic on this benchmark suite.

## 3.4 Derivation: Coefficient of Variation (Robustness)

Suppose 30 independent runs of a heuristic on the same instance yield fitness values with:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_f &= 425.6 \\
\sigma_f &= 12.4 \\
\Rightarrow CV &= \frac{12.4}{425.6} \times 100 = 2.91\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A CV $< 5\%$ indicates **high robustness**. A CV $> 20\%$ indicates the heuristic is **unstable** and may need parameter tuning or restarts.

## 3.5 Comparative Computational Matrix — Major Meta-heuristics

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Convergence Guarantee | Solution Quality | Robustness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GA** | $O(N_p \cdot G \cdot n)$ | $O(N_p \cdot n)$ | Probabilistic (Schema Theorem) | High (with tuning) | Medium |
| **SA** | $O(n \cdot t_{max})$ | $O(n)$ | In-probability (slow cooling) | High | Medium |
| **TS** | $O(n^2 \cdot t_{max})$ | $O(L_{tabu})$ | No guarantee | High | High |
| **ACO** | $O(N_p \cdot t_{max} \cdot n^2)$ | $O(N_p \cdot n)$ | Probabilistic | Very High (TSP) | Medium |
| **PSO** | $O(N_p \cdot n \cdot t_{max})$ | $O(N_p \cdot n)$ | No guarantee | High | High |
| **NN (TSP)** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ | No guarantee | Medium | High |
| **2-opt** | $O(n^2 \cdot t_{max})$ | $O(n)$ | Local optimum guaranteed | Medium-High | High |

Where $N_p$ = population size, $G$ = generations, $t_{max}$ = max iterations, $L_{tabu}$ = tabu list length.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary of Trade-offs

The **Pareto-front of heuristics** lies in a 3D space:

$$
\text{Trade-off Hyper-surface: } \quad (T(n), S(n), Q(n))
$$

where $Q(n)$ = solution quality. Moving along this surface:

- $\uparrow N_p$ (in GA) $\Rightarrow$ better $Q$ but $\uparrow S$ and $\uparrow T$
- $\uparrow t_{max}$ (in SA) $\Rightarrow$ better $Q$ but $\uparrow T$
- $\uparrow$ problem size $n$ $\Rightarrow$ $\uparrow T$, often $\downarrow$ quality stability

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five Pillars — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heuristic Algorithm Execution] --> B[Time Complexity T of n]
    A --> C[Space Complexity S of n]
    A --> D[Convergence Behavior]
    A --> E[Solution Quality Q]
    A --> F[Robustness CV]

    B --> B1[Big-O Notation O of n^k]
    B --> B2[Function Evaluations Count]
    B --> B3[Wall Clock CPU Time]

    C --> C1[Population Memory]
    C --> C2[Tabu List Memory]
    C --> C3[Working Buffers]

    D --> D1[Convergence in Probability]
    D --> D2[Convergence Rate]
    D --> D3[Diversity vs Quality Tradeoff]

    E --> E1[Optimality Gap Percent]
    E --> E2[Approximation Ratio]
    E --> E3[Best Mean Std-Dev]

    F --> F1[Inter Run Consistency]
    F --> F2[Inter Instance Consistency]
    F --> F3[Coefficient of Variation CV]

    B1 --> G[Benchmark Report]
    B2 --> G
    B3 --> G
    C1 --> G
    C2 --> G
    C3 --> G
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

## 4.2 Algorithm Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Optimization Problem] --> Q1{Is Problem Size Large?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need Global Optimum?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use Exact Method]

    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4{Population Available?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Use Local Search SA or TS]

    Q4 -- Yes --> Q6[Use GA or PSO]
    Q4 -- No --> Q7[Use ACO or SA]

    Q6 --> R1[High Time High Space High Quality]
    Q7 --> R2[Medium Time Low Space Medium Quality]
    Q5 --> R3[Low Time Low Space Medium Quality]
    Q3 --> R4[Guaranteed Optimality]

    R1 --> OUT[Algorithm Selected]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Convergence Trajectory Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Iter[Iteration Timeline]
        I1[Iter 0 High Fitness Low Quality] --> I2[Iter t1 Descending Curve]
        I2 --> I3[Iter t2 Knee Point]
        I3 --> I4[Iter tmax Plateau Near Optimum]
    end

    subgraph Metrics[Tracked Metrics]
        M1[Fitness f of x]
        M2[Diversity sigma]
    end

    Iter --> Metrics
```

## 4.4 Heuristic vs. Exact — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ExactBlock[Exact Algorithms]
        E1[Guaranteed Optimal Solution]
        E2[Exponential Time Complexity]
        E3[Small to Medium Instances]
    end

    subgraph HeurBlock[Heuristic Algorithms]
        H1[Near Optimal Solution]
        H2[Polynomial Time Complexity]
        H3[Large Scale Instances]
        H4[Tradeoff Quality for Speed]
    end

    E1 -.Compare.-> H1
    E2 -.Compare.-> H2
    E3 -.Compare.-> H3
    H4 --> Decision[Application Dependent Selection]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define time complexity of a heuristic algorithm. How does it differ from that of an exact algorithm for NP-Hard problems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Time complexity** of a heuristic algorithm is the asymptotic upper bound $T(n) = O(\cdot)$ on the number of elementary operations required as a function of problem size $n$.

For **exact algorithms** on NP-Hard problems, time complexity is typically **exponential**, e.g., $O(2^n)$ or $O(n!)$, making them intractable for large $n$.

For **heuristic algorithms**, time complexity is typically **polynomial**, e.g., $O(n^2)$, $O(n \log n)$, or $O(n^3)$, making them tractable for large instances. **(1 mark)**

The key difference is that heuristics trade **guarantee of optimality** for **polynomial-time tractability**. **(1 mark)**

Example: TSP — Exact: $O(n!)$ vs. Nearest Neighbor: $O(n^2)$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is the Coefficient of Variation (CV)? How is it used to characterize a heuristic's robustness?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** is a normalized measure of dispersion: **(1 mark)**

$$CV = \frac{\sigma_f}{\mu_f} \times 100\%$$

where $\sigma_f$ is the standard deviation and $\mu_f$ is the mean of the fitness values obtained over multiple independent runs. **(1 mark)**

**Robustness Interpretation:**
- **CV < 5%** → Highly robust heuristic (consistent across runs)
- **CV 5–15%** → Moderately robust
- **CV > 20%** → Unstable; needs parameter tuning or restarts **(1 mark)**

Thus CV is the **quantitative signature of a heuristic's reliability** across stochastic re-executions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **five major computational characteristics** used to evaluate heuristic algorithms. For each, give the formal definition and one example metric.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem** with its implications on heuristic algorithm selection. How does it justify the existence of multiple competing meta-heuristics?

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) The Five Major Computational Characteristics [7 marks]**

1. **Time Complexity [1.5 marks]**
   Asymptotic bound $T(n) = O(g(n))$ on operations as a function of input size $n$.
   Example: Simulated Annealing with $t_{max}$ iterations and inner-loop cost $O(n)$ gives $T(n) = O(n \cdot t_{max})$.

2. **Space Complexity [1.5 marks]**
   Memory footprint $S(n) = O(\cdot)$ as a function of input size.
   Example: GA with population $N_p$ and chromosome length $n$ requires $S(n) = O(N_p \cdot n)$.

3. **Convergence Behavior [1.5 marks]**
   Does the algorithm approach a quality solution as iterations increase?
   Formal definition: $\lim_{t \to \infty} P(|f(x_t) - f^*| \leq \varepsilon) = 1$.
   Example: SA with logarithmic cooling converges in probability to global optimum.

4. **Solution Quality [1.5 marks]**
   How close is the heuristic's solution to the (known or best-known) optimum?
   Measured by Optimality Gap $= \frac{|f_H - f^*|}{|f^*|} \times 100\%$.
   Example: NN heuristic on TSP-100 achieves $\sim$25% average gap.

5. **Robustness [1 mark]**
   Consistency of performance across runs/instances, measured by CV.
   Example: TS with $CV = 2.5\%$ is considered highly robust.

---

**(b) No Free Lunch Theorem [7 marks]**

**Statement [2 marks]:** Formulated by Wolpert and Macready (1997), the NFL Theorem states:

> *"When averaged over all possible problem instances, every heuristic algorithm exhibits identical performance."*

Formally, for any two algorithms $A$ and $B$:

$$\sum_{f} P(d_m^y \vert f, m, A) = \sum_{f} P(d_m^y \vert f, m, B)$$

where the sum is over all possible objective functions $f$, $m$ is the number of samples seen so far, and $d_m^y$ is the sequence of outputs.

**Implications [3 marks]:**
1. No algorithm is universally superior — a heuristic that excels on one problem class may be poor on another.
2. Algorithm selection must be **problem-aware** — exploiting problem structure (symmetry, decomposability, neighborhood topology) is essential.
3. Justifies the proliferation of specialized meta-heuristics (GA, SA, TS, ACO, PSO, DE, etc.) — each is tuned to specific problem structures.

**Justification for Multiple Meta-heuristics [2 marks]:**
- Each meta-heuristic encodes a different **exploration vs. exploitation strategy**
- GA emphasizes recombination (genetic operators)
- SA emphasizes probabilistic escape from local optima (Boltzmann acceptance)
- TS emphasizes memory-driven prohibition (tabu list)
- ACO emphasizes pheromone-mediated collective learning
- Since NFL proves no universal winner, the heuristic "zoo" is a **necessary consequence** of problem diversity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive and explain the **time complexity trade-off** between an exact brute-force algorithm and the Nearest Neighbor (NN) heuristic for the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) with $n$ cities. Use $n = 20$ as a numerical illustration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **convergence in probability** for heuristic algorithms. Discuss the **premature convergence** problem in Genetic Algorithms and how diversity-preservation mechanisms address it.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Exact vs. NN Heuristic Complexity for TSP [7 marks]**

**Exact (Brute-Force) Complexity [3 marks]:**

The number of distinct Hamiltonian cycles in $K_n$ (undirected) is:

$$N_{exact}(n) = \frac{(n-1)!}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$T_{exact}(n) = O(n!) = \frac{(n-1)!}{2}$$

**For $n = 20$:**

$$T_{exact}(20) = \frac{19!}{2} = \frac{1.216 \times 10^{17}}{2} = 6.08 \times 10^{16} \text{ tours}$$

Assuming $10^9$ tours/sec evaluation speed:

$$\text{Time} = \frac{6.08 \times 10^{16}}{10^9} = 6.08 \times 10^7 \text{ sec} \approx 1.93 \text{ years}$$

**Nearest Neighbor (NN) Heuristic Complexity [2 marks]:**

For each of the $n$ cities, scan the $(n-1)$ unvisited cities to find the nearest:

$$T_{NN}(n) = n \times (n-1) = O(n^2)$$

**For $n = 20$:**

$$T_{NN}(20) = 20 \times 19 = 380 \text{ operations}$$

$$\text{Time} = \frac{380}{10^9} \approx 3.8 \times 10^{-7} \text{ sec}$$

**Trade-off [2 marks]:**

$$\text{Speedup Factor} = \frac{6.08 \times 10^{16}}{380} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{14}$$

The heuristic is **$1.6 \times 10^{14}$ times faster**, but produces tours typically **20–30% longer** than optimal — the classical **exact-speed-quality trade-off**.

---

**(b) Convergence in Probability & Premature Convergence [7 marks]**

**Convergence in Probability [3 marks]:**

A heuristic algorithm generating a sequence of solutions $x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_t$ with corresponding fitness values $f(x_t)$ is said to **converge in probability** to a global optimum $x^*$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} P(|f(x_t) - f(x^*)| \leq \varepsilon) = 1$$

This means that as the number of iterations grows unbounded, the probability of being within $\varepsilon$ of the optimum approaches 1. SA satisfies this under logarithmic cooling schedules.

**Premature Convergence in GA [2 marks]:**

In Genetic Algorithms, **premature convergence** occurs when the population loses **diversity** too rapidly, with all individuals clustering around a **local optimum**. Causes include:
- Excessive selection pressure (elitism too strong)
- Low mutation rate
- Small population size
- High similarity among parents

**Diversity-Preservation Mechanisms [2 marks]:**

| Mechanism | How It Helps |
|---|---|
| **Fitness Sharing** | Reduces fitness of individuals in crowded regions |
| **Crowding / Niching** | New individuals replace similar (not best) ones |
| **Mutation Operator** | Reintroduces lost genetic material |
| **Random Immigrants** | Periodically inject fresh random individuals |
| **Island Model GA** | Sub-populations evolve independently (spatial isolation) |
| **Adaptive Mutation Rate** | Increases mutation when diversity drops below threshold |

These mechanisms trade **short-term convergence speed** for **long-term exploration**, improving the GA's computational signature on multimodal landscapes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Time Complexity" with "Wall-Clock Time":** $O(n^2)$ is an asymptotic bound on *operations*, not seconds. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this conflation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the NFL Pre-condition:** The NFL Theorem applies only when averaged **over ALL possible problems**. It does NOT mean "all heuristics are equivalent on a specific problem." Students who write the latter get **zero** on that sub-question.
>
> 3. **Mixing "Convergence" of sequences with "Convergence" of GA populations:** Sequence convergence (SA, TS) ≠ Population convergence (GA). Be precise.
>
> 4. **Optimality Gap Calculation Errors:** Always use absolute value $|f_H - f^*|$ — sign errors lead to negative gaps and lose full marks on numerical sub-parts.
>
> 5. **Omitting the Constant in CV:** $CV = \sigma/\mu \times 100\%$ (with the 100%). Forgetting the percentage sign loses 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Computational Characteristics**

- ✅ Computational characteristics are the **quantitative descriptors** of heuristic behavior: time, space, convergence, quality, robustness.
- ✅ Heuristics convert **exponential** NP-Hard complexity into **polynomial** time, sacrificing **optimality guarantees**.
- ✅ **Big-O notation hierarchy:** $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$.
- ✅ **Time complexity** = operations count; **Space complexity** = memory footprint.
- ✅ **Convergence in probability:** $\lim_{t \to \infty} P(|f_t - f^*| \leq \varepsilon) = 1$.
- ✅ **Optimality Gap** $= \frac{|f_H - f^*|}{|f^*|} \times 100\%$ — lower is better.
- ✅ **Approximation Ratio** $\rho = f_H / f^*$ — bounded $\rho$ defines an approximation scheme.
- ✅ **CV** $= \sigma_f / \mu_f \times 100\%$ — quantifies robustness; $<5\%$ is excellent.
- ✅ **No Free Lunch Theorem:** No universal best heuristic — choice must be problem-aware.
- ✅ **Population-based** algorithms (GA, PSO) have $S(n) = O(N_p \cdot n)$; **Single-solution** (SA, TS) have $S(n) = O(n)$.
- ✅ **Premature convergence** in GA is mitigated by fitness sharing, niching, mutation, random immigrants, island models.
- ✅ **TSP reference example:** Brute-force $O(n!)$ vs. NN heuristic $O(n^2)$ — speedup $\sim 10^{14}$ for $n=20$.
- ✅ **Five Pillars Framework:** Time, Space, Convergence, Quality, Robustness — examiners love this framework.
- ✅ **Computational characteristics enable fair benchmarking** across diverse heuristic algorithms.
- ✅ **Key insight for exam:** Heuristics = *engineering pragmatism* over *mathematical perfection*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameter Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parameter Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of metaheuristic and heuristic optimization algorithms, **Parameter Selection** refers to the systematic process of identifying the most appropriate combination of configuration values (e.g., population size, crossover probability, mutation rate, inertia weight, pheromone evaporation rate) that governs the operational behavior of a stochastic search algorithm. The objective is to maximize algorithmic performance metrics such as solution quality, convergence speed, robustness, and reliability across a given set of problem instances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Parameter selection is the procedure of choosing values for algorithm-internal parameters such that the heuristic achieves an optimal trade-off between **exploration** (diversification of the search space) and **exploitation** (intensification around promising regions).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Tuning a Cooking Recipe**
> Imagine you are a chef preparing a new dish (the optimization problem). You have ingredients like salt, sugar, spice, and heat level (the parameters of your heuristic algorithm — population size, mutation rate, crossover probability, inertia weight). If you add too much salt (over-exploitation), the dish becomes inedible (premature convergence to a local optimum). If you add too little (over-exploration), the flavor is bland (slow convergence). Parameter selection is like running a series of taste-tests (experiments) to find the exact "golden ratio" of ingredients that produces the best dish (global optimum) consistently.

### 1.3 Types of Parameters in Heuristics

| Parameter Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Numerical / Continuous** | Real-valued parameters controlling algorithm dynamics | Inertia weight $\omega \in [0, 1]$, learning factors $c_1, c_2$ |
| **Categorical / Discrete** | Integer-valued or symbolic choices | Population size $N \in \{20, 50, 100\}$, selection scheme |
| **Structural** | Choice of algorithmic components | Crossover operator (SBX vs. Two-Point) |

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard KTU-recognized metrics for evaluating parameter configurations are:
> - **Solution Quality**: Best objective value $f(x^*)$ found.
> - **Convergence Speed**: Number of function evaluations (FEs) required to reach a target fitness.
> - **Robustness**: Standard deviation of solutions over multiple independent runs.
> - **Success Rate (SR)**: Percentage of runs reaching the global optimum within a budget.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Strategic Paradigms

#### A. Parameter Tuning (Offline / Static)
The algorithm is configured *before* execution with a fixed set of parameters that remain constant throughout the search. This is treated as a "black-box" optimization problem where the metaheuristic itself is the object being optimized.

#### B. Parameter Control (Online / Adaptive)
Parameters are dynamically adjusted *during* the run using feedback from the search progress (e.g., success-based mutation rate adaptation in (1+1)-EA).

| Strategy | Timing | Feedback | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deterministic** | Predefined schedule | None | Low |
| **Adaptive** | During run | Search progress | Medium |
| **Self-Adaptive** | Encoded in solution | Evolutionary feedback | High |

---

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundations

#### 2.2.1 The Tuning Objective Function

Given an algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ with parameter vector $\theta = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_k)$, the tuning problem is formally expressed as:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \ \mathbb{E}_{I \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ c(\mathcal{A}(\theta), I) \right]$$

Where:
- $\theta^*$ = optimal parameter configuration
- $\Theta$ = parameter search space
- $c(\cdot)$ = cost function (e.g., mean best objective)
- $I$ = problem instance drawn from distribution $\mathcal{D}$
- $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ = expected value over independent runs

#### 2.2.2 Performance Aggregation (KTU Formula Sheet)

| Aggregation Method | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic Mean** | $\bar{c} = \frac{1}{R}\sum_{r=1}^{R} c_r$ | Symmetric, normal distributions |
| **Median** | $\tilde{c} = \text{median}(c_1, \ldots, c_R)$ | Robust to outliers |
| **Success Performance (SP)** | $SP = \frac{\bar{c}_{suc}}{p_s} \cdot 100$ | Penalizes low success rates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Success Performance (SP)** is the canonical metric used in the CEC competition benchmarks. Here $\bar{c}_{suc}$ is the mean number of evaluations over successful runs and $p_s$ is the success probability.

#### 2.2.3 Variance and Robustness Indicators

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{R-1} \sum_{r=1}^{R} (c_r - \bar{c})^2$$

$$\text{CV} = \frac{\sigma}{\vert \bar{c} \vert} \quad \text{(Coefficient of Variation)}$$

A low CV indicates a robust configuration — the algorithm performs consistently across runs.

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

In real-world engineering design (structural optimization, antenna tuning, VLSI floor planning, hyperparameter tuning of deep neural networks), parameter selection is the bridge between a *theoretically proposed* heuristic and a *practically deployable* solver. Without systematic parameter selection, an algorithm's published results are not reproducible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Expected Cost over a Distribution of Instances

**Step 1: Define the algorithmic cost as a random variable.**
Each run of algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ with configuration $\theta$ on instance $I$ yields cost $c(\mathcal{A}(\theta), I) = C$. This is a random variable because of stochastic operators.

**Step 2: Define the distribution over instances.**
Assume instances are sampled i.i.d. from an unknown distribution $\mathcal{D}$. For empirical tuning, we use a finite benchmark suite $B = \{I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_M\}$.

**Step 3: Compute the empirical expected cost.**
The theoretical expectation $\mathbb{E}_{I}[c(\cdot)]$ is approximated by the empirical mean:

$$\hat{\mathbb{E}}_{I}[c] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} c(\mathcal{A}(\theta), I_j)$$

**Step 4: Compute the expected cost over runs.**
Because each run is also stochastic, we take the mean over $R$ independent runs per instance:

$$\hat{c}(\theta) = \frac{1}{R} \sum_{r=1}^{R} c(\mathcal{A}_r(\theta), I_j)$$

**Step 5: Combine Step 3 and Step 4 (Nested Averaging).**

$$\hat{c}(\theta) = \frac{1}{M \cdot R} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \sum_{r=1}^{R} c(\mathcal{A}_r(\theta), I_j)$$

**Step 6: Final tuning objective.**

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \ \frac{1}{M \cdot R} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \sum_{r=1}^{R} c(\mathcal{A}_r(\theta), I_j)$$

This double-loop formulation is the **gold standard** for offline parameter tuning and is exactly what tools like **irace** (Iterated Racing) implement.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Success Performance (SP) Metric

**Step 1: Define successful runs.** A run $r$ is "successful" if it reaches the target fitness $f_{target}$ within the evaluation budget $B_{max}$.

$$s_r = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f_r \le f_{target} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Compute success rate.**

$$p_s = \frac{1}{R} \sum_{r=1}^{R} s_r$$

**Step 3: Compute mean evaluations over successful runs only.**

$$\bar{c}_{suc} = \frac{\sum_{r=1}^{R} s_r \cdot c_r}{\sum_{r=1}^{R} s_r}$$

**Step 4: Combine to yield Success Performance.**

$$SP = \frac{\bar{c}_{suc}}{p_s} \cdot 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $p_s = 0$ (no successful runs), $SP$ is undefined / set to infinity. If $p_s = 1$ (all runs succeed), $SP = \bar{c}_{suc} \cdot 100$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Major Tuning Methods

| Method | Year | Strategy | Sample Efficiency | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Factorial (FF)** | — | Exhaustive grid | Low | Complete coverage |
| **Taguchi DOE** | 1986 | Orthogonal Arrays | High | Industrial standard |
| **F-RACE** | 2002 | Friedman + racing | High | Statistical rigor |
| **I/F-RACE** | 2009 | Iterated F-RACE | Very High | irace package |
| **REVAC** | 2009 | Entropy-based | High | Information-theoretic |
| **CALIBRA** | 2009 | Markov Chain | Medium | Convergence guarantees |
| **SMAC (Sequential Model-Based)** | 2011 | Bayesian (RF) | Very High | Scalable to many params |
| **Hyperopt / TPE** | 2011 | Bayesian (Tree-Parzen) | Very High | ML integration |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Parameter Selection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ProblemInstanceSet"] --> B["ParameterSpaceTheta"]
    B --> C["SamplingStrategy"]
    C --> D["RunAlgorithmAthetaI"]
    D --> E["CollectCostMetrics"]
    E --> F["StatisticalEvaluation"]
    F --> G{"PerformanceImprovement"}
    G -- Yes --> H["UpdateParameterConfig"]
    H --> C
    G -- No --> I["ConvergenceCheck"]
    I --> J{"BudgetExhausted"}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K["OutputOptimalTheta"]
    K --> L["DeployConfiguration"]
```

### 4.2 Racing Method Workflow (irace Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["SampleInitialCandidates"] --> S2["EvaluateFirstNInstances"]
    S2 --> S3["ApplyFriedmanTest"]
    S3 --> S4{"SignificantDifference"}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5["DiscardWorstCandidates"]
    S4 -- No --> S6["EvaluateNextInstance"]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7{"SurvivorsRemain"}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8["AddNewCandidates"]
    S7 -- No --> S9["ReturnBestConfig"]
    S8 --> S2
```

### 4.3 Parameter Control Feedback Loop (Adaptive Tuning)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["AlgorithmExecution"] --> P2["MonitorSearchState"]
    P2 --> P3["ComputeFeedbackSignal"]
    P3 --> P4["UpdateRule:thetaNew"]
    P4 --> P1
    A1["PopulationDiversity"] --> P2
    A2["ImprovementRate"] --> P2
    A3["BestFitnessStagnation"] --> P2
```

### 4.4 Taguchi Orthogonal Array Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["DesignOrthogonalArrayL9"] --> R["Run9Experiments"]
    R --> T["ComputeSNRatio"]
    T --> U["FactorAnalysisANOVA"]
    U --> V["OptimalLevelCombination"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Parameter Tuning and Parameter Control. List one example method for each.**

**Model Answer:**
Parameter Tuning is performed *offline, before* algorithm execution; parameters remain fixed during the run. Example: Taguchi DOE or irace.

Parameter Control adjusts parameters *online, during* execution using feedback from search progress. Example: 1/5-success rule for mutation rate in Evolution Strategies (ES).

| Aspect | Tuning | Control |
|---|---|---|
| Timing | Offline | Online |
| Feedback Used | Statistical aggregation | Real-time search state |
| Computational Cost | High (many runs) | Low (overhead per iteration) |

*(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the Success Performance (SP) metric and why is it preferred over plain mean cost?**

**Model Answer:**
Success Performance (SP) is defined as:

$$SP = \frac{\bar{c}_{suc}}{p_s} \cdot 100$$

where $\bar{c}_{suc}$ is the mean evaluations over successful runs and $p_s$ is the success rate.

It is preferred because it penalizes configurations that fail to reach the target objective. A configuration with very low mean cost but zero success rate is penalized by $p_s \to 0$, making $SP \to \infty$. Hence SP balances both **quality** and **reliability** in a single scalar.

*(3 marks)*

---

### **Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(7 Marks)** Explain the **iterated F-RACE (I/F-RACE)** algorithm for parameter selection. Describe its four main stages.

**Model Answer:**

**Stage 1 — Initial Sampling:**
Sample $N_0$ candidate configurations from the parameter space $\Theta$ using Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) to ensure broad coverage.

**Stage 2 — Racing Phase:**
Evaluate all $N_0$ configurations on a sequence of problem instances. After each instance, apply the **Friedman non-parametric test** to rank the surviving configurations. Discard configurations that are statistically inferior.

**Stage 3 — Iterative Refinement:**
Once the number of surviving candidates drops below a threshold $\epsilon$, sample new candidates around the surviving ones (local intensification). Continue racing.

**Stage 4 — Termination & Output:**
Stop when the evaluation budget is exhausted. Return the best surviving configuration $\theta^*$ as the optimal tuning.

**Advantages:**
- Sample-efficient: discards poor configs early.
- Statistical rigor: controls family-wise error via Friedman.
- Handles stochastic noise via multiple runs.

*(7 marks)*

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(7 Marks)** For a Genetic Algorithm with two parameters: crossover probability $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$ and mutation probability $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$, design a **Taguchi L9 orthogonal array** experiment. Justify the choice of S/N ratio type.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Factor Levels.**
Assign 3 levels for each factor:

| Level | $p_c$ | $p_m$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.60 | 0.001 |
| 2 | 0.75 | 0.020 |
| 3 | 0.90 | 0.050 |

**Step 2: L9 Orthogonal Array.**

| Exp | $p_c$ | $p_m$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 1 | 2 |
| 3 | 1 | 3 |
| 4 | 2 | 1 |
| 5 | 2 | 2 |
| 6 | 2 | 3 |
| 7 | 3 | 1 |
| 8 | 3 | 2 |
| 9 | 3 | 3 |

**Step 3: Run $R = 25$ replications per experiment.**

**Step 4: Compute S/N Ratio.**
Since we aim to **minimize** the objective (smaller-is-better):

$$\text{S/N} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{R}\sum_{r=1}^{R} c_r^2\right)$$

**Step 5: ANOVA and Factor Effects.**
Compute mean S/N for each level. The optimal levels are those with the highest S/N.

**Justification:** The "smaller-is-better" S/N is chosen because the objective function value (error / cost) is to be minimized; "larger-is-better" applies when maximizing fitness.

*(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(7 Marks)** Explain **deterministic, adaptive, and self-adaptive** parameter control strategies with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Deterministic Parameter Control:**
Parameters follow a predefined schedule independent of search feedback. Example: Linearly decreasing inertia weight in PSO:

$$\omega(t) = \omega_{max} - \frac{(\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}) \cdot t}{T_{max}}$$

**2. Adaptive Parameter Control:**
Parameters are adjusted based on real-time feedback from the search. Example: **1/5-success rule** in (1+1)-ES — if more than 1/5 of mutations succeed, increase step size $\sigma$; otherwise decrease.

**3. Self-Adaptive Parameter Control:**
Parameter values are encoded into the individual and undergo evolution themselves. Example: In self-adaptive ES, the mutation strength $\sigma$ is part of the genome and is mutated alongside the decision variables.

**Comparison Table:**

| Strategy | Information Source | Robustness |
|---|---|---|
| Deterministic | Time $t$ only | Low |
| Adaptive | Search state | Medium |
| Self-Adaptive | Evolutionary pressure | High |

*(7 marks)*

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(7 Marks)** A particle swarm optimization algorithm is run 30 times with parameters $\omega = 0.7$, $c_1 = 1.5$, $c_2 = 1.5$ on a benchmark function. The target optimum is $10^{-5}$. Results: 24 runs succeeded (mean evaluations = 8500), 6 runs failed. Compute Success Performance (SP).

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Identify success rate.**

$$p_s = \frac{24}{30} = 0.8$$

**Step 2: Mean evaluations over successful runs.**

$$\bar{c}_{suc} = 8500$$

**Step 3: Apply SP formula.**

$$SP = \frac{\bar{c}_{suc}}{p_s} \cdot 100$$

$$SP = \frac{8500}{0.8} \cdot 100 = 10625 \cdot 100 / 100 = 1{,}062{,}500$$

Wait — recompute carefully:

$$SP = \frac{8500}{0.8} = 10{,}625$$

Multiplied by 100 as per convention:

$$SP = 10{,}625 \cdot 100 = 1{,}062{,}500$$

**Interpretation:** A lower SP indicates a better configuration. If another configuration yielded $SP = 1{,}250{,}000$, the given one is superior because it reaches the target faster and more reliably.

*(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Tuning with Control**: Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if students mix "offline tuning" with "online control." State the timing explicitly.
> 2. **S/N Ratio Direction**: Always verify whether the objective is minimization or maximization. Selecting the wrong S/N formula loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **irace vs. F-RACE**: F-RACE is single iteration; I/F-RACE is iterated. Mixing up the two loses 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Units in SP**: While SP is dimensionless, the multiplication factor of 100 is conventional — forgetting it is a common mistake. State the unit explicitly.
> 5. **Friedman Test Prerequisite**: irace uses Friedman test, *not* ANOVA, because configurations are compared on multiple instances (blocked design).

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Parameter Selection** is the procedure of choosing values for algorithm-internal parameters to balance exploration vs. exploitation.
- **Tuning** = offline, static, statistical; **Control** = online, dynamic, feedback-driven.
- **Three control categories**: deterministic, adaptive, self-adaptive (in increasing order of robustness).
- **irace (Iterated F-RACE)** uses Friedman test + racing + iterative resampling.
- **Taguchi DOE** uses orthogonal arrays (e.g., L9, L18) and S/N ratios for sample-efficient tuning.
- **Success Performance (SP)** = $\frac{\bar{c}_{suc}}{p_s} \cdot 100$ — the canonical CEC metric.
- **Bayesian methods** (SMAC, TPE, Hyperopt) scale better to high-dimensional parameter spaces.
- **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** = $\sigma / \vert \bar{c} \vert$ — measures robustness.
- **Self-adaptation** encodes parameters into the chromosome — no external tuning needed.
- **KTU High-Yield Formula Trio**: $\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta \mathbb{E}[c]$, $SP = \bar{c}_{suc}/p_s \cdot 100$, $\text{CV} = \sigma/\vert\bar{c}\vert$.
- **Common Pitfall**: Always clarify whether tuning is instance-specific or algorithm-wide.
- **Reproducibility Rule**: All KTU evaluations require reporting the random seed, instance set, and run count $R$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete PSO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete PSO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Discrete Particle Swarm Optimization (DPSO)**, also known as **Binary Particle Swarm Optimization (BPSO)**, is a population-based metaheuristic algorithm introduced by **James Kennedy and Russell Eberhart (1997)** as a discrete adaptation of the original continuous PSO developed in 1995. It is specifically engineered to solve **combinatorial optimization problems** whose decision variables belong to a finite, discrete set — most commonly the binary domain $\{0, 1\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEIET614, Module 2):**
> *Discrete PSO is a variant of Particle Swarm Optimization in which the particle's position is restricted to discrete values (typically 0 or 1). The velocity of the particle is interpreted as the probability of the corresponding bit taking the value '1', and a sigmoid (logistic) transfer function is used to map velocity into a probability for stochastic position update.*

Mathematically, a swarm of $N$ particles explores a $D$-dimensional binary search space $\mathbb{B}^D = \{0, 1\}^D$. For the $i$-th particle at iteration $t$:

$$
\mathbf{x}_i(t) = \left[x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{iD}\right], \quad x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_i(t) = \left[v_{i1}, v_{i2}, \ldots, v_{iD}\right], \quad v_{ij} \in [V_{\min}, V_{\max}]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike continuous PSO where position update is deterministic ($x = x + v$), in DPSO the velocity is reinterpreted as a **probability bias**, and the actual position is sampled using a stochastic rule.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Swarm of Decision-Makers"

Imagine a **committee of 50 voting members** trying to decide which subset of 20 projects (out of 100) should be funded. Each member holds an opinion vector — a binary list answering *"Yes (1) / No (0)"* for each project. They have two guiding references:

- Their **own best past judgment** (`pbest`) — the best subset they ever voted for.
- The **committee's overall best member** (`gbest`) — the most successful subset identified by the group.

At every meeting (iteration):

1. Each member calculates a **confidence score** (velocity) for each project, indicating how strongly they lean toward selecting it.
2. They convert this confidence into a **probability** using the logistic (S-shaped) curve — high confidence means "almost surely yes," low confidence means "almost surely no," neutral confidence means "toss a fair coin."
3. They cast a randomized vote based on this probability.
4. The swarm collectively drifts toward better subsets over time.

This is exactly what Discrete PSO does — it transforms an abstract search for *the best combination* into a probabilistic learning process.

## 1.3 Scope and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Inertia weight $w$ | **0.7298** | Momentum of previous velocity |
| Cognitive coefficient $c_1$ | **1.49618** | Pull toward personal best |
| Social coefficient $c_2$ | **1.49618** | Pull toward global best |
| $c_1 + c_2$ | **≤ 4.0** | Stability condition (Clerc & Kennedy) |
| Velocity clamp $[V_{\min}, V_{\max}]$ | **[-6, 6]** or **[-4, 4]** | Bounds the sigmoid slope |
| Swarm size $N$ | **20 to 50** | Population cardinality |
| Max iterations $T_{\max}$ | **100 to 1000** | Termination horizon |

> [!TIP]
> The values $w = 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.49618$ form the famous **Constriction Coefficient** configuration, mathematically derived to guarantee convergence of the swarm without explicit velocity clamping.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of Discrete PSO

The algorithm executes in **five distinct phases** for every iteration $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T_{\max}$.

### Phase 1 — Initialization
- Initialize particle positions $\mathbf{x}_i(0)$ randomly from $\{0, 1\}^D$.
- Initialize particle velocities $\mathbf{v}_i(0)$ randomly from $[0, 1]$ or a small range.
- Set **personal best** $\mathbf{p}_i = \mathbf{x}_i(0)$.
- Evaluate fitness $f(\mathbf{x}_i(0))$; set the global best $\mathbf{g}$ to the index of the best particle.

### Phase 2 — Velocity Update (Continuous Operation)
Despite discrete positions, the velocity remains a **real-valued quantity**, since it represents a *rate of change of probability*, not a spatial movement.

$$
v_{ij}(t+1) = w \cdot v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_1 \left(p_{ij} - x_{ij}(t)\right) + c_2 r_2 \left(g_j - x_{ij}(t)\right)
$$

where:
- $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0, 1)$ — independent uniform random numbers.
- $p_{ij}$ — $j$-th bit of particle $i$'s personal best.
- $g_j$ — $j$-th bit of the swarm's global best.

### Phase 3 — Velocity Clamping
To prevent the sigmoid saturating to 0 or 1 too early (which freezes exploration):

$$
v_{ij}(t+1) = \begin{cases} V_{\max}, & v_{ij}(t+1) > V_{\max} \\ V_{\min}, & v_{ij}(t+1) < V_{\min} \\ v_{ij}(t+1), & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $V_{\max} = \pm 6$, the sigmoid $s(\pm 6) \approx 0.9975$ and $s(\mp 6) \approx 0.0025$, ensuring probabilities stay within a useful exploration range.

### Phase 4 — Position Update (Stochastic via Sigmoid Transfer)
The velocity is mapped to a probability using the **logistic (sigmoid) function**:

$$
s\left(v_{ij}(t+1)\right) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-v_{ij}(t+1)}}
$$

Then position is sampled:

$$
x_{ij}(t+1) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \rho < s\left(v_{ij}(t+1)\right) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $\rho \sim U(0, 1)$.

### Phase 5 — Memory Update
$$
\mathbf{p}_i = \begin{cases} \mathbf{x}_i(t+1), & \text{if } f\left(\mathbf{x}_i(t+1)\right) \text{ is better than } f(\mathbf{p}_i) \\ \mathbf{p}_i, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\mathbf{g} = \underset{i}{\arg\min}\; f(\mathbf{p}_i) \quad \text{(for minimization problems)}
$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_{ij}(t+1) = w v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{ij} - x_{ij}) + c_2 r_2 (g_j - x_{ij})$ | Velocity update | Continuous |
| 2 | $s(v) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-v}}$ | Sigmoid transfer (logistic) | $s \in (0,1)$ |
| 3 | $x_{ij}(t+1) = 1$ if $\rho < s(v_{ij})$ else $0$ | Binary sampling | $\{0,1\}$ |
| 4 | $V_{\max} = +6$, $V_{\min} = -6$ | Clamp bounds | $\vert v \vert \le 6$ |
| 5 | $w = 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.49618$ | Constriction parameters | Constants |
| 6 | $\chi = \dfrac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}$, $\phi = c_1 + c_2$ | Clerc's constriction factor | $\chi \approx 0.7298$ |
| 7 | $\Delta_{ij} = c_1 r_1 (p_{ij} - x_{ij}) + c_2 r_2 (g_j - x_{ij})$ | Cognitive + social bias | Real |
| 8 | $\text{Prob}(x_{ij} = 1) = s(v_{ij})$ | Probabilistic interpretation | $(0,1)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bar (pipe) symbols** appear inside the table cells because all magnitudes use $\vert \cdot \vert$ in LaTeX — this protects the markdown table syntax from breaking.

## 2.3 Why Sigmoid? The Engineering Rationale

The sigmoid function is chosen because:

1. **Smoothness** — It is differentiable everywhere, supporting gradient-based convergence analysis.
2. **Bounded output** — $s(v) \in (0, 1)$ is a legitimate probability distribution.
3. **Monotonicity** — Larger $v_{ij}$ monotonically increases the probability of $x_{ij} = 1$.
4. **Asymptotic saturation** — As $\vert v \vert \to \infty$, probability locks to 1 or 0, mimicking "commitment."

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Discrete PSO |
|---|---|
| **Machine Learning** | Feature selection in high-dimensional datasets |
| **VLSI Design** | Circuit partitioning, gate assignment |
| **Telecommunications** | Wavelength routing, antenna array configuration |
| **Logistics** | Vehicle routing, knapsack, set cover, bin packing |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene selection, molecular docking |
| **Cyber Security** | Intrusion detection feature subset selection |
| **Power Systems** | Unit commitment (on/off decisions) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sigmoid Transfer Function from Binary Probability

We want a function $s: \mathbb{R} \to (0, 1)$ such that:

- $s(0) = 0.5$ (neutral bias).
- $s(+\infty) \to 1$, $s(-\infty) \to 0$.
- $s$ is monotonic increasing.

Starting from the canonical exponential-decay model of probability bias:

$$
P(x_{ij} = 1 \mid v_{ij}) = \frac{e^{v_{ij}}}{e^{v_{ij}} + e^{0}} = \frac{e^{v_{ij}}}{1 + e^{v_{ij}}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $e^{-v_{ij}}$:

$$
P(x_{ij} = 1) = \frac{1}{e^{-v_{ij}} + 1}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\;s(v_{ij}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-v_{ij}}}\;}
$$

This is the standard logistic function.

**Derivative (used in convergence analysis):**

$$
\frac{d s(v)}{dv} = s(v) \cdot \left[1 - s(v)\right]
$$

This derivative attains its **maximum value $0.25$ at $v = 0$** and decays to $0$ as $\vert v \vert \to \infty$. This is precisely why velocity clamping is critical — without it, most particles saturate prematurely.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Constriction Coefficient (Clerc & Kennedy, 2002)

The constriction factor $\chi$ is derived from the eigenvalues of the particle dynamics matrix. Consider the simplified 1-D recurrence (without $r_1, r_2$):

$$
v(t+1) = w v(t) + \phi (p - x(t))
$$

$$
x(t+1) = x(t) + v(t+1)
$$

Define the position-deviation $y(t) = p - x(t)$. Then:

$$
y(t+1) = -w y(t) - \phi y(t) + y(t) = (1 - w - \phi) y(t) + w y(t-1)
$$

Characteristic equation:

$$
\lambda^2 - (1 + w - \phi) \lambda + w = 0
$$

For stability, $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$. Setting the discriminant and magnitude to 1 leads to:

$$
\chi = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}, \quad \phi = c_1 + c_2
$$

Substituting $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$, $\phi = 4.1$:

$$
\chi = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - 4.1 - \sqrt{16.81 - 16.4} \vert} = \frac{2}{\vert -2.1 - 0.6403 \vert} \approx \frac{2}{2.7403} \approx 0.7298
$$

Then $w = \chi$, $c_1 = \chi \cdot 2.05 \approx 1.49618$, $c_2 = 1.49618$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Velocity Update + Position Sample

**Given:**
- Particle $i$ at iteration $t = 5$.
- $w = 0.7$, $c_1 = 1.5$, $c_2 = 1.5$.
- $r_1 = 0.42$, $r_2 = 0.88$.
- $v_{i3}(5) = -1.2$.
- $x_{i3}(5) = 0$.
- $p_{i3} = 1$, $g_3 = 1$.

**Step 1 — Velocity Update:**

$$
v_{i3}(6) = 0.7 \cdot (-1.2) + 1.5 \cdot 0.42 \cdot (1 - 0) + 1.5 \cdot 0.88 \cdot (1 - 0)
$$

$$
v_{i3}(6) = -0.84 + 0.63 + 1.32
$$

$$
v_{i3}(6) = 1.11
$$

**Step 2 — Clamp:** $1.11 \in [-6, 6]$, so no clamping. Velocity remains $v_{i3}(6) = 1.11$.

**Step 3 — Sigmoid Mapping:**

$$
s(1.11) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.11}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.3296} = \frac{1}{1.3296} \approx 0.7521
$$

**Step 4 — Sample:** Draw $\rho = 0.61$ (uniform). Since $0.61 < 0.7521$:

$$
x_{i3}(6) = 1
$$

**Interpretation:** Bit 3 of particle $i$ flips from 0 to 1 with $\approx 75\%$ probability, which materialized as a flip in this trial.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of PSO Variants

| Property | Continuous PSO | Discrete (Binary) PSO |
|---|---|---|
| Position $x$ | $\mathbb{R}^D$ | $\{0, 1\}^D$ |
| Velocity $v$ | $\mathbb{R}^D$ | $[V_{\min}, V_{\max}]^D$ |
| Position update | $x = x + v$ (deterministic) | Stochastic via sigmoid |
| Velocity interpretation | Rate of position change | Probability of bit being 1 |
| Transfer function | None (identity) | Logistic sigmoid |
| Convergence | Smooth trajectories | Probabilistic sampling |
| Best for | Continuous optimization | Combinatorial/binary problems |
| Mutation needed? | No | Optional (improved DPSO) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow of Discrete Particle Swarm Optimization

The following Mermaid diagram presents a **block-level functional architecture flow** capturing every operational decision of the DPSO loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INITIALIZE SWARM"] --> B["Set positions x_i in 0,1 to the power D randomly"]
    A --> C["Set velocities v_i in Vmin Vmax randomly"]
    A --> D["Set pbest_i = x_i for each particle"]
    A --> E["Find global best gbest"]
    B --> F["EVALUATION LOOP"]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{"ITERATION t LESS THAN Tmax"}
    G -- NO --> Z["RETURN gbest as optimal solution"]
    G -- YES --> H["FOR EACH PARTICLE i"]
    H --> I["Compute v_i using inertia cognitive and social components"]
    I --> J["CLAMP v_i to Vmin Vmax"]
    J --> K["Apply sigmoid transfer s v = 1 over 1 plus exp of minus v"]
    K --> L["Sample uniform random rho in 0 1"]
    L --> M{"rho LESS THAN s v"}
    M -- YES --> N["Set x_i = 1"]
    M -- NO --> O["Set x_i = 0"]
    N --> P["EVALUATE fitness f of x_i"]
    O --> P
    P --> Q{"f x_i BETTER THAN f pbest_i"}
    Q -- YES --> R["UPDATE pbest_i = x_i"]
    Q -- NO --> S["KEEP pbest_i unchanged"]
    R --> T["UPDATE global gbest if any pbest improved"]
    S --> T
    T --> U["INCREMENT t by 1"]
    U --> G
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Information Flow Between Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SE["STOCHASTIC ENGINE"]
        A1["Random Number Generator rho uniform 0 1"]
    end
    subgraph UP["UPDATE MODULE"]
        B1["Velocity Update Module"]
        B2["Clamping Module"]
        B3["Sigmoid Transfer Module"]
    end
    subgraph MEM["MEMORY MODULE"]
        C1["Personal Best Store pbest"]
        C2["Global Best Store gbest"]
    end
    subgraph FIT["FITNESS MODULE"]
        D1["Objective Function f x"]
    end
    SE --> B3
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    C1 --> B1
    C2 --> B1
    B3 --> D1
    D1 --> C1
    D1 --> C2
```

## 4.3 Sigmoid Curve — Probability Mapping Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["v equals minus 6"] --> A1["s equals 0.0025 Strong push to 0"]
    B["v equals minus 2"] --> B1["s equals 0.119 Lean toward 0"]
    C["v equals 0"] --> C1["s equals 0.5 Neutral coin flip"]
    D["v equals 2"] --> D1["s equals 0.881 Lean toward 1"]
    E["v equals 6"] --> E1["s equals 0.9975 Strong push to 1"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The visual reference above illustrates why DPSO bounds velocities within $[-6, +6]$ — beyond this range, the sigmoid effectively saturates, and additional velocity has no influence on the probability distribution.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Explain why the sigmoid (logistic) function is used to map velocity to probability in Discrete PSO. List its key properties relevant to the algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The sigmoid function $s(v) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-v}}$ is used because it transforms the continuous velocity $v \in \mathbb{R}$ into a valid probability $s \in (0, 1)$.

**Key Properties:**
1. **Bounded output** — Always within $(0, 1)$, satisfying axioms of probability (1 mark).
2. **Monotonic** — Increasing, so higher velocity always increases $P(x=1)$ (1 mark).
3. **Differentiable with simple derivative** $\dfrac{ds}{dv} = s(1-s)$, maximum $0.25$ at $v=0$, decays at extremes — hence the need to clamp $v$ to $[-6, 6]$ (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between continuous PSO and Discrete (Binary) PSO in terms of position representation and update mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Continuous PSO | Discrete PSO |
|---|---|---|
| **Position domain** | $x \in \mathbb{R}^D$ (1 mark) | $x \in \{0, 1\}^D$ (1 mark) |
| **Position update** | Deterministic: $x(t+1) = x(t) + v(t+1)$ | Stochastic via sigmoid: $x = 1$ if $\rho < s(v)$ else $x = 0$ (1 mark) |

The velocity update equation is structurally identical in both; only its interpretation differs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the sigmoid transfer function used in Discrete PSO from the underlying probability model. Show its derivative and explain why velocity clamping is essential.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Probabilistic Foundation (2 marks):**
Assume the probability that bit $j$ becomes 1 is proportional to the exponential of its "confidence" velocity $v_{ij}$:

$$
P(x_{ij} = 1 \mid v_{ij}) = \frac{e^{v_{ij}}}{e^{v_{ij}} + e^{0}} = \frac{e^{v_{ij}}}{1 + e^{v_{ij}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Algebraic Simplification (2 marks):**
Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{-v_{ij}}$:

$$
s(v_{ij}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-v_{ij}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Derivative and Saturation Behaviour (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{ds}{dv} = s(v) [1 - s(v)]
$$

Maximum slope $0.25$ at $v = 0$. As $v \to +\infty$, $s \to 1$, derivative $\to 0$.

**Step 4 — Justification for Velocity Clamping (1 mark):**
Without clamping, velocities blow up, pushing $s$ to saturation extremes (0 or 1). The particle loses its ability to flip bits adaptively. Standard clamp $[-6, +6]$ keeps probabilities in the useful range $[0.0025, 0.9975]$.

---

**(b) A particle in a 5-D binary search space has the current state given below. Compute the new velocity $v_{i3}$, apply clamping, derive the probability $s(v_{i3})$, and determine the new bit $x_{i3}$.** (7 marks)

Given:
- $w = 0.7298$, $c_1 = 1.49618$, $c_2 = 1.49618$.
- $r_1 = 0.35$, $r_2 = 0.78$.
- $x_{i3} = 0$, $p_{i3} = 1$, $g_3 = 1$.
- $v_{i3} = 1.8$, $V_{\max} = 4.0$, $V_{\min} = -4.0$.
- Sampled random number $\rho = 0.55$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Velocity Update (3 marks):**

$$
v_{i3}(t+1) = 0.7298 \cdot 1.8 + 1.49618 \cdot 0.35 \cdot (1 - 0) + 1.49618 \cdot 0.78 \cdot (1 - 0)
$$

$$
v_{i3}(t+1) = 1.31364 + 0.52366 + 1.16702
$$

$$
v_{i3}(t+1) = 3.00432
$$

**Step 2 — Clamping (1 mark):** Since $3.00432 \in [-4, +4]$, no clamping needed. $v_{i3}(t+1) = 3.00432$.

**Step 3 — Sigmoid Probability (2 marks):**

$$
s(3.00432) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-3.00432}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.04956} = \frac{1}{1.04956} \approx 0.9528
$$

**Step 4 — Position Sample (1 mark):**
Since $\rho = 0.55 < 0.9528$:

$$
x_{i3}(t+1) = 1
$$

The third bit flips from 0 → 1 with $\approx 95.3\%$ confidence, which manifested as a flip in this draw.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the role of inertia weight $w$, cognitive coefficient $c_1$, and social coefficient $c_2$ in Discrete PSO. How does the constriction factor formulation of Clerc & Kennedy improve algorithm stability?** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Inertia Weight $w$ (2 marks):**
$w$ controls how much of the previous velocity is retained. Higher $w$ encourages exploration (global search), while lower $w$ favors exploitation (local refinement). Standard choice: $w = 0.7298$.

**Step 2 — Cognitive Coefficient $c_1$ (1 mark):**
$c_1$ scales the pull toward the particle's own best-known position $\mathbf{p}_i$. Represents *individual learning* or *self-confidence*.

**Step 3 — Social Coefficient $c_2$ (1 mark):**
$c_2$ scales the pull toward the swarm's global best $\mathbf{g}$. Represents *social learning* or *herd behavior*.

**Step 4 — Constriction Factor (3 marks):**
Clerc & Kennedy showed that introducing a multiplier $\chi$ such that:

$$
v_{ij}(t+1) = \chi \left[ v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{ij} - x_{ij}) + c_2 r_2 (g_j - x_{ij}) \right]
$$

with $\chi = \dfrac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}$ and $\phi = c_1 + c_2$, guarantees convergence by ensuring all eigenvalues of the particle-dynamics matrix satisfy $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$. The standard values $w = 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.49618$ are direct outputs of this analysis, providing **stability without requiring explicit velocity clamping**.

---

**(b) Apply two iterations of Discrete PSO on the following 3-bit binary optimization problem. Use the fitness function $f(x) = 4x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3$. Initialize particle with $x = [1, 0, 1]$, $v = [0.5, -1.0, 0.8]$. Assume $p_{best} = [1, 1, 0]$ and $g_{best} = [1, 1, 1]$. Use $w = 0.5$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.0$, $r_1 = r_2 = 0.5$ every step. $V_{max} = 4$.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1 — Bit 1:**
- $v_1' = 0.5 \cdot 0.5 + 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1 - 1) + 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1 - 1) = 0.25$.
- $s(0.25) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-0.25}} = \dfrac{1}{1.7788} = 0.5622$.
- Sample $\rho_1 = 0.50 < 0.5622 \Rightarrow x_1 = 1$.

**Iteration 1 — Bit 2:**
- $v_2' = 0.5 \cdot (-1.0) + 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1 - 0) + 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1 - 0) = 0.5$.
- $s(0.5) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-0.5}} = \dfrac{1}{1.6065} = 0.6225$.
- Sample $\rho_2 = 0.50 < 0.6225 \Rightarrow x_2 = 1$.

**Iteration 1 — Bit 3:**
- $v_3' = 0.5 \cdot 0.8 + 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (0 - 1) + 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1 - 1) = -0.1$.
- $s(-0.1) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{0.1}} = \dfrac{1}{1.1052} = 0.4750$.
- Sample $\rho_3 = 0.50 > 0.4750 \Rightarrow x_3 = 0$.

**New position after Iteration 1:** $\mathbf{x} = [1, 1, 0]$, $f = 4 + 2 + 0 = 6$.

**Iteration 2 — Apply same procedure starting from updated state.** *(Examiner accepts any valid step-by-step sampling.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently lose marks** by forgetting the **clamping step** before sigmoid evaluation. Always check $\vert v \vert \le V_{\max}$ before applying $s(v)$.
> - Many omit the **personal best update logic** — only update $\mathbf{p}_i$ if the new fitness is *strictly better*, not just different.
> - In multi-bit examples, examiners expect **each bit processed independently** with possibly different sampled $\rho$ values. Do not reuse the same random number.
> - **Sign error trap**: $p_{ij} - x_{ij}$ is $+1$ if pbest has 1 and current has 0 (encouraging flip toward 1). Getting this wrong inverts the entire swarm dynamics.
> - Marks are awarded for **explicit intermediate values** — do not jump from the formula to the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DPSO (Binary PSO)** was proposed by **Kennedy & Eberhart (1997)** as a discrete adaptation of continuous PSO for combinatorial problems.
- Particle **position is binary** ($x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$), but **velocity is continuous** ($v_{ij} \in [V_{\min}, V_{\max}]$).
- The **sigmoid function** $s(v) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-v}}$ converts velocity to probability of bit being 1.
- **Position update is stochastic**: $x_{ij} = 1$ if $\rho < s(v_{ij})$, else $0$, where $\rho \sim U(0, 1)$.
- **Velocity update equation is structurally identical** to continuous PSO but **interpreted differently** (as a probability bias).
- The **constriction coefficient** configuration ($w = 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.49618$) guarantees convergence.
- **Velocity clamping** at $[-6, +6]$ (or $[-4, +4]$) is essential to prevent sigmoid saturation and preserve exploration.
- **Personal best (pbest)** is the best position a particle has ever occupied; **global best (gbest)** is the best across the swarm.
- **Applications**: feature selection, knapsack, unit commitment, set cover, VLSI partitioning, gene selection.
- **Key derivative**: $\dfrac{ds}{dv} = s(v)[1 - s(v)]$, with maximum slope $0.25$ at $v = 0$.
- **Major pitfall**: forgetting the clamp, mixing up $p_{ij} - x_{ij}$ sign, or reusing the same random number for multiple bits.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variants and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Heuristics and Solution Techniques**, the term **"Variants"** refers to *modified, enhanced, or specialized versions of a parent metaheuristic algorithm* that adapt the original search mechanism (neighbourhood exploration, acceptance criterion, parameter control, or solution representation) to overcome specific weaknesses such as premature convergence, slow cooling, parameter sensitivity, or constraint-handling limitations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEIET614, Module 2):**
> A *variant* of a metaheuristic is a structurally faithful yet parametrically or operationally altered derivative of the canonical algorithm (e.g., Simulated Annealing, Genetic Algorithm, Tabu Search) designed to improve performance on a particular problem class — combinatorial, continuous, dynamic, or multi-objective.

The term **"Applications"** denotes the *mapping of these heuristic variants onto real-world engineering and decision-making problems* — such as VLSI design, job-shop scheduling, travelling salesman routing, network design, and parameter estimation — where exact deterministic methods (branch-and-bound, dynamic programming, linear/integer programming) become computationally intractable due to **NP-hardness**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Mountain Climber at Dusk":**
>
> Imagine a lost mountaineer in thick fog trying to descend from a peak to the lowest valley. The mountain represents the **solution landscape**, the altitude represents the **objective function value** (cost), and the fog prevents a global view.
>
> - In a **greedy descent (local search)**, the climber always takes the steepest step downward — but may get trapped in a shallow foothill (local optimum).
> - In **Simulated Annealing (SA)**, the climber sometimes accepts an uphill step with a probability controlled by a "temperature" that gradually cools down — early in the journey (high temperature), bold uphill moves are accepted (exploration); near the base (low temperature), only downhill steps are accepted (exploitation).
> - **Variants of SA** are like different climbing strategies — some carry a compass (deterministic threshold), some follow a fixed schedule (logarithmic cooling), and some adapt their pace based on terrain difficulty (adaptive cooling).
> - **Applications** are the actual mountains — Everest (TSP), K2 (scheduling), and the Andes (VLSI layout).

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Parameters (Bolded Constants)

- **Boltzmann Constant (k_B)** — connects thermodynamic probability with algorithmic acceptance.
- **Initial Temperature T₀** — typically set so that initial acceptance probability ≈ **0.8** for uphill moves of average magnitude.
- **Cooling Factor α** — geometric cooling uses **0.8 ≤ α ≤ 0.99**.
- **Markov Chain Length L_k** — number of iterations at each temperature plateau.
- **Freezing Temperature T_f** — final temperature (often **≈ 0** or a small ε).

### 1.4 Classification of Variants (High-Level Map)

| Category | Representative Variant | Core Modification |
|----------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Acceptance-Modified** | Threshold Accepting (TA), Great Deluge (GD), Record-to-Record Travel (RTR) | Replaces probabilistic acceptance with deterministic threshold |
| **Cooling-Modified** | Fast SA, Adaptive SA, Re-annealing SA | Alters cooling schedule to balance speed vs quality |
| **Memory-Augmented** | Tabu SA, Population-based SA | Adds short/long-term memory structures |
| **Neighbourhood-Modified** | Variable Neighbourhood SA | Dynamically expands/shrinks neighbourhood |
| **Hybrid** | GA-SA, PSO-SA, ACO-SA | Couples two metaheuristics for co-evolution |
