> 5. **Not Justifying the "Worse Move Acceptance":** If asked why TS allows worse moves, simply saying "to escape local optima" is insufficient. Mention the **Tabu List memory mechanism** that prevents the search from cycling.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Tabu Search Principles**

- **Definition:** Tabu Search is a **memory-based trajectory metaheuristic** by Fred Glover (1986) that uses short-term and long-term memory to escape local optima.
- **Tabu List:** FIFO queue storing **move attributes** (not full solutions) of recently executed moves; size = **Tabu Tenure $\mathcal{T}$**.
- **Tabu Tenure $\mathcal{T}$:** Number of iterations a move remains forbidden. Common rule: $\mathcal{T} = \lfloor 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \rfloor \times b$, where $b$ is the neighborhood size.
- **Aspiration Criterion:** A tabu move is admissible if $f(s') < f(s^*_{\text{best}})$.
- **Selection Rule:** $s_{t+1} = \arg\min_{s' \in \mathcal{C}_t} f(s')$ — best **admissible** neighbor, not just best neighbor.
- **Accepts Worse Moves:** Yes, by design. This is the primary mechanism for escaping local optima.
- **Intensification:** Uses **long-term memory of elite solutions** to focus search on promising regions.
- **Diversification:** Uses **frequency-based memory** to penalize overused moves and force exploration of new regions.
- **Three Memory Types:** Short-Term (Tabu List), Long-Term (Elite Solutions), Frequency-Based (Move Usage Counts).
- **Stopping Criteria:** Fixed iteration budget $t_{\max}$, or convergence detection (no improvement over last $k$ iterations).
- **Tuning Parameters:** Tabu Tenure $\mathcal{T}$, aspiration level, intensity of diversification penalty, neighborhood size.
- **Contrast with Simulated Annealing:** SA accepts worse moves **probabilistically** (temperature-driven); TS accepts them **deterministically** (best admissible move rule).
- **Contrast with Local Search:** Local search stops at first local minimum; TS uses memory to push past it.
- **KTU Memory Tip:** Remember the formula triplet — **N (Neighborhood) → L (Tabu List) → A (Aspiration)** — the three pillars of any TS answer.
- **Common Applications:** Job-shop scheduling, vehicle routing, VLSI design, graph coloring, portfolio optimization, and telecom network design.
- **Key Advantage:** Highly effective for **large-scale combinatorial** problems where exact methods are intractable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neighbourhood
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Neighbourhood

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the formal language of combinatorial optimization and metaheuristics, a **Neighbourhood** is a fundamental structural construct that defines the local topology of the solution search space. For any given candidate solution $x$ belonging to a feasible solution set $S$, the neighbourhood $N(x)$ is mathematically defined as the set of all solutions that can be generated from $x$ by applying exactly one elementary perturbation, transformation, or *move* operation drawn from a predefined operator set $M$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *Neighbourhood* $N(x)$ of a solution $x \in S$ is the set of all feasible solutions obtained by applying a single move operator $m \in M$ to $x$. The neighbourhood structure is the cornerstone of every local search, simulated annealing, and tabu search algorithm.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spider's Web" Intuition

Imagine you are a spider sitting at the center of your web. Your current position represents your **current solution** $x$. The strands of the web radiating outward represent all the **possible moves** you could make. Every strand connects you to an immediate neighbour — a position exactly one step away. The collection of all those immediately reachable positions forms your **neighbourhood** $N(x)$.

- You cannot teleport to a point on the far edge of the web — that would require multiple moves.
- If you shrink your web (fewer strands), your neighbourhood is small and the search is greedy.
- If you expand your web (more strands), your neighbourhood is large and the search is exploratory.

This perfectly mirrors the central engineering trade-off in heuristic design: **small neighbourhood = fast but myopic**, **large neighbourhood = thorough but expensive**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU 2024)

| Metric | Symbol | Default Value Convention |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Neighbourhood size | $\vert N(x) \vert$ | Cardinality — number of neighbours |
| Move operator | $m \in M$ | Defines permissible perturbations |
| Search space dimension | $n$ | Problem size (cities, jobs, bits, etc.) |
| Neighbourhood radius | $r$ | Number of moves applied in sequence |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In KTU board examinations, when a question asks "define neighbourhood", always mention the *move operator* explicitly. A neighbourhood without specifying the move is mathematically incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Neighbourhood Concept

The neighbourhood construct rests on three foundational pillars that examiners at KTU consistently test:

1. **The Move Operator ($M$)** — A deterministic or stochastic rule that transforms one solution into another. Examples include bit-flip (binary problems), swap (permutation problems), insertion, and inversion.

2. **Feasibility Filtering** — Not every candidate generated by a move is acceptable. A valid neighbourhood filters out infeasible solutions (e.g., violating capacity constraints in VRP) or repairs them using a penalty / repair function.

3. **Symmetry vs Asymmetry** — A neighbourhood is **symmetric** if $y \in N(x) \Leftrightarrow x \in N(y)$. Most classical operators (swap, 2-opt) are symmetric, which simplifies convergence proofs.

### 2.2 Classification of Neighbourhood Structures

- **Static vs Dynamic** — Static neighbourhoods are fixed a priori; dynamic neighbourhoods change during the search (e.g., Variable Neighbourhood Search).
- **Uniform vs Variable Sized** — Uniform means $\vert N(x) \vert$ is constant for all $x$; variable means it depends on the local structure of $x$.
- **Connected vs Disconnected** — A connected neighbourhood ensures the search graph forms a single component, guaranteeing theoretical reachability of the global optimum.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Formulation | Typical Magnitude (for TSP with $n$ cities) | Engineering Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Neighbourhood definition | $N(x) = \{ y \in S \mid \exists m \in M: y = m(x) \}$ | — | Universal — every local-search heuristic |
| Neighbourhood size (k-opt) | $\vert N(x) \vert = \dfrac{n!}{(n-k)! \cdot 2^{k} \cdot k!}$ | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ for 2-opt | Vehicle routing, circuit design |
| Single swap size | $\vert N(x) \vert = \binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $O(n^{2})$ | Permutation scheduling |
| Insertion move size | $\vert N(x) \vert = n(n-1)$ | $O(n^{2})$ | TSP, sequence-dependent setup |
| Inversion (2-opt like) | $\vert N(x) \vert = \binom{n}{2}$ | $O(n^{2})$ | DNA sequencing, TSP |
| Bit-flip (binary) | $\vert N(x) \vert = n$ | $O(n)$ | Knapsack, SAT, feature selection |
| Neighbourhood union (VNS) | $N_{k_{1}} \cup N_{k_{2}} \cup \dots \cup N_{k_{\max}}$ | Variable | Variable Neighbourhood Search |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Warning for Tables:** In KTU examination answer sheets (and within this note), always write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ — never using the vertical pipe `|` inside markdown tables, as it breaks the rendering engine used by KTU's digital evaluation platforms.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Neighbourhood Structures

The neighbourhood concept is the **engineer's compass** in combinatorial optimization. Without it:

- **Logistics & Supply Chain**: TSP/VRP solvers (used by Amazon, FedEx) cannot enumerate nearby tours.
- **VLSI Design**: Placement and routing algorithms cannot explore alternative wire layouts.
- **Manufacturing Scheduling**: Job-shop scheduling heuristics cannot evaluate swap-of-two-jobs moves.
- **Bioinformatics**: DNA fragment assembly relies on inversion-based neighbourhoods.
- **Machine Learning Hyperparameter Tuning**: Discrete search spaces treat each hyperparameter tweak as a move.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Neighbourhood Size for a 2-opt Move on the TSP

The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) is the canonical KTU example. We derive the neighbourhood size rigorously.

**Starting Premise:** A TSP tour visits $n$ cities exactly once and returns to the start. A 2-opt move deletes two non-adjacent edges and reconnects the resulting two paths in the only other possible way.

**Step 1 — Identify the number of edges in a complete graph on $n$ vertices:**
The complete graph $K_{n}$ contains all possible pairwise connections.

$$
E(K_{n}) = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Count the number of ways to select 2 edges from $E(K_{n})$:**
We must choose 2 edges to remove.

$$
\text{Number of edge pairs} = \binom{E(K_{n})}{2} = \binom{n(n-1)/2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract invalid configurations:**
We must exclude:
- (a) Adjacent edge pairs (sharing a vertex) — these give the same tour back.
- (b) Pairs that produce disconnected tours.

After combinatorial simplification (omitting the lengthy but standard inclusion-exclusion for KTU exam brevity but requiring familiarity), the resulting 2-opt neighbourhood size for a single tour $x$ simplifies to:

$$
\vert N_{2\text{-opt}}(x) \vert = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical implication:**
For $n = 50$ cities, $\vert N \vert = 1225$ neighbours per solution. This is why KTU examiners emphasize that local search at every iteration evaluates all neighbours — the cost is $O(n^{2})$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Neighbourhood Size for an Insertion Move

**Step 1 — Choose the element to remove:** $n$ choices.
**Step 2 — Choose the new position to insert it:** $n - 1$ remaining slots.
**Step 3 — Multiply by the rule of product:**

$$
\vert N_{\text{insert}}(x) \vert = n \times (n-1) = n(n-1)
$$

**Step 4 — Verification at $n=4$:** $4 \times 3 = 12$ distinct insertions. Manual enumeration of tour $\{1,2,3,4\}$ confirms exactly 12 valid insertion neighbours.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Move Type | Neighbourhood Size | Best Suited For | Search Character |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Bit-flip | $n$ | Binary problems (SAT, Knapsack) | Aggressive, fast convergence |
| 1-swap | $n(n-1)/2$ | Permutations (TSP, scheduling) | Balanced |
| Insertion | $n(n-1)$ | Sequencing with priority | Smooth landscape |
| 2-opt | $n(n-1)/2$ | TSP, routing | Moderate escape from local optima |
| Or-opt | $O(n^{2})$ | VRP with clusters | Cluster-aware |
| 3-opt | $O(n^{3})$ | Large-scale TSP | Expensive but powerful |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Compute the neighbourhood size under a 2-opt move for a TSP instance with $n = 20$ cities.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert N_{2\text{-opt}}(x) \vert &= \binom{20}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{20 \times 19}{2} \\[4pt]
&= 190 \text{ neighbours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** At every iteration of the local search, the algorithm must evaluate the cost function $f(y)$ for all 190 candidate solutions $y \in N(x)$. If each evaluation costs $O(n)$, the total per-iteration complexity is $O(n^{3}) = 8000$ operations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Neighbourhood Topology — Mermaid Schematic

The following diagram illustrates the local search graph: nodes represent feasible solutions, and edges represent single-move transitions between neighbours.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeX["Current Solution X"]
    nodeY1["Neighbour Y1 via swap 1-2"]
    nodeY2["Neighbour Y2 via swap 1-3"]
    nodeY3["Neighbour Y3 via swap 2-3"]
    nodeY4["Neighbour Y4 via insertion"]
    nodeY5["Neighbour Y5 via 2-opt"]
    nodeZ["Outside Neighbourhood Z"]

    nodeX --> nodeY1
    nodeX --> nodeY2
    nodeX --> nodeY3
    nodeX --> nodeY4
    nodeX --> nodeY5
    nodeY1 -.-> nodeZ
    nodeY2 -.-> nodeZ
    nodeY3 -.-> nodeZ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows are legal single-move transitions (these constitute $N(x)$). The dashed arrow to $Z$ indicates $Z$ is **not** in the neighbourhood — it requires at least two moves to reach from $X$.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Neighbourhood Search Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["Input Solution x in S"]
    blockB["Move Operator Generator M"]
    blockC["Feasibility Filter"]
    blockD["Neighbourhood Set N of x"]
    blockE["Cost Evaluator f of y"]
    blockF["Selection Logic Best or Probabilistic"]
    outputG["New Current Solution x prime"]

    inputA --> blockB
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
    blockE --> blockF
    blockF --> outputG
    outputG -.feedback loop.-> inputA
```

### 4.3 Variable Neighbourhood Search (VNS) — Multi-Radius Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    radius1["N sub k1 small radius"]
    radius2["N sub k2 medium radius"]
    radius3["N sub k3 large radius"]
    radius4["N sub kmax maximum radius"]

    radius1 --> radius2
    radius2 --> radius3
    radius3 --> radius4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term *neighbourhood* in the context of heuristic search. State any two properties of a good neighbourhood structure.**

**Model Answer:**
A neighbourhood $N(x)$ of a solution $x$ is the set of all solutions reachable from $x$ by applying exactly one predefined move operator $m \in M$. Formally,

$$
N(x) = \{ y \in S \mid \exists \, m \in M, \; y = m(x) \}
$$

**Two desirable properties:**
1. **Reasonable Size** — $\vert N(x) \vert$ should be large enough to escape local optima but small enough for tractable evaluation.
2. **Connectivity** — The union of all neighbourhoods should make the search space a connected graph, guaranteeing that every solution is reachable from every other solution through a finite sequence of moves.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Compare 1-opt (swap) and 2-opt neighbourhoods for the Travelling Salesman Problem with $n$ cities. Which is more powerful and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | 1-opt (Swap) | 2-opt |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Moves 2 elements | Removes crossing edges | |
| Neighbourhood size | $\binom{n}{2}$ | $\binom{n}{2}$ |
| Escape from local optima | Weak | Strong |
| Computational cost per move | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |

2-opt is more powerful because it can eliminate edge crossings that 1-opt cannot resolve, leading to structurally shorter tours and fewer local optima traps.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of neighbourhood in heuristic optimization. Derive the neighbourhood size for the following move operators on a permutation of $n$ elements: (i) single swap, (ii) single insertion, (iii) 2-opt.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a TSP instance with 15 cities, compute the size of the 2-opt neighbourhood and discuss the practical implication of this size on the running time of a basic local search algorithm that evaluates every neighbour at each iteration.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Concept and Derivations:**

**Conceptual Explanation:**
A neighbourhood $N(x)$ of a solution $x$ is defined as the set of all feasible solutions obtained by applying exactly one move from a predefined operator set $M$.

**Derivation (i) — Single Swap on a Permutation of $n$ Elements:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Choose the first position} &: n \text{ choices} \\
\text{Choose the second position} &: (n-1) \text{ choices} \\
\text{Eliminate double counting} &: \div 2 \\
\therefore \quad \vert N_{\text{swap}}(x) \vert &= \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation (ii) — Single Insertion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Choose element to remove} &: n \text{ choices} \\
\text{Choose new insertion position} &: (n-1) \text{ choices} \\
\therefore \quad \vert N_{\text{insert}}(x) \vert &= n(n-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation (iii) — 2-opt:**

In a TSP tour represented as a cyclic permutation, a 2-opt move removes two non-adjacent edges and reconnects the path in the alternative way. The number of valid 2-opt moves equals the number of unordered edge pairs that are not adjacent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert N_{2\text{-opt}}(x) \vert &= \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Computation for $n = 15$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert N_{2\text{-opt}}(x) \vert &= \binom{15}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{15 \times 14}{2} \\[4pt]
&= 105 \text{ neighbours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Implication:**
A basic hill-climbing local search evaluates all 105 neighbours at each iteration to find the best improvement. If the cost function evaluation takes $O(n)$ time, then:

$$
\text{Per-iteration cost} = 105 \times O(15) \approx 1575 \text{ operations}
$$

For 1000 iterations, this totals $\approx 1.575 \times 10^{6}$ operations — feasible for offline planning but prohibitive for real-time embedded systems. This motivates **candidate list strategies** (e.g., nearest-neighbour restriction) to prune the neighbourhood in practice.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the concept of Variable Neighbourhood Search (VNS). How does changing the neighbourhood radius during the search improve algorithm performance?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider a binary optimization problem with $n = 12$ bits using the bit-flip neighbourhood. Calculate the neighbourhood size and explain why such problems are well-suited for Simulated Annealing.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Variable Neighbourhood Search (VNS) Explanation:**

VNS systematically changes the neighbourhood structure $N_{k}(x)$ during the search, where $k \in \{k_{1}, k_{2}, \dots, k_{\max}\}$ defines the radius of perturbation.

**Algorithm Skeleton (Conceptual):**
1. Find initial solution $x$.
2. Repeat until termination:
   - Set $k \leftarrow k_{1}$.
   - Generate $x'$ randomly from $N_{k}(x)$ (shaking phase).
   - Apply local search to $x'$ to obtain $x''$.
   - If $f(x'') < f(x)$, set $x \leftarrow x''$ and reset $k$.
   - Else, increment $k$.

**Why VNS Improves Performance:**
- **Diversification** — Larger neighbourhoods explore distant regions, escaping local optima.
- **Intensification** — Smaller neighbourhoods refine promising solutions.
- **No parameter tuning** — The structured sequence of radii avoids manual calibration.

**(b) Binary Problem with $n = 12$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert N_{\text{bit-flip}}(x) \vert &= n = 12 \text{ neighbours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Simulated Annealing (SA) is Suitable:**
1. The neighbourhood is small ($O(n)$), allowing exhaustive evaluation within each temperature step.
2. SA's probabilistic acceptance criterion (Metropolis rule) lets the algorithm accept worse neighbours with probability $\exp(-\Delta f / T)$, providing the **escape mechanism** that pure hill-climbing lacks.
3. The cooling schedule gradually tightens acceptance, transitioning from exploration to exploitation — perfectly aligned with the small but well-structured bit-flip neighbourhood.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** "neighbourhood" with "neighbourhood size" — the former is a set, the latter is its cardinality. Examiners deduct marks for interchange.
> 2. **Always specify the move operator** when defining $N(x)$. Writing "the neighbourhood of $x$" without stating $M$ is incomplete and loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Unit confusion:** Neighbourhood size is dimensionless (a count), never in seconds or joules.
> 4. **Common derivation error:** Students often write $\binom{n}{2}$ without showing the combinatorial reasoning — always include the *selection logic* for full marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $N(x) = \{ y \in S \mid y = m(x), \; m \in M \}$
- **Neighbourhood size** is the cardinality $\vert N(x) \vert$, a dimensionless integer.
- **Swap on permutation:** $\vert N \vert = \binom{n}{2}$
- **Insertion on permutation:** $\vert N \vert = n(n-1)$
- **2-opt on TSP:** $\vert N \vert = \binom{n}{2}$
- **Bit-flip on binary string:** $\vert N \vert = n$
- **Properties of a good neighbourhood:** reasonable size, connectivity, symmetry (desirable), locality-preserving.
- **Static vs Dynamic neighbourhoods:** Static is fixed (used in SA, basic LS); Dynamic changes during search (used in VNS, ALNS).
- **Symmetry property:** $y \in N(x) \Leftrightarrow x \in N(y)$ — true for most classical operators.
- **Engineering trade-off:** Small $\vert N \vert$ = fast but traps in local optima; Large $\vert N \vert$ = slow but escapes optima.
- **VNS** uses multiple radii $N_{k_{1}}, N_{k_{2}}, \dots$ to balance intensification and diversification.
- **Always specify the move operator** $M$ in any KTU answer for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Candidate List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Candidate List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 : CANDIDATE LIST

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the field of **heuristics and metaheuristic optimization**, a **Candidate List (CL)** is a strategically filtered subset of feasible elements, decisions, or partial solutions from which the next construction step of an algorithm draws its choice. When this filtering is governed by a quality threshold or cardinality bound, it is formally termed a **Restricted Candidate List (RCL)** — the central data structure introduced by **Feo and Resende** within the **GRASP (Greedy Randomized Adaptive Search Procedure)** framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEIET614 - Module 3):**
> The *Candidate List Strategy* is the cornerstone of the **construction phase** in any constructive metaheuristic. It balances the **determinism of greedy selection** with the **stochasticity of random selection**, enabling the algorithm to sample diverse, high-quality regions of the search space.

### 1.2 Mathematical Statement

Let $C$ be the set of all currently feasible candidate elements (unvisited cities, unscheduled jobs, unselected items, etc.), and let $c(e)$ denote the **greedy function value** (incremental cost or benefit) of element $e \in C$. The Restricted Candidate List is defined as:

$$
\text{RCL} = \{ e \in C \mid c(e) \text{ satisfies the selection threshold} \}
$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy : The Interview Shortlist**
> Imagine you are hiring for one position and receive **10,000 applications**. A *purely greedy* HR approach would automatically select the single applicant with the highest test score — fast, but you may miss a creative genius. A *purely random* approach is unfair and risky. The **Candidate List Strategy** is analogous to the HR department creating a **shortlist of the top 50 candidates** (the RCL), and then **randomly interviewing one** from that shortlist. The shortlist ensures **quality**, and the random draw ensures **diversity and fairness** — exactly what GRASP achieves in optimization.

### 1.4 Why Candidate Lists Matter

| Aspect | Pure Greedy | Pure Random | **Candidate List (RCL)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solution Quality | High (locally) | Unpredictable | Consistently high |
| Diversity | Very Low | Very High | **Tunable (Balanced)** |
| Risk of Local Optimum | Very High | Low | **Reduced Significantly** |
| Computation Cost | Low | Low | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** The Candidate List is *the* mechanism that converts a deterministic greedy algorithm into a **probabilistic** one without sacrificing solution quality. Examiners frequently test this concept through the GRASP lens.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Fundamental Constructions of the RCL

The Restricted Candidate List can be built using **two principal strategies**, both of which are **high-yield KTU topics**.

#### A. Value-Based (Quality-Based) RCL

The most widely-used formulation. A threshold $T$ is computed using a parameter $\alpha \in [0, 1]$, and only candidates whose greedy values lie within the "elite band" are admitted.

$$
\text{RCL}_{\text{value}} = \left\{ e \in C \;\middle|\; c(e) \in \left[c_{\min},\; c_{\min} + \alpha \left(c_{\max} - c_{\min}\right) \right] \right\}
$$

where:
- $c_{\min} = \min_{e \in C} c(e)$
- $c_{\max} = \max_{e \in C} c(e)$
- $\alpha$ = **greedy-random balance parameter**, $\alpha \in [0, 1]$

#### B. Cardinality-Based RCL

A simpler alternative where exactly the **top-$k$ ranked candidates** by greedy value are admitted, regardless of their absolute numerical spread.

$$
\text{RCL}_{\text{card}} = \{ e \in C \mid e \text{ is among the } k \text{ best elements ranked by } c(e) \}
$$

where $k$ is a fixed positive integer (typically $k = 3$ to $k = 15$).

### 2.2 Role of the Parameter $\alpha$

The parameter $\alpha$ is the **tuning knob** that controls the **intensification–diversification spectrum**.

| Value of $\alpha$ | Behaviour | RCL Size | Algorithm Character |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha = 0$ | Only the single best candidate is selected | $\vert \text{RCL} \vert = 1$ | Pure Greedy |
| $0 < \alpha < 1$ | Mix of top-ranked candidates | $\vert \text{RCL} \vert > 1$ | **GRASP Regime** |
| $\alpha = 1$ | All candidates admitted | $\vert \text{RCL} \vert = \vert C \vert$ | Pure Random |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula reference** for the Candidate List topic. All symbols are KTU-conformant.

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Typical Range / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Master candidate set | Finite set of feasible elements |
| $c(e)$ | Greedy function value of element $e$ | Cost or benefit score |
| $\text{RCL}$ | Restricted Candidate List | Subset of $C$ |
| $c_{\min}$ | Minimum greedy value in $C$ | Numeric (e.g., km, ₹, hours) |
| $c_{\max}$ | Maximum greedy value in $C$ | Numeric |
| $\alpha$ | Greediness-randomness parameter | $[0, 1]$ (dimensionless) |
| $k$ | Cardinality bound | Positive integer, typically $3 \le k \le 15$ |
| $T$ | Threshold value (value-based RCL) | $T = c_{\min} + \alpha(c_{\max} - c_{\min})$ |
| $\vert \text{RCL} \vert$ | Size of restricted candidate list | $\ge 1$ |
| $f(x)$ | Objective function value of solution $x$ | Problem-specific |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Candidate List Strategy

| **Engineering Domain** | **Application of RCL** |
|---|---|
| Vehicle Routing (TSP/VRP) | Selecting next city to visit |
| Job-Shop Scheduling | Selecting next job to schedule on a machine |
| VLSI Circuit Design | Selecting next module to place on the chip |
| Network Design | Selecting next node/link to include in the spanning tree |
| Project Portfolio Selection | Selecting next project to fund within a budget |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world takeaway:** Almost every industrial *constructive heuristic* — from Google's route optimization to airline crew scheduling — uses some form of restricted candidate list to ensure both quality and computational tractability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Value-Based RCL Threshold

The threshold $T$ that defines the upper bound of the value-based RCL is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Identify the Extreme Greedy Values**

$$
c_{\min} = \min_{e \in C} c(e), \qquad c_{\max} = \max_{e \in C} c(e)
$$

This finds the best and worst greedy scores currently available in the candidate pool.

**Step 2 — Define the Normalized Range**

$$
\Delta c = c_{\max} - c_{\min}
$$

This is the *spread* of greedy values — a measure of how heterogeneous the candidates are.

**Step 3 — Apply the $\alpha$-Weighted Band**

$$
T = c_{\min} + \alpha \cdot \Delta c = c_{\min} + \alpha \left(c_{\max} - c_{\min}\right)
$$

The threshold $T$ sits at fraction $\alpha$ of the way from $c_{\min}$ toward $c_{\max}$.

**Step 4 — Construct the RCL**

$$
\text{RCL} = \{ e \in C \mid c_{\min} \le c(e) \le T \}
$$

**Step 5 — Random Selection from RCL**

$$
e^{*} \leftarrow \text{UniformRandomSample}(\text{RCL})
$$

The chosen element $e^{*}$ is added to the partial solution, the candidate set $C$ is updated, and the loop repeats until a complete solution is formed.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem:** A salesperson is currently at City 0 and must choose the next city to visit. The distances (in km) from City 0 to each of the 6 unvisited cities are:

$$
\{ 35,\; 110,\; 75,\; 180,\; 95,\; 220 \}
$$

Construct the **value-based Restricted Candidate List** with $\alpha = 0.4$.

**Step 1 — Sort the candidates by greedy value (ascending)**

$$
\text{Sorted } c(e): \{35,\; 75,\; 95,\; 110,\; 180,\; 220\}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify extremes**

$$
c_{\min} = 35 \text{ km}, \qquad c_{\max} = 220 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the range**

$$
\Delta c = 220 - 35 = 185 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the threshold**

$$
T = c_{\min} + \alpha \cdot \Delta c = 35 + (0.4)(185) = 35 + 74 = 109 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 5 — Build the RCL**

Select every $e$ such that $c(e) \le 109$:

$$
\text{RCL} = \{\, \text{City A (35 km)},\; \text{City C (75 km)},\; \text{City E (95 km)} \,\}
$$

**Step 6 — Random Selection**

Suppose the random draw picks **City E (95 km)**. The partial route becomes `0 → E`, the candidate set is updated (City E removed, new distances computed from E), and the loop continues.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Impact of $\alpha$ on RCL Size

| $\alpha$ | Threshold $T$ (km) | $\vert \text{RCL} \vert$ | Cities in RCL |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0.0$ | $35.00$ | $1$ | A (35) |
| $0.2$ | $72.00$ | $2$ | A (35), C (75) |
| **$0.4$** | **$109.00$** | **$3$** | **A (35), C (75), E (95)** |
| $0.6$ | $146.00$ | $4$ | A, C, E, B (110) |
| $0.8$ | $183.00$ | $5$ | A, C, E, B, D (180) |
| $1.0$ | $220.00$ | $6$ | A, B, C, D, E, F (all) |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **classic KTU numerical question pattern**. Students are expected to demonstrate the monotonic relationship: **as $\alpha$ increases, RCL size increases, and the algorithm becomes more random.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GRASP Master Flow with Candidate List Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START GRASP] --> B[Initialize BestSolution = Empty]
    B --> C{Iterations < MaxIter?}
    C -- No --> Z[Return BestSolution]
    C -- Yes --> D[PHASE 1: Greedy Randomized Construction]
    D --> D1[Initialize Candidate Set C]
    D1 --> D2{Candidate Set Empty?}
    D2 -- No --> D3[Evaluate Greedy Function c of e for all e in C]
    D3 --> D4[Build Restricted Candidate List RCL]
    D4 --> D5[Randomly Select One Element from RCL]
    D5 --> D6[Add Selected Element to Partial Solution]
    D6 --> D7[Update Candidate Set C]
    D7 --> D2
    D2 -- Yes --> E[PHASE 2: Local Search Refinement]
    E --> F{New Solution Better than Best?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Update BestSolution]
    F -- No --> H[Keep BestSolution]
    G --> C
    H --> C
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of RCL Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1[Candidate Set C]
        A2[Greedy Function c of e]
        A3[Parameter alpha]
    end
    subgraph CORE["RCL CONSTRUCTION CORE"]
        B1[Compute c min]
        B2[Compute c max]
        B3[Compute Threshold T]
        B4[Filter Candidates by T]
        B5[Form Restricted List]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1[RCL Ready for Random Draw]
        C2[Selected Element Added to Solution]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    A1 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the RCL Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the candidate set size variable across iterations?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Do you have reliable upper lower bounds?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> P1[Use VALUE BASED RCL with alpha]
    Q2 -- No --> P2[Use CARDINALITY BASED RCL with fixed k]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is computational simplicity a priority?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P2
    Q3 -- No --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Viva:** When asked to "explain how the candidate list is built", trace the path from the *Candidate Set C* through the *Greedy Function evaluation*, into the *Threshold Computation*, and finally into the *Filtered Restricted List*. Always mention that this happens **iteratively** — once per construction step.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1. Define the Restricted Candidate List (RCL) used in the GRASP metaheuristic. Why is it preferred over both pure greedy and pure random selection strategies?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
The *Restricted Candidate List (RCL)* is a filtered subset of the available candidate elements, constructed by retaining only those whose greedy function values $c(e)$ lie within a controlled threshold band defined by the parameter $\alpha \in [0,1]$.

$$
\text{RCL} = \{ e \in C \mid c(e) \in [c_{\min},\, c_{\min} + \alpha(c_{\max} - c_{\min})] \}
$$

It is preferred because:
1. **Pure greedy** ($\alpha = 0$) suffers from premature convergence to poor local optima.
2. **Pure random** ($\alpha = 1$) wastes computational effort on infeasible low-quality regions.
3. **RCL** strikes a balance — high-quality candidates are guaranteed, yet a random draw from them promotes *diversity* across iterations.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between the cardinality-based and value-based Restricted Candidate Lists with appropriate formulas.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cardinality-Based RCL | Value-Based RCL |
|---|---|---|
| Selection Rule | Top $k$ candidates by rank | Candidates within threshold $T$ |
| Control Parameter | Integer $k$ | Real $\alpha \in [0,1]$ |
| Adaptivity | Fixed size per iteration | Size adapts to data spread |
| Formula | $\text{RCL} = \{ e \in C \mid \text{rank}(e) \le k \}$ | $\text{RCL} = \{ e \in C \mid c(e) \le c_{\min} + \alpha(c_{\max} - c_{\min}) \}$ |
| Best Suited For | Uniform candidate pools | Highly heterogeneous pools |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Evaluation, Model Question Pattern]**
> *(Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE full question)*

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **two-phase structure** of the GRASP metaheuristic. Within this structure, describe in detail how the **Restricted Candidate List (RCL)** is constructed and used in the construction phase. **`[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 1 — Greedy Randomized Construction:**
GRASP begins from an empty solution. At each step, a candidate set $C$ of feasible elements is identified (e.g., unvisited cities in TSP). The greedy function $c(e)$ is evaluated for every $e \in C$. The RCL is then built by retaining only the elite candidates satisfying the threshold $T = c_{\min} + \alpha (c_{\max} - c_{\min})$. A uniform random draw from the RCL selects the next element $e^{*}$, which is appended to the partial solution. The candidate set is updated (e.g., the chosen city is removed) and the process iterates until a complete feasible solution is formed.

**Phase 2 — Local Search:**
The constructed solution is then improved via a local search procedure (e.g., 2-opt for TSP). The neighbourhood is explored and any improving move is accepted until a local optimum is reached.

**Role of RCL within GRASP:**
The RCL is the *bridge* between the two phases — it produces a high-quality but non-deterministic starting point for the local search. Without the RCL, GRASP collapses into either a pure greedy algorithm (one good start, no diversity) or random restart local search (poor starts, wasted effort).

**Pseudo-code Summary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{GRASP}(\text{MaxIter},\,\alpha):\\
&\quad x^{*} \leftarrow \emptyset\\
&\quad \text{for } i = 1 \text{ to MaxIter } \textbf{ do}\\
&\quad\quad x \leftarrow \text{Construction}(\alpha)\\
&\quad\quad x \leftarrow \text{LocalSearch}(x)\\
&\quad\quad \textbf{if } f(x) < f(x^{*}) \textbf{ then } x^{*} \leftarrow x\\
&\quad \textbf{ end for}\\
&\quad \textbf{return } x^{*}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** A logistics manager at City 0 must construct a delivery route. The distances (in km) from City 0 to the five remaining unvisited cities are:

$$
\{ 42,\; 78,\; 110,\; 165,\; 205 \}
$$

Using a **value-based RCL with $\alpha = 0.5$**, construct the RCL explicitly and list all elements it contains. **`[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sort the candidates:**
$$
\text{Sorted } c(e): \{42,\; 78,\; 110,\; 165,\; 205\}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify extremes:**
$$
c_{\min} = 42 \text{ km}, \qquad c_{\max} = 205 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute range:**
$$
\Delta c = 205 - 42 = 163 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute threshold:**
$$
T = c_{\min} + \alpha \cdot \Delta c = 42 + (0.5)(163) = 42 + 81.5 = 123.5 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 5 — Construct the RCL:**
$$
\text{RCL} = \{ e \in C \mid c(e) \le 123.5 \}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{RCL} = \{\,\text{City A (42 km)},\; \text{City B (78 km)},\; \text{City C (110 km)} \,\}}
$$

**Step 6 — Random Selection:**
One of the three cities is selected uniformly at random. Suppose City C (110 km) is chosen. The route extends to `0 → C`, and the algorithm proceeds to the next iteration with updated distances from City C.

**Verification (Sanity Check):** Since $\alpha = 0.5$ (midway between greedy and random), we expect roughly half the candidates to be included — and indeed 3 out of 5 cities (60%) are admitted. ✓

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the role of the parameter $\alpha$ in the GRASP metaheuristic. How does varying $\alpha$ affect the **balance between intensification and diversification**? Support your answer with a graphical description. **`[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

The parameter $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is the **master tuning knob** of GRASP. It governs the size and character of the RCL at every construction step, and thereby the trade-off between two competing search strategies:

- **Intensification:** Exploiting known high-quality regions of the search space.
- **Diversification:** Exploring new, previously unseen regions.

**Behavioural Analysis:**

| $\alpha$ Value | RCL Character | Search Mode | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha \to 0$ | Single best candidate | Pure Intensification | Fast convergence, poor diversity |
| $\alpha \approx 0.3$ to $0.7$ | Moderate elite set | **Balanced (Recommended)** | Healthy diversity + quality |
| $\alpha \to 1$ | Entire candidate set | Pure Diversification | Slow, inconsistent quality |

**Graphical Description:**
A typical plot of $\alpha$ (x-axis) versus average solution quality (y-axis) forms an **inverted-U curve**. Optimal $\alpha$ lies at the peak — usually between **0.3 and 0.6** depending on the problem instance. Tuning $\alpha$ is therefore a critical implementation step.

**Engineering Insight:** Most production GRASP implementations either fix $\alpha$ at a tuned constant or use a **reactive GRASP** scheme where $\alpha$ is dynamically updated based on the quality of recently found solutions.

**(b)** Compare the **cardinality-based** and **value-based** Restricted Candidate Lists. Under what conditions is each method preferred in practice? **`[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Cardinality-Based RCL:**
- Selects exactly the **top $k$** candidates ranked by greedy value.
- $\text{RCL} = \{ e \in C \mid \text{rank}(e) \le k \}$
- **Advantages:** Predictable size; simple to implement; computationally cheap.
- **Disadvantages:** Ignores the actual numerical spread of values; may include very poor candidates if $c_{\max}$ is an outlier.

**Value-Based RCL:**
- Selects candidates whose values lie within $[c_{\min}, c_{\min} + \alpha(c_{\max} - c_{\min})]$.
- **Advantages:** Self-adapting size; robust to outliers; quality strictly controlled.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires evaluation of all candidates to find $c_{\min}, c_{\max}$; RCL size varies unpredictably.

**When to Use Each:**

| Condition | Preferred RCL |
|---|---|
| Candidate set size is huge and ranking is expensive | Cardinality-based (small $k$) |
| Greedy values have heavy-tailed distributions with outliers | **Value-based** |
| Solution quality is paramount; computational budget is generous | **Value-based** |
| Real-time systems with strict latency requirements | Cardinality-based |
| Standard benchmark problems with stable characteristics | Either, but **value-based** is the default in literature |

**Conclusion:** While cardinality-based RCL offers simplicity, the **value-based RCL is the canonical GRASP formulation** used in the vast majority of research and industrial applications due to its superior robustness and adaptive behaviour.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Candidate List questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the candidate set iteratively** — the RCL is *recomputed* at every construction step, not built once globally. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if this is missed.
> 2. **Misapplying the threshold formula** — students often write $c_{\min} + \alpha \cdot c_{\max}$ instead of $c_{\min} + \alpha(c_{\max} - c_{\min})$. This is a **fatal arithmetic error**.
> 3. **Confusing $\alpha = 0$ with $\alpha = 1$** — recall: $\alpha = 0$ is **pure greedy**, $\alpha = 1$ is **pure random**. Marks are routinely lost here.
> 4. **Omitting the random draw step** — the RCL is not the final answer; one element must be **uniformly randomly selected** from it.
> 5. **Not mentioning the local search phase** — for GRASP questions, examiners expect both phases. Forgetting local search costs 2–3 marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Candidate List (CL)** is a filtered subset of feasible elements used to drive constructive heuristics.
- ✅ A **Restricted Candidate List (RCL)** is a CL governed by a quality threshold — the cornerstone of the **GRASP** metaheuristic.
- ✅ **Two construction methods:** *Value-based* (parameter $\alpha$) and *Cardinality-based* (parameter $k$).
- ✅ The **threshold formula** is: $T = c_{\min} + \alpha (c_{\max} - c_{\min})$.
- ✅ $\alpha \in [0,1]$: $\alpha = 0$ implies **pure greedy**; $\alpha = 1$ implies **pure random**; intermediate values give **balanced GRASP**.
- ✅ The RCL must be **recomputed at every iteration** of the construction phase, with the candidate set updated after each draw.
- ✅ After construction, a **local search phase** refines the solution to a local optimum.
- ✅ GRASP is **multi-start**: the construction + local search cycle is repeated for `MaxIter` iterations, retaining the best solution found.
- ✅ The **value-based RCL is the canonical GRASP formulation** and is the most frequently examined variant in KTU papers.
- ✅ Real-world applications span **TSP, scheduling, VLSI design, network optimization, and portfolio selection**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short-Term and Long-Term Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Short-Term and Long-Term Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Metaheuristic Optimization** (specifically within **Tabu Search** and adaptive memory programming), **Short-Term Memory (STM)** and **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** are the two foundational categories of memory structures used to guide a heuristic search through the solution space.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework for Heuristics Solution Techniques:

- **Short-Term Memory (STM)** is a *recency-based* memory structure that records the attributes of recently visited solutions or the moves (transformations) applied between iterations. Its primary objective is to **prevent the search from revisiting recently explored regions** and to **avoid cycling** (the phenomenon where the algorithm oscillates between a small set of solutions). It is implemented as a **Tabu List** or **Tabu Queue**.

- **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** is a *frequency-based* (or *intensity-based*) memory structure that records global statistics about the search history — for example, how many times a particular attribute has appeared, or how often a region of the search space has been visited. Its primary objective is to implement **Diversification** (forcing exploration of unvisited regions) and **Intensification** (deeper exploration of promising regions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Memory structures are the *soul* of Tabu Search. STM + LTM together convert a simple local search (which would terminate at the first local optimum) into a powerful **adaptive memory programming** framework capable of escaping local optima.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **traveler exploring a maze** to find a hidden treasure (the global optimum):

- **Short-Term Memory (STM)** is like the **trail of breadcrumbs** you leave behind on the paths you just walked. You mark the last few paths as *"tabu" (forbidden)* so you don't waste time going back the way you came. This is purely **recency-based**.

- **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** is like the **map you keep in your notebook**, where you tally which areas of the maze you have visited the most. If you notice you've been circling the *eastern wing* 50 times already, your map tells you: *"Diversify! Go explore the western wing."* Conversely, if the eastern wing has produced great treasure recently, your map says: *"Intensify! Search that wing more deeply."*

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Range / Value | Purpose |
|-----------|----------------------|---------|
| **Tabu List Size (TL)** | 7 to 50 (typical) | Number of recent moves stored in STM |
| **Tabu Tenure** | Solution-dependent | Duration an attribute remains tabu |
| **Frequency Counter** $f(e)$ | Integer $\geq 0$ | Times attribute $e$ has been used |
| **Aspiration Level** $\alpha$ | Problem-specific threshold | Override criterion for tabu status |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, remember the symbols: **TL = Tabu List, TT = Tabu Tenure, $f(e)$ = Frequency of element $e$, $A(x)$ = Aspiration criterion**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Short-Term Memory

Short-Term Memory operates on the principle of **Recency**. The logical flow is:

- **Step 1:** Initialize an empty Tabu List $TL = \emptyset$.
- **Step 2:** At each iteration $k$, evaluate a candidate move $m_k$.
- **Step 3:** If the attribute(s) of $m_k$ are present in $TL$, the move is classified as **Tabu (forbidden)**.
- **Step 4:** If the move passes an **Aspiration Criterion** $A(x)$, the tabu status may be overridden.
- **Step 5:** Otherwise, the best admissible (non-tabu) move is executed.
- **Step 6:** The executed move's attributes are appended to $TL$. The oldest entry is removed once $|TL| > TT$ (Tabu Tenure limit).

> The **Why** behind STM: Local search can cycle because the neighborhood of $x$ contains $x$ itself or previously visited points. STM breaks this symmetry.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown of Long-Term Memory

Long-Term Memory operates on the principle of **Frequency / Intensity**. It enables two complementary strategies:

- **Diversification** — when the search is stuck in a sub-region. The algorithm **penalizes frequently used attributes** to drive the search into new territory.
- **Intensification** — when certain regions show promise. The algorithm **rewards frequently successful attributes** to focus search around elite solutions.

Logical steps:
- **Step 1:** Maintain a frequency counter $f(e)$ for each attribute $e \in E$ (the attribute universe).
- **Step 2:** Update $f(e)$ each time $e$ is used in a generated/accepted move.
- **Step 3:** At decision time, modify the objective function with a penalty or bonus:

  **Diversification Penalty:**
  $$\hat{f}(x) = f(x) - \lambda \sum_{e \in x} f(e)$$

  **Intensification Bonus:**
  $$\hat{f}(x) = f(x) + \mu \sum_{e \in x} f^*(e)$$

  where $f^*(e)$ is the frequency of $e$ in elite solutions, and $\lambda, \mu$ are tunable weights.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notation Key |
|---------|------------------------|--------------|
| Tabu List Membership | $e \in TL$ | Attribute $e$ is currently tabu |
| Tabu Tenure | $TT = \lvert TL \rvert_{\max}$ | Max size of the Tabu List |
| Recency Memory Update | $TL_{k+1} = TL_k \cup \{m_k\} \setminus \{m_{k-TT}\}$ | Sliding window update |
| Frequency Counter | $f(e) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{e \in x_k\}$ | Number of times attribute $e$ appeared |
| Aspiration Criterion | $A(x): f(x) < f_{\text{best}}$ | Override tabu if better than best-known |
| Diversified Objective | $\hat{f}(x) = f(x) - \lambda \cdot d(x)$ | $d(x) = \sum f(e), \; e \in x$ |
| Intensified Objective | $\hat{f}(x) = f(x) + \mu \cdot i(x)$ | $i(x) = \sum f^*(e), \; e \in x$ |
| Cycle Avoidance Condition | $x_{k} \neq x_{k-\tau}, \; \forall \tau \leq TT$ | No revisit within tenure window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule for KTU:** The vertical bar notation $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ denotes **cardinality** (size) of the set/memory — NOT absolute value. Always distinguish contextually.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLSI Circuit Design:** STM prevents re-routing the same wire path; LTM identifies under-utilized routing channels for diversification.
- **Job-Shop Scheduling:** STM blocks re-assigning a job to a machine it recently used; LTM diversifies across machines with low utilization.
- **Telecommunications:** STM in dynamic channel assignment prevents immediate re-assignment of recently released channels.
- **Logistics & Vehicle Routing:** LTM identifies rarely-served customer zones for diversification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Memory-Augmented Objective Function

Consider a combinatorial optimization problem $\min f(x)$ subject to $x \in S$, where $S$ is the discrete solution space. Standard local search updates $x_{k+1} = \arg\min_{y \in N(x_k)} f(y)$, where $N(x_k)$ is the neighborhood of $x_k$. This **deterministic rule fails** at local optima and is prone to cycling.

**Derivation of Memory-Augmented Selection Rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Define the Augmented Neighborhood: } & N^{\star}(x_k) = N(x_k) \setminus TL \\
\text{Define the Modified Objective: } & \hat{f}_k(y) = f(y) - \lambda \sum_{e \in y} f(e) \\
\text{Augmented Update Rule: } & x_{k+1} = \arg\min_{y \in N^{\star}(x_k) \,\cup\, A} \hat{f}_k(y) \\
\text{where: } & A = \{y \in TL : f(y) < f_{\text{best}} \} \;\; \text{(Aspiration Override Set)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step transition explanation:**

- **Line 1:** We construct an admissible neighborhood by *subtracting* the tabu list from the standard neighborhood. This eliminates forbidden moves.
- **Line 2:** We augment the original objective $f(y)$ with a penalty term proportional to the cumulative frequency $\sum f(e)$. When $\lambda > 0$, frequently-used attributes are *disfavored*, producing **diversification pressure**.
- **Line 3:** The update rule selects the best candidate from the union of the admissible neighborhood and the aspiration-overridden tabu moves.
- **Line 4:** The Aspiration Override Set $A$ contains tabu moves whose objective value beats the current best-known — these are *so good* that the tabu restriction is waived.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Short-Term Memory in Tabu Search

**Problem:** Solve $\min f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 7$ over the discrete set $S = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$. Initial solution: $x_0 = 5$, Tabu Tenure $TT = 3$. Each iteration uses Tabu Search with STM only.

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_0 &= 5, \quad f(5) = 25 - 20 + 7 = 12 \\
TL &= \emptyset, \quad f_{\text{best}} = 12 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Iteration 1 ($k = 1$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Neighborhood: } & N(x_0) = \{4, 6\} \\
f(4) &= 16 - 16 + 7 = 7 \\
f(6) &= 36 - 24 + 7 = 19 \\
\text{Select: } & x_1 = 4 \;\; (\text{better}) \\
\text{Update: } & TL = \{5\}, \quad f_{\text{best}} = 7 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 2 ($k = 2$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Neighborhood: } & N(x_1) = \{3, 5\} \\
f(3) &= 9 - 12 + 7 = 4 \\
f(5) &= 12 \;\; \text{(but } 5 \in TL \rightarrow \text{TABU)} \\
\text{Select: } & x_2 = 3 \;\; (\text{non-tabu, better}) \\
\text{Update: } & TL = \{5, 4\}, \quad f_{\text{best}} = 4 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Iteration 3 ($k = 3$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Neighborhood: } & N(x_2) = \{2, 4\} \\
f(2) &= 4 - 8 + 7 = 3 \\
f(4) &= 7 \;\; (\text{but } 4 \in TL \rightarrow \text{TABU, unless } f(4) < f_{\text{best}}=4? \\
& \text{No, } 7 > 4, \text{ so aspiration fails}) \\
\text{Select: } & x_3 = 2 \\
\text{Update: } & TL = \{5, 4, 3\}, \quad f_{\text{best}} = 3 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Iteration 4 ($k = 4$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Neighborhood: } & N(x_3) = \{1, 3\} \\
f(1) &= 1 - 4 + 7 = 4 \\
f(3) &= 4 \;\; (\text{but } 3 \in TL \rightarrow \text{TABU}) \\
\text{Select: } & x_4 = 1 \\
\text{Update: } & TL = \{4, 3, 2\}, \quad f_{\text{best}} = 3 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Iteration 5 ($k = 5$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Neighborhood: } & N(x_4) = \{0, 2\} \\
f(0) &= 7 \\
f(2) &= 3 \;\; (\text{but } 2 \notin TL \text{ since it dropped out}) \\
\text{Select: } & x_5 = 2 \\
\text{Update: } & TL = \{3, 2, 1\}, \quad f_{\text{best}} = 3 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analytical Matrix Summary:**

| Iter $k$ | $x_k$ | $f(x_k)$ | TL (after update) | $f_{\text{best}}$ |
|----------|-------|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| 0 | 5 | 12 | $\emptyset$ | 12 |
| 1 | 4 | 7 | $\{5\}$ | 7 |
| 2 | 3 | 4 | $\{5,4\}$ | 4 |
| 3 | 2 | 3 | $\{5,4,3\}$ | 3 |
| 4 | 1 | 4 | $\{4,3,2\}$ | 3 |
| 5 | 2 | 3 | $\{3,2,1\}$ | 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The search descended toward the global minimum region ($x = 2$, $f = 3$). Without STM, a naive local search could have oscillated between $x = 2$ and $x = 3$ indefinitely. STM broke this cycle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Memory Hierarchy in Tabu Search

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Memory Structures in Tabu Search"] --> B["Short-Term Memory STM"]
    A --> C["Intermediate-Term Memory"]
    A --> D["Long-Term Memory LTM"]

    B --> B2["Tabu List TL"]
    B --> B3["Tabu Tenure TT"]

    C --> C1["Sequence of Moves"]
    C --> C2["Influence on Selection"]
    
    D --> D1["Frequency Based"]
    D --> D2["Intensity Based"]
    D --> E["Diversification Strategy"]
    D --> F["Intensification Strategy"]
    
    B1 --> G["Penalize Recent Attributes"]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> F
    E --> H["Explore Unvisited Regions"]
    F --> I["Exploit Promising Regions"]
    
    B4 --> J["Escape Local Optima"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    
    J --> K["Adaptive Memory Programming"]
    
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C fill:#f9a825,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow with Aspiration Criterion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start Iteration k"] --> N["Generate Candidate Moves N xk"]
    N --> CL["Classify Each Candidate"]
    CL --> Q1{"Is attribute in TL?"}

    Q1 -- "Yes" --> AS{"Aspiration Criterion Met?"}
    AS -- "Yes" --> OVR["Override Tabu Status"]
    AS -- "No" --> TBM["Mark as Tabu"]
    ADM --> SEL["Select Best Admissible Move"]
    OVR --> SEL
    TBM --> DISC["Disqualify"]
    SEL --> UPD["Update TL and Frequency Counters"]
    UPD --> FR{"Frequency Threshold Breached?"}
    FR -- "Yes" --> DIV["Trigger Diversification Phase"]
    FR -- "No" --> NX["Proceed to Iteration k+1"]
    DIV --> NX
    NX --> S
    
    style S fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style SEL fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Comparative Feature Map — STM vs LTM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STM["SHORT-TERM MEMORY"]
        S1["Stores: Recent Attributes"]
        S2["Basis: Recency"]
        S3["Structure: Tabu List"]
```
