```mermaid
        S5["Scope: Last TT iterations"]
    end
    
    subgraph LTM["LONG-TERM MEMORY"]
        L1["Stores: Cumulative Counts"]
        L2["Basis: Frequency or Intensity"]
        L3["Structure: Frequency Matrix"]
        L4["Function: Diversify and Intensify"]
        L5["Scope: Entire Search History"]
    end
    
    S1 --- L1
    S2 --- L2
    S3 --- L3
    S4 --- L4
    S5 --- L5
    
    style STM fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    style LTM fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Short-Term Memory in Tabu Search. What is its primary role?*

**Model Answer:**
Short-Term Memory (STM) in Tabu Search is a *recency-based* memory structure that stores the attributes of recently visited solutions or the moves applied between successive iterations. It is implemented as a **Tabu List (TL)** of fixed maximum size, governed by the **Tabu Tenure (TT)** parameter. Its primary role is to **prevent the search from cycling** by forbidding the re-application of recently used moves or attributes. STM thus allows the heuristic to *escape local optima* by forcing the search into unexplored regions of the neighborhood. *(3 marks — Definition: 1m, Implementation: 1m, Role: 1m)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Diversification and Intensification strategies used in Long-Term Memory.*

**Model Answer:**
Both are frequency-based LTM strategies but with opposing objectives:

| Aspect | Diversification | Intensification |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Goal** | Explore new regions | Exploit promising regions |
| **Mechanism** | Penalize frequently used attributes | Reward successful attributes |
| **Objective** | $\hat{f}(x) = f(x) - \lambda \sum f(e)$ | $\hat{f}(x) = f(x) + \mu \sum f^*(e)$ |
| **Trigger** | Search stagnation detected | Elite solutions identified |
| **Effect** | Broadens search | Narrows search |

*(3 marks — Each strategy with one distinguishing feature: 1.5m each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the structure and functioning of **Short-Term Memory** in Tabu Search. Discuss the role of the **Tabu List**, **Tabu Tenure**, and **Aspiration Criterion** with suitable expressions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Tabu List (TL):** A finite queue $TL = \{m_{k-1}, m_{k-2}, \ldots, m_{k-TT}\}$ storing the attributes of the last $TT$ executed moves. Membership test: $e \in TL$ → move forbidden.

- **Tabu Tenure (TT):** The cardinality constraint $\lvert TL \rvert \leq TT$. Typical values: $5 \leq TT \leq 50$. Too small → cycling resumes; too large → excessive restrictions.

- **Update Rule:**
$$TL_{k+1} = (TL_k \cup \{m_k\}) \setminus \{m_{k-TT}\}$$

- **Aspiration Criterion $A(x)$:** A *override mechanism* allowing a tabu move to be accepted if it produces a sufficiently good solution. The most common form:
$$A(x): \quad f(x) < f_{\text{best-so-far}}$$

- **Engineering Insight:** Aspiration criteria prevent the algorithm from being *over-restricted* by its own memory, preserving feasibility of excellent moves. *(7 marks complete)*

**(b)** Describe the **Long-Term Memory** structure used in Tabu Search. How does it implement Diversification and Intensification? Provide the mathematical formulations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **LTM Definition:** A *frequency-based* memory $f: E \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ where $f(e)$ counts how often attribute $e$ has appeared across all visited solutions.

- **Update Rule:**
$$f_{k+1}(e) = f_k(e) + \mathbb{1}\{e \in x_{k+1}\}$$

- **Diversification:** Modify objective as:
$$\hat{f}(x) = f(x) - \lambda \cdot d(x), \quad d(x) = \sum_{e \in x} f(e)$$
The penalty $d(x)$ discourages solutions rich in overused attributes, pushing the search toward *unvisited* regions.

- **Intensification:** Modify objective as:
$$\hat{f}(x) = f(x) + \mu \cdot i(x), \quad i(x) = \sum_{e \in x} f^*(e)$$
where $f^*(e)$ is the frequency of $e$ in elite (best) solutions. This rewards *reinforcement* of successful patterns.

- **Balance:** The weights $\lambda$ and $\mu$ control the exploration–exploitation trade-off. *(7 marks complete)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** With a clear diagram, explain the **memory classification** in Tabu Search. Differentiate between STM, Intermediate-Term Memory, and LTM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Memory Type | Basis | Storage | Purpose |
|-------------|-------|---------|---------|
| **STM** | Recency | Last $TT$ moves | Anti-cycling, escape local optima |
| **Intermediate-Term** | Sequence/Quality | Selected elite trajectories | Focus search around promising paths |
| **LTM** | Frequency/Intensity | Counters over all history | Diversify / Intensify globally |

- **STM:** Operates on $N(x_k) \setminus TL$ — local, short-horizon.
- **Intermediate-Term:** Stores *regions* or *paths* of consecutive improving moves; used for elite solution recovery.
- **LTM:** Operates globally via $\hat{f}(x)$ augmentation — long-horizon strategic guidance.

- **Diagram:**
```
Memory Hierarchy (ascending scope):
   STM  →  Intermediate  →  LTM
   (Recency)   (Sequence)    (Frequency)
```

*(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Tabu Search is run on a discrete minimization problem. The current best solution has $f = 18$. The Tabu List contains $\{2, 5, 7\}$ with tenure $TT = 3$. Three candidate moves yield $f$-values $14, 16, 19$, with corresponding attributes $\{5\}, \{8\}, \{7\}$. The Aspiration Criterion is: accept any tabu move with $f(x) < 18$. Determine which move is selected and update the Tabu List. Justify each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Step 1 — Classify each candidate:**
  - Move 1: $f = 14$, attribute $= 5$. Since $5 \in TL$, the move is **tabu**.
  - Move 2: $f = 16$, attribute $= 8$. Since $8 \notin TL$, the move is **admissible**.
  - Move 3: $f = 19$, attribute $= 7$. Since $7 \in TL$, the move is **tabu**.

- **Step 2 — Apply Aspiration Criterion ($f < 18$):**
  - Move 1: $f = 14 < 18$ → **Aspiration Override applies** → admissible.
  - Move 3: $f = 19 > 18$ → aspiration fails → remains tabu.

- **Step 3 — Select best admissible move:**
  - Eligible: Move 1 ($f = 14$) and Move 2 ($f = 16$).
  - Selected: **Move 1** (minimum $f = 14$).
  - New best: $f_{\text{best}} = \min(18, 14) = 14$.

- **Step 4 — Update Tabu List:**
  - Append attribute $5$: $TL = \{2, 5, 7\} \cup \{5\} = \{2, 5, 7\}$ (no growth since already present).
  - Since $\lvert TL \rvert = 3 = TT$, no eviction needed.
  - **Final Tabu List:** $TL = \{2, 5, 7\}$.

*(7 marks — Classification: 2m, Aspiration: 2m, Selection: 2m, TL update: 1m)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing STM with LTM:** Students frequently interchange "recency" and "frequency." STM = recency (Tabu List); LTM = frequency (diversification/intensification). Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the Aspiration Criterion:** When asked why a tabu move was accepted, *always* check if $f(x) < f_{\text{best}}$. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Notation slip-up:** Writing $\lvert x \rvert$ instead of $\lvert TL \rvert$ for tabu list size — penalizes 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Missing diversification formula penalty term $\lambda \cdot d(x)$** when asked for LTM mathematical formulation — costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Tabu Tenure vs. Tabu List confusion:** Tenure is the *limit* on list size; the list is the *actual* structure. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **STM = Recency = Tabu List:** Stores attributes of last $TT$ moves; primary job is **anti-cycling**.
- **LTM = Frequency = Counters:** Stores cumulative visit counts; enables **diversification** (penalty) and **intensification** (bonus).
- **Aspiration Criterion:** Overrides tabu status when $f(x) < f_{\text{best}}$; prevents over-restriction.
- **Tabu Tenure (TT):** Critical hyperparameter — too small causes cycling, too large causes stagnation.
- **Modified Objective (LTM-Diversification):** $\hat{f}(x) = f(x) - \lambda \sum_{e \in x} f(e)$.
- **Modified Objective (LTM-Intensification):** $\hat{f}(x) = f(x) + \mu \sum_{e \in x} f^*(e)$.
- **Memory Update:** Sliding window $TL_{k+1} = (TL_k \cup \{m_k\}) \setminus \{m_{k-TT}\}$.
- **Tabu Search = Adaptive Memory Programming:** STM + Intermediate + LTM together distinguish Tabu Search from simple local search.
- **Cycle-Avoidance Guarantee:** No solution revisits within tenure window — mathematically: $x_k \neq x_{k-\tau}, \forall \tau \leq TT$.
- **Engineering Applications:** VLSI routing, job-shop scheduling, TSP, frequency assignment, vehicle routing.
- **Examiner Favorite Topics:** (1) Compare STM vs LTM in tabular form; (2) trace a small numerical example; (3) explain aspiration criterion with formula; (4) write diversification objective function.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threshold Accepting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Threshold Accepting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Accepting (TA)** is a deterministic trajectory-based metaheuristic for global optimization, introduced by **Günter Dueck and Tobias Scheuer (1990)** as a deterministic alternative to Simulated Annealing. It iteratively improves a candidate solution by accepting a neighboring solution not only when it is better, but also when it is *slightly worse*, provided the deterioration does not exceed a pre-defined, monotonically decreasing **threshold value** $T_k$.

Formally, given an objective function $f: S \to \mathbb{R}$ to be **minimized**, a current solution $x \in S$, a generated neighbor $x' \in N(x)$, and the cost degradation:

$$\Delta f = f(x') - f(x)$$

the acceptance rule is:

$$\begin{cases}
\text{Accept } x' \text{ as new current, if } \Delta f \leq T_k \\[4pt]
\text{Reject } x', \text{ if } \Delta f > T_k
\end{cases}$$

The sequence $\{T_k\}_{k=0}^{K_{\max}}$ is called the **threshold schedule**, typically a strictly positive, monotonically non-increasing sequence converging to zero.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Mountain Hiker in the Dark"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Climbing Down a Foggy Hill at Dusk**

Imagine you are standing on a hilly terrain in dense fog, trying to reach the lowest valley. You can only feel the slope immediately beneath your feet (local information). At the start, you are willing to climb **uphill a little** (up to a threshold height) because you suspect the deeper valley lies beyond the current hill. As dusk turns to night, your "tolerance" for going uphill shrinks. By midnight, you accept only moves that strictly go downhill. By dawn, you accept zero uphill moves — you are at the local minimum you can find.

| Real-World Element | Threshold Accepting Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Hiker at a point on the terrain | Current solution $x$ |
| Step to an adjacent position | Move to neighbor $x'$ |
| Feeling whether you go up or down | Cost difference $\Delta f = f(x') - f(x)$ |
| Willingness to climb uphill at dusk | Threshold value $T_k$ (initially large) |
| Decreasing tolerance over time | Cooling of the threshold schedule |
| Stopping at dawn | Termination condition $T_k \approx 0$ |

## 1.3 Why Threshold Accepting Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> TA is a **Module 3** topic in *OEIET614 – Heuristics Solution Techniques*. It is a sibling technique to **Simulated Annealing (SA)**. The key KTU-differentiating point examiners test is: **TA uses a deterministic threshold sequence, NOT a probabilistic Metropolis criterion**, which makes TA conceptually simpler and often computationally faster than SA while achieving comparable solution quality.

---

## 1.4 Standard Threshold Sequences (Notation Bank)

The threshold value at iteration $k$ is denoted $T_k$. Three standard schedules used in KTU problems:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Geometric (exponential) cooling**: $T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$, where $0 < \alpha < 1$
> 2. **Linear cooling**: $T_k = \max\left(0,\; T_0 - k \cdot \beta\right)$
> 3. **Logarithmic cooling**: $T_k = \dfrac{T_0}{\ln(k + e)}$ (rare in undergraduate problems)

---

## 1.5 Distinguishing TA from Simulated Annealing

| Property | Threshold Accepting (TA) | Simulated Annealing (SA) |
|---|---|---|
| Acceptance rule | Deterministic (yes/no) | Probabilistic (Metropolis) |
| Parameter | Threshold $T_k$ | Temperature $T_k$ |
| Worst move allowed | $\Delta f \leq T_k$ | $e^{-\Delta f / T_k} > r$, $r \sim U(0,1)$ |
| Random number generator | Not required at accept step | Required at accept step |
| Source of stochasticity | Neighbor generation only | Neighbor generation + acceptance |
| Tuning complexity | Moderate (threshold + schedule) | Higher (temperature + schedule) |
| Inventor / Year | Dueck & Scheuer, 1990 | Kirkpatrick et al., 1983 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Threshold Accepting algorithm proceeds in **seven canonical steps**, which KTU examiners frequently request in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

1. **Initialization**: Select an initial feasible solution $x^{(0)} \in S$. Initialize the threshold sequence with $T_0 > 0$. Set the iteration counter $k \leftarrow 0$.
2. **Neighbor Generation**: From the current solution $x^{(k)}$, generate a neighboring solution $x' \in N(x^{(k)})$ using a stochastic perturbation operator.
3. **Cost Evaluation**: Evaluate the objective function at the new solution, $f(x')$, and compute the cost difference $\Delta f = f(x') - f(x^{(k)})$.
4. **Deterministic Acceptance Test**:
   - If $\Delta f \leq T_k$ (improvement or slight degradation within the threshold), set $x^{(k+1)} \leftarrow x'$.
   - Else, set $x^{(k+1)} \leftarrow x^{(k)}$ (rejection).
5. **Threshold Update**: Update the threshold using the chosen cooling schedule, e.g., $T_{k+1} = T_k \cdot \alpha$, where $0 < \alpha < 1$.
6. **Stopping Criterion Check**: Terminate if a maximum iteration count $K_{\max}$ is reached, $T_k < \epsilon$ for some small $\epsilon > 0$, or a computational budget is exhausted.
7. **Return Best**: Output $x_{\text{best}} = \arg\min_{0 \leq j \leq K_{\max}} f(x^{(j)})$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Step 1** provides a starting point; poor initialization can be partially rescued by the algorithm's hill-climbing ability.
> - **Step 2** is the *only* source of randomness in TA; the acceptance step is **fully deterministic**.
> - **Step 4** is the heart of TA — unlike greedy hill-climbing, the algorithm can escape local minima by allowing controlled uphill moves.
> - **Step 5** ensures that as iterations proceed, the algorithm transitions from broad exploration to focused exploitation.
> - **Step 7** ensures the algorithm reports the *best encountered* solution, not merely the *final* one — this is essential because rejection steps may improve the running best.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta f = f(x') - f(x)$ | Cost degradation of move | $\Delta f \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | $T_k$ | Threshold at iteration $k$ | $T_k > 0$ |
| 3 | $T_0$ | Initial threshold | $T_0 \approx (5\%-10\%) \cdot \vert f(x^{(0)}) \vert$ or set empirically |
| 4 | $T_{k+1} = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k+1}$ | Geometric cooling schedule | $\alpha \in (0.9,\; 0.999)$ |
| 5 | $\alpha$ | Cooling rate (geometric) | $0.9 \leq \alpha < 1$ |
| 6 | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \beta$ | Linear cooling schedule | $\beta > 0$ |
| 7 | Acceptance rule | $\Delta f \leq T_k \Rightarrow$ accept | deterministic |
| 8 | $K_{\max}$ | Max iterations | tuned to budget |
| 9 | $\epsilon$ | Termination tolerance | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 10 | $x_{\text{best}}$ | Best solution found | $\arg\min f(x^{(j)})$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Safety**: For absolute value or modular notation in your own KTU answer scripts, **always** use `\vert \cdot \vert` or `\lvert \cdot \rvert`. Never type literal `|x|` inside markdown tables or it will break the table grid in many Markdown renderers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Threshold Accepting has been successfully deployed in:
> - **VLSI circuit placement and routing** (Dueck & Scheuer's original 1990 application).
> - **Vehicle routing problems (VRP)** in logistics.
> - **Job-shop scheduling** in manufacturing.
> - **Antenna array design** in telecommunications.
> - **Neural network weight training** (TA-trained MLPs).

It is preferred over SA in **real-time or embedded systems** where generating high-quality pseudo-random numbers for the Metropolis step is expensive or non-deterministic behavior is undesirable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Geometric Threshold Schedule

The **geometric cooling schedule** is the most frequently tested form in KTU examinations. We derive the number of iterations required to reduce the threshold from $T_0$ to a final $T_f$.

Given:

$$T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

We seek the smallest integer $K$ such that $T_K \leq T_f$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_K &\leq T_f \\[4pt]
T_0 \cdot \alpha^K &\leq T_f \\[4pt]
\alpha^K &\leq \frac{T_f}{T_0} \\[4pt]
\ln(\alpha^K) &\leq \ln\!\left(\frac{T_f}{T_0}\right) \quad \text{(log is monotonic since } \alpha \in (0,1)\text{)} \\[4pt]
K \cdot \ln(\alpha) &\leq \ln\!\left(\frac{T_f}{T_0}\right) \\[4pt]
K &\geq \frac{\ln(T_f / T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)}
\end{aligned}$$

Because $\ln(\alpha) < 0$, dividing **flips the inequality sign**:

$$\boxed{\;K_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{\ln(T_f \,/\, T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)} \right\rceil\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This closed-form expression lets KTU exam problems compute the required iteration budget directly without simulation.

### 3.1.1 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

Suppose $T_0 = 100$, $T_f = 0.01$, $\alpha = 0.95$. Compute $K_{\min}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_{\min} &= \left\lceil \frac{\ln(0.01 \,/\, 100)}{\ln(0.95)} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{\ln(10^{-4})}{\ln(0.95)} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{-9.2103}{-0.05129} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil 179.57 \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= 180 \text{ iterations}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic**: Numerator becomes $-9.2103$ because $\ln(10^{-4}) = -4 \ln(10) \approx -9.2103$. Denominator is $\ln(0.95) \approx -0.05129$. Their quotient is positive $\approx 179.57$. Since $K$ must be an integer $\geq 179.57$, we **ceiling** it to $180$. This means at $k = 180$, $T_{180} \leq 0.01$.

---

## 3.2 Acceptance Probability Comparison (TA vs SA)

The expected number of accepted *uphill* moves in TA is determined by the threshold, not by a probability distribution. However, one can compute an **equivalent** acceptance probability by comparing the threshold rule to a Boltzmann-like form.

For SA with temperature $T$, the Metropolis acceptance probability is:

$$P_{\text{SA}}(\Delta f, T) = e^{-\Delta f \,/\, T}$$

For TA with threshold $T_k$, the deterministic acceptance rule is:

$$P_{\text{TA}}(\Delta f, T_k) = \begin{cases} 1, & \Delta f \leq T_k \\ 0, & \Delta f > T_k \end{cases}$$

If we ask: *At what temperature would SA mimic a TA threshold $T_k$ exactly?* Equating:

$$e^{-T_k \,/\, T} = 0.5 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T = \frac{T_k}{\ln 2}$$

Thus, **TA's threshold $T_k$ corresponds to an SA temperature of roughly $T_k / 0.693$** when both yield 50% acceptance at the boundary $\Delta f = T_k$.

---

## 3.3 Convergence Property — The Threshold Sequence Must Vanish

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A necessary (but not sufficient) condition for TA to converge to a global optimum with probability one is:

$$\lim_{k \to \infty} T_k = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} e^{-c / T_k} = \infty, \; \forall c > 0$$

The first condition ensures eventual greedy behavior; the second is analogous to SA's Geman–Geman condition and guarantees enough exploratory visits to every state.

For geometric cooling $T_k = T_0 \alpha^k$, $\lim T_k = 0$ is satisfied but the divergence condition may fail unless $\alpha$ is sufficiently close to 1.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Effects on TA

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Exploration | Increase Effect on Convergence Speed | Typical Tuning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_0$ (initial threshold) | More exploration | Slower | Set to $\sim$5–10% of $\vert f(x^{(0)})\vert$ |
| $\alpha$ (closer to 1) | Slower cooling → more exploration | Slower | $0.95$–$0.99$ |
| Neighborhood size $\vert N(x) \vert$ | More diverse moves | Depends | Problem-specific |
| $K_{\max}$ | Longer search | Slower | $10^3$–$10^6$ |
| $\beta$ (linear cooling step) | Less exploration (faster cooling) | Faster | $T_0 / K_{\max}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Threshold Accepting — Algorithm Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Initialize x, T0, k=0"] --> B["k less than Kmax?"]
    B -- No --> Z["RETURN x_best"]
    B -- Yes --> C["Generate neighbor x_prime from x"]
    C --> D["Compute Delta f = f(x_prime) minus f(x)"]
    D --> E{"Delta f less than or equal to T_k?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["x becomes x_prime (accept)"]
    E -- No --> G["x unchanged (reject)"]
    F --> H["Update x_best if f(x) less than f(x_best)"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Update threshold T_k+1 = T_k times alpha"]
    I --> J["k becomes k + 1"]
    J --> B
    Z --> K["END"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note**: All node IDs are alphanumeric strings prefixed with letters (`A`, `B`, `C`, …). No reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` appear as standalone node identifiers. All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold (`**`) or italics (`*`) markers inside the quoted labels.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Solver Core: x initialization"] --> P2["Neighborhood Engine: x_prime generator"]
    P2 --> P3["Cost Evaluator: f x_prime"]
    P3 --> P4["Acceptance Controller: threshold comparator"]
    P4 --> P5["Schedule Manager: T_k updater"]
    P5 --> P6["Loop Driver: iteration counter"]
    P6 --> P2
    P6 --> P7["Convergence Monitor: stop criterion"]
    P7 -- Active --> P8["Best Tracker: x_best archive"]
    P7 -- Terminated --> P9["Final Output: report x_best"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the architecture**: The five functional blocks (Solver Core → Neighborhood Engine → Cost Evaluator → Acceptance Controller → Schedule Manager) form a closed feedback loop, while the **Best Tracker** is an *open-loop* memory element that records the historical optimum independently of acceptance decisions — this separation is what guarantees $x_{\text{best}}$ is correct even when the final move is rejected.

## 4.3 Threshold Schedule Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0["T0 = 100, k=0"] --> A1["T1 = 95, k=1, alpha=0.95"]
    A1 --> A2["T2 = 90.25, k=2"]
    A2 --> A3["T3 = 85.74, k=3"]
    A3 --> A4["Tk = T0 times alpha^k"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The threshold decays **exponentially**, meaning most threshold reduction happens in early iterations and the algorithm quickly transitions from "broad exploration" to "local refinement" — this is the signature behavior of geometric cooling.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Threshold Accepting algorithm. How does it differ from Simulated Annealing in its acceptance criterion?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Accepting (TA)** is a deterministic metaheuristic proposed by Dueck and Scheuer (1990) that iteratively improves a solution by accepting a neighboring solution if the increase in cost does not exceed a pre-defined, monotonically decreasing threshold value $T_k$. The acceptance rule is:

> $$\Delta f = f(x') - f(x) \leq T_k \implies \text{Accept } x'$$

> The key difference from Simulated Annealing is the **deterministic** nature of acceptance: while SA uses the **probabilistic Metropolis criterion** $e^{-\Delta f/T_k} > r$ (with $r$ uniform on $[0,1]$), TA uses a **hard threshold** comparison. Hence TA does not require a random number generator for the acceptance step — its only stochasticity comes from neighbor generation.

*(Examiner breakdown: 1 mark for TA definition, 1 mark for acceptance rule, 1 mark for SA contrast.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** List any three threshold schedules used in Threshold Accepting and write their mathematical forms.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> Three standard threshold schedules are:

> 1. **Geometric (Exponential) Cooling**: $\;T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k,\; 0 < \alpha < 1$
> 2. **Linear Cooling**: $\;T_k = \max(0,\; T_0 - k\beta),\; \beta > 0$
> 3. **Logarithmic Cooling**: $\;T_k = \dfrac{T_0}{\ln(k + e)}$

> Among these, geometric cooling is the most widely used due to its simplicity and rapid convergence to a low threshold.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly lose marks by **confusing TA with SA** — TA is *deterministic* at the accept step; SA is *probabilistic*.
> - Students write the threshold rule in the **wrong direction** ($\Delta f \geq T_k$). The correct inequality is $\Delta f \leq T_k$ for minimization problems.
> - For **maximization** problems, students forget to flip the inequality. The KTU-accepted convention is: rewrite as a minimization by negating $f$, or state clearly that the threshold applies to $-f$.
> - Students often **omit the "best-so-far" archive** step, which is mandatory for guaranteed convergence reporting.
> - Do not forget to specify the **cooling schedule parameter** (e.g., $\alpha = 0.95$) — without it, the algorithm is incomplete.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Module 3 Internal Choice Option 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the Threshold Accepting algorithm with a clearly labeled flowchart. Discuss the role of the threshold sequence and outline any two threshold cooling schedules. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the minimization problem $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 7$ over integers $x \in [0, 10]$. Starting from $x^{(0)} = 8$, with initial threshold $T_0 = 3$ and cooling schedule $T_{k+1} = 0.5 \cdot T_k$, perform **3 iterations** of Threshold Accepting using the symmetric integer neighborhood $N(x) = \{x-1, x+1\}$ and report the best solution found. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)** *(7 marks)*

> **Definition (2 marks):** Threshold Accepting is a deterministic iterative heuristic for global optimization that accepts a move to a neighboring solution if and only if the cost increase does not exceed a pre-defined threshold $T_k$ that monotonically decreases with iteration $k$.

> **Flowchart (2 marks):** See the flow diagram in **Section 4.1** above. The examiner expects: Initialize → Generate neighbor → Compute $\Delta f$ → Compare with $T_k$ → Accept/Reject → Update threshold → Loop until $K_{\max}$.

> **Role of threshold (2 marks):** The threshold controls the exploration–exploitation tradeoff. A large $T_k$ permits broad exploration of the search space, allowing escape from local minima; a small $T_k$ enforces greedy exploitation near the current solution. The decreasing schedule transforms the algorithm from a global search into a local refinement tool.

> **Two cooling schedules (1 mark):**
> - **Geometric**: $T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$, $\alpha \in (0.95, 0.99)$.
> - **Linear**: $T_k = \max(0,\; T_0 - k\beta)$.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** *(7 marks)*

> **Given data:**
> - $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 7$
> - Initial: $x^{(0)} = 8$, so $f(8) = 64 - 32 + 7 = 39$
> - $T_0 = 3$, $T_{k+1} = 0.5 \cdot T_k$
> - Neighbor set: $N(x) = \{x-1, x+1\}$

> **Iteration 1 ($k=0$, $T_0 = 3$):**
> - Neighbors of 8: $\{7, 9\}$
> - $f(7) = 49 - 28 + 7 = 28$; $\Delta f = 28 - 39 = -11 \leq 3$ → **Accept**. Choose $x^{(1)} = 7$.
> - Note: $f(9) = 81 - 36 + 7 = 52$; $\Delta f = 13 > 3$ → Reject.
> - Best so far: $x_{\text{best}} = 7$, $f = 28$.

> **Iteration 2 ($k=1$, $T_1 = 1.5$):**
> - Neighbors of 7: $\{6, 8\}$
> - $f(6) = 36 - 24 + 7 = 19$; $\Delta f = 19 - 28 = -9 \leq 1.5$ → **Accept**. $x^{(2)} = 6$.
> - $f(8) = 39$; $\Delta f = 11 > 1.5$ → Reject.
> - Best so far: $x_{\text{best}} = 6$, $f = 19$.

> **Iteration 3 ($k=2$, $T_2 = 0.75$):**
> - Neighbors of 6: $\{5, 7\}$
> - $f(5) = 25 - 20 + 7 = 12$; $\Delta f = 12 - 19 = -7 \leq 0.75$ → **Accept**. $x^{(3)} = 5$.
> - $f(7) = 28$; $\Delta f = 9 > 0.75$ → Reject.
> - Best so far: $x_{\text{best}} = 5$, $f = 12$.

> **Final Answer:** Best solution found in 3 iterations is $x_{\text{best}} = 5$ with $f(5) = 12$. Note that the analytical global minimum of $f$ over reals is at $x^* = 2$ with $f(2) = 3$, which the algorithm has not yet reached but is converging towards. *(1 mark for correct final answer + 1 mark for analytical verification.)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Module 3 Internal Choice Option 2)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2025 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Compare Threshold Accepting and Simulated Annealing with respect to: (i) acceptance criterion, (ii) source of randomness, (iii) parameter tuning, and (iv) convergence behavior. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a Threshold Accepting run with $T_0 = 50$, cooling rate $\alpha = 0.9$, and a target final threshold $T_f = 0.001$, compute the minimum number of iterations required to ensure the threshold has dropped below $T_f$. Justify each step of your derivation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)** *(7 marks)*

| Aspect | Threshold Accepting (TA) | Simulated Annealing (SA) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Acceptance Criterion | Deterministic: $\Delta f \leq T_k$ | Probabilistic: $e^{-\Delta f/T_k} > r$ |
| (ii) Source of Randomness | Only neighbor generation | Neighbor generation + acceptance step |
| (iii) Parameter Tuning | Threshold $T_k$ + cooling schedule | Temperature $T_k$ + cooling schedule + RNG |
| (iv) Convergence Behavior | Guaranteed local refinement as $T_k \to 0$; global optimum with proper schedule | Asymptotic global convergence under Geman–Geman conditions |

> *(Each correct row carries ~1.5 marks; partial credit for partially correct rows.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** *(7 marks)*

> **Given:** $T_0 = 50$, $\alpha = 0.9$, $T_f = 0.001$.

> **Step 1 — Write the geometric cooling law (1 mark):**
> $$T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k = 50 \cdot (0.9)^k$$

> **Step 2 — Impose the stopping condition $T_K \leq T_f$ (1 mark):**
> $$50 \cdot (0.9)^K \leq 0.001$$

> **Step 3 — Isolate the exponential term (1 mark):**
> $$(0.9)^K \leq \frac{0.001}{50} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$$

> **Step 4 — Take natural logarithms on both sides (1 mark):**
> $$\ln\!\left((0.9)^K\right) \leq \ln(2 \times 10^{-5})$$
> $$K \cdot \ln(0.9) \leq \ln(2) + \ln(10^{-5})$$

> **Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithms (1 mark):**
> $$\ln(0.9) \approx -0.10536$$
> $$\ln(2 \times 10^{-5}) = 0.6931 - 11.5129 = -10.8198$$

> **Step 6 — Solve for $K$ (1 mark):**
> $$K \geq \frac{-10.8198}{-0.10536} \approx 102.69$$

> **Step 7 — Apply the ceiling operation (1 mark):**
> $$\boxed{\;K_{\min} = 103 \text{ iterations}\;}$$

> **Verification:** $T_{103} = 50 \cdot (0.9)^{103} \approx 50 \cdot 1.83 \times 10^{-5} \approx 9.13 \times 10^{-4} < 0.001$ ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Threshold Accepting**

- **Inventors**: Günter Dueck & Tobias Scheuer, 1990.
- **Core idea**: Accept a worse move only if its deterioration is at most the current threshold $T_k$.
- **Acceptance rule (minimization)**: $\Delta f = f(x') - f(x) \leq T_k$.
- **TA is deterministic at the accept step**; only neighbor generation is stochastic.
- **SA vs TA**: SA uses probabilistic Metropolis; TA uses deterministic threshold.
- **Three common threshold schedules**: geometric $T_0\alpha^k$, linear $T_0 - k\beta$, logarithmic $T_0 / \ln(k+e)$.
- **Initial threshold $T_0$**: typically 5–10% of $\vert f(x^{(0)})\vert$.
- **Cooling rate $\alpha$**: typically in $[0.90, 0.99]$ for geometric cooling.
- **Always maintain a best-so-far archive** $x_{\text{best}}$ separately from current $x$.
- **Convergence requirement**: $\lim_{k \to \infty} T_k = 0$.
- **Iteration budget for geometric cooling**:
$$K_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{\ln(T_f \,/\, T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)} \right\rceil$$
- **Engineering applications**: VLSI design, vehicle routing, scheduling, antenna design, neural network training.
- **Common pitfall**: writing the inequality reversed; forgetting to negate $f$ for maximization problems.
- **Equivalent SA temperature for TA threshold $T_k$** (at 50% acceptance boundary): $T_{\text{SA}} = T_k / \ln 2 \approx 1.4427 \cdot T_k$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of TS in Decision Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTIC SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application of TS in Decision Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tabu Search (TS) in Decision Making** is a high-level metaheuristic framework used to guide a local search procedure to explore the solution space **beyond local optimality** by employing **flexible memory structures** (tabu lists) and **strategic constraints/aspiration conditions** to make robust, near-optimal decisions in complex combinatorial and multi-criteria problem environments.

In the context of decision-making, a *decision problem* is formulated as a combinatorial optimization task where the decision-maker must select, allocate, sequence, or design an action set $x \in X$ that optimizes a vector of objectives $F(x) = \{f_1(x), f_2(x), \ldots, f_k(x)\}$ subject to a set of feasibility constraints $g_j(x) \leq 0$ and $h_k(x) = 0$.

The formal mathematical envelope for a TS-driven decision problem is stated as:

$$\text{Decision: } x^* = \arg\min_{x \in \mathcal{F}} \left[ f(x) \right]$$

where $\mathcal{F} \subseteq X$ denotes the **feasible decision region**, $f(x)$ is the scalarized (or multi-objective) decision objective, and $x^*$ is the optimal decision.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Memory-Aware Manager"**

Imagine a CEO who must schedule projects every quarter. A *greedy manager* would keep picking the most profitable local project, but might get stuck in a cycle of similar bad decisions. A **Tabu-Search-driven manager**, however:

1. **Remembers recent bad choices** (tabu list) — e.g., "Don't re-allocate resources to Department X this week; we did it last week and it failed."
2. **Forbids revisiting those choices temporarily** (tabu tenure).
3. **Allows exceptions** when a forbidden move is extraordinarily good (aspiration criterion).
4. **Balances multiple goals** (profit, employee satisfaction, deadline adherence) — multi-criteria decision making.

This mirrors the human decision-making process: we use **short-term memory** to avoid repeating mistakes while remaining open to **exceptionally promising alternatives**.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary for KTU 2024 Scheme

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Tabu List ($T$)** | A short-term memory structure holding attributes of forbidden moves. |
| **Tabu Tenure ($\tau$)** | The number of iterations a move remains tabu. |
| **Aspiration Criterion ($A$)** | A rule overriding tabu status if a move yields a new global best. |
| **Neighborhood ($N(x)$)** | The set of all candidate decisions reachable from current decision $x$. |
| **Best-Acceptance Move** | The non-tabu move (or aspiration-allowed) yielding the greatest improvement. |
| **Diversification** | Long-term strategy to explore unvisited regions of decision space. |
| **Intensification** | Short-term strategy to exploit promising regions. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Decision-making applications of TS emphasize **multi-criteria trade-offs**, **constraint handling**, and **stochastic search robustness** — distinguish these from pure single-objective optimization in your exam answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TS Decision-Making Pipeline

A Tabu Search application to decision-making follows a structured operational sequence:

- **Step 1 — Encode the Decision Space:** Map the real-world decision variables into a discrete/integer representation $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ where each $x_i$ belongs to a domain $D_i$.
- **Step 2 — Construct the Initial Decision:** Generate a feasible starting decision $x^{(0)} \in \mathcal{F}$ using a heuristic, greedy rule, or random seed.
- **Step 3 — Define the Neighborhood Operator:** Specify a transformation $N(x)$ that produces neighbouring decisions (e.g., swap two tasks, change one allocation).
- **Step 4 — Initialize the Tabu Memory:** Set tabu tenure $\tau$, aspiration level $A$, and the tabu list $T \leftarrow \emptyset$.
- **Step 5 — Iterative Best-Acceptance Loop:** At each iteration $k$, evaluate the fitness of every $x' \in N(x^{(k)})$, filter out tabu moves (unless aspiration is met), and select the best admissible move.
- **Step 6 — Update Memory Structures:** Add the chosen move's attributes to $T$; remove entries whose tenure has expired; optionally update long-term frequency memory.
- **Step 7 — Stopping Rule:** Halt when a maximum iteration count, time limit, or convergence threshold is satisfied. Return $x^{best}$ as the recommended decision.

## 2.2 Why TS Excels in Decision Making

- **Adaptive Memory:** Unlike rigid local search, TS *remembers* — critical when decisions have temporal or contextual dependencies.
- **Escape from Local Traps:** The tabu mechanism forces exploration of worse-looking moves, mimicking real-world "strategic detours."
- **Multi-Criteria Capability:** TS can incorporate weighted scalarization $f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(x)$, where $w_i$ are decision-maker preferences, enabling **Pareto-aware search**.
- **Constraint-Aware:** Infeasible decisions can be penalized using a **penalty function** $P(x) = \sum_{j} \lambda_j \cdot \max(0, g_j(x))$, integrating soft constraints.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Decision objective | $x^* = \arg\min_{x \in \mathcal{F}} f(x)$ | Selecting best decision |
| 2 | Scalarized multi-criteria | $f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(x), \quad \sum w_i = 1$ | Multi-objective decisions |
| 3 | Penalty-augmented fitness | $\Phi(x) = f(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \cdot \max(0, g_j(x))$ | Constraint handling |
| 4 | Tabu tenure rule (static) | $\tau = \text{const}$ (e.g., $7$) | Simple forbidden memory |
| 5 | Tabu tenure rule (dynamic) | $\tau(t) = \tau_0 + \alpha \cdot N_t(x_i)$ | Frequency-adapted memory |
| 6 | Aspiration criterion | If $f(x') < f^{best}$, override tabu | Allow exceptional moves |
| 7 | Move attribute hash | $h(x) = (i, j, \Delta f)$ | Encodes move identity |
| 8 | Frequency-based diversification | $P_{div}(x) = \sum_{c \in C} \frac{1}{1 + \text{freq}(c, x)}$ | Visit novel regions |
| 9 | Stopping criterion (iteration) | $k \geq K_{\max}$ | Computational budget |
| 10 | Stopping criterion (convergence) | $\vert f^{best}_{k} - f^{best}_{k-K_s} \vert \leq \epsilon$ | Saturation detection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 valuations, you may be asked to *justify* the choice of $\tau$ and $A$. Always state: "$\tau$ balances exploration vs. exploitation; $A$ prevents the algorithm from rejecting globally superior moves."

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Job-shop scheduling decisions under machine-availability constraints.
- **Logistics:** Vehicle routing decisions minimizing fuel/time while satisfying customer SLAs.
- **Finance:** Portfolio decisions maximizing expected return under risk and budget constraints.
- **Telecommunications:** Network design decisions minimizing latency and cost.
- **Healthcare:** Treatment-scheduling decisions respecting patient priority and resource capacity.
- **Smart Grid:** Energy dispatch decisions balancing cost, emissions, and demand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Formulation of a TS-Based Decision Problem

Consider a generic **multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) problem** solved by Tabu Search. The formal derivation proceeds as follows.

### Step 1 — Define Decision Variables

Let $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ represent the decision vector. Each variable $x_i$ belongs to a discrete or continuous domain:

$$x_i \in D_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

The complete decision space is the Cartesian product:

$$X = D_1 \times D_2 \times \cdots \times D_n$$

### Step 2 — Define the Feasible Region

The feasible decision region is constrained by inequality and equality constraints:

$$\mathcal{F} = \left\{ x \in X \,\Big|\, g_j(x) \leq 0, \; j = 1, \ldots, m; \quad h_k(x) = 0, \; k = 1, \ldots, p \right\}$$

### Step 3 — Multi-Objective Function

For a decision involving $k$ competing criteria, the objective vector is:

$$F(x) = \left( f_1(x), f_2(x), \ldots, f_k(x) \right)^T$$

### Step 4 — Scalarization (Weighted Sum Method)

The decision-maker assigns weights $w_i \geq 0$ with $\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i = 1$, reducing $F(x)$ to a scalar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(x) \\
&= w_1 f_1(x) + w_2 f_2(x) + \cdots + w_k f_k(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Augment with Penalty for Infeasible Decisions

Infeasible candidates are penalized using a Lagrange-style penalty term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(x) &= f(x) + P(x) \\
&= f(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \cdot \max\!\left(0, g_j(x)\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda_j > 0$ is a penalty coefficient chosen large enough to discourage violations.

### Step 6 — Define the Neighborhood Structure

A typical swap-based neighborhood is:

$$N(x) = \left\{ x' \,\Big|\, x'_i = x_i, \; \forall i \neq r, s; \quad x'_r, x'_s \in D_r \times D_s \right\}$$

i.e., exchanging the values of two decision variables $x_r$ and $x_s$.

### Step 7 — Tabu Membership Condition

A move $m = (r, s)$ is *tabu* at iteration $k$ if and only if its attribute hash appears in the active tabu list:

$$
m \in T(k) \iff h(m) = h(m_{k'}) \text{ for some } k' \geq k - \tau
$$

### Step 8 — Aspiration Override

A tabu move is admissible if it satisfies the aspiration criterion:

$$
\text{Admit } m \iff \left[ m \notin T(k) \right] \;\lor\; \left[ f(x'_m) < f^{best} \right]
$$

### Step 9 — Selection of Best-Admissible Move

At each iteration, the algorithm selects:

$$
x^{(k+1)} = \arg\min_{x' \in \mathcal{A}(k)} \Phi(x')
$$

where $\mathcal{A}(k) = N(x^{(k)}) \cap \text{Admissible Moves}(k)$ is the set of admissible candidates.

### Step 10 — Memory Update

The chosen move's hash is enqueued; the oldest expired entry is dequeued:

$$T(k+1) = \left( T(k) \cup \{h(m^*)\} \right) \setminus \{h(m_{k-\tau})\}$$

### Step 11 — Convergence Check

Stop when either:

$$
k \geq K_{\max} \quad \text{or} \quad \left\vert f^{best}_{k} - f^{best}_{k-K_s} \right\vert \leq \epsilon
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Illustrative)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Capital Budgeting Decision**

A firm must choose 3 of 5 projects (each with cost $c_i$ and profit $p_i$) under a budget constraint of $B = 100$. Weights: profit $w_1 = 0.7$, risk $w_2 = 0.3$.

**Data:**

| Project $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost $c_i$ | 40 | 30 | 50 | 20 | 35 |
| Profit $p_i$ | 70 | 50 | 90 | 30 | 60 |
| Risk $r_i$ | 25 | 15 | 40 | 10 | 20 |

**Initial Decision:** $x^{(0)} = \{1, 2, 3\}$ — Total cost $= 120$ (infeasible). Apply penalty.

**Penalized objective:**

$$
\Phi(x) = 0.7 \sum p_i - 0.3 \sum r_i + \lambda \cdot \max(0, \sum c_i - 100)
$$

With $\lambda = 10$:

$$
\Phi(\{1,2,3\}) = 0.7(210) - 0.3(80) + 10(20) = 147 - 24 + 200 = 323
$$

**Apply swap neighborhood** $N(x) = \{ \{2,3,4\}, \{1,3,4\}, \{1,2,4\}, \{1,2,5\}, \{1,3,5\}, \ldots \}$. Evaluate each admissible set and pick best non-tabu. The TS iterations converge toward the budget-feasible optimum $x^* = \{1,3,5\}$ (cost $= 125$, still over) or $\{2,3,4\}$ (cost $= 100$, feasible):

$$
\Phi(\{2,3,4\}) = 0.7(170) - 0.3(65) + 10(0) = 119 - 19.5 = 99.5
$$

**Recommended decision:** $x^* = \{2,3,4\}$ is the optimal feasible selection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tabu Search Decision-Making Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Decision Problem]) --> initB[Encode Decision Variables x]
    initB --> initC[Generate Initial Feasible Decision x0]
    initC --> initD[Initialize Tabu List T, Tenure tau, Aspiration A]
    initD --> iterE{Iteration k less than Kmax}
    iterE -- No --> stopF[Output xbest as Recommended Decision]
    iterE -- Yes --> neighG[Generate Neighbourhood N of xk]
    neighG --> evalH[Evaluate Fitness phi of each x prime]
    evalH --> filterI[Filter Tabu Moves using T]
    filterI --> aspJ{Aspiration Criterion Met}
    aspJ -- Yes --> acceptK[Admit Move to Candidate Set]
    aspJ -- No --> tabul[L[Reject Move as Tabu]
    acceptK --> pickM[Select Best Admissible Move x prime star]
    tabulL --> pickM
    pickM --> updN[Update Tabu List T and Memory]
    updN --> updO[Update x best if f x prime star is better]
    updO --> convP{Convergence or Budget Reached}
    convP -- No --> iterE
    convP -- Yes --> stopF
```

## 4.2 Multi-Criteria Decision Framework with TS

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Decision Maker Preferences] --> B[Weight Assignment w1 to wk]
    B --> C[Scalarization Function fx]
    D[Decision Variables x1 to xn] --> E[Feasibility Checker]
    E -- Feasible --> F[Tabu Search Engine]
    E -- Infeasible --> G[Penalty Function Px]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Best Admissible Move Selection]
    H --> I[Recommended Decision x star]
    C --> F
    F --> J[Tabu Memory: Short term and Long term]
    J --> F
```

## 4.3 Tabu Memory Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[Short Term Memory T] --> R[Recency based Tabu List]
    S --> F[Frequency based Penalty]
    L[Long Term Memory] --> D1[Diversification: Penalize Visited Regions]
    L --> I1[Intensification: Reward Promising Regions]
    Q[Quality Memory] --> E[Elite Solutions Archive]
    Q --> P[Best Found so far f best]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The short-term memory (tabu list) provides *recency-based restrictions*. The long-term memory tracks *frequency* and is used to diversify the search. The quality memory stores elite solutions for **intensification**. In a KTU exam, sketching this three-tier memory model can earn full methodology marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Definition**
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
> *Explain with an example how Tabu Search can be applied in multi-criteria decision making.*

**Model Answer:**

Tabu Search is applied to multi-criteria decision making by first **encoding the decision variables** as a discrete vector $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$, and **aggregating the conflicting objectives** using a weighted scalarization:

$$f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(x), \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i = 1$$

The weights $w_i$ encode the **decision-maker's preferences**. A tabu list $T$ stores attributes of recent moves to prevent **cycling**, while the **aspiration criterion** permits an exceptional move to override tabu status. **Example:** In a portfolio selection decision involving three objectives (return, risk, liquidity), the decision-maker assigns weights $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$. TS then searches the space of asset allocations, forbids revisiting recent combinations, and recommends the allocation that best balances the three criteria.

---

### **Question 2: Concept Clarification**
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> *Differentiate between the tabu list and the aspiration criterion in Tabu Search-based decision making.*

**Model Answer:**

The **tabu list** $T$ is a **short-term memory** structure that records the attributes of recently made moves and **prohibits** the algorithm from revisiting them, with a tenure $\tau$ controlling the duration. The **aspiration criterion** $A$ is an **override mechanism**: it permits a tabu move to be accepted if the move produces a solution better than the best-known solution $f^{best}$. In essence, the tabu list enforces *forbidding* to escape local optima, while the aspiration criterion enforces *excellence* to ensure the algorithm never rejects a globally superior decision.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Application with Formulation**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Style]**
> A manufacturing firm must decide the daily production quantities of 4 products. Each product $i$ has profit $p_i$, machine-hour consumption $m_i$, and labour-hour consumption $l_i$. The daily machine capacity is $M$ and labour capacity is $L$. Formulate this decision problem and explain how Tabu Search solves it, including the role of tabu list, tenure, and aspiration criterion.

#### **Part (a) — Mathematical Formulation (7 Marks)**

**Decision Variables:** Let $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ be the units of product $i$ produced daily, $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Objective:** Maximize total profit.

$$\max \; f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i x_i$$

**Constraints:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{4} m_i x_i &\leq M \quad \text{(Machine capacity)} \\
\sum_{i=1}^{4} l_i x_i &\leq L \quad \text{(Labour capacity)} \\
x_i &\in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Feasible Region:**

$$\mathcal{F} = \left\{ x \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}^4 \,\Big|\, \sum m_i x_i \leq M, \; \sum l_i x_i \leq L \right\}$$

**Tabu Search Formulation:**

- **Encoding:** $x = (x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4)$.
- **Neighborhood:** $N(x) = \{ x' : x'_i = x_i \pm 1 \text{ for some single } i \}$.
- **Fitness (with penalty):** $\Phi(x) = -\sum p_i x_i + \lambda_1 \max(0, \sum m_i x_i - M) + \lambda_2 \max(0, \sum l_i x_i - L)$.
- **Tabu list:** Stores the index $i$ of the most recently changed variable, with tenure $\tau = 5$.
- **Aspiration:** Accept tabu move $i$ if $\Phi(x'_i) < \Phi(x^{best})$.

#### **Part (b) — TS Algorithm Steps (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Initialization:** Generate initial feasible decision $x^{(0)}$ using a greedy heuristic (allocate capacity to highest $p_i / (m_i + l_i)$ ratio). Set $T \leftarrow \emptyset$, $x^{best} \leftarrow x^{(0)}$, $k \leftarrow 0$.

**Step 2 — Neighborhood Generation:** Produce $N(x^{(k)})$ by varying one $x_i$ at a time.

**Step 3 — Tabu Filtering:** Discard any $x' \in N(x^{(k)})$ whose move attribute $i$ is in $T$ and does not satisfy the aspiration criterion.

**Step 4 — Selection:** Choose the admissible $x'$ minimizing $\Phi(x')$.

**Step 5 — Update Memory:** Add the index $i^*$ of the chosen move to $T$. Remove entries older than $\tau = 5$ iterations.

**Step 6 — Update Best:** If $f(x') > f(x^{best})$, update $x^{best} \leftarrow x'$.

**Step 7 — Termination:** Stop when $k = K_{\max}$ (e.g., 500 iterations) or when $\vert f^{best}_{k} - f^{best}_{k-50} \vert \leq 10^{-4}$.

**Step 8 — Output:** Recommended production plan $x^* = x^{best}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Style]**
> Discuss the role of memory structures in Tabu Search. With a suitable decision-making example, show how short-term, intermediate-term, and long-term memory influence the search trajectory.

#### **Part (a) — Memory Structure Theory (7 Marks)**

Tabu Search uses **three types of memory** to enhance decision-making:

**1. Short-Term Memory (Recency-Based):** Maintains the **tabu list** $T$ of recent move attributes. Prevents cycling. Example: a scheduling decision where the same job-swap is forbidden for the next $\tau = 7$ iterations.

**2. Intermediate-Term Memory (Frequency-Based):** Tracks how often attributes have been used over a medium horizon. Used for **intensification** — concentrating the search around elite decisions. Frequency $freq(i, k)$ counts how many times variable $x_i$ was modified in the last $K$ iterations.

**3. Long-Term Memory (Quality/Diversification):** Stores the best solutions found in an **elite archive** and tracks long-term visitation frequencies. Used for **diversification** — penalizing heavily-visited regions to drive the search toward unexplored decision zones.

**Mathematical Encoding:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Recency: } & T(k) = \{ h(m_j) : k - \tau \leq j \leq k \} \\
\text{Frequency: } & \text{freq}(a, k) = \sum_{j=0}^{K-1} \mathbb{1}[a \in m_{k-j}] \\
\text{Elite Archive: } & E = \{ x^{best}_1, x^{best}_2, \ldots, x^{best}_{N_e} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Illustrative Decision Example (7 Marks)**

**Scenario:** A logistics manager must assign 6 delivery trucks to 6 zones to minimize total fuel cost subject to capacity constraints.

**Step 1 — Encoding:** $x = (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_6)$, a permutation of zone indices.

**Step 2 — Short-Term Memory:** Maintain tabu list $T$ of recent *swap pairs* $(\pi_i, \pi_j)$ with tenure $\tau = 7$. Prevents the algorithm from undoing the same swap repeatedly.

**Step 3 — Intermediate-Term Memory:** Track $freq(\text{zone}_i)$ — how often zone $i$ has been moved in the last 30 iterations. If $freq(\text{zone}_i) > 8$, *intensify* by keeping $\text{zone}_i$ in its current position and exploring swaps among the remaining zones.

**Step 4 — Long-Term Memory:** Maintain elite archive $E$ of top 5 assignments. If the current solution is similar to an archive entry, *diversify* by forcing a swap of two rarely-used zones, injecting fresh exploration.

**Step 5 — Combined Influence:** The search trajectory becomes **non-monotonic** — it strategically revisits promising regions (intensification) while escaping overly-exploited ones (diversification), guided by three memory tiers.

**Result:** The algorithm converges to a near-optimal assignment, e.g., $x^* = (2, 5, 1, 6, 3, 4)$ with fuel cost $f(x^*) = 142$ units, balancing the three memory influences effectively.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in TS Decision-Making Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the aspiration criterion:** Many students describe only the tabu list and overlook that aspirational overrides are essential — *expect to lose 2 marks* if you do.
> 2. **No penalty function shown:** Infeasible decisions must be handled. Always include a $\lambda \cdot \max(0, g(x))$ penalty term; otherwise, your formulation is incomplete.
> 3. **Confusing intensification with diversification:** Intensification = *exploit* promising areas; Diversification = *explore* new areas. Mixing them up costs full methodology marks.
> 4. **Missing the scalarization step:** For multi-criteria problems, always show the weighted sum $f(x) = \sum w_i f_i(x)$ and justify $\sum w_i = 1$.
> 5. **No stopping criterion stated:** Every TS algorithm must declare a termination rule (iteration limit or convergence threshold).
> 6. **Tabu tenure chosen arbitrarily:** Always justify the choice of $\tau$ — e.g., "$\tau = 7$ balances exploration breadth with computational efficiency."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Application of TS in Decision Making"**

- **Definition:** TS is a memory-based metaheuristic that solves decision problems by maintaining a tabu list to forbid recent moves and an aspiration criterion to allow exceptional ones.
- **Three Pillars of TS in Decision Making:** (1) Tabu list with tenure $\tau$, (2) Aspiration criterion $A$, (3) Best-admissible move selection.
- **Decision Encoding:** Real-world decisions are mapped to vectors $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$.
- **Feasibility:** Use **penalty function** $P(x) = \sum \lambda_j \max(0, g_j(x))$ to handle constraints.
- **Multi-Criteria:** Use **weighted scalarization** $f(x) = \sum w_i f_i(x)$ with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- **Memory Tiers:** Recency (short-term) → Frequency (intermediate) → Quality/Diversification (long-term).
- **Neighborhood Design:** Must be problem-specific (swap, insertion, reallocation, 2-opt, etc.).
- **Stopping Criteria:** Iteration limit $K_{\max}$ OR convergence $\vert \Delta f^{best} \vert \leq \epsilon$.
- **Applications:** Scheduling, routing, portfolio, facility location, network design, energy dispatch.
- **Trade-off:** Small $\tau$ risks cycling; large $\tau$ over-forbids good moves. Balance via experimentation.
- **Pareto Awareness:** In multi-criteria TS, maintain a **Pareto archive** of non-dominated solutions, not just a single $x^{best}$.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Application of TS in Decision Making**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulated Annealing: Main Components of Simulated Annealing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simulated Annealing: Main Components of Simulated Annealing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a stochastic, trajectory-based metaheuristic optimization algorithm inspired by the physical process of **annealing in metallurgy**, where a metal is heated to a high temperature and then slowly cooled to reach a low-energy, defect-free crystalline state. Formally, it is a probabilistic technique proposed by **Kirkpatrick, Gelatt, and Vecchi (1983)** that approximates the global optimum of a given objective function in a large search space by accepting worse solutions with a controlled probability that decreases over iterations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Simulated Annealing is a probabilistic metaheuristic that escapes local optima by occasionally accepting inferior solutions based on the Metropolis criterion, governed by a temperature parameter that is gradually reduced according to a cooling schedule.*

---

## 1.2 The Five Core Components of Simulated Annealing

The architecture of any SA algorithm is built upon **five inseparable pillars**. These components must be carefully designed for the algorithm to function correctly:

| # | Component | KTU Terminology | Role |
|---|-----------|----------------|------|
| 1 | **Solution Space (S)** | State Space | The set of all feasible candidate solutions |
| 2 | **Neighborhood Structure (N(s))** | Move Operator | Defines how a new candidate is generated from current solution |
| 3 | **Objective Function E(s)** | Cost / Energy Function | The metric being minimized (or maximized) |
| 4 | **Acceptance Criterion** | Metropolis Criterion | Decides whether to accept a worse solution |
| 5 | **Cooling Schedule T(k)** | Annealing Schedule | Controls the temperature decrement over iterations |

---

## 1.3 Real-World Intuition — The Metallurgical Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Simple Analogy: "The Mountain Trekker"**
>
> Imagine a blindfolded trekker trying to find the lowest valley in a foggy mountain range (the global minimum). At each step, the trekker feels the slope. In a pure greedy descent (like hill climbing), the trekker would only step downhill — getting trapped in the nearest valley (local minimum).
>
> Now imagine the trekker has a **"temperature" knob**:
> - At **high temperature** → the trekker occasionally takes **uphill steps** (worse solutions) to escape valleys.
> - At **low temperature** → the trekker only takes **downhill steps** (better solutions), fine-tuning the final valley.
>
> This **probabilistic uphill movement** is precisely what allows Simulated Annealing to escape local optima and find the deepest valley (global optimum).

The metaphor maps directly:

- **Metal Crystal** → Candidate Solution
- **Energy of Molecules (E)** → Objective Function Value
- **Temperature (T)** → Control Parameter that decays over time
- **Thermal Equilibrium at T** → Inner-loop iterations at constant T

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Boltzmann Constant (k_B) ≈ 1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K** — used in the original physical analogy, but in computational SA, the constant is absorbed into the temperature parameter.
- **Acceptance Probability Threshold (r)** — a uniform random number drawn from **U(0,1)**.
- **Cooling Ratio (α)** — typically in the range **0.8 ≤ α ≤ 0.99**.
- **Initial Temperature (T₀)** — chosen so that initial acceptance probability ≈ **e⁻¹ ≈ 0.367** for an average energy increase ΔE.

---

## 1.5 Historical Context (KTU Knowledge Block)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Origin:** Independently developed by **Scott Kirkpatrick, C. Daniel Gelatt, and Mario P. Vecchi (1983)** and by **Vladimír Černý (1985)**. The algorithm is a computational simulation of the **Metropolis-Hastings algorithm** from statistical mechanics (N. Metropolis et al., 1953), which simulates the evolution of a solid to thermal equilibrium.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analytical Breakdown of Each Main Component

### 🔷 Component 1 — Solution Representation (State Space)

- A solution $s \in S$ encodes a complete candidate answer to the optimization problem.
- The encoding may be binary, real-valued, integer, permutation, or tree-based depending on the problem.
- **Why it matters:** An inappropriate encoding creates a rugged, hard-to-navigate energy landscape.

### 🔷 Component 2 — Neighborhood Structure $N(s)$

- For each state $s$, the neighborhood $N(s)$ defines all states reachable by a **single move** (perturbation).
- Formally: $N(s) = \{ s' : s' \text{ is reachable from } s \text{ by one move operator} \}$.
- **How it works:** In each iteration, $s' \in N(s)$ is sampled uniformly or using a specific distribution.
- A well-designed neighborhood balances **reachability** and **locality** — every feasible solution must be reachable through a finite sequence of moves (**strong connectivity**).

### 🔷 Component 3 — Objective (Energy) Function $E(s)$

- The function to be minimized: $\min_{s \in S} E(s)$.
- For maximization problems, transform as $E'(s) = -F(s)$ or use $-E(s)$.
- **Why it matters:** Smooth energy landscapes converge faster; rugged landscapes require slower cooling.

### 🔷 Component 4 — Acceptance Criterion (Metropolis Criterion)

- The heart of SA. The algorithm accepts:
  - Any **improving** move ($\Delta E \leq 0$) unconditionally.
  - Any **worsening** move ($\Delta E > 0$) with probability:

$$
P(\text{accept } s') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)
$$

  - where $\Delta E = E(s') - E(s)$ and $T$ is the current temperature.

- **Why it matters:** This Boltzmann-type probability is what allows the algorithm to escape local minima. The smaller $T$ is, the smaller the acceptance probability of a bad move.

### 🔷 Component 5 — Cooling Schedule $T(k)$

- The cooling schedule defines how $T$ decreases with iteration count $k$.
- Must satisfy the **logarithmic cooling condition** for theoretical convergence to global optimum:

$$
T(k) \geq \frac{C}{\log(k+1)}
$$

  - where $C$ is a constant related to the depth of the deepest local minimum not being a global minimum.
- Practical schedules (used in KTU problems) are described in the formula sheet below.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for ESE/University exams.**

| # | Formula | Name | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\Delta E = E(s') - E(s)$ | Energy Difference | Determines acceptance direction |
| 2 | $P = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta E}{T}\right)$ | Metropolis Acceptance Probability | For $\Delta E > 0$ (worsening move) |
| 3 | $P = 1$ | Deterministic Acceptance | For $\Delta E \leq 0$ (improving move) |
| 4 | $T(k+1) = \alpha \cdot T(k)$ | **Geometric Cooling** | Most common; $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$ |
| 5 | $T(k) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}$ | Geometric (Explicit Form) | Equivalent to (4) |
| 6 | $T(k) = T_0 - \alpha \cdot k$ | Linear Cooling | Simple, less efficient |
| 7 | $T(k) = \dfrac{T_0}{1 + \alpha \cdot k}$ | Cauchy / Fast Annealing | Smoother descent |
| 8 | $T(k) = \dfrac{C}{\log(k + k_0)}$ | Logarithmic Cooling | Theoretical convergence guarantee |
| 9 | $T_0 \approx \dfrac{-\Delta E_{avg}}{\ln(p_0)}$ | Initial Temperature Calibrator | Sets initial acceptance to $p_0$ |
| 10 | $T_f \approx 10^{-3}$ or after no improvement for $L$ steps | Final Temperature / Stopping | Termination criterion |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In any KTU answer sheet, do **not** write $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table cell — always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or absolute-value phrasing to avoid formatting corruption.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

Simulated Annealing is used across engineering domains:

- **VLSI Circuit Design** — optimal placement of cells on a chip to minimize wire length.
- **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — finding shortest tour visiting N cities.
- **Job-Shop Scheduling** — minimizing makespan in manufacturing.
- **Image Processing** — image segmentation and restoration.
- **Neural Network Training** — Boltzmann machines for learning weights.
- **Structural Optimization** — truss design and topology optimization.

---

## 2.4 Algorithm Pseudo-Logic (Conceptual Flow)

```
Initialize s, T = T₀, k = 0
WHILE stopping criterion not met:
    WHILE inner-loop equilibrium not reached:
        Pick s' ∈ N(s) randomly
        ΔE ← E(s') − E(s)
        IF ΔE ≤ 0:
            s ← s'
        ELSE:
            r ← random number in U(0,1)
            IF r < exp(−ΔE / T):
                s ← s'
    T ← CoolingSchedule(k)        # Decrement temperature
    k ← k + 1
RETURN s (best solution found)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Metropolis Acceptance Probability

The acceptance probability in SA is derived from **statistical mechanics**, specifically the **Boltzmann distribution**.

### Step 1 — The Physical Foundation

In thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$, a physical system occupies a state with energy $E$ with probability proportional to the **Boltzmann factor**:

$$
P(\text{state}) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{k_B \cdot T}\right)
$$

where:
- $E$ = energy of the state
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature (in Kelvin)

### Step 2 — Ratio of Two States

Consider two states: current solution $s$ with energy $E(s)$ and candidate solution $s'$ with energy $E(s')$. The relative probability of being in $s'$ versus $s$ at equilibrium is:

$$
\frac{P(s')}{P(s)} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\frac{E(s')}{k_B T}\right)}{\exp\!\left(-\frac{E(s)}{k_B T}\right)}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplification

Applying the exponential quotient rule:

$$
\frac{P(s')}{P(s)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{E(s') - E(s)}{k_B T}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{P(s')}{P(s)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)
$$

where $\Delta E = E(s') - E(s)$.

### Step 4 — Algorithmic Absorption of $k_B$

Since algorithmic SA treats $T$ as a dimensionless control parameter, $k_B$ is absorbed into the definition of $T$:

$$
P(\text{accept } s') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)
$$

This is the **Metropolis criterion** used in all SA implementations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this form?** As $T \to \infty$, $\exp(-\Delta E / T) \to 1$, so all moves are accepted (pure exploration). As $T \to 0^+$, the probability $\to 0$ for $\Delta E > 0$ (pure exploitation). This dual behaviour is the genius of SA.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem Statement:**
A SA algorithm is minimizing $f(x) = x^2$ on integers. The current solution is $s = 5$ with energy $E(s) = 25$. A neighbor $s' = 7$ is proposed with $E(s') = 49$. The current temperature is $T = 30$. Determine whether the move is accepted if $r = 0.4$ (random number).

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Compute the energy difference.

$$
\Delta E = E(s') - E(s) = 49 - 25 = 24
$$

**Step 2:** Since $\Delta E > 0$, the move worsens the solution. Apply the Metropolis criterion.

$$
P(\text{accept}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{24}{30}\right) = \exp(-0.8)
$$

**Step 3:** Numerical evaluation.

$$
P(\text{accept}) \approx e^{-0.8} \approx 0.4493
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with random number $r = 0.4$.

$$
r = 0.4 < P(\text{accept}) = 0.4493
$$

**Decision:** ✅ **The worsening move is ACCEPTED** — even though $s' = 7$ is worse than $s = 5$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students frequently forget the condition "if $r < \exp(-\Delta E / T)$". Note the strict inequality direction.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Cooling Schedule Calculation

**Problem:**
Starting from $T_0 = 1000$, find the temperature at iteration $k = 10$ using **geometric cooling** with $\alpha = 0.9$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Recall geometric cooling formula.

$$
T(k) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
T(10) = 1000 \cdot (0.9)^{10}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $(0.9)^{10}$.

$$
(0.9)^{10} \approx 0.3487
$$

**Step 4:** Final answer.

$$
T(10) = 1000 \cdot 0.3487 \approx 348.68
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Move to Accept?

| Condition | Energy Change | Random Number $r$ | Decision | Algorithmic Meaning |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|----------|---------------------|
| $\Delta E < 0$ | Improving | Any | **Accept** | Greedy downhill step |
| $\Delta E = 0$ | Neutral | Any | **Accept** | Sideways move |
| $\Delta E > 0$ | Worsening | $r < \exp(-\Delta E/T)$ | **Accept** | Escape local minimum |
| $\Delta E > 0$ | Worsening | $r \geq \exp(-\Delta E/T)$ | **Reject** | Stay at current $s$ |

---

## 3.5 Initial Temperature Calibration — Derivation

A common heuristic sets $T_0$ such that the initial acceptance probability for an average worsening move equals a target value $p_0$.

Setting $\exp(-\Delta E_{\text{avg}} / T_0) = p_0$, taking the natural log of both sides:

$$
\ln p_0 = -\frac{\Delta E_{\text{avg}}}{T_0}
$$

Solving for $T_0$:

$$
T_0 = -\frac{\Delta E_{\text{avg}}}{\ln p_0}
$$

For $p_0 = 0.5$, $\ln(0.5) \approx -0.693$, so:

$$
T_0 \approx 1.443 \cdot \Delta E_{\text{avg}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **board-exam favorite** — practice deriving this expression.

---

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Simulated Annealing Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Initialize s0, T0, k=0"] --> B["Set current s = s0"]
    B --> C{"Stopping Criterion Met?"}
    C -->|"No"| D["Inner Loop: Set j=0"]
    D --> E["Generate s' from N(s)"]
    E --> F["Compute deltaE = E(s') - E(s)"]
    F --> G{"deltaE <= 0?"}
    G -->|"Yes (Improving)"| H["Accept s = s'"]
    G -->|"No (Worsening)"| I["Compute P = exp(-deltaE / T)"]
    I --> J{"r < P? where r ~ Uniform(0,1)"}
    J -->|"Yes"| H
    J -->|"No"| K["Reject: Keep s"]
    H --> L["j = j + 1"]
    K --> L
    L --> M{"Inner Loop Equilibrium?"}
    M -->|"No"| E
    M -->|"Yes"| N["Apply Cooling: T = CoolSchedule(k)"]
    N --> O["k = k + 1"]
    O --> C
    C -->|"Yes"| P["RETURN Best Solution Found"]
```

## 4.4 State Diagram — Acceptance vs Rejection Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ComputeDeltaE
    ComputeDeltaE --> CheckSign: deltaE = E(s') - E(s)
    CheckSign --> Accept: deltaE <= 0
    CheckSign --> ComputeProb: deltaE > 0
    ComputeProb --> GenerateRandom: P = exp(-deltaE/T)
    GenerateRandom --> CompareRandom: r in U(0,1)
    CompareRandom --> Accept: r < P
    CompareRandom --> Reject: r >= P
    Accept --> UpdateSolution: s = s'
    Reject --> KeepCurrent: s unchanged
    UpdateSolution --> [*]
    KeepCurrent --> [*]
```
