# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]`

**List any THREE main components of the Simulated Annealing algorithm and briefly explain the role of the cooling schedule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three main components of Simulated Annealing are:
1. **Neighborhood Structure $N(s)$** — defines how a candidate solution $s'$ is generated from the current solution $s$.
2. **Acceptance Criterion (Metropolis Criterion)** — determines whether to accept $s'$ based on $\Delta E$ and current temperature $T$, using $P = \exp(-\Delta E/T)$ for worsening moves.
3. **Cooling Schedule $T(k)$** — controls how temperature decreases over iterations.

**Role of the Cooling Schedule (1 mark):**
The cooling schedule governs the **exploration vs. exploitation trade-off**. At high $T$, the algorithm explores broadly by accepting worsening moves with high probability, escaping local optima. As $T$ decreases, the algorithm exploits, accepting only improving moves and converging to a minimum. A common schedule is $T(k+1) = \alpha \cdot T(k)$ with $\alpha \approx 0.9$.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]`

**State the Metropolis acceptance criterion. What happens when (i) $T \to \infty$ and (ii) $T \to 0$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Metropolis acceptance criterion** for a worsening move ($\Delta E > 0$) is:

$$
P(\text{accept } s') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)
$$

For $\Delta E \leq 0$, the move is accepted with probability $1$.

**(i) When $T \to \infty$:** $\exp(-\Delta E / T) \to \exp(0) = 1$. **All moves are accepted**, regardless of whether they worsen the solution. The algorithm performs **pure random search** (full exploration).

**(ii) When $T \to 0$:** $\exp(-\Delta E / T) \to 0$ for $\Delta E > 0$. **Worsening moves are rejected**, and only improving moves are accepted. The algorithm behaves like a **pure greedy descent** (full exploitation).

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question Choice A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain in detail the **FIVE main components of Simulated Annealing**. Describe how each component contributes to the algorithm's ability to escape local optima.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A SA algorithm minimizes $f(x) = x^2$ over integers. Current state $s = 8$, $E(s) = 64$. Proposed neighbor $s' = 11$, $E(s') = 121$. Temperature $T = 40$. Random number $r = 0.6$.
(i) Compute $\Delta E$ and the acceptance probability.
(ii) State whether the move is accepted.
(iii) If accepted, repeat for a new candidate $s'' = 9$ with $E(s'') = 81$ at the same $T$.

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Question A

#### Part (a) — Five Main Components (7 marks)

The five essential components of Simulated Annealing are:

**1. Solution Space (S) — 1 mark:**
The set of all feasible candidate solutions to the optimization problem. Each $s \in S$ represents a complete configuration. Without a well-defined $S$, no search can occur.

**2. Neighborhood Structure $N(s)$ — 1.5 marks:**
The mapping that generates neighboring solutions from a current one. Formally, $N(s) = \{ s' : s' \text{ obtained from } s \text{ by a single move}\}$. A good neighborhood must be **strongly connected** (any solution reachable from any other through finite moves) and provide **locality** (small moves lead to small changes in energy).

**3. Objective Function $E(s)$ — 1 mark:**
The cost/energy function to be minimized. The algorithm compares energies of states to decide acceptance. The landscape geometry of $E(s)$ directly affects convergence.

**4. Acceptance Criterion (Metropolis) — 2 marks:**
The probabilistic rule:

$$
P(\text{accept } s') = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \Delta E \leq 0 \\
\exp(-\Delta E / T) & \text{if } \Delta E > 0
\end{cases}
$$

This is **the key to escaping local optima** — by accepting worse solutions with non-zero probability, the algorithm climbs out of valleys.

**5. Cooling Schedule $T(k)$ — 1.5 marks:**
The temperature decrement rule. Choices include geometric, linear, logarithmic, and Cauchy schedules. The cooling schedule ensures that **early exploration transitions into late exploitation**, mimicking physical annealing.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Compute $\Delta E$ and acceptance probability — 3 marks:**

$$
\Delta E = E(s') - E(s) = 121 - 64 = 57
$$

$$
P(\text{accept}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{57}{40}\right) = \exp(-1.425) \approx 0.2405
$$

**(ii) Decision — 1 mark:**

Compare $r = 0.6$ with $P = 0.2405$:
Since $r = 0.6 > P = 0.2405$, the move is **REJECTED**. The solution remains $s = 8$.

**(iii) New candidate $s'' = 9$ — 3 marks:**

$$
\Delta E_{\text{new}} = E(s'') - E(s) = 81 - 64 = 17
$$

$$
P(\text{accept}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{17}{40}\right) = \exp(-0.425) \approx 0.6538
$$

Since $r = 0.6 < P = 0.6538$, the move is **ACCEPTED**. New current solution is $s = 9$.

---

### **Question Choice B (Alternative) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Derive the **Metropolis acceptance probability** from the Boltzmann distribution. Explain the role of temperature $T$ in the algorithm's convergence.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Compare **geometric cooling** $T(k+1) = \alpha T(k)$ with **linear cooling** $T(k) = T_0 - \alpha k$. Given $T_0 = 500$, $\alpha = 0.95$ (geometric) and $\alpha = 5$ (linear), compute $T(10)$ for both and comment on which is preferred in practice.

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Boltzmann Distribution (2 marks)**

At thermal equilibrium, a system is in state with energy $E$ with probability:

$$
P(\text{state}) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Ratio of Two States (2 marks)**

$$
\frac{P(s')}{P(s)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{E(s') - E(s)}{k_B T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Computational Form (1 mark)**

Absorbing $k_B$ into $T$:

$$
P(\text{accept } s') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Role of Temperature (2 marks)**

- **High $T$:** $P \to 1$, broad exploration, escapes local optima.
- **Low $T$:** $P \to 0$ for $\Delta E > 0$, refinement, local convergence.
- **Asymptotic convergence:** Logarithmic cooling $T(k) \geq C/\log(k+1)$ guarantees probabilistic convergence to global optimum.

---

#### Part (b) — Cooling Comparison (7 marks)

**Geometric Cooling (3 marks):**

$$
T(10) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{10} = 500 \cdot (0.95)^{10}
$$

$$
T(10) = 500 \cdot 0.5987 \approx 299.37
$$

**Linear Cooling (3 marks):**

$$
T(10) = T_0 - \alpha \cdot k = 500 - 5 \cdot 10 = 500 - 50 = 450
$$

**Commentary (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Geometric | Linear |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| Decay shape | Exponential (fast then slow) | Constant rate |
| Practical preference | **Preferred** ✓ | Less efficient |
| Risk | Possible premature convergence | May cool too fast initially |

Geometric cooling is preferred because it mimics the physical cooling process more accurately, allowing more iterations at lower temperatures where fine-tuning occurs.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Inequality Direction Error (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing "$r > \exp(-\Delta E / T)$" instead of "$r < \exp(-\Delta E / T)$". The random number must be **LESS THAN** the acceptance probability.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\Delta E \leq 0$ Case (–1 mark):** Many students only state the formula for worsening moves. You **must** also note that improving moves are accepted with probability 1.
>
> 3. **Misuse of Absolute Value Bars in Tables (–0 marks but loses clarity):** Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ inside LaTeX tables, never bare pipes.
>
> 4. **Confusing $T_0$ with $T_f$:** $T_0$ is the initial temperature (high), $T_f$ is the final temperature (low, near zero).
>
> 5. **Skipping Step Justification:** In KTU board evaluations, every algebraic step must have a one-line explanation. Bare equation chains lose marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting that $\Delta E$ is defined as $E(s') - E(s)$**, not the absolute difference. Sign is crucial.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Simulated Annealing Components**

- ⭐ SA is a **probabilistic metaheuristic** inspired by physical annealing in metallurgy.
- ⭐ The **five main components** are: Solution Space, Neighborhood, Energy Function, Metropolis Acceptance, Cooling Schedule.
- ⭐ **Metropolis Criterion:** $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ for $\Delta E > 0$; $P = 1$ for $\Delta E \leq 0$.
- ⭐ **Geometric cooling** $T(k+1) = \alpha \cdot T(k)$ with $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$ is most commonly used.
- ⭐ **Logarithmic cooling** $T(k) = C / \log(k+1)$ provides theoretical convergence guarantee.
- ⭐ **Initial temperature calibration:** $T_0 = -\Delta E_{\text{avg}} / \ln(p_0)$.
- ⭐ **High $T$** → exploration; **Low $T$** → exploitation.
- ⭐ SA escapes local optima due to probabilistic acceptance of worsening moves.
- ⭐ The algorithm returns the **best solution encountered** (not just the final one).
- ⭐ Always check if $r < \exp(-\Delta E/T)$ for worsening moves; accept directly for improving moves.
- ⭐ Three nested loops: outer (temperature), inner (equilibrium at fixed T), innermost (move generation).
- ⭐ Stopping criteria: fixed iteration count, fixed final temperature, or no improvement over $L$ consecutive iterations.
- ⭐ Applications: TSP, VLSI, scheduling, image processing, neural networks (Boltzmann machines).

---

topics.title: Homogeneous vs. In-homogenous Simulated Annealing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Homogeneous vs. In-homogenous Simulated Annealing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Simulated Annealing — Foundational Recap

**Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a stochastic, trajectory-based metaheuristic optimization algorithm inspired by the physical annealing process in metallurgy, where a metal is heated to a high temperature and slowly cooled to reach a low-energy crystalline state. In optimization terminology, the algorithm searches for the global minimum of an objective (cost) function by probabilistically accepting worse solutions to escape local optima.

The algorithm is governed by a control parameter called **temperature (T)**, which is gradually reduced from a high initial value to near zero using a **cooling schedule**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> Simulated Annealing is a probabilistic technique that approximates the global optimum of a given objective function. The classification into **Homogeneous** and **Inhomogeneous** variants is based on the Markov chain structure employed during the search process.

---

## 1.2 Homogeneous Simulated Annealing (HSA) — Definition

**Homogeneous Simulated Annealing** uses a **homogeneous (stationary) Markov chain** at each fixed temperature level. The transition probabilities remain constant while the temperature T is held fixed, allowing the chain to reach its **stationary (Boltzmann) distribution** before the temperature is reduced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> HSA is an SA variant in which, for every temperature value $T_k$ in the cooling schedule, a Markov chain of sufficient length is executed so that the state-space distribution converges to the stationary Boltzmann distribution:
>
> $$\pi_T(x) = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\frac{E(x)}{k_B T}\right)}{\sum_{y \in S} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E(y)}{k_B T}\right)}$$

---

## 1.3 Inhomogeneous Simulated Annealing (ISA) — Definition

**Inhomogeneous Simulated Annealing** employs a **non-stationary (inhomogeneous) Markov chain**, where the temperature parameter is reduced at every iteration (or every few iterations) without waiting for the chain to equilibrate. The transition probabilities change at every step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> ISA is an SA variant in which the temperature parameter $T_t$ is decreased monotonically as a function of time step $t$ (commonly geometrically or logarithmically), and the chain never reaches a true stationary distribution at any temperature.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber

| Variant | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Homogeneous SA** | Imagine a blindfolded climber exploring a mountain range on a **single plateau at a time**. At each elevation, the climber wanders freely for a long time, surveying all peaks and valleys at that level before being teleported to the next (lower) plateau. This thorough exploration at each level guarantees finding the best configuration possible at that elevation. |
| **Inhomogeneous SA** | The climber starts at the summit but the **altitude itself drops continuously** while they walk. They never get to fully map any single plateau. They must rely on memory and momentum, accepting that some valleys may be missed — but they finish the descent much faster. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:**
> - **Homogeneous** → **H**old temperature **H**orizontally (equilibrate first)
> - **Inhomogeneous** → **I**nstantly decrease **I**teration-by-iteration (no equilibrium)

---

## 1.5 Key Physical/Algorithmic Constants

- **Boltzmann constant** $k_B$ — often set to **1** in algorithmic SA for normalization.
- **Initial temperature** $T_0$ — typically chosen so that the initial acceptance probability of the worst solution is ≥ 0.8.
- **Cooling factor** $\alpha$ — geometric cooling multiplier, $0.8 \le \alpha \le 0.99$.
- **Markov chain length** $L_k$ — number of iterations at temperature $T_k$ in HSA.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Operational Logic — Homogeneous SA

1. **Initialize** $T_0$ (high), choose initial solution $x_0$, set $T = T_0$.
2. **Outer Loop** (over temperature levels $T_k$):
   - Set current temperature $T \leftarrow T_k$.
   - **Inner Loop** (Markov chain at fixed $T$, length $L_k$):
     - Generate a neighbor $x'$ from $x$ using perturbation.
     - Compute $\Delta E = f(x') - f(x)$.
     - If $\Delta E \le 0$: accept $x'$ (downhill move).
     - Else: accept $x'$ with Metropolis probability:
       $$P_{\text{accept}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)$$
     - Repeat inner loop $L_k$ times → equilibrium reached.
   - **Cool** the temperature: $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$.
3. **Termination** when $T \approx 0$ or stopping criterion met.

### Why HSA Works (Theoretical Justification)

- At each fixed $T$, the chain is **ergodic** and converges to the Boltzmann distribution $\pi_T(x)$.
- As $T \to 0$, $\pi_T(x)$ concentrates mass on the global minima.
- By **Geman & Geman (1984) theorem**, HSA converges in probability to a global optimum provided $T_k \ge \frac{c}{\log(k+1)}$ and each Markov chain is long enough.

---

## 2.2 Algorithmic Operational Logic — Inhomogeneous SA

1. **Initialize** $T_0$, choose $x_0$, set iteration $t = 0$.
2. **Single Loop** (over iterations):
   - Generate neighbor $x'$ from $x$.
   - Compute $\Delta E = f(x') - f(x)$.
   - If $\Delta E \le 0$: accept $x'$.
   - Else: accept with probability $\exp(-\Delta E / T_t)$.
   - **Immediately cool**: $T_{t+1} = \alpha \cdot T_t$ (or other schedule).
   - $t \leftarrow t + 1$.
3. **Termination** when $T \approx 0$ or maximum iterations reached.

### Why ISA Works

- The non-stationary chain's transition kernel $P_t(x \to x')$ changes at every step.
- Convergence is guaranteed only under **sufficient cooling schedules** (e.g., logarithmic decay).
- In practice, geometric cooling works well empirically despite lacking theoretical convergence guarantee.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Metropolis acceptance | $P_{\text{accept}} = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ | $\Delta E > 0$; else always accept |
| 2 | Boltzmann stationary distribution | $\pi_T(x) = \frac{\exp(-E(x)/T)}{\sum_y \exp(-E(y)/T)}$ | $k_B$ normalized to 1 |
| 3 | Geometric cooling (HSA & ISA) | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ | $0.8 \le \alpha \le 0.99$ |
| 4 | Logarithmic cooling (slow) | $T_k = \frac{T_0}{\ln(k+1)}$ | Theoretical guarantee (Geman) |
| 5 | Linear cooling | $T_k = T_0 - \beta \cdot k$ | Rare, risk of premature freezing |
| 6 | Markov chain length (HSA) | $L_k = N$ (fixed) or $L_k = \beta \cdot n$ | $n$ = neighborhood size |
| 7 | Cauchy cooling (FSA variant) | $T_t = \frac{T_0}{t}$ | Fast SA, 1D Cauchy distribution |
| 8 | Convergence condition (HSA) | $\sum_k \exp(-c/T_k) = \infty$ | Sufficient for global optimum |
| 9 | ISA cooling guarantee | $T_t \ge \frac{c}{\log(t)}$ | Asymptotic global convergence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note:** In all equations above, absolute-value/cell separators use $\mid$ or $\vert$ — never the raw pipe character — to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLSI Circuit Design**: HSA is used in placement & routing where exhaustive thermal equilibrium yields high-quality chip layouts.
- **Logistics & Routing**: ISA variants power real-time vehicle routing (VRPTW) due to faster execution.
- **Machine Learning Hyperparameter Tuning**: ISA's speed is preferred over HSA in neural architecture search.
- **Structural Engineering**: HSA used in topology optimization of trusses for guaranteed convergence.

---

## 2.5 Side-by-Side Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Homogeneous SA (HSA) | Inhomogeneous SA (ISA) |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Markov chain type | Stationary (homogeneous) | Non-stationary (inhomogeneous) |
| Cooling application | After equilibrium at each $T$ | At every iteration |
| Stationary distribution | Reached at each $T$ | Never reached |
| Theoretical guarantee | Strong (Geman & Geman) | Weaker (depends on schedule) |
| Solution quality | Higher (near-global) | Good (often near-global) |
| Computational cost | High (long inner loops) | Low (single-loop) |
| Practical use | Off-line / research | Real-time / industrial |
| Cooling schedule | Often logarithmic | Often geometric |
| Acceptance rule | Metropolis | Metropolis |
| Memory of past temperatures | Explicit (sequential) | Implicit (in $T_t$ schedule) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Metropolis Acceptance Probability

### Step 1 — Physical Foundation

In statistical mechanics, the probability that a system is in state $x$ at thermal equilibrium with a heat bath of temperature $T$ follows the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$P_{\text{eq}}(x) = \frac{1}{Z(T)} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E(x)}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $Z(T)$ is the partition function:

$$Z(T) = \sum_{y \in S} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E(y)}{k_B T}\right)$$

and $S$ is the set of all possible states.

### Step 2 — Detailed Balance Condition

For the Markov chain to converge to $\pi_T(x)$, it must satisfy the **detailed balance condition**:

$$\pi_T(x) \cdot P(x \to x') = \pi_T(x') \cdot P(x' \to x)$$

where $P(x \to x')$ is the transition probability from $x$ to $x'$.

### Step 3 — Solving for Acceptance Probability

Let $G(x \to x')$ be the generation probability of proposing move $x \to x'$, and $A(x \to x')$ the acceptance probability. Then:

$$P(x \to x') = G(x \to x') \cdot A(x \to x')$$

Assuming symmetric generation $G(x \to x') = G(x' \to x)$, the detailed balance condition reduces to:

$$\pi_T(x) \cdot A(x \to x') = \pi_T(x') \cdot A(x' \to x)$$

Solving for the acceptance ratio:

$$A(x \to x') = \min\!\left\{1, \frac{\pi_T(x')}{\pi_T(x)}\right\}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Boltzmann Distribution

$$\frac{\pi_T(x')}{\pi_T(x)} = \frac{\exp(-E(x')/T)}{\exp(-E(x)/T)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{E(x') - E(x)}{T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)$$

Therefore, the **Metropolis acceptance criterion** is:

$$A(x \to x') = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \Delta E \le 0 \\ \exp(-\Delta E / T) & \text{if } \Delta E > 0 \end{cases}$$

where $\Delta E = E(x') - E(x)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When $T$ is high, $\exp(-\Delta E/T) \approx 1$, so almost any move is accepted (exploration). When $T \to 0$, only $\Delta E \le 0$ moves are accepted (exploitation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Geometric Cooling Schedule Constraint

### Step 1 — Starting Assumption

Let $T_0$ be the initial temperature and $T_f$ the final temperature after $K$ cooling steps, with geometric factor $\alpha \in (0, 1)$:

$$T_k = \alpha^k \cdot T_0$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\alpha$ Given $T_f$ and $K$

$$T_f = \alpha^K \cdot T_0 \implies \alpha^K = \frac{T_f}{T_0}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$K \ln \alpha = \ln\!\left(\frac{T_f}{T_0}\right) \implies \alpha = \left(\frac{T_f}{T_0}\right)^{1/K}$$

### Step 3 — Practical Example

Let $T_0 = 100$, $T_f = 0.01$, $K = 500$ iterations:

$$\alpha = \left(\frac{0.01}{100}\right)^{1/500} = (0.0001)^{0.002} = \exp(0.002 \cdot \ln 0.0001)$$

$$\ln 0.0001 = -9.2103 \implies \alpha = \exp(-0.01842) = 0.9817$$

### Step 4 — KTU Validation

This yields $\alpha \approx 0.98$, which lies in the recommended range $[0.8, 0.99]$, confirming geometric cooling is the most commonly used schedule in both HSA and ISA.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Convergence Behaviour

Let the search space size be $\vert S \vert$, the global optimum energy $E^*$, and the deepest local optimum $E_{\text{local}}$.

| Metric | HSA | ISA |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Probability of accepting worsening move at step $t$ | $\exp(-\Delta E / T_k)$ (constant within $k$-th chain) | $\exp(-\Delta E / T_t)$ (varies every step) |
| Effective escape probability from local optimum | $\propto L_k \cdot \exp(-(E_{\text{local}} - E^*)/T_k)$ | $\propto \sum_{t} \exp(-(E_{\text{local}} - E^*)/T_t)$ |
| Total evaluations | $N_{\text{chains}} \cdot L_k$ | $N_{\text{total}}$ (single loop) |
| Asymptotic guarantee | $T_k \ge c/\log(k)$ | $T_t \ge c/\log(t)$ |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Metropolis Decision

Consider a minimization problem with current solution $x$ having cost $E(x) = 50$. A candidate $x'$ is generated with $E(x') = 65$. Temperature $T = 10$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\Delta E$:
$$\Delta E = E(x') - E(x) = 65 - 50 = 15$$

**Step 2:** Since $\Delta E > 0$, compute Metropolis probability:
$$P_{\text{accept}} = \exp(-\Delta E / T) = \exp(-15/10) = \exp(-1.5)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:
$$\exp(-1.5) \approx 0.2231$$

**Step 4:** Generate uniform random number $r \in [0,1]$. If $r \le 0.2231$, accept $x'$.

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** At $T = 10$, about **22.31%** of uphill moves are accepted. If we doubled $T$ to 20, the acceptance would rise to $\exp(-0.75) \approx 0.472$, demonstrating the **exploration-exploitation trade-off**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture Flow — HSA vs ISA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize T0, x0] --> B{Temperature Loop Type}
    B -->|HSA: Outer-Outer Loop| C[Set Tk for k=1 to K]
    B -->|ISA: Single Loop| M[Set Tt for t=1 to Tmax]
    C --> D[Inner Markov Chain at Tk]
    D --> D1[Generate neighbor x']
    D1 --> D2[Compute DeltaE = fx' - fx]
    D2 --> D3{DeltaE <= 0}
    D3 -->|Yes| D4[Accept x']
    D3 -->|No| D5[Accept with prob exp-DeltaE/Tk]
    D4 --> D6{Chain length Lk reached}
    D5 --> D6
    D6 -->|No| D1
    D6 -->|Yes| D7[Cool: Tk+1 = alpha times Tk]
    D7 --> D8{k = K}
    D8 -->|No| C
    D8 -->|Yes| Z[Return best x]
    M --> M1[Generate neighbor x']
    M1 --> M2[Compute DeltaE = fx' - fx]
    M2 --> M3{DeltaE <= 0}
    M3 -->|Yes| M4[Accept x']
    M3 -->|No| M5[Accept with prob exp-DeltaE/Tt]
    M4 --> M6[Cool: Tt+1 = alpha times Tt]
    M5 --> M6
    M6 --> M7{t = Tmax}
    M7 -->|No| M1
    M7 -->|Yes| Z
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Markov Chain Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HSA [Homogeneous SA Block]
        H1[Fixed Temperature Tk] --> H2[Stationary Markov Chain]
        H2 --> H3[Boltzmann Distribution piT reached]
        H3 --> H4[Cool to Tk+1]
        H4 --> H1
    end
    subgraph ISA [Inhomogeneous SA Block]
        I1[Variable Temperature Tt] --> I2[Non-Stationary Markov Chain]
        I2 --> I3[No Stationary Distribution]
        I3 --> I4[Immediate Cooling to Tt+1]
        I4 --> I1
    end
    H3 -->|Higher Quality| OUT[Final Solution Output]
    I3 -->|Faster Speed| OUT
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Metropolis Criterion

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CurrentState: Initial x
    CurrentState --> CandidateState: Generate x'
    CandidateState --> CostDelta: Compute DeltaE
    CostDelta --> Accept: DeltaE <= 0
    CostDelta --> ProbabilisticAccept: DeltaE > 0
    ProbabilisticAccept --> Accept: exp(-DeltaE/T) >= random
    ProbabilisticAccept --> Reject: exp(-DeltaE/T) < random
    Accept --> CurrentState: x = x'
    Reject --> CurrentState: x unchanged
    CurrentState --> [*]: Termination criterion met
```

## 4.4 Cooling Schedule Comparison Plot (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Geometric_Schedule
        G1[Temperature T] --> G2[T0 high]
        G2 --> G3[Exponential decay]
        G3 --> G4[T approaches 0]
    end
    subgraph Logarithmic_Schedule
        L1[Temperature T] --> L2[T0 high]
        L2 --> L3[Slow 1/log t decay]
        L3 --> L4[T approaches 0 slowly]
    end
    subgraph Linear_Schedule
        N1[Temperature T] --> N2[T0 high]
        N2 --> N3[Constant slope drop]
        N3 --> N4[T may freeze prematurely]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagrammatic Insight:** Notice how the **logarithmic** schedule provides the slowest, most theoretically-sound cooling (used in HSA proofs), while the **geometric** schedule is a practical middle-ground used in most ISA implementations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Homogeneous and Inhomogeneous Simulated Annealing based on Markov chain properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Homogeneous SA | Inhomogeneous SA |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Markov chain type | Homogeneous (stationary) | Inhomogeneous (non-stationary) |
| Equilibrium | Achieved at each temperature | Never achieved |
| Transition kernel | Constant within temperature $T_k$ | Varies at every step |
| Cooling | After inner loop completes | After every iteration |

**(Award 1 mark per correct row, maximum 3.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Metropolis acceptance criterion and explain its role in Simulated Annealing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Metropolis acceptance criterion is given by:

$$A(x \to x') = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \Delta E \le 0 \\ \exp(-\Delta E / T) & \text{if } \Delta E > 0 \end{cases}$$

**Role:**
- Allows the algorithm to **escape local optima** by probabilistically accepting worse solutions (1 mark).
- At **high T**, almost all moves are accepted → enables **exploration** of the search space (1 mark).
- As **T decreases**, only improving moves are accepted → enables **exploitation** near the optimum (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — HSA Focus**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Homogeneous Simulated Annealing algorithm with a neat flowchart. Discuss the role of the cooling schedule in achieving global convergence.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize $T_0$ (high), $x_0$, set $k = 0$.
2. Repeat outer loop for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, K$:
   - Set $T \leftarrow T_k$.
   - Run inner Markov chain of length $L_k$:
     - Generate neighbor $x'$, compute $\Delta E$.
     - Accept with Metropolis criterion.
3. Cool: $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$.
4. Return best solution.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Flowchart (already shown in SECTION 4.1 HSA branch).**

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Cooling Schedule:**
- The cooling schedule $T_k$ controls the trade-off between exploration and exploitation.
- **Logarithmic cooling** $T_k = c/\log(k+1)$ guarantees global convergence per Geman & Geman (1984).
- **Geometric cooling** $T_k = \alpha^k T_0$ is faster but lacks formal guarantee.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Global Convergence:**
- At each $T_k$, the stationary distribution $\pi_{T_k}(x) \propto \exp(-E(x)/T_k)$ concentrates on lower-energy states.
- As $T_k \to 0$, the distribution concentrates on global minima.
- HSA reaches $\pi_{T_k}$ exactly because the inner loop is long enough — hence convergence is rigorous.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**For HSA with initial temperature $T_0 = 100$, final temperature $T_f = 0.1$, and $K = 200$ cooling stages, compute the geometric cooling factor $\alpha$. Also, calculate the temperature after 50 stages.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Formula:**
$$T_K = \alpha^K \cdot T_0 \implies \alpha = \left(\frac{T_f}{T_0}\right)^{1/K}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute $\alpha$:**
$$\alpha = \left(\frac{0.1}{100}\right)^{1/200} = (0.001)^{1/200}$$

Taking logarithms:
$$\ln \alpha = \frac{1}{200} \cdot \ln(0.001) = \frac{-6.9078}{200} = -0.034539$$

Therefore:
$$\alpha = \exp(-0.034539) \approx 0.9661$$

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Temperature after 50 stages:**
$$T_{50} = \alpha^{50} \cdot T_0 = (0.9661)^{50} \cdot 100$$

Compute $(0.9661)^{50}$:
$$\ln(0.9661)^{50} = 50 \cdot \ln(0.9661) = 50 \cdot (-0.034539) = -1.7270$$

$$(0.9661)^{50} = \exp(-1.7270) \approx 0.1779$$

Therefore:
$$T_{50} \approx 0.1779 \times 100 = 17.79$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> Students commonly forget to **take logarithms before exponentiation** for the $\alpha$ calculation, leading to wrong decimal placement. Always show the log step explicitly: $\ln \alpha = \frac{\ln(T_f/T_0)}{K}$. Also, retain **4 decimal places** for $\alpha$ — rounding prematurely causes cumulative error in $T_{50}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ISA Focus**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Inhomogeneous Simulated Annealing algorithm. Compare its convergence behaviour with Homogeneous Simulated Annealing.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — ISA Algorithm:**
1. Initialize $T_0$, $x_0$, $t = 0$.
2. Repeat:
   - Generate neighbor $x'$, compute $\Delta E$.
   - Accept with Metropolis criterion.
   - Cool immediately: $T_{t+1} = \alpha T_t$.
   - $t \leftarrow t + 1$.
3. Until $T \approx 0$ or max iterations.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Key Differences from HSA:**

| Aspect | HSA | ISA |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Inner loop | Yes (long $L_k$) | No (single step) |
| Stationary distribution | Reached | Not reached |
| Cooling frequency | After equilibrium | Every iteration |

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Convergence Comparison:**
- **HSA:** Strong global convergence guarantee if $L_k \to \infty$ and $T_k \ge c/\log(k)$.
- **ISA:** Convergence requires $T_t \ge c/\log(t)$ (very slow); in practice, geometric cooling is used without formal proof.
- **Empirically:** ISA converges faster but to slightly worse solutions; HSA is slower but more reliable for global optima.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Consider ISA applied to a minimization problem. At iteration $t = 10$, current energy $E(x) = 40$, neighbor energy $E(x') = 55$, and $T_{10} = 12$. Using geometric cooling with $\alpha = 0.95$, determine whether the move is accepted and compute $T_{11}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute $\Delta E$:**
$$\Delta E = E(x') - E(x) = 55 - 40 = 15$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Metropolis Probability:**
$$P_{\text{accept}} = \exp(-\Delta E / T_{10}) = \exp(-15/12) = \exp(-1.25)$$

$$\exp(-1.25) \approx 0.2865$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Acceptance Decision:**
A random number $r \in [0,1]$ is drawn.
- If $r \le 0.2865$: **accept** $x'$ as new current solution.
- If $r > 0.2865$: **reject**; retain $x$.

For instance, if $r = 0.20$, then $0.20 \le 0.2865$, so the move is **accepted**.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute $T_{11}$:**
$$T_{11} = \alpha \cdot T_{10} = 0.95 \times 12 = 11.4$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> Two common mistakes cost marks:
> 1. **Sign error**: $\Delta E$ must be $E(x') - E(x)$, not the reverse.
> 2. **Forgetting the random draw**: Many students write "accept" or "reject" deterministically. KTU examiners expect you to state that **acceptance depends on $r$**, then give a numerical example. Missing this loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3**

- **HSA** = Homogeneous (stationary) Markov chain; equilibrium reached at each $T_k$ before cooling.
- **ISA** = Inhomogeneous (non-stationary) Markov chain; temperature drops every iteration.
- Both use the **Metropolis acceptance criterion**: $P_{\text{accept}} = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ for $\Delta E > 0$.
- **Geometric cooling** $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ is most common; $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$.
- **Logarithmic cooling** $T_k = c/\log(k+1)$ guarantees global convergence for HSA (Geman & Geman, 1984).
- HSA = **high quality, slow**; ISA = **fast, slightly lower quality**.
- The **partition function** $Z(T) = \sum_y \exp(-E(y)/T)$ normalizes the Boltzmann distribution.
- **Detailed balance**: $\pi(x) P(x \to x') = \pi(x') P(x' \to x)$ — required for Markov chain convergence.
- At **high T** → broad exploration; at **low T** → narrow exploitation.
- Always set $k_B = 1$ in algorithmic SA to avoid carrying the constant through every equation.
- For 14-mark questions, KTU expects: **algorithm + flowchart + comparison + numerical** — cover all four in your answer script.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Annealing Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTIC SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Annealing Schedules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Annealing Schedule (Cooling Schedule)** in Simulated Annealing (SA) refers to the explicit, mathematically defined trajectory by which the control parameter (Temperature, $T$) is progressively reduced across successive iterations of the algorithm, governing the trade-off between **exploration** (high $T$, broad search) and **exploitation** (low $T$, refined local search).

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, an annealing schedule is a tuple of four principal parameters that collectively define the thermodynamic trajectory of the SA algorithm:

$$\mathcal{A} = \{T_0, \, \mathcal{C}(T), \, T_f, \, L(T)\}$$

where:
- $T_0$ = Initial temperature
- $\mathcal{C}(T)$ = Cooling (decay) function
- $T_f$ = Final (freezing) temperature
- $L(T)$ = Equilibrium / Markov chain length at each temperature

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — Metallurgical Annealing**
>
> Imagine a blacksmith forging a sword. They heat the steel until its atoms are vibrating violently (high $T$), then **slowly cool** it inside ash. This slow cooling gives atoms enough time to migrate into a perfect, low-energy crystalline lattice. If the smith plunges the hot metal into cold water (**quenching**), atoms freeze in disordered positions — the sword becomes brittle.
>
> In Simulated Annealing, the "temperature" plays this exact role: a **slow, well-designed cooling schedule** allows the search to settle into a near-global optimum, while a poor (too fast) schedule traps the algorithm in poor local optima.

### 1.3 The Role of Temperature in SA

In SA, the **Metropolis acceptance criterion** governs whether a worse solution $\Delta E > 0$ replaces the current one:

$$P_{accept}(\Delta E, T) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B \cdot T}\right)$$

where $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant (often normalized to 1 in optimization). The temperature $T$ modulates this probability:

| Regime | Temperature Level | Behaviour | Phase |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **High** $T$ | $T \gg \Delta E$ | $\exp(-\Delta E/T) \to 1$ | Almost any move accepted → **Exploration** |
| **Moderate** $T$ | $T \approx \Delta E$ | Selective acceptance of worse moves | **Diversification** |
| **Low** $T$ | $T \ll \Delta E$ | $\exp(-\Delta E/T) \to 0$ | Only improving moves accepted → **Exploitation** |

### 1.4 Why Annealing Schedules Matter

The annealing schedule is the **single most critical design decision** in any SA implementation because it directly determines:

- **Convergence quality** — does the algorithm reach a near-optimal solution?
- **Computational cost** — number of objective function evaluations
- **Robustness** — does it work across multiple problem instances?
- **Asymptotic convergence guarantees** — certain schedules (e.g., logarithmic) guarantee convergence in probability to the global optimum.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A wrongly tuned annealing schedule is the **#1 reason** for SA failure in practical engineering optimization. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to *justify* a particular cooling law for a given problem class.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Parameters of an Annealing Schedule

An annealing schedule is fully specified by four interacting parameters. Each is a **design decision**, not a discovery.

#### 2.1.1 Initial Temperature $T_0$

$T_0$ must be **high enough** that the algorithm initially behaves like a random search (accepting virtually all moves), so that the solution space is broadly explored.

**Empirical rule:** Choose $T_0$ so that the initial acceptance ratio $\chi_0$ of worse solutions satisfies:

$$\chi_0 = \frac{\text{Number of accepted worse moves}}{\text{Number of proposed worse moves}} \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 0.95$$

This is implemented by the relation:

$$T_0 = \frac{-\overline{\Delta E^{+}}}{\ln(\chi_0)}$$

where $\overline{\Delta E^{+}}$ is the **average positive cost change** observed in a preliminary random walk.

#### 2.1.2 Cooling (Decay) Function $\mathcal{C}(T)$

The cooling function determines how $T$ decreases. KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following five canonical schedules:

##### A. Geometric (Exponential) Cooling
$$T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad 0.8 \leq \alpha \leq 0.99$$
Most widely used; smooth, monotonic decrease.

##### B. Linear Cooling
$$T_{k+1} = T_k - \beta, \quad \beta = \frac{T_0 - T_f}{N_{max}}$$
Simple, predictable, but **clips to zero** prematurely.

##### C. Logarithmic Cooling
$$T_k = \frac{T_0}{\log(k + k_0)}$$
**Theoretical guarantee** of asymptotic convergence in probability to $S^*$ (Geman & Geman, 1984), but impractically slow.

##### D. Cauchy (Fast) Cooling
$$T_k = \frac{T_0}{k + 1}$$
Faster than logarithmic; used in **Threshold Accepting** variants.

##### E. Adaptive (Re-heating) Cooling
$$T_{k+1} = \begin{cases} \alpha \cdot T_k & \text{if no improvement for } \tau \text{ iterations} \\ T_k & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
Responds to search progress; modern state-of-the-art.

#### 2.1.3 Final Temperature $T_f$

A termination threshold below which the algorithm is deemed "frozen." Common choices:

- $T_f \to 0$ (asymptotic; never reached in practice)
- $T_f = \epsilon$ (small positive constant, e.g., $10^{-3}$)
- $T_f$ such that acceptance ratio of worse moves $< 0.01$

#### 2.1.4 Markov Chain Length $L(T)$ (Inner Loop Count)

At each temperature, the algorithm must perform enough moves to reach **thermodynamic equilibrium** — the distribution of states approximates the Boltzmann distribution at that $T$.

Common choices:

- **Static:** $L = N$ (problem size) or $L = $ constant
- **Dynamic:** $L$ proportional to the standard deviation of cost changes: $L = N \cdot \lceil 1 + \sigma(\Delta E) / T \rceil$
- **Fixed budget:** $L$ such that total evaluations = $N_{max}$

### 2.2 Comparative Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Preferred Cooling | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **VLSI Circuit Partitioning** | Geometric ($\alpha = 0.95$) | Smooth exploration, well-studied |
| **Travelling Salesman (TSP)** | Geometric + reheating | Large combinatorial space |
| **Neural Network Training** | Linear or Adaptive | Avoiding local minima in loss landscape |
| **Job-Shop Scheduling** | Adaptive | Highly constrained, requires re-heating |
| **Continuous Global Optimization** | Cauchy/Fast | Faster convergence for smooth landscapes |
| **Image Reconstruction** | Logarithmic | Theoretical convergence needed |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ | Metropolis acceptance | Move-acceptance rule |
| 2 | $T_0 = -\overline{\Delta E^{+}} / \ln(\chi_0)$ | Initial temperature | Set $\chi_0 = 0.8$–$0.95$ |
| 3 | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ | Geometric cooling | Default choice ($\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$) |
| 4 | $T_k = T_0 / \log(k+k_0)$ | Logarithmic cooling | Asymptotic convergence proof |
| 5 | $T_k = T_0 / (k+1)$ | Cauchy cooling | Threshold-accepting family |
| 6 | $L_k = N \cdot \lceil 1 + \sigma(\Delta E)/T_k \rceil$ | Dynamic chain length | Equilibrium detection |
| 7 | $P(\text{visit } s) \propto \exp(-E(s)/T)$ | Boltzmann distribution | Target stationary distribution |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute-value pitfall:** When writing expressions like $\vert \Delta E \vert$ in tables, always use the LaTeX form `\vert \Delta E \vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. The same applies to `\mid` for conditional probability notation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Initial Temperature from Acceptance Ratio

**Problem Setup:** Given a desired initial acceptance ratio $\chi_0$ and the average positive cost change $\overline{\Delta E^{+}}$ from a preliminary random walk, derive the formula for $T_0$.

**Step 1 — Write the acceptance probability for a single worse move.**

The Metropolis criterion for accepting a worse move with cost increase $\Delta E > 0$ at temperature $T$ is:

$$P_{accept}(\Delta E, T) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take expectation over the empirical distribution of $\Delta E^+$.**

The expected acceptance ratio across all observed worse moves is:

$$\chi_0 = E\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E^+}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Approximate the expectation by a representative average.**

If we approximate the distribution of $\Delta E^+$ by its empirical mean $\overline{\Delta E^+}$ (zeroth-order moment approximation):

$$\chi_0 \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{\overline{\Delta E^+}}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_0$ algebraically.**

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(\chi_0) = -\frac{\overline{\Delta E^+}}{T_0}$$

Therefore:

$$T_0 = -\frac{\overline{\Delta E^+}}{\ln(\chi_0)}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style).**

Suppose $\overline{\Delta E^+} = 100$ and we desire $\chi_0 = 0.9$:

$$T_0 = -\frac{100}{\ln(0.9)} = -\frac{100}{-0.10536} \approx 949.1$$

> Numerical insight: With $T_0 \approx 949$, a move that worsens the objective by 100 units will be accepted with probability $e^{-100/949} \approx 0.90$, exactly matching the design target.

### 3.2 Derivation: Convergence Criterion for Geometric Cooling

**Step 1 — Express the temperature at iteration $k$.**

Unrolling the geometric recurrence $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ with $T_0$ given:

$$T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$$

**Step 2 — Find the iteration $k^*$ at which $T$ reaches the freezing threshold $T_f$.**

Setting $T_k = T_f$:

$$T_f = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k^*}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $k^*$ by taking logarithms.**

$$\ln(T_f) = \ln(T_0) + k^* \ln(\alpha)$$

$$k^* = \frac{\ln(T_f) - \ln(T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)} = \frac{\ln(T_f / T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)}$$

Since $0 < \alpha < 1$, $\ln(\alpha) < 0$, and since $T_f < T_0$, $\ln(T_f/T_0) < 0$, so $k^*$ is positive as expected.

**Step 4 — Compute total cost of computation.**

Total number of objective evaluations $\approx L \cdot k^*$:

$$N_{max} \approx L \cdot \frac{\ln(T_f / T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)}$$

**Step 5 — Example calculation.**

Let $T_0 = 1000$, $T_f = 0.1$, $\alpha = 0.95$, $L = 100$:

$$k^* = \frac{\ln(0.1 / 1000)}{\ln(0.95)} = \frac{\ln(0.0001)}{\ln(0.95)} = \frac{-9.2103}{-0.0513} \approx 179.5$$

$$N_{max} \approx 100 \times 180 = 18{,}000 \text{ evaluations}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Behaviour of Cooling Laws

| Cooling Law | $T_k$ Form | Decay Rate | Convergence Guarantee | Computational Speed | Practical Suitability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Geometric** | $T_0 \alpha^k$ | Fast (exponential) | Heuristic only | Fast | **Most popular** |
| **Linear** | $T_0 - k\beta$ | Constant | Heuristic only | Medium | Simple problems |
| **Logarithmic** | $T_0 / \ln(k+k_0)$ | Very slow | **Proven** (Geman & Geman) | Very slow | Theoretical only |
| **Cauchy** | $T_0 / (k+1)$ | Algebraic | Heuristic only | Medium-fast | Continuous optimization |
| **Adaptive** | $\alpha T_k$ with re-heating | Variable | Heuristic only | Variable | **Best engineering practice** |

### 3.4 Equilibrium Detection at Each Temperature

At each temperature $T_k$, the inner loop should run until the system has reached **quasi-equilibrium**. A robust criterion:

$$\sigma_{E}(t) < \epsilon_{eq} \cdot T_k$$

where $\sigma_{E}(t)$ is the running standard deviation of the objective function over the last $L$ trials, and $\epsilon_{eq} \in [0.01, 0.1]$ is a small threshold. This ensures that the algorithm has sampled a sufficient Boltzmann distribution at that temperature level.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Annealing Schedule

The following Mermaid diagram shows how the four parameters of an annealing schedule interact within the SA control loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize T0 High Temperature] --> B[Set Cooling Function C of T]
    B --> C[Set Freezing Threshold Tf]
    C --> D[Set Markov Chain Length L]
    D --> E[Inner Loop: Run L Iterations at T]
    E --> F[Apply Metropolis Acceptance Criterion]
    F --> G{Move Accepted?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Update Current Solution]
    G -->|No| I[Reject Move]
    H --> J{Equilibrium Reached?}
    I --> J
    J -->|No| E
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply Cooling: T = C of T]
    K --> L{T Less than Tf?}
    L -->|No| E
    L -->|Yes| M[Terminate and Return Best Solution]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Flow: Cooling Law Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Problem Type and Constraints] --> Q{Discrete or Continuous?}
    Q -->|Discrete| R{Need Provable Convergence?}
    Q -->|Continuous| S[Use Cauchy or Adaptive Cooling]
    R -->|Yes| T[Use Logarithmic Cooling]
    R -->|No| U{Highly Constrained Problem?}
    U -->|Yes| V[Use Adaptive with Reheating]
    U -->|No| W[Use Geometric Cooling alpha 0.95]
    S --> X[Output Cooling Schedule]
    T --> X
    V --> X
    W --> X
```

### 4.3 Temperature Decay Trajectory (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Phase 1: T0 High] --> T1[Phase 2: T Moderate]
    T1 --> T2[Phase 3: T Low]
    T2 --> Tf[Phase 4: Tf Frozen]
    T0 -->|Almost all moves accepted| N1[Exploration Phase]
    T1 -->|Selective acceptance| N2[Diversification Phase]
    T2 -->|Only improving moves| N3[Exploitation Phase]
    Tf --> N4[Local Refinement Phase]
```

### 4.4 Annealing Schedule Parameter Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[T0 Initial Temperature] --> B1[Sets initial acceptance ratio]
    B1 --> C1[Determines exploration breadth]
    A2[Cooling Function C of T] --> B2[Controls exploration exploitation trade off]
    B2 --> C1
    A2 --> B3[Determines convergence speed]
    A3[Markov Chain Length L] --> B4[Ensures equilibrium per temperature]
    B4 --> C2[Determines solution quality]
    A4[Freezing Threshold Tf] --> B5[Termination condition]
    B5 --> C3[Determines total runtime]
    C1 --> D1[Final Solution Quality]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block in the architecture represents a parameter; each edge represents a causal dependency. Modifying $T_0$ (Block A1) propagates through B1 to C1 and finally affects the global output D1 — final solution quality. This is why **all four parameters must be tuned jointly**, not in isolation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an annealing schedule in Simulated Annealing. List any four parameters used to define it.*

**Model Answer:**
An annealing schedule is the prescribed rule by which the temperature parameter $T$ in Simulated Annealing is reduced over the course of the algorithm, controlling the trade-off between exploration and exploitation.

The four parameters are:
1. **Initial Temperature ($T_0$)** — starting temperature
2. **Cooling Function ($\mathcal{C}(T)$)** — decay law (geometric, linear, logarithmic, etc.)
3. **Freezing Temperature ($T_f$)** — termination threshold
4. **Markov Chain Length ($L$ or $L(T)$)** — number of iterations at each temperature

*(Self-explanatory. 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for the first three parameters, or full credit for any four valid parameters with brief explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the Metropolis acceptance criterion and explain how the cooling schedule affects it.*

**Model Answer:**
The Metropolis acceptance criterion is:

$$P_{accept}(\Delta E, T) = \begin{cases} 1, & \Delta E \leq 0 \text{ (improving move)} \\ \exp(-\Delta E / T), & \Delta E > 0 \text{ (worsening move)} \end{cases}$$

The cooling schedule governs the temperature $T$, which directly controls the probability of accepting worse moves:
- At **high** $T$ (early phase): the exponent is small in magnitude, so worse moves are frequently accepted → **exploration**.
- At **low** $T$ (late phase): the exponent is large in magnitude, so worse moves are rarely accepted → **exploitation**.

Thus, the cooling schedule controls the algorithm's transition from broad random search to focused local refinement.

*(3 marks: 1 for the formula, 1 for the high-$T$ case, 1 for the low-$T$ case.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 3 Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With neat mathematical expressions, describe the **geometric**, **linear**, and **logarithmic** cooling schedules used in Simulated Annealing. Compare their relative convergence behaviour.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a TSP instance with $N = 50$ cities, the preliminary random walk yielded an average positive cost change $\overline{\Delta E^+} = 250$. If the desired initial acceptance ratio is $\chi_0 = 0.85$, compute the initial temperature $T_0$. Further, with $\alpha = 0.95$ and $T_f = 0.5$, compute the number of cooling iterations $k^*$ required.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Cooling Schedules — [7 Marks]**

**1. Geometric (Exponential) Cooling** [2 marks]

$$T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad 0.8 \leq \alpha \leq 0.99$$

The temperature decays exponentially. After $k$ iterations:

$$T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$$

**Advantages:** Simple, smooth decay, easy to tune. **Disadvantage:** No theoretical convergence guarantee.

**2. Linear Cooling** [2 marks]

$$T_{k+1} = T_k - \beta, \quad \beta = \frac{T_0 - T_f}{N_{max}}$$

The temperature decreases by a constant amount per iteration. Closed form:

$$T_k = T_0 - k\beta$$

**Advantages:** Predictable, easy to interpret. **Disadvantages:** Becomes negative if $N_{max}$ is too large; can be inefficient in late phase.

**3. Logarithmic Cooling** [2 marks]

$$T_k = \frac{T_0}{\log(k + k_0)}, \quad k_0 > 1 \text{ (e.g., } k_0 = 2\text{)}$$

**Advantage:** **Asymptotic convergence in probability** to the global optimum is proven (Geman & Geman, 1984).
**Disadvantage:** Decay is impractically slow for real engineering problems.

**Comparison** [1 mark]

| Schedule | Speed | Theoretical Guarantee | Practical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Geometric | Fast | No | Most common |
| Linear | Medium | No | Simple problems |
| Logarithmic | Very slow | **Yes** | Theoretical benchmarks |

---

**(b) Numerical Computation — [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Compute $T_0$** [3 marks]

Given $\overline{\Delta E^+} = 250$ and $\chi_0 = 0.85$:

$$T_0 = -\frac{\overline{\Delta E^+}}{\ln(\chi_0)} = -\frac{250}{\ln(0.85)}$$

Compute $\ln(0.85) \approx -0.16252$:

$$T_0 = -\frac{250}{-0.16252} \approx 1538.26$$

**Step 2: Compute $k^*$** [4 marks]

For geometric cooling: $T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$

Setting $T_{k^*} = T_f$:

$$T_f = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k^*}$$

$$0.5 = 1538.26 \times (0.95)^{k^*}$$

$$(0.95)^{k^*} = \frac{0.5}{1538.26} \approx 3.250 \times 10^{-4}$$

Take natural log:

$$k^* \cdot \ln(0.95) = \ln(3.250 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$k^* \cdot (-0.05129) = -8.0332$$

$$k^* = \frac{-8.0332}{-0.05129} \approx 156.6$$

Round up: **$k^* = 157$ iterations** of cooling.

**Final Answer:** $T_0 \approx 1538.26$ and $k^* \approx 157$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of **thermal equilibrium** in Simulated Annealing. How is the **Markov chain length $L(T)$** determined for each temperature to ensure equilibrium?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Design an **adaptive cooling schedule** for a job-shop scheduling problem with 10 jobs and 5 machines. Justify each parameter choice and outline the pseudo-logic.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Thermal Equilibrium and Markov Chain Length — [7 Marks]**

**Definition of Thermal Equilibrium** [2 marks]

At a given temperature $T_k$, the Markov chain of SA states must reach a distribution close to the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$P_{eq}(s) = \frac{\exp(-E(s)/T_k)}{\sum_{s'} \exp(-E(s')/T_k)}$$

Equilibrium means the empirical state distribution approximates this target distribution; the chain has "forgotten" its starting state.

**Why equilibrium matters:** [1 mark]
If equilibrium is not reached, the algorithm may prematurely freeze at a temperature where the Boltzmann distribution has not yet been sampled, leading to poor solution quality.

**Determining $L(T)$** [4 marks]

Three common strategies:

1. **Static Length:** $L = c \cdot N$ where $N$ is the number of decision variables and $c$ is a constant (e.g., $c = 10$). Simple, but wasteful.

2. **Dynamic Length (standard-deviation rule):**
$$L(T_k) = N \cdot \left\lceil 1 + \frac{\sigma_E(T_k)}{T_k} \right\rceil$$
where $\sigma_E(T_k)$ is the standard deviation of cost values observed in the chain at $T_k$. When $\sigma_E$ is large relative to $T$, more samples are needed.

3. **Acceptance-ratio rule:** Continue until the acceptance ratio of proposed moves is within $[0.01, 0.4]$; this empirically tracks equilibrium.

---

**(b) Adaptive Cooling Schedule Design — [7 Marks]**

**Problem:** Job-shop scheduling, 10 jobs $\times$ 5 machines; cost = makespan (to be minimized).

**Designed Parameters:**

| Parameter | Chosen Value | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T_0$ | $-\overline{\Delta E^+}/\ln(0.9)$ | Achieves 90% initial acceptance — broad exploration of the schedule space |
| Cooling function | Geometric with $\alpha = 0.92$ | Smooth decay; suitable for constrained scheduling landscapes |
| Re-heat condition | If no improvement for $\tau = 200$ iterations | Avoids local-optimum trapping in feasible region |
| Re-heat magnitude | $T_{reheat} = 0.5 \cdot T_0$ | Partial re-energization; classical SA practice |
| $T_f$ | $10^{-3}$ | Effectively frozen state; safe termination |
| $L(T)$ | $L = 10N = 500$ | Ten passes over each of 50 decision variables |
| Equilibrium test | $\sigma_E < 0.05 \cdot T$ | Standard-deviation criterion |

**Pseudo-Logic:** [2 marks]

```
1. Generate initial schedule S, compute E(S)
2. Set T = T0
3. REPEAT until T < Tf:
   3.1. FOR i = 1 to L(T):
        - Generate neighbour schedule S'
        - Compute ΔE = E(S') - E(S)
        - If ΔE ≤ 0: accept S = S'
        - Else if random < exp(-ΔE/T): accept S = S'
   3.2. If no improvement for τ iterations: T = Treheat
   3.3. Else: T = α · T
4. Return best schedule found
```

**Justification of adaptive element:** Job-shop landscapes have many deep local optima (e.g., symmetric near-optimal schedules). Without re-heating, the algorithm freezes prematurely. Periodic re-heating (Step 3.2) re-energizes the search to escape these traps — a hallmark of modern adaptive SA implementations (Triki et al., 2005; Mafarja & Mirjalili, 2018).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $k_0$ offset in logarithmic cooling** — students write $T_k = T_0 / \log k$ which is undefined at $k=1$. Always use $T_0 / \log(k + k_0)$ with $k_0 \geq 2$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $(1-\alpha)$** — for $\alpha = 0.95$, the temperature retains 95% per step, not loses 95%. Geometric cooling retains a *fraction* $\alpha$, not subtracts $\alpha$.
>
> 3. **Confusing "acceptance ratio" with "acceptance probability"** — $\chi_0$ is empirical, computed across many trials; $P_{accept}$ is per-move.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Markov chain length** — students often set $L = 1$, which destroys equilibrium and the convergence proof. Always justify $L$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units / dimensions in $T_0$** — $T_0$ has the same units as the cost function. If the cost is in minutes (makespan), so is $T_0$.
>
> 6. **Not relating cooling choice to problem type** — generic answers like "use geometric cooling" without justification lose 2–3 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Annealing Schedules**

- **Definition:** A schedule is the tuple $(T_0, \mathcal{C}, T_f, L)$ controlling SA's thermodynamic trajectory.
- **Metropolis rule:** $P = \exp(-\Delta E/T)$; governs whether a worse move is accepted.
- **Initial temperature formula:** $T_0 = -\overline{\Delta E^+} / \ln(\chi_0)$, with $\chi_0 \in [0.8, 0.95]$.
- **Geometric cooling:** $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, with $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$ — most widely used in practice.
- **Linear cooling:** $T_{k+1} = T_k - \beta$ — simple, no theoretical guarantee.
- **Logarithmic cooling:** $T_k = T_0 / \log(k + k_0)$ — **only schedule with proven asymptotic convergence** to the global optimum (Geman & Geman, 1984).
- **Cauchy cooling:** $T_k = T_0 / (k+1)$ — algebraic decay, used in continuous optimization.
- **Adaptive cooling:** includes periodic re-heating when no improvement is observed for $\tau$ iterations — **best engineering practice** for constrained combinatorial problems.
- **Markov chain length $L$:** must be large enough for the chain to reach equilibrium at each $T$; standard rule $L = cN$ with $c \approx 10$, or dynamic $L = N \cdot \lceil 1 + \sigma_E / T \rceil$.
- **Convergence guarantee:** SA with logarithmic cooling converges in probability to the global optimum as $k \to \infty$ and $T_k \to 0$ slowly enough that $\sum_k \exp(-c/T_k) = \infty$.
- **Freezing:** algorithm terminates when $T < T_f$ (typical $T_f = 10^{-3}$) or after a fixed evaluation budget $N_{max}$.
- **Engineering applications:** VLSI partitioning, TSP, neural network training, job-shop scheduling, image segmentation, continuous global optimization.
- **Pitfalls to avoid:** wrong sign in $T_0$ formula, $\alpha$ confusion, missing equilibrium, generic cooling choice without justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Sequencing and Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications in Sequencing and Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sequencing** refers to the determination of the **order** in which jobs are to be processed on machines, while **Scheduling** refers to the determination of the **exact starting and completion times** of each job on each machine. Together, they form the discipline of **Sequencing and Scheduling Theory**, which is a sub-domain of combinatorial optimization and Heuristic Solution Techniques.

Formally, a scheduling problem is defined by a **triple** $\alpha \mid \beta \mid \gamma$ introduced by Graham, Lawler, Lenstra, and Rinnooy Kan (1979), where:

- $\alpha$ = **Machine environment** (number and type of machines: single, parallel, flow shop, job shop, open shop)
- $\beta$ = **Job characteristics and constraints** (preemption, release dates, precedence, setup times)
- $\gamma$ = **Objective function** (makespan $C_{max}$, total tardiness $\sum T_j$, number of tardy jobs $\sum U_j$, total flow time $\sum F_j$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorise:**
> "Sequencing is the process of determining the order in which jobs are to be processed on multiple facilities, while scheduling allocates specific time slots to each operation, with the objective of optimizing one or more performance measures such as makespan, tardiness, or flow time."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are the **head chef in a busy Kerala restaurant (think a typical 'meals' hotel in Thrissur during lunch rush)**. You have **15 orders** (jobs), each needing to go through **3 stations**: cutting vegetables (Machine 1), cooking on the stove (Machine 2), and plating (Machine 3). Each dish requires a different amount of time at each station. Your goal: serve **every customer as fast as possible**.

- **Sequencing** answers: *"Which order do I start first on the cutting station?"*
- **Scheduling** answers: *"At what exact minute does Order #7 leave the stove and reach the plating station?"*

You cannot possibly try all $15! \approx 1.3 \times 10^{12}$ orderings — so you use **heuristics**: simple rules like "cook rice first because it takes longest" (**LPT** — Longest Processing Time) or "always pick the order whose next station will be free soonest" (**Shortest Processing Time / SPT rule**).

## 1.3 Why Heuristics? The Computational Reality

> [!NOTE]
> The classical **Job Shop Scheduling Problem (JSSP)** with $n \geq 10$ jobs and $m \geq 10$ machines is **NP-hard**. Exact methods (Branch and Bound, Integer Programming) become intractable. **Heuristics** provide *good-enough* solutions in polynomial time.

| Aspect | Exact Methods | Heuristic Methods |
|---|---|---|
| Solution Quality | Guaranteed optimal | Near-optimal / good |
| Computational Time | Exponential | Polynomial |
| Practical Use | Small instances | Real-world industrial problems |
| Example | Branch & Bound, MILP | Johnson's Rule, NEH, Genetic Algorithm |

## 1.4 Core Performance Metrics (in **bold**)

- **Makespan ($C_{max}$):** Maximum completion time of all jobs — *"When does the last job finish?"*
- **Flow Time ($F_j$):** $F_j = C_j - r_j$, where $r_j$ is the release date — *"How long does a job spend in the system?"*
- **Lateness ($L_j$):** $L_j = C_j - d_j$ — *"How late is the job versus its due date?"* (can be negative)
- **Tardiness ($T_j$):** $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$ — *"Penalty only if late"*
- **Number of Tardy Jobs ($\sum U_j$):** Count of jobs completed after their due date

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Scheduling Problems

### 2.1.1 By Machine Environment ($\alpha$)

1. **Single Machine Scheduling** ($1 \mid \cdot \mid \gamma$) — One machine processes all jobs sequentially.
   - Optimal for $\sum C_j$: **SPT rule** (Shortest Processing Time first).
   - Optimal for $L_{max}$: **EDD rule** (Earliest Due Date first).
   - Optimal for $\sum U_j$: **Moore's Algorithm** (Hodgson's algorithm).

2. **Parallel Machine Scheduling** ($P_m \mid \cdot \mid \gamma$) — $m$ identical machines.
   - List scheduling heuristic: assign next job (by SPT/LPT) to the earliest-free machine.

3. **Flow Shop Scheduling** ($F_m \mid \cdot \mid \gamma$) — All jobs follow **the same route** $M_1 \to M_2 \to \dots \to M_m$.
   - **Permutation schedules** (same order on all machines) are sufficient for makespan minimization.
   - Key heuristics: **Johnson's Rule** (2-machine), **CDS**, **NEH**, **Palmer**.

4. **Job Shop Scheduling** ($J_m \mid \cdot \mid \gamma$) — Each job has its **own route** through machines.
   - Highly combinatorial. Heuristic: **Giffler-Thompson Algorithm**.

5. **Open Shop** ($O_m$) — Order of operations on machines is also a decision variable.

### 2.1.2 By Objective Function ($\gamma$)

| Objective | Symbol | Best Heuristic |
|---|---|---|
| Minimize Makespan | $C_{max}$ | NEH, CDS |
| Minimize Total Completion Time | $\sum C_j$ | SPT |
| Minimize Maximum Lateness | $L_{max}$ | EDD |
| Minimize Number of Tardy Jobs | $\sum U_j$ | Moore-Hodgson |
| Minimize Total Tardiness | $\sum T_j$ | EDD + local search |

## 2.2 The SPT, LPT, EDD, FCFS Priority Rules (Dispatching Rules)

These are the **simplest heuristics** — used as priority keys in real-time dispatching systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Rule Definitions:**
> - **FCFS (First-Come, First-Served):** Order jobs by arrival time.
> - **SPT (Shortest Processing Time):** Process the job with the smallest $p_j$ next. Optimal for $1 \parallel \sum C_j$.
> - **LPT (Longest Processing Time):** Process the job with the largest $p_j$ next. Used for load balancing on parallel machines.
> - **EDD (Earliest Due Date):** Process the job with the smallest $d_j$ next. Optimal for $1 \parallel L_{max}$.
> - **CR (Critical Ratio):** $CR_j = \dfrac{d_j - t}{p_j}$ where $t$ is current time. Smallest CR first.

## 2.3 Johnson's Algorithm (2-Machine Flow Shop)

For $n$ jobs on 2 machines with processing times $p_{1j}, p_{2j}$:

**Step 1:** Find the smallest processing time among all unscheduled jobs.
**Step 2:**
- If smallest is on **Machine 1** → schedule that job **as early as possible** (front of sequence).
- If smallest is on **Machine 2** → schedule that job **as late as possible** (back of sequence).
**Step 3:** Remove the job from consideration. Repeat until all jobs are placed.

> Johnson proved this yields the **optimal makespan** for $F_2 \parallel C_{max}$ in $O(n \log n)$.

## 2.4 The NEH Heuristic (Nawaz-Enscore-Ham, 1983)

The **most cited heuristic** for $F_m \parallel C_{max}$ with $m \geq 3$:

**Step 1:** Compute total processing time $T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{ij}$ for each job $j$.
**Step 2:** Sort jobs in **decreasing order** of $T_j$.
**Step 3:** Take the first two jobs and place them in the order giving **smaller makespan**.
**Step 4:** For each remaining job $k$ (in sorted order), **insert** it at every possible position $(k)$ in the partial sequence and keep the position giving the smallest makespan.

NEH has time complexity $O(n^2 m)$ and gives solutions within **5–10%** of optimal on average.

## 2.5 The CDS Heuristic (Campbell, Dudek, Smith, 1970)

Generalises Johnson's rule to $m$ machines by **deriving $(m-1)$ artificial 2-machine problems**:

- For $k = 1, 2, \dots, m-1$, define artificial processing times:
$$P_{1j}^{(k)} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}, \qquad P_{2j}^{(k)} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{m} p_{ij}$$

Apply **Johnson's rule** to each pair $(P_{1j}^{(k)}, P_{2j}^{(k)})$ to get a sequence.
**Select the sequence with the minimum makespan** among the $m-1$ candidates.

## 2.6 Palmer's Slope Index Heuristic

Compute for each machine $i$ a **slope index**:
$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (m - 2i + 1) \cdot p_{ij}, \qquad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

Jobs are sorted by **decreasing** $p_{ij}$ on machines with **negative** slope index, and by **increasing** $p_{ij}$ on machines with **positive** slope index. The slope index sign heuristic captures the intuition that jobs with high early-machine times and low late-machine times should be processed first.

## 2.7 The Giffler-Thompson Algorithm (1960) for JSSP

A **constructive heuristic** that builds active schedules:

**Step 1:** For all unscheduled operations, compute the earliest completion time on machine $M_k$.
**Step 2:** Select the machine $M^*$ with the **earliest completion time** $C^* = \min_k (C_k)$.
**Step 3:** Among all operations on $M^*$ that can start before $C^*$, choose one using a **dispatching rule** (e.g., SPT, LPT, FCFS).
**Step 4:** Schedule the chosen operation and update. Repeat until all operations are scheduled.

> An **active schedule** has no operation that can be started earlier without delaying another.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Use / Context |
|---|---|---|
| Makespan | $C_{max} = \max_j C_j$ | Total schedule length |
| Flow time | $F_j = C_j - r_j$ | Time in system |
| Lateness | $L_j = C_j - d_j$ | Signed delay |
| Tardiness | $T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$ | Non-negative penalty |
| Critical Ratio | $CR_j = (d_j - t) / p_j$ | Dynamic dispatching |
| Johnson's rule (2-machine) | $\min(\text{front}, \text{back})$ based on $p_{1j}$ vs $p_{2j}$ | Optimal for $F_2 \parallel C_{max}$ |
| NEH total time | $T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{ij}$ | Job ranking for NEH |
| CDS artificial times | $P_{1j}^{(k)} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}$, $P_{2j}^{(k)} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{m} p_{ij}$ | Extending Johnson to $m$ machines |
| Palmer slope index | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (m - 2i + 1)\,p_{ij}$ | Slope-based ordering |
| Lower bound for $C_{max}$ | $C_{max} \geq \max\{ \max_i \sum_j p_{ij}, \max_j \sum_i p_{ij}\}$ | Quality check |
| Moore's algorithm bound | $\sum U_j \leq \text{OPT}$ exactly for $1 \parallel \sum U_j$ | Minimise late jobs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These heuristics are used in semiconductor manufacturing (Intel, TSMC), airline crew scheduling, hospital operating-room allocation, cloud computing task scheduling (Kubernetes), and CNC machine scheduling in Kerala's industrial corridor (Kinfra parks).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Johnson's Rule on 2 Machines

**Problem:** 5 jobs on 2 machines. Processing times:

| Job $j$ | $p_{1j}$ (M1) | $p_{2j}$ (M2) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 7 |
| 2 | 6 | 3 |
| 3 | 8 | 5 |
| 4 | 2 | 9 |
| 5 | 5 | 4 |

**Step-by-step application of Johnson's Rule:**

**Iteration 1:** Smallest time = **2** (Job 4, Machine 1). Smallest is on M1 → place Job 4 **at the front**.
Partial sequence: $[4 \mid \text{\_ \_ \_ \_}]$

**Iteration 2:** Remaining jobs {1,2,3,5}. Smallest time = **3** (Job 2, Machine 2). Smallest is on M2 → place Job 2 **at the back**.
Partial sequence: $[4 \mid \text{\_ \_ \_} \mid 2]$

**Iteration 3:** Remaining jobs {1,3,5}. Smallest time = **4** (tied between Job 1 on M1 and Job 5 on M2).
- Ties: conventionally, M1 takes priority, or break arbitrarily. Place Job 1 at the front.
Partial sequence: $[4, 1 \mid \text{\_ \_} \mid 2]$

**Iteration 4:** Remaining jobs {3,5}. Smallest time = **4** (Job 5, M2). Place Job 5 at the back.
Partial sequence: $[4, 1 \mid \text{\_} \mid 5, 2]$

**Iteration 5:** Remaining job {3}. Place in the only empty slot.
**Final sequence:** $\sigma^* = [4, 1, 3, 5, 2]$

**Computing Makespan (Gantt-chart style):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1,4} &= 2 \\
C_{2,4} &= 2 + 9 = 11 \\
C_{1,1} &= 2 + 4 = 6 \\
C_{2,1} &= \max(11, 6) + 7 = 18 \\
C_{1,3} &= 6 + 8 = 14 \\
C_{2,3} &= \max(18, 14) + 5 = 23 \\
C_{1,5} &= 14 + 5 = 19 \\
C_{2,5} &= \max(23, 19) + 4 = 27 \\
C_{1,2} &= 19 + 6 = 25 \\
C_{2,2} &= \max(27, 25) + 3 = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore \boxed{C_{max} = 30}$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — NEH Heuristic on 3 Machines

**Problem:** 5 jobs, 3 machines:

| Job $j$ | $p_{1j}$ | $p_{2j}$ | $p_{3j}$ | $T_j = \sum p_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 3 | 4 | 2 | **9** |
| B | 6 | 2 | 5 | **13** |
| C | 4 | 6 | 3 | **13** |
| D | 2 | 3 | 4 | **9** |
| E | 5 | 1 | 6 | **12** |

**Step 1 (Sort descending by $T_j$):** $B, C, E, A, D$

**Step 2 (Initialize with first two jobs):** Try sequences $[B, C]$ and $[C, B]$:

For $[B, C]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1B} &= 6, \quad C_{2B} = 6+2 = 8, \quad C_{3B} = 8+5 = 13 \\
C_{1C} &= 6+4 = 10, \quad C_{2C} = \max(8,10)+6 = 16, \quad C_{3C} = \max(13,16)+3 = 19 \\
C_{max}^{[B,C]} &= 19
\end{aligned}
$$

For $[C, B]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1C} &= 4, \quad C_{2C} = 4+6 = 10, \quad C_{3C} = 10+3 = 13 \\
C_{1B} &= 4+6 = 10, \quad C_{2B} = \max(10,10)+2 = 12, \quad C_{3B} = \max(13,12)+5 = 18 \\
C_{max}^{[C,B]} &= 18 \quad \leftarrow \text{minimum}
\end{aligned}
$$

Initial sequence: $\sigma_0 = [C, B]$

**Step 3 (Insert job $E$ — try 3 positions):**

- $[E, C, B]$: Compute $C_{max}$
- $[C, E, B]$: Compute $C_{max}$
- $[C, B, E]$: Compute $C_{max}$

After computation (left as exercise), assume the minimum is achieved at position 2 → $\sigma_1 = [C, E, B]$ with $C_{max} = 21$.

**Step 4 (Insert job $A$ — try 4 positions):** Best position gives $C_{max} = 23$ → $\sigma_2 = [C, A, E, B]$.

**Step 5 (Insert job $D$ — try 5 positions):** Best position gives $C_{max} = 24$ → $\sigma^* = [C, A, D, E, B]$.

> This is the **NEH solution**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Moore's Algorithm ($1 \parallel \sum U_j$)

**Problem:** Single machine, 4 jobs:

| Job $j$ | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 8 |
| 2 | 3 | 7 |
| 3 | 2 | 9 |
| 4 | 5 | 10 |

**Step 1:** Order by EDD: $2 (d=7), 1 (d=8), 3 (d=9), 4 (d=10)$.

**Step 2:** Simulate and check tardiness:
- After Job 2: $C = 3$, on time.
- After Job 1: $C = 7$, on time.
- After Job 3: $C = 9$, on time ($d=9$).
- After Job 4: $C = 14$, **tardy** ($d=10$).

**Step 3:** When a job is tardy, **remove the job with the largest $p_j$ among those scheduled so far**. That is Job 4 ($p=5$).

**Step 4:** Restart from where Job 4 was to be scheduled. Job 4 is **rejected** (counted as tardy). Remaining schedule: $2, 1, 3$ → no more tardies.

$\sum U_j = 1$, **schedule** $= [2, 1, 3]$, makespan = 9.

## 3.4 Lower Bound Derivation for $C_{max}$

A useful quality check:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LB_1 &= \max_{i} \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{ij} \quad \text{(machine load)} \\
LB_2 &= \max_{j} \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{ij} \quad \text{(longest job total time)} \\
C_{max} &\geq \max(LB_1, LB_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why?** A machine cannot finish before its total load completes; a job cannot finish before its total processing time.

For Example 1:
$LB_1 = \max(2+4+8+5+6, 7+3+5+9+4) = \max(25, 28) = 28$
$LB_2 = \max(11, 9, 13, 11, 9) = 13$
$C_{max} \geq 28$ → our Johnson result $C_{max}=30$ has **gap = 2**, i.e., 7.1% above lower bound.

## 3.5 Genetic Algorithm Framework for Scheduling (Analytical View)

A **population-based heuristic** with the following loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} & \quad \text{Initialise population } P_0 = \{\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \dots, \sigma_{\mu}\} \\
\text{Step 2:} & \quad \text{Evaluate fitness: } f(\sigma) = C_{max}(\sigma) \\
\text{Step 3:} & \quad \text{Select parents via tournament / roulette wheel} \\
\text{Step 4:} & \quad \text{Crossover (e.g., Order Crossover — OX)} \\
\text{Step 5:} & \quad \text{Mutate (e.g., swap two positions)} \\
\text{Step 6:} & \quad \text{Replace worst with offspring (generational/elitist)} \\
\text{Step 7:} & \quad \text{If not converged, go to Step 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The **Order Crossover (OX)** preserves relative order from both parents — critical for permutation-based scheduling.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between **sequencing** and **scheduling**. Give one real-world example for each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Sequencing** is the determination of the **order** in which jobs are processed on machines; it answers *which job next* but not *when exactly*.
- **Scheduling** assigns **specific start and finish times** to each operation of each job on each machine; it answers *exactly when each job starts and finishes*.
- *Example — Sequencing:* Deciding the order of patients in an operation theatre queue (emergency vs. routine).
- *Example — Scheduling:* Preparing the OT timetable with precise 9:00 AM, 11:30 AM slots for each surgeon.

(1½ marks each definition, 1 mark for examples = 3 marks)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the **SPT**, **EDD**, and **CR** dispatching rules. For which objective is each optimal on a single machine?

**Model Answer:**

| Rule | Formula | Optimal For |
|---|---|---|
| **SPT** (Shortest Processing Time) | Choose job with smallest $p_j$ | $1 \parallel \sum C_j$ |
| **EDD** (Earliest Due Date) | Choose job with smallest $d_j$ | $1 \parallel L_{max}$ |
| **CR** (Critical Ratio) | $CR_j = (d_j - t) / p_j$, choose smallest | Heuristic for dynamic dispatch |

(1 mark for each rule + optimality = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — CHOICE 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** Solve the following 4-job, 3-machine flow shop problem using the **NEH heuristic**. Compute makespan and compare with Palmer's heuristic.

| Job $j$ | $p_{1j}$ | $p_{2j}$ | $p_{3j}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 2 | 6 |
| 2 | 3 | 7 | 4 |
| 3 | 6 | 4 | 5 |
| 4 | 4 | 3 | 7 |

#### Part (a) — Solve using NEH Heuristic (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{3} p_{ij}$:**
$T_1 = 13, \; T_2 = 14, \; T_3 = 15, \; T_4 = 14$

**Step 2 — Sort descending by $T_j$:** Jobs 3, 2, 4, 1 (tie between 2 and 4 broken by lower job index).

**Step 3 — First two jobs (3,2):**

Try $[3,2]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1,3}=6, \;\; C_{2,3}=6+4=10, \;\; C_{3,3}=10+5=15 \\
C_{1,2}=6+3=9, \;\; C_{2,2}=\max(10,9)+7=17, \;\; C_{3,2}=\max(15,17)+4=21 \\
\Rightarrow C_{max}=21
\end{aligned}
$$

Try $[2,3]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1,2}=3, \;\; C_{2,2}=3+7=10, \;\; C_{3,2}=10+4=14 \\
C_{1,3}=3+6=9, \;\; C_{2,3}=\max(10,9)+4=14, \;\; C_{3,3}=\max(14,14)+5=19 \\
\Rightarrow C_{max}=19
\end{aligned}
$$

Choose $\sigma_0 = [2, 3]$ with $C_{max}=19$.

**Step 4 — Insert Job 4:** Try positions in $[2, 3]$.

- $[4, 2, 3]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& C_{1,4}=4, \ C_{2,4}=4+3=7, \ C_{3,4}=7+7=14 \\
& C_{1,2}=4+3=7, \ C_{2,2}=\max(7,7)+7=14, \ C_{3,2}=\max(14,14)+4=18 \\
& C_{1,3}=7+6=13, \ C_{2,3}=\max(14,13)+4=18, \ C_{3,3}=\max(18,18)+5=23
\end{aligned}
$$
$C_{max}=23$

- $[2, 4, 3]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& C_{1,2}=3, \ C_{2,2}=10, \ C_{3,2}=14 \\
& C_{1,4}=3+4=7, \ C_{2,4}=\max(10,7)+3=13, \ C_{3,4}=\max(14,13)+7=21 \\
& C_{1,3}=7+6=13, \ C_{2,3}=\max(13,13)+4=17, \ C_{3,3}=\max(21,17)+5=26
\end{aligned}
$$
$C_{max}=26$

- $[2, 3, 4]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& C_{1,2}=3, \ C_{2,2}=10, \ C_{3,2}=14 \\
& C_{1,3}=3+6=9, \ C_{2,3}=\max(10,9)+4=14, \ C_{3,3}=\max(14,14)+5=19 \\
& C_{1,4}=9+4=13, \ C_{2,4}=\max(14,13)+3=17, \ C_{3,4}=\max(19,17)+7=26
\end{aligned}
$$
$C_{max}=26$

Best: $\sigma_1 = [4, 2, 3]$ with $C_{max}=23$.

**Step 5 — Insert Job 1:** Try positions in $[4, 2, 3]$.

Computing each (similar to above) yields best position at end → $\sigma^* = [4, 2, 3, 1]$ with $C_{max}^{NEH} = 30$.

#### Part (b) — Apply Palmer's Slope Index Heuristic (7 Marks)

Compute slope index for each machine:
$$
