\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= (3 - 2 \cdot 1 + 1) \cdot \sum_j p_{1j} = (3-1)\cdot 18 = 36 \\
S_2 &= (3 - 4 + 1) \cdot \sum_j p_{2j} = 0 \cdot 16 = 0 \\
S_3 &= (3 - 6 + 1) \cdot \sum_j p_{3j} = (-2) \cdot 22 = -44
\end{aligned}
$$

- Machines with **positive** slope index (here $M_1$): order jobs by **decreasing** $p_{1j}$.
- Machines with **negative** slope index (here $M_3$): order jobs by **increasing** $p_{3j}$.
- Machines with **zero** slope index: any order.

Sort by **decreasing $p_{1j}$:** Job 3 (6), Job 1 (5), Job 4 (4), Job 2 (3).
This is the Palmer sequence: $\sigma_{Palmer} = [3, 1, 4, 2]$.

Compute $C_{max}$ for $[3, 1, 4, 2]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& C_{1,3}=6, \ C_{2,3}=6+4=10, \ C_{3,3}=10+5=15 \\
& C_{1,1}=6+5=11, \ C_{2,1}=\max(10,11)+2=13, \ C_{3,1}=\max(15,13)+6=21 \\
& C_{1,4}=11+4=15, \ C_{2,4}=\max(13,15)+3=18, \ C_{3,4}=\max(21,18)+7=28 \\
& C_{1,2}=15+3=18, \ C_{2,2}=\max(18,18)+7=25, \ C_{3,2}=\max(28,25)+4=32
\end{aligned}
$$

$C_{max}^{Palmer} = 32$.

**Comparison:** NEH = 30 < Palmer = 32. NEH gives better solution. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting to update completion times when a job waits because the previous machine is busy. **Always** use $C_{i,j} = \max(C_{i-1,j}, C_{i,j-1}) + p_{ij}$.
> - **Common mistake 2:** In NEH, students often pick the *first* insertion that improves, instead of trying **all positions** and picking the best.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Tie-breaking errors in Johnson/NEH sort. State the tie-breaking rule explicitly.
> - **Common mistake 4:** In Palmer's heuristic, computing the slope index wrong (sign error). Recheck: $S_i = \sum_j (m - 2i + 1) p_{ij}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — CHOICE 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Solve the following **single machine** scheduling problem with **4 jobs** to **minimise the number of tardy jobs** ($\sum U_j$). Use **Moore's Algorithm**. Due dates: $d_1 = 7, d_2 = 4, d_3 = 9, d_4 = 6$. Processing times: $p_1 = 3, p_2 = 5, p_3 = 2, p_4 = 4$.

#### Part (a) — Apply Moore's Algorithm (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Sort jobs by EDD → $2 (d=4), 4 (d=6), 1 (d=7), 3 (d=9)$.

**Step 2:** Simulate, flag tardy:

- After Job 2: $C=5$. Due 4 → **tardy by 1**.
- When tardy, identify the job with **largest $p_j$** so far (only Job 2, $p=5$) → **remove Job 2**.
- Continue from Job 4 with sequence: $4, 1, 3$.

Re-simulate (jobs remaining: 4, 1, 3 with $p = 4, 3, 2$ and $d = 6, 7, 9$):

- After Job 4: $C=4 \leq 6$ ✓ on time.
- After Job 1: $C=4+3=7 \leq 7$ ✓ on time.
- After Job 3: $C=7+2=9 \leq 9$ ✓ on time.

$\sum U_j = 1$, sequence $= [4, 1, 3]$, total makespan = 9. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — Justify Optimality of Moore's Algorithm and Compare with EDD (7 Marks)

**Moore-Hodgson Theorem:** Moore's algorithm is **provably optimal** for $1 \parallel \sum U_j$.

**Proof Sketch (by exchange argument):**
Suppose an optimal schedule contains two adjacent jobs $A$ then $B$ where $p_A > p_B$ but $d_A > d_B$. Swapping them:
- If both are on time before and after, sum unchanged.
- If both are late, sum of tardiness:
  - Before swap: $(C_A - d_A)^+ + (C_B - d_B)^+$
  - After swap: $(C_A - d_B)^+ + (C_B - d_A)^+$ where $C_A$ decreased by $p_A - p_B$ and $C_B$ increased.
- Formal exchange argument shows the swap does not increase $\sum U_j$.

**Comparison with EDD-only schedule** $[2, 4, 1, 3]$:
$C_2 = 5$ (late), $C_4 = 9$ (late), $C_1 = 12$ (late), $C_3 = 14$ (late) → $\sum U_j = 4$.

Moore's algorithm reduces $\sum U_j$ from 4 to **1** — a 75% improvement. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Sequencing vs Scheduling:** Sequencing = order; Scheduling = exact start/finish times.
- **Graham's notation:** $\alpha \mid \beta \mid \gamma$ — machine env, job characteristics, objective.
- **Performance metrics:** $C_{max}, \sum C_j, \sum F_j, L_{max}, \sum T_j, \sum U_j$ — know all definitions and formulas.
- **SPT rule:** Optimal for $1 \parallel \sum C_j$ (always).
- **EDD rule:** Optimal for $1 \parallel L_{max}$ (always).
- **Moore's algorithm:** Optimal for $1 \parallel \sum U_j$. Process EDD; when tardy, drop the largest $p_j$ job.
- **Johnson's Rule (2-machine):** Optimal for $F_2 \parallel C_{max}$. Smallest $p_{1j}$ → front; smallest $p_{2j}$ → back.
- **NEH Heuristic:** Best-known heuristic for $F_m \parallel C_{max}$, $m \geq 3$. Sort by $T_j$ descending; insert at best position.
- **CDS Heuristic:** Apply Johnson to $(m-1)$ artificial 2-machine pairs; pick best.
- **Palmer's Slope Index:** $S_i = \sum_j (m-2i+1) p_{ij}$. Sort jobs accordingly.
- **Giffler-Thompson:** Constructive heuristic for JSSP that yields active schedules.
- **Critical Ratio (CR):** Dynamic rule using $CR_j = (d_j - t) / p_j$.
- **Lower bound for $C_{max}$:** $C_{max} \geq \max(\text{machine load, longest job})$.
- **Permutation schedules suffice** for $F_m \parallel C_{max}$.
- **JSSP is NP-hard** for $n \geq 10$ — heuristics essential.
- **Order Crossover (OX)** preserves permutation validity in GA scheduling.
- **Always draw Gantt chart** in flow shop problems — KTU examiner expects visual proof.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** For any flow-shop makespan problem, **always** compute the lower bound first. This shows the examiner you understand solution quality — and confirms your heuristic is near-optimal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Travelling Salesman Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Travelling Salesman Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP):** Given a set of **n** cities and the symmetric/non-symmetric distance matrix $D = [d_{ij}]$ denoting the cost of travelling from city $i$ to city $j$, find a **Hamiltonian cycle** (a closed tour that visits every city exactly once and returns to the starting city) of **minimum total cost**.

The Travelling Salesman Problem is a classic **NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem** that serves as the canonical benchmark in heuristic and approximation algorithm research. It belongs to the class of **Hamiltonian cycle problems** on weighted graphs.

### 1.2 Mathematical Statement

Formally, for a graph $G = (V, E)$ with vertex set $V = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ and edge set $E$, where each edge $(i,j)$ has an associated cost $c_{ij}$:

$$\text{Find a permutation } \pi \text{ of } \{1, 2, \ldots, n\} \text{ that minimizes } \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{\pi(i), \pi(i+1)}$$

where $\pi(n+1) \equiv \pi(1)$ to ensure closure of the tour.

### 1.3 Classification of TSP

| Type | Property | Cost Matrix |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Symmetric TSP (STSP)** | $d_{ij} = d_{ji}$ for all $i,j$ | Symmetric |
| **Asymmetric TSP (ATSP)** | $d_{ij} \neq d_{ji}$ generally | Asymmetric |
| **Metric TSP** | Satisfies triangle inequality $d_{ik} \leq d_{ij} + d_{jk}$ | Symmetric |
| **Euclidean TSP** | Cities are points in $\mathbb{R}^k$ | Special metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The module emphasizes **heuristic solution techniques** for TSP. Students must understand both exact methods (for small n) and approximation algorithms (for large n).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **salesman stationed in Thiruvananthapuram** who must visit 5 clients located in **Kollam, Alappuzha, Kottayam, Ernakulam, and Thrissur**, and finally return home. The salesman wants to minimize the **total fuel cost and time** spent on the journey. The naive approach is to try all $5! = 120$ possible orderings. But if he had to visit 50 cities across Kerala, the number of possible tours explodes to a number with **64 digits** — more than the atoms in the observable universe!

This is exactly the dilemma TSP addresses: a problem that is *easy to state* but *virtually impossible to solve exactly* for large instances, making it the perfect playground for **heuristic methods** like Nearest Neighbor, 2-Opt, Genetic Algorithms, and Ant Colony Optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Complexity Insight:** The decision version of TSP is **NP-complete**. Finding the optimal tour is **NP-hard**. This motivates the entire field of heuristics and approximation algorithms covered in this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Integer Linear Programming (ILP) Formulation

The TSP can be formulated as an ILP using binary decision variables. Let:

$$x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if edge } (i,j) \text{ is used in the tour} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Objective Function (Minimize total tour cost):**

$$\min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} c_{ij} \, x_{ij}$$

**Subject to the following constraints:**

**Constraint 1 — Degree Constraints (every city is visited exactly once as origin):**

$$\sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall \, i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$

**Constraint 2 — Degree Constraints (every city is visited exactly once as destination):**

$$\sum_{i=1, i \neq j}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall \, j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$

**Constraint 3 — Subtour Elimination Constraints (DFJ Formulation by Dantzig-Fulkerson-Johnson):**

$$\sum_{i \in S} \sum_{j \in S} x_{ij} \leq |S| - 1 \quad \forall \, S \subset V, \, 2 \leq |S| \leq n-1$$

**Constraint 4 — Binary Restriction:**

$$x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall \, i, j$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Subtour Elimination?** Without Constraint 3, the ILP can yield a solution consisting of multiple disjoint cycles (e.g., a small 3-city loop plus a separate 4-city loop) instead of one Hamiltonian tour. This is a classic pitfall in TSP formulation.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Why TSP is Hard

- **Step 1 — Combinatorial Explosion:** The number of distinct Hamiltonian cycles in a complete graph $K_n$ is $\frac{(n-1)!}{2}$ for symmetric TSP.
- **Step 2 — No Polynomial Algorithm Known:** After decades of research, no algorithm is known that solves TSP exactly in polynomial time.
- **Step 3 — NP-Hardness:** Karp (1972) proved that Hamiltonian cycle problem is NP-complete, and TSP is at least as hard.
- **Step 4 — Practical Implications:** Exact algorithms are limited to $n \leq 60$ approximately, while heuristics can handle $n$ in millions (VLSI design, logistics).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Meaning |
|---------|---------------------|---------|
| Total tours in symmetric TSP | $\frac{(n-1)!}{2}$ | Eliminates direction and starting city duplicates |
| Total tours in asymmetric TSP | $(n-1)!$ | Each permutation gives a unique directed tour |
| Brute-force time complexity | $O(n!)$ | Examine every permutation |
| Dynamic Programming (Held-Karp) | $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ | Exact solution in pseudo-polynomial time |
| Nearest Neighbor heuristic | $O(n^2)$ | Greedy edge-by-edge selection |
| Triangle inequality | $d_{ik} \leq d_{ij} + d_{jk}$ | Guarantees Metric TSP |
| 2-Opt tour improvement | $O(n^2)$ per swap | Removes 2 crossing edges |
| Approximation ratio (MST-based) | $\leq 2 \cdot OPT$ | For Metric TSP |
| Christofides algorithm ratio | $\leq 1.5 \cdot OPT$ | Best known for Metric TSP |
| Cost of optimal tour | $OPT$ | Lower bound for heuristic evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` when writing absolute values or conditional expressions inside markdown tables to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **VLSI Chip Design:** Minimizing wire length for connecting millions of components on a circuit board.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Amazon, FedEx, UPS route optimization for last-mile delivery.
- **Genomics:** Reconstructing DNA sequences from fragment orderings.
- **Astronomy:** Scheduling telescope observation sequences to minimize slewing time.
- **Robotics & Manufacturing:** PCB drilling, printed circuit board assembly, warehouse robots.
- **Network Routing:** Designing efficient packet-switching routes in communication networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Counting the Number of Distinct TSP Tours

**Claim:** The number of distinct Hamiltonian cycles in a complete symmetric graph $K_n$ is $\frac{(n-1)!}{2}$.

**Step 1 — Total permutations of n cities:** Any tour is a permutation of all $n$ cities, giving $n!$ orderings.

**Step 2 — Eliminate starting city duplicates:** Since the tour is cyclic (returns to start), rotating the same cycle produces the same tour. There are $n$ rotations, so divide by $n$:

$$\text{Tours after rotation elimination} = \frac{n!}{n} = (n-1)!$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate direction duplicates:** A tour can be traversed in two directions (clockwise or counter-clockwise). Both are the *same* undirected cycle in symmetric TSP, so divide by $2$:

$$\text{Distinct tours} = \frac{(n-1)!}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration:** For $n = 10$ cities:

$$\text{Distinct tours} = \frac{9!}{2} = \frac{362880}{2} = 181440$$

For $n = 20$:

$$\text{Distinct tours} = \frac{19!}{2} \approx 6.08 \times 10^{16}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even for a modest 20 cities, brute force requires examining ~$6 \times 10^{16}$ tours. At $10^9$ tours/second, this would take ~$1900$ years.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Held-Karp Dynamic Programming Recurrence

**Definition:** Let $f(S, i)$ = minimum cost of a path starting from city $1$, visiting every city in subset $S$, and ending at city $i$, where $i \in S$.

**Base Case:**

$$f(\{i\}, i) = c_{1i} \quad \text{for all } i = 2, 3, \ldots, n$$

**Recurrence Relation:**

$$f(S, i) = \min_{j \in S, j \neq i} \left[ f(S \setminus \{i\}, j) + c_{ji} \right]$$

**Final Answer:**

$$OPT = \min_{i=2}^{n} \left[ f(\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}, i) + c_{i1} \right]$$

**Step-by-Step Worked Example:** Consider 4 cities with cost matrix:

$$C = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 10 & 0 & 35 & 25 \\ 15 & 35 & 0 & 30 \\ 20 & 25 & 30 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Compute base cases (paths from city 1 directly to i):**

$$f(\{2\}, 2) = c_{12} = 10$$
$$f(\{3\}, 3) = c_{13} = 15$$
$$f(\{4\}, 4) = c_{14} = 20$$

**Step 2 — Compute 2-element subsets:**

For $S = \{2, 3\}$, ending at city 3:

$$f(\{2, 3\}, 3) = \min \left[ f(\{2\}, 2) + c_{23} \right] = 10 + 35 = 45$$

For $S = \{2, 3\}$, ending at city 2:

$$f(\{2, 3\}, 2) = \min \left[ f(\{3\}, 3) + c_{32} \right] = 15 + 35 = 50$$

Continuing similarly for all pairs:

$$f(\{2, 4\}, 4) = 10 + 25 = 35$$
$$f(\{2, 4\}, 2) = 20 + 25 = 45$$
$$f(\{3, 4\}, 4) = 15 + 30 = 45$$
$$f(\{3, 4\}, 3) = 20 + 30 = 50$$

**Step 3 — Compute 3-element subsets:**

For $S = \{2, 3, 4\}$, ending at city 4:

$$f(\{2,3,4\}, 4) = \min \begin{cases} f(\{2,3\}, 2) + c_{24} = 50 + 25 = 75 \\ f(\{2,3\}, 3) + c_{34} = 45 + 30 = 75 \end{cases}$$

For $S = \{2, 3, 4\}$, ending at city 2:

$$f(\{2,3,4\}, 2) = \min \begin{cases} f(\{2,4\}, 3) \text{ not in subset} \\ f(\{3,4\}, 3) + c_{32} = 50 + 35 = 85 \\ f(\{3,4\}, 4) + c_{42} = 45 + 25 = 70 \end{cases}$$

So $f(\{2,3,4\}, 2) = 70$.

For $S = \{2, 3, 4\}$, ending at city 3:

$$f(\{2,3,4\}, 3) = \min \begin{cases} f(\{2,4\}, 2) + c_{23} = 45 + 35 = 80 \\ f(\{2,4\}, 4) + c_{43} = 35 + 30 = 65 \end{cases}$$

So $f(\{2,3,4\}, 3) = 65$.

**Step 4 — Compute final optimal tour:**

$$OPT = \min \begin{cases} f(\{2,3,4\}, 2) + c_{21} = 70 + 10 = 80 \\ f(\{2,3,4\}, 3) + c_{31} = 65 + 15 = 80 \\ f(\{2,3,4\}, 4) + c_{41} = 75 + 20 = 95 \end{cases}$$

Therefore, $OPT = 80$.

**Step 5 — Trace back the optimal tour:**

The optimal path ends with either city 2 or city 3 (both gave 80). Taking the path ending at city 2 with cost 70, predecessor was city 4 with cost $f(\{3,4\}, 4) = 45$, predecessor was city 3 with cost $f(\{3\}, 3) = 15$. So optimal tour is:

$$1 \to 3 \to 4 \to 2 \to 1 \quad \text{with cost } 15 + 30 + 25 + 10 = 80$$

**Verification:** Tour $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$ gives $10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = 80$ ✓ (same tour reversed).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Nearest Neighbor Heuristic Algorithm

**Algorithm Steps:**

- **Step 1:** Start at an arbitrary city $v_1$.
- **Step 2:** From current city $v_k$, visit the unvisited city $v_{k+1}$ with minimum cost:

$$v_{k+1} = \arg\min_{j \notin \{v_1, \ldots, v_k\}} c_{v_k, j}$$

- **Step 3:** Repeat Step 2 until all cities are visited.
- **Step 4:** Return to starting city $v_1$ to close the tour.

**Total cost of the constructed tour:**

$$\text{Cost}_{NN} = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} c_{v_k, v_{k+1}} + c_{v_n, v_1}$$

**Complexity Analysis:** At each of $n$ steps, we scan up to $n$ unvisited cities, giving time complexity:

$$T(n) = O(n^2)$$

**Drawback:** Nearest Neighbor is a greedy heuristic and can yield tours significantly worse than optimal. A classic counter-example is a "clustered" city arrangement where NN zig-zags between clusters.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Approximation Guarantee via MST

**Theorem:** For Metric TSP, the tour obtained by traversing a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) using a depth-first walk is at most twice the optimal tour cost.

**Proof Outline:**

- **Step 1:** MST cost $\leq$ Optimal TSP cost:

$$\text{MST} \leq OPT$$

because removing any edge from an optimal TSP tour yields a spanning tree, and MST is the *minimum* such tree.

- **Step 2:** DFS traversal of MST visits every edge twice:

$$\text{DFS walk cost} = 2 \cdot \text{MST}$$

- **Step 3:** Apply triangle inequality to "shortcut" repeated vertices:

$$\text{Shortcut tour} \leq 2 \cdot \text{MST} \leq 2 \cdot OPT$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Approximation Ratio} = \frac{\text{Heuristic Tour}}{OPT} \leq 2$$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Type | Time Complexity | Optimality | Best For |
|--------|------|-----------------|------------|----------|
| Brute Force | Exact | $O(n!)$ | Optimal | $n \leq 12$ |
| Branch & Bound (Little et al.) | Exact | Exponential (avg. faster) | Optimal | $n \leq 60$ |
| Held-Karp DP | Exact | $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ | Optimal | $n \leq 30$ |
| Nearest Neighbor | Heuristic | $O(n^2)$ | Approximate | Quick baseline |
| 2-Opt | Local Search | $O(n^2)$ per pass | Approximate | Tour improvement |
| MST Heuristic | Approximation | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $\leq 2 \cdot OPT$ | Metric TSP guarantee |
| Christofides | Approximation | Polynomial | $\leq 1.5 \cdot OPT$ | Metric TSP |
| Genetic Algorithm | Metaheuristic | Variable | Near-optimal | Very large instances |
| Ant Colony Optimization | Metaheuristic | Variable | Near-optimal | Dynamic environments |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of TSP Solution Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input TSP Instance"] --> B{"Instance Size n"}
    B -->|"n <= 30"| C["Exact Methods Block"]
    B -->|"30 < n <= 60"| D["Branch and Bound"]
    B -->|"n > 60"| E["Heuristic Methods Block"]
    
    C --> C1["Brute Force O(n!)"]
    C --> C2["Held Karp DP"]
    C --> C3["Integer Linear Programming"]
    
    D --> D1["Little Algorithm"]
    D --> D2["Branch and Cut"]
    
    E --> E1["Construction Heuristics"]
    E --> E2["Improvement Heuristics"]
    E --> E3["Metaheuristics"]
    
    E1 --> F1["Nearest Neighbor"]
    E1 --> F2["Greedy Insertion"]
    E1 --> F3["Christofides MST"]
    
    E2 --> G1["Two Opt Swap"]
    E2 --> G2["Three Opt Swap"]
    E2 --> G3["Lin Kernighan"]
    
    E3 --> H1["Genetic Algorithm"]
    E3 --> H2["Simulated Annealing"]
    E3 --> H3["Ant Colony Optimization"]
    E3 --> H4["Tabu Search"]
    
    F1 --> Z["Output Best Tour Found"]
    F2 --> Z
    F3 --> Z
    G1 --> Z
    G2 --> Z
    G3 --> Z
    H1 --> Z
    H2 --> Z
    H3 --> Z
    H4 --> Z
    C1 --> Z
    C2 --> Z
    C3 --> Z
    D1 --> Z
    D2 --> Z
    
    Z --> Y["Tour Validation and Cost Evaluation"]
```

### 4.2 Held-Karp Dynamic Programming State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Base Case S equals i only"] --> B1["f of S minus i comma j plus c of ji"]
    B1 --> B2["Subproblem Expansion by Set Size"]
    B2 --> B3["Two Element Sets"]
    B2 --> B4["Three Element Sets"]
    B2 --> B5["N Minus One Element Sets"]
    B3 --> C1["Recursive Computation"]
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> D1["Final Minimum Plus Return Edge"]
    D1 --> E1["Optimal Tour Cost OPT"]
    E1 --> F1["Backtracking for Tour Reconstruction"]
```

### 4.3 Two Opt Local Search Improvement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Initial Tour T"] --> Loop{"For All Edge Pairs i and j"}
    Loop -->|"Pair Found"| Compute["Compute Delta Cost"]
    Compute --> Decision{"Delta Less Than Zero"}
    Decision -->|"Yes"| Swap["Reverse Subpath Between i and j"]
    Decision -->|"No"| Loop
    Swap --> Updated["New Improved Tour T prime"]
    Updated --> Loop
    Loop -->|"No Improvement Possible"| Output["Local Optimum Tour"]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Mapping of TSP Tour on a Weighted Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["City A"]
    node2["City B"]
    node3["City C"]
    node4["City D"]
    node5["City E"]
    
    node1 -->|"Cost 10"| node2
    node1 -->|"Cost 8"| node3
    node1 -->|"Cost 9"| node4
    node1 -->|"Cost 7"| node5
    node2 -->|"Cost 12"| node3
    node2 -->|"Cost 6"| node4
    node2 -->|"Cost 11"| node5
    node3 -->|"Cost 5"| node4
    node3 -->|"Cost 14"| node5
    node4 -->|"Cost 9"| node5
    
    style node1 fill:#FFD700
    style node2 fill:#87CEEB
    style node3 fill:#90EE90
    style node4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style node5 fill:#DDA0DD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The complete graph $K_5$ on 5 cities has $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ edges. An optimal TSP tour selects exactly 5 edges forming a Hamiltonian cycle of minimum total weight. In this example, a possible optimal tour is $A \to B \to D \to C \to E \to A$ with cost $10 + 6 + 5 + 14 + 7 = 42$.

### 4.5 Algorithmic Decision Flow for Choosing TSP Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Is Exact Solution Required"] -->|"Yes"| Q2["Is n Less Than 30"]
    Q1 -->|"No"| Q3["Is Approximation Guarantee Needed"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| R1["Use Held Karp DP"]
    Q2 -->|"No"| R2["Use Branch and Bound Little"]
    Q3 -->|"Yes Metric"| R3["Use Christofides 1.5 Approx"]
    Q3 -->|"No General"| R4["Use Best Heuristic Available"]
    R4 --> Q4["Is Speed Critical"]
    Q4 -->|"Yes"| R5["Nearest Neighbor plus 2 Opt"]
    Q4 -->|"No Quality Critical"| R6["Genetic Algorithm or Simulated Annealing"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Define the Travelling Salesman Problem. State the differences between symmetric and asymmetric TSP.**

**Model Answer:**

The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) is a combinatorial optimization problem that seeks the shortest possible Hamiltonian cycle (a closed tour visiting each city exactly once) through a set of $n$ cities, given the pairwise travel costs.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Symmetric TSP | Asymmetric TSP |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Cost matrix property | $d_{ij} = d_{ji}$ | $d_{ij} \neq d_{ji}$ possible |
| Number of distinct tours | $\frac{(n-1)!}{2}$ | $(n-1)!$ |
| Computational hardness | NP-hard | NP-hard |
| Real-world example | Road network distances | One-way streets, flight times with winds |

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]`

**State any three heuristic methods to solve the Travelling Salesman Problem.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Nearest Neighbor Heuristic:** Greedily visit the closest unvisited city from the current location. Time complexity $O(n^2)$.
2. **2-Opt Local Search:** Iteratively removes two non-adjacent edges and reconnects the path in the other possible way if the swap reduces total tour length.
3. **Genetic Algorithm:** Population-based metaheuristic that evolves a population of tours using selection, crossover, and mutation operators toward near-optimal solutions.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Formulate the Travelling Salesman Problem as an Integer Linear Program. Explain the role of the **subtour elimination constraint** with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **Nearest Neighbor heuristic** starting from city A for the following 5-city distance matrix. Compute the total tour cost and state the tour obtained. **(7 Marks)**

$$D = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 2 & 7 & 6 & 4 \\ 2 & 0 & 5 & 3 & 8 \\ 7 & 5 & 0 & 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 3 & 4 & 0 & 5 \\ 4 & 8 & 6 & 5 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Model Solution for (a):**

**ILP Formulation:**

Decision variable: $x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$, equals $1$ if edge $(i,j)$ is used in the tour.

$$\min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij}$$

subject to:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i$$
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall j$$
$$\sum_{i \in S} \sum_{j \in S} x_{ij} \leq |S| - 1 \quad \forall S \subset V, \, 2 \leq |S| \leq n-1$$
$$x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$$

**Role of Subtour Elimination Constraint:** Without this constraint, the ILP may produce a solution consisting of multiple disjoint smaller cycles. For example, with 4 cities, the solver might return two cycles $A \to B \to A$ and $C \to D \to C$ instead of a single 4-city tour. The constraint $\sum_{i \in S} \sum_{j \in S} x_{ij} \leq |S| - 1$ for every proper subset $S$ ensures no such subtour can exist, because a Hamiltonian cycle on $|S|$ vertices requires exactly $|S|$ edges, and we cap it at $|S|-1$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Starting at city A:

- **Step 1:** From A (row 1): min cost is to B (cost 2). Move to B.
- **Step 2:** From B (row 2), unvisited {C, D, E}: min is to D (cost 3). Move to D.
- **Step 3:** From D (row 4), unvisited {C, E}: min is to C (cost 4). Move to C.
- **Step 4:** From C (row 3), unvisited {E}: cost is 6. Move to E.
- **Step 5:** Return to A: cost is 4.

**Tour:** $A \to B \to D \to C \to E \to A$

**Total cost:** $2 + 3 + 4 + 6 + 4 = 19$

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Solve the following 4-city TSP using **Held-Karp Dynamic Programming**. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

$$C = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 8 & 12 & 6 \\ 8 & 0 & 4 & 10 \\ 12 & 4 & 0 & 7 \\ 6 & 10 & 7 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**(b)** Explain the **Branch and Bound method** for TSP (Little's algorithm) with its key bounding steps. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Let cities be labeled 1, 2, 3, 4 (starting city = 1).

**Step 1 — Base cases:**

$$f(\{2\}, 2) = c_{12} = 8$$
$$f(\{3\}, 3) = c_{13} = 12$$
$$f(\{4\}, 4) = c_{14} = 6$$

**Step 2 — Two-element subsets:**

$f(\{2,3\}, 3)$ via 2: $8 + c_{23} = 8 + 4 = 12$
$f(\{2,3\}, 2)$ via 3: $12 + c_{32} = 12 + 4 = 16$
$f(\{2,4\}, 4)$ via 2: $8 + c_{24} = 8 + 10 = 18$
$f(\{2,4\}, 2)$ via 4: $6 + c_{42} = 6 + 10 = 16$
$f(\{3,4\}, 4)$ via 3: $12 + c_{34} = 12 + 7 = 19$
$f(\{3,4\}, 3)$ via 4: $6 + c_{43} = 6 + 7 = 13$

**Step 3 — Three-element subset $S = \{2, 3, 4\}$:**

$f(\{2,3,4\}, 2)$:
- via 3: $f(\{2,4\}, 4) + c_{32} = 18 + 4 = 22$
- via 4: $f(\{3,4\}, 3) + c_{42} = 13 + 10 = 23$
- minimum = **22**

$f(\{2,3,4\}, 3)$:
- via 2: $f(\{2,4\}, 4) + c_{23} = 18 + 4 = 22$
- via 4: $f(\{3,4\}, 4) + c_{43} = 19 + 7 = 26$
- minimum = **22**

$f(\{2,3,4\}, 4)$:
- via 2: $f(\{2,3\}, 2) + c_{24} = 16 + 10 = 26$
- via 3: $f(\{2,3\}, 3) + c_{34} = 12 + 7 = 19$
- minimum = **19**

**Step 4 — Final optimal tour:**

$$OPT = \min \begin{cases} f(\{2,3,4\}, 2) + c_{21} = 22 + 8 = 30 \\ f(\{2,3,4\}, 3) + c_{31} = 22 + 12 = 34 \\ f(\{2,3,4\}, 4) + c_{41} = 19 + 6 = 25 \end{cases}$$

$$OPT = 25$$

**Backtracking:** Tour ends at 4 (cost 19, predecessor 3 with $f(\{2,3\}, 3) = 12$, predecessor 2 with $f(\{2\}, 2) = 8$). Tour is:

$$1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1 \quad \text{with cost } 8 + 4 + 7 + 6 = 25$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Little's Branch and Bound Algorithm for TSP:**

**Key Idea:** Systematically enumerate partial tours using a search tree, pruning branches whose lower bound exceeds the cost of the best-known tour.

**Step 1 — Reduction of Cost Matrix:** Subtract the minimum element of each row from the row, then subtract the minimum element of each column from the column. The sum of all subtracted values is the **lower bound** of the root node.

**Step 2 — Edge Selection for Branching:** For each zero entry in the reduced matrix, compute a penalty equal to the sum of the smallest non-zero elements in its row and column. Choose the edge with the highest penalty as the branching edge.

**Step 3 — Branching (Two Subproblems):**
- **Include branch:** Fix the chosen edge in the tour and update the cost matrix (remove the row and column of the included edge to prevent subtours).
- **Exclude branch:** Set the chosen edge's cost to infinity and re-reduce the matrix.

**Step 4 — Bounding:** Compute the lower bound of each new node. If the lower bound is greater than or equal to the best tour cost found so far, prune that branch.

**Step 5 — Termination:** Continue until all branches are pruned or a leaf representing a complete tour is reached. The minimum tour among explored leaves is optimal.

**Key Insight:** The algorithm achieves efficiency because the reduced cost matrix provides a strong lower bound, enabling aggressive pruning of the search tree.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to state that TSP is **NP-hard** and not just NP-complete — this distinction is critical in exam answers.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In Held-Karp, students frequently miscompute the **return edge** in Step 4. Always remember to add $c_{i1}$ (return to starting city).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** For the subtour elimination constraint, students often write $\sum x_{ij} = |S|$ instead of $\leq |S| - 1$. The correct bound is $|S| - 1$, not $|S|$, because a Hamiltonian cycle on $|S|$ vertices has exactly $|S|$ edges, and we want to forbid *cycles* on proper subsets.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In Nearest Neighbor, students sometimes revisit the *starting city too early*. The starting city should only be visited last, when closing the tour.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to mention the **triangle inequality** property when discussing MST-based 2-approximation. The guarantee holds *only* for Metric TSP.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Travelling Salesman Problem**

- **Definition:** TSP = Find minimum-cost Hamiltonian cycle visiting all $n$ cities exactly once and returning to start.
- **Problem Class:** NP-hard (decision version is NP-complete); no known polynomial-time exact algorithm.
- **Symmetric TSP Tours:** $\frac{(n-1)!}{2}$ distinct tours. **Asymmetric TSP Tours:** $(n-1)!$ distinct tours.
- **ILP Formulation:** Uses binary variables $x_{ij}$, degree constraints (row sums = column sums = 1), and DFJ **subtour elimination constraints**.
- **Brute Force:** $O(n!)$ time — infeasible beyond $n \approx 12$.
- **Held-Karp DP:** $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ time — feasible up to $n \approx 30$. Recurrence: $f(S, i) = \min_{j \in S \setminus \{i\}} [f(S \setminus \{i\}, j) + c_{ji}]$.
- **Nearest Neighbor Heuristic:** $O(n^2)$ — greedy, fast, but no approximation guarantee.
- **2-Opt Local Search:** Removes crossing edges iteratively; $O(n^2)$ per improvement pass.
- **MST-Based 2-Approximation:** Works only for Metric TSP (triangle inequality). Christofides improves ratio to $1.5$.
- **Common Variants:** Metric TSP, Euclidean TSP, ATSP, Max TSP.
- **Best Known Practical Methods:** Lin-Kernighan (local search), Genetic Algorithms, Ant Colony Optimization, Concorde solver (exact for large instances).
- **Real-World Apps:** VLSI design, logistics, genomics, robotics, network routing.
- **Key Formula to Memorize:** Optimal tour cost $= \min_{i=2}^{n} [f(\{1,\ldots,n\}, i) + c_{i1}]$.
- **Subtour Elimination Formula:** $\sum_{i \in S} \sum_{j \in S} x_{ij} \leq |S| - 1$ for all $S \subset V$, $2 \leq |S| \leq n-1$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variants of Simulated Annealing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Variants of Simulated Annealing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Foundational Definition of Simulated Annealing

**Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a trajectory-based, stochastic metaheuristic inspired by the physical annealing process of solids in thermal equilibrium. It approximates the global optimum of an objective function over a large search space by probabilistically accepting worse solutions, governed by a temperature parameter that is gradually reduced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Simulated Annealing is a probabilistic metaheuristic that emulates the metallurgical process of controlled heating and slow cooling. It escapes local optima by accepting inferior moves with a probability governed by the Metropolis criterion, ensuring asymptotic convergence to a global optimum under specific annealing schedule conditions.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blind Mountain Climber

Imagine a **foggy mountain range** at night. You are a blindfolded climber trying to reach the **lowest valley** (global optimum).

- **Pure Hill-Descent:** You only step downhill. You inevitably get trapped in the nearest small valley (**local minimum**).
- **Simulated Annealing:** You carry a magical "thermal energy meter." When the meter is hot (early stages), you randomly jump **uphill** (accept worse solutions) to explore distant ridges. As the meter cools down (annealing schedule), you become more cautious — eventually you only step downhill.
- **Variants of SA:** They differ in *how the meter cools*, *how high you are willing to jump*, and *whether you can reheat the meter* to escape new traps.

This thermal acceptance mechanism is what gives SA its **asymptotic convergence guarantee** to the global optimum.

### 1.3 What Are "Variants" of Simulated Annealing?

> [!NOTE]
> A **variant** is a controlled modification to one or more of the four SA control levers:
> 1. **Cooling Schedule** — the law governing $T_k \rightarrow 0$
> 2. **Acceptance Probability Function** — Boltzmann vs Cauchy vs other distributions
> 3. **Neighbourhood Generation Rule** — step size and distribution shape
> 4. **Re-Annealing / Re-Heating Protocol** — periodic temperature boost

### 1.4 Catalog of Principal Variants (KTU High-Yield)

| Variant | Acronym | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| Classical Simulated Annealing | CSA | Boltzmann acceptance, geometric cooling |
| Fast Simulated Annealing | FSA | Cauchy-distributed neighbourhood, $T_k = T_0 / k$ |
| Adaptive Simulated Annealing | ASA | Self-tuning learning rates for each parameter |
| Very Fast Simulated Re-Annealing | VFSR | Per-variable temperature rescaling + re-annealing |
| Boltzmann Annealing | BA | Logarithmic cooling schedule |
| Threshold Accepting | TA | Deterministic threshold instead of probabilistic |
| Quantum Annealing | QA | Quantum tunneling via adiabatic evolution |

### 1.5 Critical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following quantities are treated as **bold-marked canonical parameters** throughout KTU valuation:

- **$T_0$** — initial temperature (typically chosen so initial acceptance probability $\geq \mathbf{0.8}$)
- **$T_f$** — final (freezing) temperature
- **$\alpha$** — cooling coefficient, typically $\alpha \in [\mathbf{0.8}, \mathbf{0.99}]$
- **$L_k$** — Markov chain length (number of iterations per temperature)
- **$\Delta E$** — energy / cost differential between candidate and current solution

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Operational Phases of Every SA Variant

Every variant of Simulated Annealing executes the same **abstract algorithmic skeleton** with parameter-level substitutions:

### Phase 1 — Initialisation
- Generate an initial solution $s_0 \in \mathcal{S}$.
- Initialise temperature $T \leftarrow T_0$.
- Set epoch counter $k \leftarrow 0$.

### Phase 2 — Neighbourhood Sampling
- Generate a candidate solution $s' \in \mathcal{N}(s)$ using the variant's specific neighbourhood operator.

### Phase 3 — Acceptance Decision (the heart of every variant)
- Compute $\Delta E = f(s') - f(s)$.
- **If** $\Delta E \leq 0$: accept $s' \leftarrow s$ (downhill move).
- **Else**: accept with probability governed by the variant's acceptance function $\mathcal{A}(\Delta E, T)$.

### Phase 4 — Cooling & Convergence
- Update temperature using the variant's cooling schedule.
- Repeat Phases 2–4 until $T \leq T_f$ or stopping criterion met.

## 2.2 Detailed Variant Profiles

### 2.2.1 Classical Simulated Annealing (CSA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CSA** uses the **Metropolis-Hastings criterion** derived from statistical mechanics. The acceptance probability is the **Boltzmann factor**.

- **Neighbourhood:** Gaussian-distributed perturbation.
- **Acceptance:** $P_{CSA} = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ when $\Delta E > 0$.
- **Cooling:** Geometric — $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$.

### 2.2.2 Fast Simulated Annealing (FSA)

**FSA** by Szu and Hartley (1987) replaces the Boltzmann distribution with the **Cauchy distribution** to permit larger escape steps at high temperature.

- **Neighbourhood:** Cauchy-distributed perturbation.
- **Acceptance:** $P_{FSA} = T / (T^2 + \Delta E^2)$.
- **Cooling:** $T_k = T_0 / k$ — much faster than geometric cooling.

### 2.2.3 Adaptive Simulated Annealing (ASA)

> [!NOTE]
> **ASA** by Ingber (1993) introduces **per-parameter learning rates**, allowing each decision variable to have its own temperature and step size.

- **Cooling:** $T_k^{(i)} = T_0^{(i)} \cdot \exp(-c^{(i)} \cdot k^{1/D})$ where $D$ is dimensionality.
- **Sensitivities:** $\rho^{(i)} = \rho_0 \cdot \exp(-\beta^{(i)} \cdot k^{1/D})$.
- **Re-annealing:** Automatic periodic adjustment based on acceptance statistics.

### 2.2.4 Very Fast Simulated Re-Annealing (VFSR)

**VFSR** extends ASA with **periodic re-annealing** — the temperature is selectively reheated for parameters that have converged too quickly.

- Re-annealing criterion: parameters whose cost-sensitivity has dropped below a threshold are reheated.

### 2.2.5 Threshold Accepting (TA)

**TA** by Dueck and Scheuer eliminates probabilistic acceptance, replacing it with a deterministic threshold.

- **Acceptance rule:** accept $s'$ if $\Delta E \leq \tau_k$, where $\tau_k$ is a deterministic threshold.
- **Cooling:** $\tau_k$ decreases monotonically to zero.

### 2.2.6 Quantum Annealing (QA)

**QA** uses quantum tunneling instead of thermal hopping.

- **Hamiltonian:** $H(s) = H_0 + \Gamma(t) \cdot H_p$
- Adiabatic evolution from transverse field $\Gamma_0$ to $\Gamma_f \approx 0$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann acceptance (CSA) | $P_{accept} = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ | $\Delta E > 0$, $T > 0$ |
| Cauchy acceptance (FSA) | $P_{accept} = T / (T^2 + \Delta E^2)$ | Faster escape at high $T$ |
| Geometric cooling (CSA) | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ | $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$ |
| Logarithmic cooling (Boltzmann) | $T_k = T_0 / \ln(k + k_0)$ | Guarantees convergence |
| FSA cooling | $T_k = T_0 / k$ | Cauchy schedule |
| ASA cooling per parameter | $T_k^{(i)} = T_0^{(i)} \cdot \exp(-c^{(i)} \cdot k^{1/D})$ | $D$ = dimensionality |
| Initial temperature heuristic | $T_0 = -\Delta E_{avg} / \ln(P_0)$ | For target $P_0 \approx 0.8$ |
| Equilibrium detection | $\chi_k \approx 0$ (variance of acceptance) | Markov chain completion |
| Stopping criterion | $T_k \leq T_f$ **OR** $\vert f_k - f_{k-L} \vert \leq \epsilon$ | Numerical termination |
| Re-annealing criterion | $\rho_k^{(i)} < \rho_{threshold}$ | VFSR per-parameter |
| Threshold accepting | Accept iff $\Delta E \leq \tau_k$ | Deterministic TA |
| Quantum Hamiltonian | $H(s) = H_0 + \Gamma(t) \cdot H_p$ | Adiabatic QA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked to compare variants, students MUST mention (i) the acceptance function, (ii) the cooling schedule, AND (iii) the neighbourhood generation rule. Missing any one loses **3 marks out of 7** in a sub-question.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of SA Variants

| Variant | Best-Suited Engineering Problem |
|---|---|
| CSA | VLSI circuit partitioning, TSP with $n \leq 500$ |
| FSA | Large-scale continuous optimisation (control tuning) |
| ASA | Neural-network weight training, parameter identification |
| VFSR | Multi-modal function optimisation, hyperparameter search |
| TA | Real-time embedded scheduling (deterministic runtime) |
| QA | Combinatorial NP-hard problems (D-Wave hardware) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Initial Temperature $T_0$ for Target Acceptance Probability

> **Goal:** Given a desired initial acceptance probability $P_0$ for uphill moves of average magnitude $\overline{\Delta E}$, determine $T_0$.

The Boltzmann acceptance criterion for $\Delta E > 0$ is:

$$
P_{accept} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)
$$

We want this to equal a chosen target probability $P_0$ when $\Delta E = \overline{\Delta E}$:

$$
P_0 = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\overline{\Delta E}}{T_0}\right)
$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln P_0 = -\frac{\overline{\Delta E}}{T_0}
$$

Multiply both sides by $-1$:

$$
-\ln P_0 = \frac{\overline{\Delta E}}{T_0}
$$

Invert the ratio:

$$
T_0 = \frac{\overline{\Delta E}}{-\ln P_0} = \frac{\overline{\Delta E}}{\ln(1 / P_0)}
$$

For the canonical KTU choice $P_0 = 0.8$:

$$
T_0 = \frac{\overline{\Delta E}}{\ln(1.25)} \approx \frac{\overline{\Delta E}}{0.2231} \approx 4.48 \cdot \overline{\Delta E}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_0 \approx 4.48 \cdot \overline{\Delta E}\,}
$$

This explains the engineering heuristic: **$T_0$ should be roughly 4 to 5 times the mean uphill cost differential** to start with 80% acceptance.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Convergence of Logarithmic Cooling

The **Boltzmann Annealing** cooling schedule is:

$$
T_k = \frac{T_0}{\ln(k + k_0)}
$$

We wish to verify that the total "thermal energy" delivered to the system over $K$ iterations remains bounded:

$$
\mathcal{E} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} T_k = T_0 \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{\ln(k + k_0)}
$$

For large $K$, the harmonic series of logarithms is dominated by the largest term:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{\ln(k + k_0)} \approx \int_{1}^{K} \frac{dk}{\ln(k + k_0)}
$$

Substituting $u = \ln(k + k_0)$, $du = dk / (k + k_0)$:

$$
\int \frac{du}{u} \cdot \frac{(k + k_0)}{1} \approx \int \frac{e^u \, du}{u}
$$

This integral evaluates asymptotically to:

$$
\int_{1}^{K} \frac{dk}{\ln(k + k_0)} \sim \frac{K}{\ln K} \quad \text{(as } K \to \infty\text{)}
$$

Therefore the cumulative thermal energy satisfies:

$$
\mathcal{E} \sim T_0 \cdot \frac{K}{\ln K} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \infty \text{ as } K \to \infty
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Logarithmic cooling guarantees asymptotic convergence to the global optimum, but at computational cost } \mathcal{O}(K / \ln K).\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Acceptance Probability Ratio: CSA vs FSA

We compare the two acceptance functions at the **same temperature $T$** and the **same uphill move $\Delta E$**.

**CSA (Boltzmann):**

$$
P_{CSA}(\Delta E, T) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)
$$

**FSA (Cauchy):**

$$
P_{FSA}(\Delta E, T) = \frac{T}{T^2 + \Delta E^2}
$$

Take the ratio:

$$
R = \frac{P_{FSA}}{P_{CSA}} = \frac{T}{T^2 + \Delta E^2} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)
$$

Let $x = \Delta E / T$ (dimensionless ratio). Then:

$$
R(x) = \frac{\exp(x)}{1 + x^2}
$$

At $x = 0$: $R(0) = 1 / 1 = 1$ (both accept everything when $\Delta E = 0$).

Take the first derivative:

$$
\frac{dR}{dx} = \frac{(1 + x^2)\exp(x) - 2x \exp(x)}{(1 + x^2)^2} = \frac{\exp(x)\bigl(1 + x^2 - 2x\bigr)}{(1 + x^2)^2}
$$

The numerator factorises as:

$$
1 + x^2 - 2x = (x - 1)^2 \geq 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{dR}{dx} \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } x \geq 0
$$

This proves $R(x)$ is **monotonically non-decreasing**, meaning:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{FSA} \geq P_{CSA} \quad \text{for all } \Delta E > 0, T > 0\,}
$$

**Engineering implication:** FSA accepts **more** uphill moves than CSA at any given temperature, enabling faster exploration of the search space.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cooling Coefficient Selection for Geometric Schedule

Given the geometric law $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ and total iteration budget $K$:

$$
T_K = T_0 \cdot \alpha^K
$$

We require $T_K \leq T_f$:

$$
\alpha^K \leq \frac{T_f}{T_0}
$$

Taking logarithms:

$$
K \ln \alpha \leq \ln\!\left(\frac{T_f}{T_0}\right)
$$

Since $\alpha < 1$, $\ln \alpha < 0$. Dividing flips the inequality:

$$
K \geq \frac{\ln(T_f / T_0)}{\ln \alpha}
$$

For typical values $T_0 = 100$, $T_f = 0.01$, $\alpha = 0.95$:

$$
K \geq \frac{\ln(0.0001)}{\ln(0.95)} = \frac{-9.210}{-0.0513} \approx 179.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\,K \geq 180 \text{ iterations minimum for } \alpha = 0.95,\,}
$$

showing why geometric cooling with $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$ demands **hundreds to thousands** of iterations.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | CSA | FSA | ASA / VFSR | TA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acceptance rule | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | Deterministic |
| Acceptance function | $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$ | $T / (T^2 + \Delta E^2)$ | Per-parameter Boltzmann | $\Delta E \leq \tau_k$ |
| Cooling schedule | Geometric | $T_0 / k$ | $T_0 \exp(-c k^{1/D})$ | $\tau_{k+1} = \gamma \tau_k$ |
| Per-variable temperature | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| Re-annealing | No | No | **Yes** (VFSR) | No |
| Asymptotic convergence | Yes (slow) | Yes (faster) | Yes (fastest classical) | Empirical only |
| Computational cost | Low | Low | Moderate | Very low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Simulated Annealing Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialise solution s, T = T0, k = 0] --> B[Generate candidate s' in Neighbourhood of s]
    B --> C[Compute deltaE = f of s' minus f of s]
    C --> D{deltaE less than or equal to 0?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Accept: s = s']
    D -->|No| F[Compute acceptance probability P]
    F --> G{Random number less than P?}
    G -->|Yes| E
    G -->|No| H[Reject: keep s]
    E --> I[Update epoch counter and Markov chain length]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Markov chain complete at T?}
    J -->|No| B
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply cooling schedule to update T]
    K --> L{T less than or equal to Tf?}
    L -->|No| B
    L -->|Yes| M[Return best s found]
```

## 4.2 Variant Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose SA Variant] --> B{Problem Type?}
    B -->|Discrete Combinatorial| C{Critical: Convergence Guarantee?}
    B -->|Continuous Large-Scale| D{Need Per-Variable Tuning?}
    B -->|Real-Time Embedded| E{Deterministic Runtime Needed?}

    C -->|Yes| F[Classical SA CSA]
    C -->|Speed Matters| G[Fast SA FSA]

    D -->|Yes| H[Adaptive SA ASA]
    D -->|Plus Re-Annealing| I[Very Fast Simulated Re-Annealing VFSR]

    E -->|Yes| J[Threshold Accepting TA]
    E -->|Quantum Hardware| K[Quantum Annealing QA]

    F --> L[Boltzmann acceptance, geometric cooling]
    G --> M[Cauchy acceptance, T0 over k cooling]
    H --> N[Per-parameter Boltzmann, exponential cooling]
    I --> O[ASA plus automatic reheating]
    J --> P[Deterministic threshold tau_k]
    K --> Q[Adiabatic Hamiltonian evolution]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SA Variant Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Subsystem]
        A1[Initial Solution s0] --> A2[Initial Temperature T0]
        A2 --> A3[Cost Function f of s]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Core SA Engine]
        B1[Neighbourhood Generator NG] --> B2[Acceptance Module AM]
        B2 --> B3[Cooling Schedule Controller CSC]
        B3 --> B4[Re-Annealing Logic RAL]
        B4 --> B1
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Subsystem]
        C1[Best Solution Tracker BST] --> C2[Stopping Criterion Evaluator SCE]
        C2 --> C3[Final Optimal Solution]
    end

    A3 --> B1
    A1 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C2 -->|Continue| B1
    C2 -->|Terminate| C3
```

## 4.4 Temperature Decay Comparison Across Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Temperature Schedule Comparison] --> B[CSA: Geometric T alpha T]
    A --> C[FSA: Inverse T0 over k]
    A --> D[Boltzmann: Logarithmic T0 over ln k]
    A --> E[ASA: Exponential T0 exp negative c k power 1 over D]

    B --> B1[Smooth exponential decay, slow]
    C --> C1[Rapid decay at first, then slow]
    D --> D1[Extremely slow decay, provable convergence]
    E --> E1[Per-parameter adaptive, fastest in practice]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Classical Simulated Annealing (CSA) and Fast Simulated Annealing (FSA) with respect to acceptance probability and cooling schedule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CSA | FSA |
|---|---|---|
| Acceptance function | $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$ (Boltzmann) | $T / (T^2 + \Delta E^2)$ (Cauchy) |
| Cooling schedule | Geometric: $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ | Inverse: $T_k = T_0 / k$ |
| Neighbourhood | Gaussian | Cauchy-distributed |

The Cauchy acceptance in FSA gives a **heavier tail**, enabling larger escape steps at high temperature, hence "fast."

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is re-annealing in Simulated Annealing? Which variant employs it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Re-annealing is the **periodic selective reheating** of the temperature parameter — typically per variable — based on observed cost-sensitivity. If a parameter's sensitivity drops below a threshold, its temperature is **reset upward** to allow renewed exploration. It is the defining feature of **Very Fast Simulated Re-Annealing (VFSR)** introduced by Ingber (1993).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With neat algorithmic steps, explain the working of **Classical Simulated Annealing (CSA)**. State clearly the role of the **Metropolis criterion** and the **geometric cooling schedule**. Derive the expression for initial temperature $T_0$ if a target acceptance probability $P_0$ is desired. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Fast Simulated Annealing (FSA)**. Derive and prove that $P_{FSA} \geq P_{CSA}$ for all $\Delta E > 0$, $T > 0$. Discuss the engineering significance of this inequality. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution A(a) — CSA Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Algorithm of CSA**

1. Initialise $s \leftarrow s_0$, $T \leftarrow T_0$, $k \leftarrow 0$.
2. Repeat until $T \leq T_f$:
   - For $i = 1$ to $L_k$ (Markov chain length):
     - Generate $s' \in \mathcal{N}(s)$ (Gaussian perturbation).
     - Compute $\Delta E = f(s') - f(s)$.
     - If $\Delta E \leq 0$: accept $s \leftarrow s'$.
     - Else: accept with probability $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$.
   - Update temperature: $T \leftarrow \alpha \cdot T$.
3. Return best $s$ encountered.

**Step 2: Role of the Metropolis Criterion**

The Metropolis criterion $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$ is derived from the **Boltzmann distribution** in statistical mechanics. It allows uphill moves with a probability that **decreases** as the system cools, balancing **exploration** (early) and **exploitation** (late).

**Step 3: Role of Geometric Cooling**

$T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ with $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$ ensures slow, monotonic temperature decay — a discrete approximation of the thermodynamic cooling required to maintain **quasi-equilibrium** at each temperature.

**Step 4: Derivation of $T_0$** (full derivation as in Section 3.1)

$$
T_0 = \frac{\overline{\Delta E}}{-\ln P_0} = \frac{\overline{\Delta E}}{\ln(1 / P_0)}
$$

For $P_0 = 0.8$:

$$
T_0 \approx 4.48 \cdot \overline{\Delta E}
$$

---

### Model Solution A(b) — FSA Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition of FSA**

FSA replaces the Gaussian neighbourhood and Boltzmann acceptance with a **Cauchy-distributed neighbourhood** and acceptance probability:

$$
P_{FSA}(\Delta E, T) = \frac{T}{T^2 + \Delta E^2}
$$

**Step 2: Proof that $P_{FSA} \geq P_{CSA}$**

Let $x = \Delta E / T$. Then:

$$
R(x) = \frac{P_{FSA}}{P_{CSA}} = \frac{\exp(x)}{1 + x^2}
$$

Differentiate:

$$
\frac{dR}{dx} = \frac{\exp(x)\bigl[(1 + x^2) - 2x\bigr]}{(1 + x^2)^2} = \frac{\exp(x)(x - 1)^2}{(1 + x^2)^2} \geq 0
$$

Since $(x - 1)^2 \geq 0$ and $\exp(x) > 0$, the derivative is non-negative. Hence $R(x)$ is monotonically non-decreasing, and $R(x) \geq R(0) = 1$ for all $x \geq 0$.

$$
\boxed{\,P_{FSA} \geq P_{CSA} \quad \forall \Delta E > 0,\, T > 0\,}
$$

**Step 3: Engineering Significance**

This inequality guarantees that **FSA escapes local optima more aggressively** than CSA at any given temperature. Practical implications:

- **Faster convergence** to feasible regions.
- **Better suited for large-scale** continuous optimisation (control tuning, neural-network training).
- **Trade-off:** more iterations wasted on uphill moves → careful tuning of $L_k$ needed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss **Adaptive Simulated Annealing (ASA)** in detail. Explain how it generalises the cooling schedule using the parameter $D$ (dimensionality) and the per-variable sensitivities $\rho^{(i)}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Very Fast Simulated Re-Annealing (VFSR)**? Explain the re-annealing criterion with a suitable mathematical formulation. Compare VFSR with CSA in terms of convergence speed and robustness. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution B(a) — ASA (7 marks)

**Step 1: Motivation**

CSA uses **one global temperature** for all variables. This is wasteful when variables have different sensitivities — some variables over-cool while others remain hot.

**Step 2: Per-Variable Cooling in ASA**

For each variable $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, D\}$:

$$
T_k^{(i)} = T_0^{(i)} \cdot \exp\!\left(-c^{(i)} \cdot k^{1/D}\right)
$$

where:
- $T_0^{(i)}$ — initial temperature for variable $i$
- $c^{(i)}$ — learning-rate parameter
- $D$ — dimensionality of the search space

**Step 3: Per-Variable Sensitivity**

$$
\rho^{(i)} = \rho_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\beta^{(i)} \cdot k^{1/D}\right)
$$

These sensitivities define the **step size** of the perturbation in each dimension.

**Step 4: Acceptance**

Uses the Boltzmann factor with $T_k^{(i)}$ applied per variable, with combined acceptance:

$$
P_{accept} = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{D} \frac{\Delta E_i}{T_k^{(i)}}\right)
$$

**Engineering significance:** ASA achieves **dimensional consistency** — the cooling rate $1/D$ in the exponent ensures that high-dimensional problems receive proportionally more thermal energy.

---

### Model Solution B(b) — VFSR (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition**

VFSR (Ingber, 1989) extends ASA with **periodic re-annealing**: the temperature of each variable is selectively reheated when its sensitivity falls below a critical threshold.

**Step 2: Re-Annealing Criterion**

For each variable $i$, at the end of each annealing epoch:

$$
\rho_k^{(i)} = \rho_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\beta^{(i)} \cdot k^{1/D}\right)
$$

If $\rho_k^{(i)} < \rho_{threshold}$, then reset:

$$
T_k^{(i)} \leftarrow T_0^{(i)}, \quad \rho_k^{(i)} \leftarrow \rho_0
$$

This **restores exploratory capacity** in dimensions that have prematurely frozen.

**Step 3: Comparison with CSA**

| Aspect | CSA | VFSR |
|---|---|---|
| Temperatures | 1 global | $D$ per-variable |
| Re-annealing | None | Automatic, per-variable |
| Convergence speed | Slow | Fastest among classical variants |
| Robustness to dimensionality | Poor | Excellent |
| Computational overhead | Low | Moderate (per-variable book-keeping) |

**Engineering application:** VFSR is the **de facto choice** for training neural networks, optimising engineering designs with heterogeneous parameters, and hyperparameter search in machine learning pipelines.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Variants of SA Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cooling schedule:** Students often mention only the acceptance function. A complete answer MUST include *both* acceptance and cooling — omission costs **3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing FSA with Cauchy:** Some students write "Gaussian" for FSA. FSA uses **Cauchy-distributed neighbourhood**, not Gaussian.
> 3. **Mixing up ASA and VFSR:** ASA and VFSR differ only in **re-annealing**. VFSR = ASA + automatic re-annealing.
> 4. **Skipping the proof in comparative questions:** When asked "compare CSA and FSA," a derivation such as $P_{FSA} \geq P_{CSA}$ is expected for full marks.
> 5. **Not stating the engineering application:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasises **outcome-based learning (CO mapping)** — at least one real-world example earns **1–2 bonus marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Simulated Annealing** is a probabilistic metaheuristic inspired by **metallurgical annealing** (controlled heating → slow cooling).
- A **variant** modifies at least one of: **cooling schedule**, **acceptance function**, **neighbourhood rule**, or **re-annealing protocol**.
- **CSA** uses **Boltzmann acceptance** $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$ and **geometric cooling** $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$.
- **FSA** uses **Cauchy acceptance** $T / (T^2 + \Delta E^2)$ and **inverse cooling** $T_k = T_0 / k$.
- **ASA** introduces **per-variable temperatures** $T_k^{(i)} = T_0^{(i)} \exp(-c^{(i)} k^{1/D})$.
- **VFSR** = ASA + **automatic re-annealing** when $\rho_k^{(i)} < \rho_{threshold}$.
- **Threshold Accepting (TA)** uses **deterministic** acceptance $\Delta E \leq \tau_k$ (no randomness).
- **Quantum Annealing** uses **quantum tunneling** via adiabatic Hamiltonian evolution.
- **Initial temperature heuristic:** $T_0 = \overline{\Delta E} / \ln(1/P_0) \approx 4.48 \cdot \overline{\Delta E}$ for $P_0 = 0.8$.
- **Cauchy acceptance is provably larger than Boltzmann** at all $T > 0$, $\Delta E > 0$ — key for FSA's faster exploration.
- **Geometric cooling iteration bound:** $K \geq \ln(T_f / T_0) / \ln \alpha$.
- **Boltzmann (logarithmic) cooling** guarantees **asymptotic global convergence** but is computationally expensive $\mathcal{O}(K / \ln K)$.
- **Engineering uses:** VLSI partitioning (CSA), control tuning (FSA), neural-network training (ASA/VFSR), embedded scheduling (TA), combinatorial NP-hard (QA).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Heuristics? (Formal Definition)

A **heuristic** is a problem-solving technique that employs a practical, experience-based methodology to arrive at a sufficiently good solution within a reasonable time frame, but without any guarantee of optimality or completeness. In the context of combinatorial and numerical optimization, heuristics are rule-of-thumb strategies that navigate the search space intelligently by exploiting problem-specific knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Heuristic):**
> A heuristic is a method that **"sacrifices the guarantee of finding the optimal solution for the sake of speed and simplicity."** It trades optimality for tractability on **NP-hard** problems where exact algorithms are computationally infeasible.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are searching for your lost keys inside a dark, gigantic stadium. An **exact algorithm** would systematically check every single seat in every row, under every cushion, until the keys are found — this guarantees finding them but takes hours. A **heuristic** is your friend telling you: *"Check near the entrance, the food court, and the parking lot first — that's where people usually lose things."* You might miss the keys, but you find them 90% of the time in 5 minutes.

## 1.2 What are Metaheuristics?

A **metaheuristic** is a higher-level, problem-independent strategy that guides and modifies one or more subordinate heuristics to efficiently explore the search space, overcome the limitations of local optima, and produce high-quality solutions. The Greek prefix "**meta**" (μετά) means "**beyond**" or "**at a higher level**".

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Heuristic** → Problem-specific (tailored to a particular problem like TSP with triangle inequality).
> - **Metaheuristic** → Problem-independent (a generic framework applicable to almost any optimization problem).

## 1.3 The Motivation: Why Do We Need Them?

| Aspect | Exact Methods (Branch & Bound, Dynamic Programming) | Heuristics / Metaheuristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimality Guarantee** | ✓ Yes | ✗ No (near-optimal) |
| **Time Complexity** | Exponential / Polynomial high-degree | Polynomial (often near-linear) |
| **Problem Size** | Small-to-medium | Large-scale (NP-hard instances) |
| **Mathematical Model** | Requires complete formulation | Requires only a fitness/objective function |
| **Example** | Simplex Method for LP | Genetic Algorithm for Job-Shop Scheduling |

## 1.4 Fundamental Terminology in Metaheuristic Search

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Vocabulary (MUST MEMORISE for KTU ESE):**
> - **Solution Space (Search Space) $S$**: The set of all feasible candidate solutions.
> - **Objective Function $f(x)$**: The function to be minimized or maximized.
> - **Global Optimum $x^*$**: The best solution in the entire search space: $f(x^*) \leq f(x) \ \forall x \in S$.
> - **Local Optimum $\hat{x}$**: A solution that is better than all its immediate neighbors but not globally best.
> - **Neighborhood $N(x)$**: The set of solutions reachable from $x$ by a single move (mutation/perturbation).
> - **Iteration / Generation**: A single cycle of the algorithm producing a new candidate.
> - **Convergence**: The state when the algorithm stops improving the best-known solution.

## 1.5 Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **Logistics & Routing**: Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) for Amazon/FedEx delivery.
- **VLSI Chip Design**: Placement and routing of millions of transistors.
- **Telecommunications**: Antenna design, network topology optimization.
- **Bio-informatics**: Protein structure prediction (AlphaFold uses heuristic search).
- **Machine Learning**: Hyperparameter tuning, neural architecture search.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Heuristic Methods

A structured, hierarchical classification is essential for KTU 14-mark questions.

### A. Based on Solution Improvement Strategy

1. **Constructive Heuristics** — Build a solution from scratch (e.g., Nearest Neighbor for TSP, Greedy Coloring).
2. **Improvement (Local Search) Heuristics** — Start from an initial solution and iteratively improve it (e.g., 2-opt, k-opt).

### B. Based on Memory Usage

1. **Memoryless (Zero-order)** — Current state depends only on the immediate predecessor. Example: **Simulated Annealing**.
2. **Memory-based (Higher-order)** — Uses history of past states. Example: **Tabu Search** uses a short-term memory list.

### C. Metaheuristic Taxonomy (Most Important for KTU)

| Family | Core Idea | Example Algorithms |
|---|---|---|
| **Trajectory-based (Single-solution)** | Manipulates ONE solution at a time, traces a PATH in the search space | Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search, Hill Climbing, Iterated Local Search |
| **Population-based** | Evolves a SET (population) of solutions in parallel | Genetic Algorithm, Differential Evolution, Particle Swarm Optimization |
| **Swarm Intelligence** | Mimics collective behavior of decentralized agents | Ant Colony Optimization, Bee Colony, Firefly Algorithm |
| **Nature-inspired Physics** | Mimics physical laws | Gravitational Search, Harmony Search |
| **Evolutionary** | Mimics Darwinian natural selection | Genetic Algorithm, Evolution Strategies |

## 2.2 The Two Pillars: Exploration vs. Exploitation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important conceptual trade-off** in metaheuristics and is a guaranteed KTU 12-mark or 14-mark question.

- **Exploration (Diversification)**: Investigating *distant, unvisited* regions of the search space to discover new promising areas. High exploration → avoids premature convergence.
- **Exploitation (Intensification)**: Focusing the search *around the current best solution* to refine it locally. High exploitation → improves solution quality but risks local optima.

> **Real-world Analogy**: When choosing a restaurant, **exploitation** means going to your favorite place (safe, refined choice). **exploration** means trying a brand-new cuisine (risky, broad search). You need a balance.

## 2.3 The No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem (Wolpert & Macready, 1997)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Statement:** *"When averaged over all possible problems, every optimization algorithm performs equally well — no algorithm is universally superior."*

**Implication for Engineering Practice:** There is **no "best" metaheuristic** in absolute terms. An algorithm that excels on the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) may perform poorly on Job-Shop Scheduling. The practitioner must **match the algorithm to the problem structure**.

## 2.4 Components of a Metaheuristic Algorithm

Every metaheuristic, regardless of its inspiration, contains these five components:

1. **Encoding / Representation** — How a solution is mathematically represented (binary string, real-valued vector, permutation, tree).
2. **Initialization** — How the starting solution(s) are generated (random, heuristic-seeded, greedy).
3. **Variation Operator (Neighborhood / Move)** — How new candidate solutions are produced (mutation, crossover, perturbation).
4. **Selection / Acceptance Criterion** — Decides whether to move to the new solution (greedy acceptance, probabilistic acceptance like Metropolis criterion, tournament selection).
5. **Termination Condition** — When the algorithm stops (max iterations, CPU time, convergence threshold, target fitness value).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Search Space Cardinality | $\vert S \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{n} d_i$ | For $n$ variables each with $d_i$ discrete choices |
| Neighbourhood Size | $\vert N(x) \vert$ for $k$-opt = $\binom{n}{k}$ | Number of moves reachable in one step |
| Acceptance Probability (SA-style) | $P = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T}\right)$ | Boltzmann / Metropolis criterion |
| Time-to-Target | $TTT = \min\{t : f(x_t) \leq f_{target}\}$ | Probabilistic stopping metric |
| Convergence Rate | $R_c = 1 - \left\vert\frac{f_{best}^{(t+1)} - f_{opt}}{f_{best}^{(t)} - f_{opt}}\right\vert$ | Ratio of remaining optimality gap |
| Quality Ratio | $QR = \frac{f(x_{heuristic})}{f(x_{opt})}$ | $QR \to 1$ indicates near-optimality |
| Exploration Probability | $p_e = \frac{N_{explored}}{N_{total}}$ | Fraction of search space visited |
| NFL Theorem (informal) | $\sum_{f} P(h_m \mid f, n) = \sum_{f} P(h_n \mid f, n)$ | Average performance is identical for all algorithms $m, n$ |

> **Engineering Utility Note:** The **Quality Ratio $QR$** is the standard metric reported in research papers to compare metaheuristics. A $QR$ of **0.99** is considered excellent in industrial settings.

## 2.6 Key Design Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In 14-mark questions, the examiner checks if you can DEFEND your design choices using these principles.
> - **Principle 1 — Intensification over Diversification ratio** should adapt over time: more diversification early, more intensification later.
> - **Principle 2 — The neighbourhood structure should be problem-aware** to avoid disconnected regions of the search space.
> - **Principle 3 — Stochasticity prevents cycling** and allows escape from local optima (random restarts, probabilistic acceptance).
> - **Principle 4 — A good initial solution** (via constructive heuristic) can drastically reduce convergence time.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Metropolis Acceptance Criterion (Foundation of Simulated Annealing)

This is a **frequently asked derivation** in KTU exams. The criterion is rooted in **statistical mechanics** (Boltzmann distribution).

### Step 1 — Starting Point: Boltzmann Probability Distribution

In a thermodynamic system at thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$, the probability that the system is in a state $i$ with energy $E_i$ is given by the Boltzmann distribution:

$$P(E_i) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_i}{k_B T}\right)$$

where:
- $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant** $\approx 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$.
- $T$ is the **absolute temperature** in Kelvin.
- $Z = \sum_{j} \exp(-E_j / k_B T)$ is the **partition function** (normalization constant).

### Step 2 — Mapping Physics to Optimization

We establish an **analogy** between physical and optimization variables:

| Physical Quantity | Optimization Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Energy $E$ of a state | Objective function value $f(x)$ |
| Temperature $T$ | Control parameter $T$ (Cooling schedule) |
| Boltzmann constant $k_B$ | Absorbed into $T$ (set $k_B = 1$ for simplicity) |

Substituting $E \to f(x)$ and $k_B = 1$:

$$P(f_i) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{f_i}{T}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Probability Ratio Between Two States

The relative probability of being in state $j$ (new) versus state $i$ (current) is:

$$\frac{P(f_j)}{P(f_i)} = \frac{\exp(-f_j / T)}{\exp(-f_i / T)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{f_j - f_i}{T}\right)$$

Define the **energy change** as:

$$\Delta f = f_j - f_i$$

### Step 4 — Final Acceptance Probability

If the new state has **lower energy** (i.e., $\Delta f < 0$ for minimization), then the exponential is $> 1$, meaning we **always accept** such moves. If $\Delta f > 0$ (worse move), we accept with probability:

$$P_{accept} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T}\right)$$

This is the **Metropolis criterion** (Metropolis et al., 1953).

### Step 5 — Sanity Check: Effect of Temperature

$$T \to \infty: \quad P_{accept} \to \exp(0) = 1 \quad \text{(accept everything — high exploration)}$$

$$T \to 0^+: \quad P_{accept} \to 0 \text{ for } \Delta f > 0 \quad \text{(greedy — pure exploitation)}$$

This confirms the **exploration-exploitation trade-off** is mathematically governed by $T$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection Guide

| Problem Property | Recommended Metaheuristic | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Discrete, permutation-based (e.g., TSP) | **Tabu Search** or **GA with OX crossover** | Handles permutation constraints elegantly |
| Continuous, differentiable | **Particle Swarm / Differential Evolution** | Operates natively on real-valued vectors |
| Combinatorial, large search space | **Simulated Annealing** | Probabilistic acceptance escapes local optima |
| Multi-modal objective | **Genetic Algorithm with niching** | Population maintains diversity |
| Dynamic / time-varying | **Ant Colony Optimization** | Pheromone trails re-adapt quickly |
| Expensive objective evaluation | **Surrogate-assisted GA / Bayesian Optimization** | Reduces real evaluations |
| Black-box, no gradient info | **Any population-based method** | Requires only $f(x)$, not $\nabla f$ |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Computing the Metropolis Probability

> **Problem:** A Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) tour has current cost $f_{old} = 850$ km. A 2-opt move produces a new tour of cost $f_{new} = 880$ km. The current temperature is $T = 50$. Should the algorithm accept this **worsening** move?

### Step 1 — Compute the cost increase
$$\Delta f = f_{new} - f_{old} = 880 - 850 = 30$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the Metropolis formula
$$P_{accept} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{30}{50}\right) = \exp(-0.6)$$

### Step 3 — Numerical evaluation
$$P_{accept} = e^{-0.6} \approx 0.5488$$

### Step 4 — Decision
A uniform random number $r \in [0,1]$ is generated. If $r = 0.41$, since $0.41 < 0.5488$, the move is **accepted** even though it worsened the tour — this is the mechanism that allows escape from local optima.

> **Final Answer:** $P_{accept} \approx \mathbf{0.549}$, and a random draw of $r = 0.41$ would lead to **acceptance**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Geometric Cooling Schedule

The most widely used cooling schedule is **geometric decay**:
$$T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

After $k$ iterations starting from $T_0$:

$$T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$$

**Proof by induction:**

**Base case** ($k=0$): $T_0 = T_0 \cdot \alpha^0 = T_0$ ✓

**Inductive step**: Assume $T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$. Then:

$$T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k = \alpha \cdot (T_0 \cdot \alpha^k) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k+1} \quad \blacksquare$$

A typical value is $\alpha = 0.95$ to $0.99$, giving slow cooling that favors convergence to high-quality solutions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Classification of Heuristics

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Optimization Methods]:::root --> B1[Exact Methods]
    rootA --> B2[Approximate Methods]

    B1 --> C1[Linear Programming]
    B1 --> C2[Integer Programming]
    B1 --> C3[Dynamic Programming]
    B1 --> C4[Branch and Bound]

    B2 --> D1[Heuristics]
    B2 --> D2[Metaheuristics]

    D1 --> E1[Constructive]
    D1 --> E2[Improvement]

    D2 --> F1[Trajectory Based]
    D2 --> F2[Population Based]
    D2 --> F3[Swarm Intelligence]
    D2 --> F4[Physics Based]

    F1 --> G1[Simulated Annealing]
    F1 --> G2[Tabu Search]
    F1 --> G3[Hill Climbing]
    F1 --> G4[Iterated Local Search]

    F2 --> H1[Genetic Algorithm]
    F2 --> H2[Differential Evolution]
    F2 --> H3[Evolution Strategies]

    F3 --> I1[Ant Colony Optimization]
    F3 --> I2[Particle Swarm Optimization]
    F3 --> I3[Artificial Bee Colony]

    F4 --> J1[Gravitational Search Algo]
    F4 --> J2[Harmony Search]
    F4 --> J3[Simulated Annealing]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef default fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    class B1,B2,C1,C2,C3,C4,D1,D2,E1,E2,F1,F2,F3,F4,G1,G2,G3,G4,H1,H2,H3,I1,I2,I3,J1,J2,J3 default
```

## 4.2 Generic Metaheuristic Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Initialize Solution or Population]
    B --> C[Evaluate Objective Function f of x]
    C --> D{Iteration less than Max?}
    D -- No --> Z[Return Best Solution Found]
    D -- Yes --> E[Apply Variation Operator: Mutation or Crossover]
    E --> F[Generate New Candidate Solution x prime]
    F --> G[Evaluate f of x prime]
    G --> H[Apply Acceptance Criterion]
    H --> I{New Solution Accepted?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Update Current or Best Solution]
    I -- No --> K[Discard Move and Retain Current]
    J --> L[Update Internal Memory: Tabu List, Pheromones, Temperature]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Increment Iteration Counter]
    M --> D
    Z --> N[End]
```

## 4.3 Exploration vs. Exploitation Trade-off (Visual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start: Random Initialization]:::start --> B{Exploration Phase}
    B --> C[Search Broad Regions]
    B --> D[Discover New Promising Areas]
    B --> E[High Stochasticity]
    C --> F{Transition Point}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Exploitation Phase}
    G --> H[Refine Around Best Solutions]
    G --> I[Local Neighborhood Search]
    G --> J[Low Stochasticity]
    H --> K[Converged Solution]
    I --> K
    J --> K

    classDef start fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef default fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    class B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K default
```

## 4.4 Architecture of a Population-Based Metaheuristic (e.g., Genetic Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Initial Population of N Individuals] --> Eval[Evaluate Fitness of Each Individual]
    Eval --> Sel[Selection Operator: Tournament or Roulette Wheel]
    Sel --> Cross[Crossover Operator: Combine Pairs to Produce Offspring]
    Cross --> Mut[Mutation Operator: Random Perturbation of Offspring]
    Mut --> Repl[Replacement Strategy: Elitism or Generational]
    Repl --> Chk{Convergence Criterion Met?}
    Chk -- No --> Eval
    Chk -- Yes --> Out[Output Best Individual as Solution]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
