## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define an artificial neural network. List any two activation functions used in ANN architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

An Artificial Neural Network (ANN) is a computational model inspired by the structure and functioning of biological neurons in the human brain. It consists of interconnected processing elements called neurons, organized into layers, which process information through weighted connections.

Two commonly used activation functions are:

1. **Sigmoid function:** $\varphi(u) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-u}}$, output range $(0, 1)$ — used for binary classification.
2. **ReLU function:** $\varphi(u) = \max(0, u)$, output range $[0, \infty)$ — used in deep hidden layers for computational efficiency.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Differentiate between Single-Layer Perceptron (SLP) and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | SLP | MLP |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | Only input and output (no hidden layer) | One or more hidden layers |
| Problem type | Solves only linearly separable problems | Solves nonlinear problems |
| Activation | Often step function | Sigmoid, ReLU, tanh |
| Training | Perceptron rule | Backpropagation |
| Capability | Limited (XOR fails) | Universal approximator |

The MLP overcomes the primary limitation of the SLP — the inability to solve the XOR problem — through the use of hidden layers with nonlinear activations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the architecture of an artificial neuron in detail. Compare it with a biological neuron. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a neuron with inputs $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 0$, $x_3 = -1$, weights $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = -0.3$, $w_3 = 0.8$, and bias $b = 0.1$, compute the output using (i) Step function and (ii) Sigmoid activation. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — Artificial Neuron Architecture and Comparison:**

The artificial neuron (also called a **Perceptron** unit) consists of three blocks:

1. **Input block:** Accepts $n$ input signals $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.
2. **Weighting block:** Each input is multiplied by a synaptic weight $w_i$.
3. **Summation and activation block:** Sum is computed, bias added, and the result passes through an activation function $\varphi(\cdot)$.

The mathematical model is:
$$y_k = \varphi\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ki} x_i + b_k\right)$$

**Biological vs. Artificial Comparison:**

| Biological Neuron | Artificial Neuron |
|---|---|
| Dendrites | Inputs $x_i$ |
| Synapses (strength) | Weights $w_i$ |
| Cell body (soma) | Summation unit $\Sigma$ |
| Axon hillock (threshold) | Bias $b$ |
| Action potential (firing) | Activation function $\varphi$ |
| Axon | Output $y$ |
| Synaptic terminals | Output connections to next layer |

*(Diagram reference: see Section 4.1)*

---

**Solution (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1:** Compute the weighted sum.
$$u = (0.5)(1) + (-0.3)(0) + (0.8)(-1) + 0.1$$

**Step 2:** Expand.
$$u = 0.5 + 0 + (-0.8) + 0.1 = -0.2$$

**Step 3 (i):** Apply the Step (Heaviside) activation.
$$y = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } u \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } u < 0 \end{cases}$$

Since $u = -0.2 < 0$:
$$y_{\text{step}} = 0$$

**Step 3 (ii):** Apply the Sigmoid activation.
$$y = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(-0.2)}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.2}}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $e^{0.2} \approx 1.2214$.
$$y_{\text{sigmoid}} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.2214} = \frac{1}{2.2214} \approx 0.4502$$

**Final Answer:** Step output = **0**, Sigmoid output ≈ **0.4502**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) architecture with a suitable diagram. Discuss how it overcomes the limitations of a single-layer perceptron with reference to the XOR problem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the generalized delta rule for weight updation in a feedforward neural network using gradient descent on the squared error function. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — MLP Architecture and XOR Capability:**

An MLP contains:
- One **input layer** (passive, simply distributes inputs).
- One or more **hidden layers** (perform nonlinear feature extraction).
- One **output layer** (produces final prediction).

Each neuron in successive layers is fully connected to all neurons in the previous layer. Mathematically, for layer $l$ with activation $\varphi^{(l)}$:

$$\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = \varphi^{(l)}\!\left(W^{(l)} \, \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\right)$$

**XOR Problem Demonstration:**

The XOR function is **not linearly separable**. With a single-layer perceptron, no single straight line in input space can separate $\{0,1\}$ and $\{1,0\}$ from $\{0,0\}$ and $\{1,1\}$.

MLP with **one hidden layer** containing two neurons can solve XOR:

Hidden neuron $h_1$: implements one decision boundary.
Hidden neuron $h_2$: implements another decision boundary.
Output neuron: combines these linearly to produce the XOR surface.

This is the **Kolmogorov-Arnold representation** in essence — nonlinear combinations of linear functions can produce any nonlinear decision boundary.

*(Diagram reference: see Section 4.3)*

---

**Solution (b) — Derivation of the Generalized Delta Rule:**

**Step 1:** Define the total error for a single training sample as the sum of squared errors across all output neurons.
$$E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k \in \text{output}} (t_k - y_k)^2$$

**Step 2:** The gradient descent update rule for weight $w_{ki}$ (between neuron $i$ in layer $l-1$ and neuron $k$ in layer $l$) is:
$$\Delta w_{ki} = -\eta \, \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ki}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the chain rule.
$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ki}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial u_k} \cdot \frac{\partial u_k}{\partial w_{ki}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute each partial.

First term: $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} = -(t_k - y_k)$

Second term: $\dfrac{\partial y_k}{\partial u_k} = \varphi'(u_k)$

Third term: $\dfrac{\partial u_k}{\partial w_{ki}} = y_i^{(l-1)}$ (the output of neuron $i$ in the previous layer)

**Step 5:** Combine.
$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ki}} = -(t_k - y_k) \cdot \varphi'(u_k) \cdot y_i^{(l-1)}$$

**Step 6:** Define the local gradient $\delta_k$ for output neurons.
$$\delta_k = (t_k - y_k) \cdot \varphi'(u_k)$$

**Step 7:** Write the final update rule.
$$\Delta w_{ki} = \eta \cdot \delta_k \cdot y_i^{(l-1)}$$

**Step 8:** Equivalent compact form.
$$w_{ki}^{\text{new}} = w_{ki}^{\text{old}} + \eta \cdot \delta_k \cdot y_i^{(l-1)}$$

For hidden-layer neurons, $\delta_k$ is computed recursively:
$$\delta_i^{(l-1)} = \varphi'(u_i^{(l-1)}) \sum_{k} \delta_k^{(l)} w_{ki}^{(l)}$$

This recursive propagation of error backwards gives the algorithm its name: **Backpropagation**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\delta_k$ sign:** The weight update must be $+\eta \delta_k y_i$, not $-\eta \delta_k y_i$. The negative sign is absorbed into the $\delta$ definition. Many students lose **2 marks** here.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{2}$:** When deriving, never omit the $\frac{1}{2}$ in the error definition — its purpose is to cancel the derivative's leading coefficient, simplifying the final formula.
> 3. **Activation derivative confusion:** For sigmoid, students often write $\varphi'(u) = \varphi(u)(1-\varphi(u))$ — correct. But for tanh, the derivative is $1 - \tanh^2(u)$, not $\tanh(u)(1-\tanh(u))$. **Memorize both.**
> 4. **Architecture vs. Learning:** Examiners frequently test whether students can distinguish *architecture* (static structure) from *learning algorithm* (dynamic weight tuning). Mixing these two costs **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Hidden layer derivative index:** In BPTT-style derivations, students forget that the third term $\partial u_k / \partial w_{ki}$ involves the previous layer's *output* $y_i^{(l-1)}$, not the raw input. This is a classic **3-mark trap**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for ANN Architecture**

- **ANN definition:** Massively parallel distributed processor; 5-tuple $\{N, C, W, \Phi, \Sigma\}$.
- **Biological mapping:** Dendrites → inputs, Synapses → weights, Soma → summation, Axon hillock → bias, Action potential → activation.
- **Single neuron output equation:** $y = \varphi(\sum_i w_i x_i + b)$.
- **Sigmoid activation:** $\varphi(u) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-u}}$, output range $(0, 1)$.
- **Sigmoid derivative:** $\varphi'(u) = \varphi(u)(1-\varphi(u))$ — memorize this identity.
- **Tanh derivative:** $\varphi'(u) = 1 - \tanh^2(u)$.
- **ReLU:** $\varphi(u) = \max(0, u)$; derivatives 0 or 1.
- **SLP vs MLP:** SLP cannot solve XOR; MLP with hidden layers can.
- **Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko):** An MLP with **one hidden layer** and a sufficient number of neurons can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision.
- **Architecture types:** Feedforward (SLP, MLP, RBF), Recurrent (Hopfield, RNN), Competitive (SOM), Stochastic (Boltzmann).
- **Generalized Delta Rule:** $w_{ki}^{\text{new}} = w_{ki}^{\text{old}} + \eta \delta_k y_i^{(l-1)}$.
- **Local gradient for output:** $\delta_k = (t_k - y_k) \varphi'(u_k)$.
- **Local gradient for hidden:** $\delta_i^{(l-1)} = \varphi'(u_i^{(l-1)}) \sum_k \delta_k^{(l)} w_{ki}^{(l)}$.
- **Learning paradigms:** Supervised (target available), Unsupervised (no target), Reinforcement (reward signal).
- **Typical KTU problem:** Compute neuron output given inputs/weights/bias with a specified activation function — practice at least 3 such numerical problems.
- **Common values:** $\eta \in \{0.01, 0.1, 0.5\}$; momentum $\alpha \approx 0.9$.
- **Architecture vs. Learning:** Architecture = *structure* (layers, connectivity); Learning = *weight adjustment rule*. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Layer and Multi Layer Feed Forward Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single Layer and Multi Layer Feed Forward Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Feed Forward Neural Network?

A **Feed Forward Neural Network (FFNN)** is a class of Artificial Neural Network (ANN) in which information moves in only **one direction** — forward — from the input nodes, through any hidden nodes (if present), and finally to the output nodes. There are **no cycles or loops** in the network architecture. Each layer feeds only into the next layer, and the network contains no feedback connections.

These networks are the foundational architectures of **supervised heuristic learning systems**, where the network "learns" an approximate mapping from inputs to outputs by iteratively adjusting connection strengths (weights) using optimization techniques such as gradient descent.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> The module treats Single Layer and Multi Layer Feed Forward Networks as **function approximators** used in heuristic optimization. They form the computational backbone of soft-computing techniques used to solve problems that classical deterministic algorithms cannot solve in polynomial time.

---

### 1.2 Single Layer Feed Forward Network (SLFFN)

**Formal Definition:**
A *Single Layer Feed Forward Network* is the simplest form of an artificial neural network consisting of exactly **one output layer** of neurons that directly receives input from the source/input nodes. The term "single layer" is reserved for the **output layer only**; the input layer is not counted as a computational layer since it merely buffers the input signals.

The classical realization of SLFFN is the **Rosenblatt Perceptron** (1958) — a linear threshold unit that historically laid the foundation for modern neural learning theory.

**Mathematical Model of a Single Neuron:**

$$y = f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$$

where:
- $x_i$ = $i^{th}$ input feature
- $w_i$ = synaptic weight associated with input $x_i$
- $b$ = bias term (external threshold)
- $f(\cdot)$ = activation function
- $y$ = neuron output

---

### 1.3 Multi Layer Feed Forward Network (MLFFN)

**Formal Definition:**
A *Multi Layer Feed Forward Network* (also called **Multilayer Perceptron, MLP**) is an FFNN with **one or more hidden layers** between the input and output layers. Every neuron in one layer is connected to every neuron in the next layer (fully-connected / dense architecture), and signals propagate strictly forward through successive nonlinear transformations.

The presence of at least one hidden layer with a **nonlinear activation function** enables the MLP to learn **non-linearly separable** decision boundaries, overcoming the famous *XOR problem* that paralyzed the single-layer perceptron in the late 1960s.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: A Team Decision-Making Pipeline**
> Imagine a corporate hiring committee:
> - **Single Layer Network** is like a **single manager** looking at resumes (inputs) and making a hire/no-hire decision based on a simple weighted checklist. This manager can only separate "good" vs "bad" candidates along a **straight line** in feature space.
> - **Multi Layer Network** is like a **chain of committees**: a junior panel first filters applicants into clusters, a mid-level panel refines these clusters, and a final board makes the call. Each committee adds a layer of reasoning, allowing the team to draw **complex, curved boundaries** between candidates — capturing subtle, nonlinear patterns.
> - The **weights** are the importance each committee member gives to each criterion, and **learning** is the process of repeatedly tweaking these weights whenever the team makes a wrong call.

---

### 1.5 Key Architectural Components

| Component | Role | Common Values |
|-----------|------|---------------|
| Input Layer | Receives raw feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | $n$ = feature dimensionality |
| Weight Matrix $\mathbf{W}$ | Learns feature importance | Initialized small random |
| Bias $\mathbf{b}$ | Shifts the decision boundary | Often initialized to 0 |
| Summation Unit | Computes net input $u = \sum w_i x_i + b$ | Linear combiner |
| Activation $f(\cdot)$ | Introduces nonlinearity | Step, Sigmoid, ReLU, Tanh |
| Output Layer | Produces final prediction $\hat{y}$ | 1 neuron (regression/binary) or $k$ neurons (multiclass) |
| Hidden Layer(s) | Extracts hierarchical features | 1 to N layers |

---

### 1.6 Why Are These "Heuristic"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These networks are termed **heuristic solution techniques** because they do not guarantee an exact, provably optimal solution in finite steps (like the simplex method does for LP). Instead, they iteratively converge toward a *good enough* solution by:
> 1. Sampling the loss landscape using gradient-based local search.
> 2. Adjusting weights via stochastic/random perturbations.
> 3. Using universal approximation principles to fit complex mappings.
>
> This makes them ideal for **NP-hard, noisy, or ill-defined problems** such as pattern recognition, forecasting, and combinatorial optimization — where deterministic algorithms fail or are too slow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Single Layer Feed Forward Network

The Single Layer FFNN (Perceptron) can be visualized as a bipartite graph:

- **Input layer:** $n$ source nodes $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$
- **Output layer:** $m$ perceptron neurons (typically $m=1$ for binary classification)
- **Connections:** Each input is connected to each output via a weight; there are no hidden units.

**Net Input (Induced Local Field):**

$$u_k = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{kj} x_j + b_k$$

**Output (after activation):**

$$y_k = f(u_k)$$

For a single-output perceptron, the **Hard-Limit Activation** is:

$$y = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } u \geq 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } u < 0 \end{cases}$$

---

### 2.2 Perceptron Learning Rule (Single Layer)

**Objective:** Adjust weights so that the perceptron correctly classifies every training sample.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Initialize weights $\mathbf{w}(0)$ to small random values (or zeros) and set learning rate $\eta \in (0, 1]$.
2. For each training pair $(\mathbf{x}^{(p)}, d^{(p)})$:
   - Compute the actual output $y^{(p)} = \text{sgn}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(p)})$.
   - Compute the error $e^{(p)} = d^{(p)} - y^{(p)}$.
   - Update weights:

$$\mathbf{w}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}(t) + \eta \, e^{(p)} \, \mathbf{x}^{(p)}$$

3. Iterate over all training samples (an **epoch**) until convergence or maximum epochs reached.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ represents the normal to a decision hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} = 0$. The learning rule rotates and translates this hyperplane until it separates the two classes correctly.

---

### 2.3 Convergence Theorem (Rosenblatt, 1958; Novikoff, 1962)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perceptron Convergence Theorem:** If the training data is **linearly separable**, the perceptron learning algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a separating hyperplane in a **finite number of steps** $t^{\ast} \leq \left(\frac{R}{\gamma}\right)^{2}$, where $R = \max_p \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(p)} \Vert$ is the maximum norm of input vectors and $\gamma$ is the geometric margin.

**Key Limitation:** Minsky & Papert (1969) proved the **XOR function is not linearly separable** and hence cannot be learned by a single-layer perceptron. This triggered the "AI winter" and motivated multi-layer networks.

---

### 2.4 Multi Layer Feed Forward Network — Architecture

Consider an MLP with $L$ layers. Layer $\ell$ has $n_\ell$ neurons. The forward pass is:

$$\mathbf{u}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$$

$$\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)} = f^{(\ell)}(\mathbf{u}^{(\ell)})$$

where $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$ is the input and $\mathbf{a}^{(L)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is the network output.

**Layer-wise notation:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_\ell \times n_{\ell-1}}$ | Weight matrix of layer $\ell$ |
| $\mathbf{b}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_\ell}$ | Bias vector of layer $\ell$ |
| $f^{(\ell)}$ | Activation function of layer $\ell$ |
| $\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)}$ | Activation (output) of layer $\ell$ |

---

### 2.5 Backpropagation Learning (Multi Layer)

The MLP is trained using the **Backpropagation Algorithm** (Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams, 1986), which efficiently computes gradients of a scalar loss $E$ w.r.t. every weight in the network using the chain rule.

**Loss Function (Mean Squared Error):**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{m} (d_k - \hat{y}_k)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{d} - \hat{\mathbf{y}} \Vert^2$$

**Output-Layer Gradient (using $\delta$-rule with sigmoid output):**

$$\delta_k^{(L)} = (d_k - \hat{y}_k) \, f'(u_k^{(L)})$$

**Hidden-Layer Gradient (error backpropagation):**

$$\delta_j^{(\ell)} = \left( \sum_{k} w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)} \delta_k^{(\ell+1)} \right) f'(u_j^{(\ell)})$$

**Weight Update (Gradient Descent):**

$$w_{ij}^{(\ell)}(t+1) = w_{ij}^{(\ell)}(t) - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(\ell)}} = w_{ij}^{(\ell)}(t) + \eta \, \delta_j^{(\ell)} \, a_i^{(\ell-1)}$$

---

### 2.6 Activation Functions — KTU Cheat Sheet

| Function | Formula | Derivative | Range | Use Case |
|----------|---------|------------|-------|----------|
| **Step (Heaviside)** | $f(u) = \begin{cases} 1, u \geq 0 \\ 0, u < 0 \end{cases}$ | Undefined at 0 | $\{0,1\}$ | Classical perceptron (non-differentiable) |
| **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $f(u) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-u}}$ | $f'(u) = f(u)[1-f(u)]$ | $(0,1)$ | Output layer for binary classification |
| **Tanh** | $f(u) = \frac{e^{u}-e^{-u}}{e^{u}+e^{-u}}$ | $f'(u) = 1 - \tanh^2(u)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Hidden layers; zero-centered |
| **ReLU** | $f(u) = \max(0, u)$ | $f'(u) = \begin{cases} 1, u > 0 \\ 0, u \leq 0 \end{cases}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Default for deep networks |
| **Softmax** | $f(u_i) = \frac{e^{u_i}}{\sum_j e^{u_j}}$ | See KTU notes | $(0,1)$, sums to 1 | Multiclass output layer |

> [!TIP]
> **Softmax formula in determinant-free form:** $f(u_i) = \frac{e^{u_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{u_j}}$

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | $u = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$ | Net input to a neuron |
| 2 | $y = f(u)$ | Output of a neuron |
| 3 | $y = \text{sgn}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x})$ | Hard-limit perceptron output |
| 4 | $\mathbf{w}_{new} = \mathbf{w}_{old} + \eta \, e \, \mathbf{x}$ | Perceptron weight update |
| 5 | $e^{(p)} = d^{(p)} - y^{(p)}$ | Error signal at output |
| 6 | $E = \frac{1}{2} \sum (d_k - \hat{y}_k)^2$ | MSE loss for MLFFN |
| 7 | $\delta^{(L)} = (\mathbf{d} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}) \odot f'(\mathbf{u}^{(L)})$ | Output-layer sensitivity |
| 8 | $\delta^{(\ell)} = ((\mathbf{W}^{(\ell+1)})^T \delta^{(\ell+1)}) \odot f'(\mathbf{u}^{(\ell)})$ | Hidden-layer sensitivity |
| 9 | $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}}$ | Generic weight update |
| 10 | $t^{\ast} \leq (R/\gamma)^2$ | Perceptron max iterations (Novikoff bound) |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $\odot$ denotes **element-wise (Hadamard) product** — used in vectorized backprop.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where MLFFNs Are Used in Practice (KTU Examiner's Favourite Examples):**
> 1. **Civil Engineering:** Structural health monitoring, concrete strength prediction from mix design inputs.
> 2. **Electrical Engineering:** Load forecasting, fault classification in power systems, motor control.
> 3. **Mechanical Engineering:** Predictive maintenance, vibration-based defect detection, CNC parameter optimization.
> 4. **Computer Science:** Handwritten digit recognition (MNIST), spam filtering, medical imaging.
> 5. **Bioinformatics:** Protein secondary structure prediction, drug response modeling.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Perceptron Learning Rule

**Starting Point:** We want to minimize the perceptron criterion (loss) function:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = -\sum_{p \,\in\, \mathcal{M}} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(p)} d^{(p)}$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is the set of **misclassified** patterns.

> The minus sign arises because for misclassified points, $d^{(p)} (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(p)}) < 0$.

**Step 1:** Apply gradient descent to minimize $J(\mathbf{w})$:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w} = -\eta \, \nabla J(\mathbf{w})$$

**Step 2:** Compute the gradient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla J(\mathbf{w}) &= \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \left[ -\sum_{p \in \mathcal{M}} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(p)} d^{(p)} \right] \\
&= -\sum_{p \in \mathcal{M}} \mathbf{x}^{(p)} d^{(p)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the weight update rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}(t+1) &= \mathbf{w}(t) - \eta \nabla J(\mathbf{w}) \\
&= \mathbf{w}(t) - \eta \left( -\sum_{p \in \mathcal{M}} \mathbf{x}^{(p)} d^{(p)} \right) \\
&= \mathbf{w}(t) + \eta \sum_{p \in \mathcal{M}} d^{(p)} \mathbf{x}^{(p)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** For a single misclassified sample presented in online (sample-by-sample) mode, $e^{(p)} = d^{(p)} - y^{(p)}$. Since for misclassified samples $d^{(p)} = -y^{(p)}$, we have $d^{(p)} = e^{(p)}/2$ which (absorbing the factor of 2 into $\eta$) reduces to the canonical form:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}(t) + \eta \, e^{(p)} \, \mathbf{x}^{(p)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** If the sample is **correctly classified**, $e^{(p)} = 0$ and weights are unchanged. If misclassified, the rule pushes the weight vector in the direction that would correct the error.

---

### 3.2 Backpropagation — Chain Rule Derivation

**Goal:** Compute $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(\ell)}}$ efficiently for all weights in a multi-layer network.

**Step 1:** Express error as a function of the net input of the output layer:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(L)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial \hat{y}_j} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial u_j^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_j^{(L)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(L)}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute individual partial derivatives:

- $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial \hat{y}_j} = - (d_j - \hat{y}_j)$
- $\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial u_j^{(L)}} = f'(u_j^{(L)})$
- $\dfrac{\partial u_j^{(L)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(L)}} = a_i^{(L-1)}$

**Step 3:** Combine to get the output-layer gradient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(L)}} &= -(d_j - \hat{y}_j) \, f'(u_j^{(L)}) \, a_i^{(L-1)} \\
&= -\delta_j^{(L)} \, a_i^{(L-1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where we define the **local gradient** (sensitivity):

$$\delta_j^{(L)} = (d_j - \hat{y}_j) \, f'(u_j^{(L)})$$

**Step 4:** For a hidden layer $\ell < L$, use the chain rule recursively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_j^{(\ell)} &= -\frac{\partial E}{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}} \\
&= -\sum_{k} \frac{\partial E}{\partial u_k^{(\ell+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_k^{(\ell+1)}}{\partial a_j^{(\ell)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_j^{(\ell)}}{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}} \\
&= \left( \sum_{k} w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)} \delta_k^{(\ell+1)} \right) f'(u_j^{(\ell)})
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **error backpropagation** step — the local gradient at any hidden layer is the weighted sum of downstream gradients, multiplied by the derivative of the activation.

**Step 5:** Generic weight update:

$$\boxed{w_{ij}^{(\ell)}(t+1) = w_{ij}^{(\ell)}(t) + \eta \, \delta_j^{(\ell)} \, a_i^{(\ell-1)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1: Perceptron Learning the AND Gate

**Task:** Train a single-layer perceptron to learn the AND logic function using $\eta = 1$.

**Training Set (bias augmented):**

| $p$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $x_3=1$ (bias) | $d$ |
|-----|-------|-------|----------------|-----|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

**Initialize:** $\mathbf{w} = (0.2,\ 0.3,\ 0.1)^T$ and $f(u) = \begin{cases}1, & u \geq 0 \\ 0, & u < 0\end{cases}$

**Epoch 1, Sample 1: $(0, 0, 1)$, $d=0$**

- $u = (0.2)(0) + (0.3)(0) + (0.1)(1) = 0.1 \geq 0 \Rightarrow y = 1$
- $e = d - y = 0 - 1 = -1$
- $\mathbf{w}_{new} = \mathbf{w} + \eta e \mathbf{x} = (0.2, 0.3, 0.1) + (1)(-1)(0, 0, 1) = (0.2,\ 0.3,\ 0.0)$

**Epoch 1, Sample 2: $(0, 1, 1)$, $d=0$**

- $u = (0.2)(0) + (0.3)(1) + (0.0)(1) = 0.3 \geq 0 \Rightarrow y = 1$
- $e = -1$
- $\mathbf{w}_{new} = (0.2, 0.3, 0.0) + (-1)(0, 1, 1) = (0.2,\ 0.2,\ -0.1)$

**Epoch 1, Sample 3: $(1, 0, 1)$, $d=0$**

- $u = (0.2)(1) + (0.2)(0) + (-0.1)(1) = 0.1 \geq 0 \Rightarrow y = 1$
- $e = -1$
- $\mathbf{w}_{new} = (0.2, 0.2, -0.1) + (-1)(1, 0, 1) = (-0.8,\ 0.2,\ -1.1)$

**Epoch 1, Sample 4: $(1, 1, 1)$, $d=1$**

- $u = (-0.8)(1) + (0.2)(1) + (-1.1)(1) = -1.7 < 0 \Rightarrow y = 0$
- $e = 1 - 0 = +1$
- $\mathbf{w}_{new} = (-0.8, 0.2, -1.1) + (1)(1, 1, 1) = (0.2,\ 1.2,\ -0.1)$

Continuing in this manner, the algorithm converges in a few epochs to a separating hyperplane such as $\mathbf{w} = (0.5,\ 0.5,\ -0.7)$ which classifies AND correctly.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2: Forward Pass Through a 2-Layer MLP

**Network:** 2 inputs → 3 hidden neurons (sigmoid) → 1 output neuron (sigmoid).

**Given Weights (with biases absorbed into extra inputs of value 1):**

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & -0.7 \\ 0.8 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & -0.4 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b^{(2)} = 0.05
$$

**Input:** $\mathbf{x} = (1.0,\ 0.5)^T$

**Step 1: Hidden Layer Net Input**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{u}^{(1)} &= \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} (0.5)(1.0) + (0.4)(0.5) \\ (0.3)(1.0) + (-0.7)(0.5) \\ (0.8)(1.0) + (0.1)(0.5) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 + 0.1 \\ -0.05 + 0.2 \\ 0.85 - 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0.15 \\ 0.75 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Hidden Activations (sigmoid $\sigma(u) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-u}}$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1^{(1)} &= \sigma(0.8) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.8}} = \frac{1}{1+0.449} \approx 0.690 \\
a_2^{(1)} &= \sigma(0.15) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.15}} = \frac{1}{1+0.861} \approx 0.537 \\
a_3^{(1)} &= \sigma(0.75) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.75}} = \frac{1}{1+0.472} \approx 0.679
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Output Layer Net Input**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u^{(2)} &= \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + b^{(2)} \\
&= (0.6)(0.690) + (-0.4)(0.537) + (0.5)(0.679) + 0.05 \\
&= 0.414 - 0.215 + 0.340 + 0.050 \\
&= 0.589
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Output Activation**

$$
\hat{y} = \sigma(0.589) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.589}} = \frac{1}{1+0.555} \approx 0.643
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Network Output:** $\hat{y} \approx 0.643$ (e.g., 64.3% probability of the positive class in a binary classifier).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison

| Property | Single Layer FFNN | Multi Layer FFNN |
|----------|------------------|------------------|
| Hidden layers | 0 | $\geq 1$ |
| Activation | Step / Linear | Sigmoid / ReLU / Tanh |
| Decision boundary | Linear (hyperplane) | Nonlinear (complex curve) |
| Solves XOR? | No (Minsky-Papert 1969) | Yes (with hidden layer) |
| Learning algorithm | Perceptron rule / LMS | Backpropagation |
| Universal approximator? | No | Yes (Cybenko/Hornik, 1989) |
| Computational cost | $O(n)$ | $O(\sum_\ell n_\ell n_{\ell-1})$ |
| Use case | Linearly separable problems | Complex pattern recognition |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single Layer Feed Forward Network (Perceptron)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1[INPUT X1] --> n1[WEIGHT W1]
    x2[INPUT X2] --> n2[WEIGHT W2]
    x3[INPUT X3] --> n3[WEIGHT W3]
    xn[INPUT XN] --> nn[WEIGHT WN]
    b1[BIAS B] --> sumNode
    n1 --> sumNode
    n2 --> sumNode
    n3 --> sumNode
    nn --> sumNode
    sumNode[SUMMATION UNIT] --> actNode[ACTIVATION F]
    actNode --> outNode[OUTPUT Y]
```

**Explanation:** All inputs $x_i$ are multiplied by weights $w_i$ and summed with a bias $b$. The sum passes through a hard-limit or linear activation $f(\cdot)$ to produce a single output $y$. No hidden processing exists.

---

### 4.2 Multi Layer Feed Forward Network (MLP Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputLayer[INPUT LAYER]
        i1[NODE I1]
        i2[NODE I2]
        i3[NODE I3]
    end
    subgraph hiddenLayer1[HIDDEN LAYER 1]
        h1a[NODE H1A]
        h1b[NODE H1B]
        h1c[NODE H1C]
    end
    subgraph hiddenLayer2[HIDDEN LAYER 2]
        h2a[NODE H2A]
        h2b[NODE H2B]
    end
    subgraph outputLayer[OUTPUT LAYER]
        o1[NODE O1]
        o2[NODE O2]
    end
    i1 --> h1a
    i1 --> h1b
    i1 --> h1c
    i2 --> h1a
    i2 --> h1b
    i2 --> h1c
    i3 --> h1a
    i3 --> h1b
    i3 --> h1c
    h1a --> h2a
    h1a --> h2b
    h1b --> h2a
    h1b --> h2b
    h1c --> h2a
    h1c --> h2b
    h2a --> o1
    h2a --> o2
    h2b --> o1
    h2b --> o2
```

**Explanation:** The input layer has 3 nodes, hidden layer 1 has 3 neurons, hidden layer 2 has 2 neurons, and the output layer has 2 neurons. Every neuron in a layer is connected to every neuron in the next layer. Signals flow strictly left-to-right.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Forward Pass

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT VECTOR X] --> B[WEIGHTED SUMMATION WX PLUS B]
    B --> C[ACTIVATION FUNCTION F]
    C --> D[ACTIVATION OUTPUT A]
    D --> E{IS THIS OUTPUT LAYER}
    E -- NO --> F[NEXT LAYER INPUT]
    F --> B
    E -- YES --> G[FINAL OUTPUT Y HAT]
    G --> H[LOSS FUNCTION E]
    H --> I[BACKPROP GRADIENTS]
    I --> J[WEIGHT UPDATE W NEW]
    J --> B
```

**Explanation:** During training, the forward pass produces a prediction $\hat{y}$, the loss $E$ is computed, and backpropagation propagates the error gradient backward to update weights iteratively.

---

### 4.4 Information Flow Summary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TRAINING DATA] --> B[FORWARD PROPAGATION]
    B --> C[COMPUTE LOSS E]
    C --> D[BACKPROPAGATION]
    D --> E[UPDATE WEIGHTS]
    E --> F[CONVERGED MODEL]
    F --> G[INFERENCE / PREDICTION]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Note:** The diagram above represents the **complete learning cycle** of an MLFFN. The examiner may ask you to label each block. Memorize: *Forward → Loss → Backward → Update → Iterate → Predict*.

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Set A)]

> **Differentiate between Single Layer and Multi Layer Feed Forward Networks with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single Layer FFNN | Multi Layer FFNN |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| Hidden layers | None | One or more |
| Architecture | Input → Output | Input → Hidden(s) → Output |
| Decision boundary | Linear hyperplane | Nonlinear, complex |
| Solves XOR? | No | Yes |
| Example | Rosenblatt perceptron | Backpropagation MLP |
| Learning rule | Perceptron rule / LMS | Backpropagation |
| Limitation | Cannot handle non-linearly separable data | Computationally expensive, prone to overfitting |

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper Set B)]

> **List and briefly explain any three activation functions used in feed forward neural networks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — write any three):**

1. **Sigmoid (Logistic):** $f(u) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-u}}$ — squashes input to $(0,1)$; smooth and differentiable; used in output layer for binary classification. Suffers from vanishing gradients at extremes.
2. **ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit):** $f(u) = \max(0, u)$ — outputs 0 for negative inputs and the input itself for positives; computationally efficient; default choice for hidden layers in deep networks. Avoids vanishing gradient in positive region.
3. **Tanh (Hyperbolic Tangent):** $f(u) = \frac{e^{u}-e^{-u}}{e^{u}+e^{-u}}$ — squashes input to $(-1, 1)$; zero-centered, generally preferred over sigmoid in hidden layers.
4. **Softmax:** $f(u_i) = \frac{e^{u_i}}{\sum_j e^{u_j}}$ — generalization of sigmoid; converts a vector of scores into a probability distribution for multiclass classification in the output layer.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Anticipated Pattern)]

> **(a)** Derive the **Perceptron Convergence Theorem** and explain its significance. State its key assumption.
>
> **(b)** Train a single-layer perceptron to learn the **OR gate** using the perceptron learning rule. Use $\eta = 1$ and initial weight vector $\mathbf{w} = (0.1, 0.2, 0.0)^T$ (with bias as $x_3 = 1$). Show at least **two complete epochs** and verify convergence.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Perceptron Convergence Theorem (7 marks):**

**Statement:** If the training set $\{\mathbf{x}^{(p)}, d^{(p)}\}$ is **linearly separable** and inputs are bounded ($\Vert \mathbf{x}^{(p)} \Vert \leq R$ for all $p$), then the perceptron learning algorithm converges in a finite number of steps $t^{\ast} \leq (R/\gamma)^2$, where $\gamma > 0$ is the margin.

**Proof Sketch:**

Define a target weight vector $\mathbf{w}^{\ast}$ that perfectly separates the data, normalized so $\Vert \mathbf{w}^{\ast} \Vert = 1$ and $d^{(p)} \mathbf{w}^{\ast T} \mathbf{x}^{(p)} \geq \gamma$ for all $p$.

**Step 1 — Lower bound on inner product growth:**

For each misclassified update $\mathbf{w}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}(t) + \eta d^{(p)} \mathbf{x}^{(p)}$ (using $\eta = 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}^{\ast T} \mathbf{w}(t+1) &= \mathbf{w}^{\ast T} \mathbf{w}(t) + d^{(p)} \mathbf{w}^{\ast T} \mathbf{x}^{(p)} \\
&\geq \mathbf{w}^{\ast T} \mathbf{w}(t) + \gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

By induction after $t$ misclassifications:

$$\mathbf{w}^{\ast T} \mathbf{w}(t) \geq t\gamma$$

**Step 2 — Upper bound on weight norm growth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert \mathbf{w}(t+1) \Vert^2 &= \Vert \mathbf{w}(t) \Vert^2 + 2 d^{(p)} \mathbf{w}(t)^T \mathbf{x}^{(p)} + \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(p)} \Vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the sample is misclassified, $d^{(p)} \mathbf{w}(t)^T \mathbf{x}^{(p)} \leq 0$:

$$\Vert \mathbf{w}(t+1) \Vert^2 \leq \Vert \mathbf{w}(t) \Vert^2 + R^2$$

By induction: $\Vert \mathbf{w}(t) \Vert^2 \leq t R^2$.

**Step 3 — Combine bounds using Cauchy-Schwarz:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t\gamma \leq \mathbf{w}^{\ast T} \mathbf{w}(t) &\leq \Vert \mathbf{w}^{\ast} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w}(t) \Vert = \Vert \mathbf{w}(t) \Vert \\
&\leq \sqrt{t} \, R
\end{aligned}
$$

Squaring: $t^2 \gamma^2 \leq t R^2 \Rightarrow t \leq (R/\gamma)^2$.

**Significance:** Guarantees finite-step convergence for linearly separable data, but provides **no bound** for non-separable data — in that case weights diverge or oscillate.

---

**(b) OR Gate Learning — Perceptron (7 marks):**

**Training set with bias $x_3=1$:**

| $p$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $x_3$ | $d$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-----|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

**Initial weights:** $\mathbf{w}(0) = (0.1, 0.2, 0.0)$, $\eta = 1$, $f(u) = \begin{cases}1, & u \geq 0 \\ 0, & u < 0\end{cases}$

**Epoch 1:**

- **Sample 1:** $u = 0.0 \Rightarrow y=1$, $e = -1 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = (0.1, 0.2, -1.0)$
- **Sample 2:** $u = (0.1)(0) + (0.2)(1) + (-1.0)(1) = -0.8 \Rightarrow y=0$, $e = 1 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = (0.1, 1.2, 0.0)$
- **Sample 3:** $u = (0.1)(1) + (1.2)(0) + (0.0)(1) = 0.1 \Rightarrow y=1$, $e = 0$ → no change
- **Sample 4:** $u = (0.1)(1) + (1.2)(1) + (0.0)(1) = 1.3 \Rightarrow y=1$, $e = 0$ → no change

**Epoch 2:**

- **Sample 1:** $u = (0.1)(0) + (1.2)(0) + (0.0)(1) = 0 \Rightarrow y=1$ (boundary), treat as $y=1$, $e = -1 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = (0.1, 1.2, -1.0)$
- **Sample 2:** $u = (0.1)(0) + (1.2)(1) + (-1.0)(1) = 0.2 \Rightarrow y=1$, $e = 0$ ✓
- **Sample 3:** $u = (0.1)(1) + (1.2)(0) + (-1.0)(1) = -0.9 \Rightarrow y=0$, $e = 1 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = (1.1, 1.2, 0.0)$
- **Sample 4:** $u = (1.1)(1) + (1.2)(1) + (0.0)(1) = 2.3 \Rightarrow y=1$, $e = 0$ ✓

After a few epochs, weights converge to a valid separating hyperplane, e.g., $\mathbf{w} \approx (1.5, 1.5, -0.5)$ which implements $y = 1$ iff $1.5 x_1 + 1.5 x_2 - 0.5 \geq 0$, i.e., $x_1 + x_2 \geq 1/3$ — exactly the OR boundary.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Anticipated Pattern)]

> **(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the structure of a **Multi Layer Feed Forward Network**. Derive the **backpropagation update rule** for hidden layer weights using the chain rule.
>
> **(b)** For the 2-2-1 MLP shown below, perform a **single forward pass** for input $(x_1, x_2) = (0.6, 0.9)$ using sigmoid activations. Given:
> - Hidden layer weights: $w_{11}^{(1)}=0.4$, $w_{12}^{(1)}=0.5$, $w_{21}^{(1)}=-0.3$, $w_{22}^{(1)}=0.8$, $b_1^{(1)}=0.1$, $b_2^{(1)}=-0.2$
> - Output layer weights: $w_{11}^{(2)}=0.7$, $w_{12}^{(2)}=-0.4$, $b_1^{(2)}=0.05$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MLP Architecture & Backprop Derivation (7 marks):**

*(Architecture: Refer to Section 4.2 diagram.)*

**Derivation outline:**

For an MLP with $L$ layers, define the squared error at the output:
$$E = \tfrac{1}{2}(d - \hat{y})^2$$

For a hidden-layer weight $w_{ij}^{(\ell)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(\ell)}} &= \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j^{(\ell)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_j^{(\ell)}}{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(\ell)}} \\
&= \underbrace{\left( \sum_{k} w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)} \delta_k^{(\ell+1)} \right)}_{\text{backpropagated error}} \cdot f'(u_j^{(\ell)}) \cdot a_i^{(\ell-1)} \\
&= \delta_j^{(\ell)} \cdot a_i^{(\ell-1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where the **hidden-layer local gradient** is:

$$\delta_j^{(\ell)} = \left( \sum_{k} w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)} \delta_k^{(\ell+1)} \right) f'(u_j^{(\ell)})$$

**Update rule:**

$$\boxed{w_{ij}^{(\ell)}(t+1) = w_{ij}^{(\ell)}(t) - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(\ell)}} = w_{ij}^{(\ell)}(t) + \eta \, \delta_j^{(\ell)} \, a_i^{(\ell-1)}}$$

For sigmoid: $f'(u) = f(u)[1-f(u)] = a(1-a)$, simplifying computation.

---

**(b) Numerical Forward Pass (7 marks):**

**Hidden Layer Net Inputs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1^{(1)} &= w_{11}^{(1)} x_1 + w_{12}^{(1)} x_2 + b_1^{(1)} = (0.4)(0.6) + (0.5)(0.9) + 0.1 \\
&= 0.24 + 0.45 + 0.1 = 0.79
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_2^{(1)} &= w_{21}^{(1)} x_1 + w_{22}^{(1)} x_2 + b_2^{(1)} = (-0.3)(0.6) + (0.8)(0.9) + (-0.2) \\
&= -0.18 + 0.72 - 0.2 = 0.34
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hidden Activations (sigmoid):**

$$
a_1^{(1)} = \sigma(0.79) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.79}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.4538} \approx 0.6879
$$

$$
a_2^{(1)} = \sigma(0.34) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.34}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.7118} \approx 0.5841
$$

**Output Layer Net Input:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1^{(2)} &= w_{11}^{(2)} a_1^{(1)} + w_{12}^{(2)} a_2^{(1)} + b_1^{(2)} \\
&= (0.7)(0.6879) + (-0.4)(0.5841) + 0.05 \\
&= 0.4815 - 0.2336 + 0.05 = 0.2979
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output Activation:**

$$
\hat{y} = \sigma(0.2979) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.2979}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.7424} \approx 0.5739
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Prediction:** $\hat{y} \approx 0.574$ (57.4% confidence of the positive class). If the true label $d = 1$, the error is $E = 0.5(1 - 0.574)^2 \approx 0.0908$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the bias:** Always treat bias as an extra input $x_{n+1} = 1$ with its own weight. Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark if bias is omitted.
> 2. **Confusing activation derivatives:** Many students write $f'(u) = f(u)$ for sigmoid. The correct derivative is $f'(u) = f(u)[1 - f(u)]$.
> 3. **Sign error in delta rule:** The error must be $(d - y)$, not $(y - d)$. Wrong sign leads to divergence, not convergence.
> 4. **Skipping epochs in perceptron questions:** Examiners want at least **2 full epochs** with **all 4 samples** shown. Incomplete iterations = −2 marks.
> 5. **Conflating "single layer" terminology:** KTU follows Haykin's convention — input layer is **not** counted. Always state the number of computational (output/hidden) layers.
> 6. **Not stating the linear separability assumption:** When applying the perceptron rule, you **must** explicitly mention the assumption; otherwise the convergence argument is invalid.
> 7. **Treating perceptron and ADALINE as the same:** ADALINE uses the **LMS (Delta) rule** with linear activation; perceptron uses the **hard-limit rule**. Examiners distinguish them.
> 8. **Wrong dimensionality in backprop:** The sensitivity $\delta^{(\ell)}$ is a column vector of size $n_\ell$, and the weight gradient requires outer product. Vectors of mismatched shape = −1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **Single Layer FFNN** has only an output layer of computational neurons; the input is source-only. It can solve **only linearly separable** problems.
- [x] A **Multi Layer FFNN (MLP)** has one or more hidden layers with nonlinear activations; it is a **universal approximator** (Cybenko 1989).
- [x] The **Perceptron Learning Rule** updates weights only on misclassification: $\mathbf{w}_{new} = \mathbf{w} + \eta e \mathbf{x}$.
- [x] The **LMS (Delta) rule** used in ADALINE updates weights on every pattern using the linear output error.
- [x] **Backpropagation** is just the **chain rule applied to a multi-layer composition of functions** — it propagates local gradients $\delta^{(\ell)}$ from output to input.
- [x] **Vanishing gradient problem**: Sigmoid/tanh activations saturate, causing small gradients and slow learning in deep nets → use **ReLU** as a remedy.
- [x] **Universal Approximation Theorem (Hornik, 1991):** A feedforward MLP with a single hidden layer and sufficient neurons can approximate **any** continuous function on a compact set to arbitrary accuracy.
- [x] The **XOR problem** (Minsky-Papert 1969) cannot be solved by a single layer perceptron, but is trivially solved by a 2-2-1 MLP.
- [x] KTU exam focus: **Architecture diagrams + numerical perceptron convergence + forward pass on a small MLP** are the most frequently asked question types.
- [x] Memorize: net input $u$, activation $a = f(u)$, error $e = d - y$, perceptron update $\Delta w = \eta e x$, backprop $\delta^{(\ell)} = (W^{(\ell+1)T} \delta^{(\ell+1)}) \odot f'(u^{(\ell)})$.
- [x] **Heuristic nature:** FFNNs do not provide optimality guarantees; they are iterative, gradient-based, local-search optimization methods for complex function approximation tasks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Back Propagation Networks and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTICS SOLUTION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Back Propagation Networks and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back Propagation (BP) Network** is a *systematic, supervised learning algorithm* used to train **Multi-Layer Feed-Forward Neural Networks (MLFFNN)**. It employs the **Generalized Delta Rule**, propagating the output error *backwards* from the output layer through the hidden layers to the input layer, and adjusts the connection weights using **Gradient Descent Optimization** to minimize the Mean Squared Error (MSE) between the actual and desired outputs.

In KTU parlance, BP is the *workhorse* of soft-computing — it is the algorithm that makes "deep learning" possible. It is essentially a numerical implementation of the **chain rule of calculus** combined with **iterative weight correction**.

---

## 🌎 Conceptual Analogy — "The Student and the Strict Teacher"

Imagine a student appearing for a surprise test:

1. **Forward Pass (Attempting the Paper)**: The student reads the question (input), thinks through layers of logic (hidden layer neurons), and writes an answer (output). 
2. **Error Calculation (The Teacher Checks)**: The teacher compares the student's answer with the correct answer and writes "−3 marks" in red ink (computed error).
3. **Backward Pass (Learning from Mistakes)**: The teacher walks the student backwards through the *reasoning steps* — "you lost 2 marks here in step 3, 1 mark there in step 2." Each earlier step is told *how much it contributed* to the final error.
4. **Weight Update (Studying Harder)**: The student strengthens the neural connections that led to correct reasoning and weakens the ones that caused mistakes.

> [!NOTE]
> This is **literally** what a back propagation network does. The "marks lost" are the **error gradients (δ)**, and the "studying harder" is the **weight update (Δw)**.

---

## 🔑 Key Terminology (Board-Favorite Definitions)

| Term | Board-Safe Definition |
|---|---|
| **Epoch** | One complete forward + backward pass of **all training samples** through the network. |
| **Learning Rate (η)** | A scalar (typically **0 < η < 1**) that controls the step size of weight updates. |
| **Momentum (α)** | A factor (0 to 1) that adds a fraction of the previous weight change to the current one to *smooth convergence*. |
| **Activation Function** | A non-linear function (sigmoid, tanh, ReLU) applied at each neuron to introduce non-linearity. |
| **Net Input (net)** | The weighted sum of inputs arriving at a neuron, *before* activation. |

---

## 🎯 Why BP Matters in KTU's Heuristics Course

Back propagation is the **bridge** between classical optimization (gradient descent) and biologically-inspired computing (neural networks). It is *the* canonical heuristic used to solve problems where:
- No closed-form analytical solution exists.
- The function is non-linear, noisy, or high-dimensional.
- Traditional rule-based systems fail (pattern recognition, function approximation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🏗️ Network Architecture — The Anatomy of a BP Network

A standard BP network has **three (or more) layers** of neurons, fully connected in a *feed-forward* manner:

1. **Input Layer** — Distributes the feature vector $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ to the network. **No computation** happens here.
2. **Hidden Layer(s)** — One or more layers where the actual feature transformation and abstraction occurs. Each neuron computes a weighted sum and passes it through a non-linear activation.
3. **Output Layer** — Produces the final prediction $\hat{Y} = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_m)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pearl**: For a network to be a *true* "back propagation network," it **must have at least one hidden layer** with a **non-linear activation function**. Without non-linearity, the network collapses to a single-layer perceptron and BP becomes meaningless.

---

## 🔄 The Two-Phase Operational Flow

### Phase 1: Forward Propagation (Signal Flow)
The input signal flows from input → hidden → output. At each neuron:

$$\text{net}_j^{(l)} = \sum_{i} w_{ij}^{(l)} \cdot a_i^{(l-1)} + b_j^{(l)}$$

The net input is then transformed by the activation function:

$$a_j^{(l)} = f\left(\text{net}_j^{(l)}\right)$$

This produces the final output $\hat{y}_k$, which is compared to the desired output $d_k$ to compute the error.

### Phase 2: Backward Propagation (Error Flow)
The error is propagated *in reverse* — from output → hidden → input. The **local gradient** $\delta$ is computed at each neuron, and weights are updated using the **Generalized Delta Rule**.

---

## 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the table below — it covers ~80% of Part-B derivations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Net input to neuron $j$ in layer $l$ | $\text{net}_j^{(l)} = \sum_i w_{ij}^{(l)} x_i$ | Weighted sum |
| 2 | Activated output | $a_j^{(l)} = f(\text{net}_j^{(l)})$ | Non-linear transformation |
| 3 | Sigmoid activation | $f(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Most common in BP |
| 4 | Derivative of sigmoid | $f'(z) = f(z)\cdot[1-f(z)]$ | Used in gradient |
| 5 | Sum-squared error (one sample) | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_k (d_k - y_k)^2$ | Objective to minimize |
| 6 | Output-layer gradient | $\delta_k^{(L)} = (d_k - y_k) \cdot f'(\text{net}_k^{(L)})$ | Local error at output |
| 7 | Hidden-layer gradient | $\delta_j^{(l)} = f'(\text{net}_j^{(l)}) \cdot \sum_k w_{jk}^{(l+1)} \delta_k^{(l+1)}$ | Back-propagated error |
| 8 | Weight update rule | $w_{ij}^{(new)} = w_{ij}^{(old)} + \Delta w_{ij}$ | The generalized delta rule |
| 9 | Delta of weight | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(l)} \cdot a_i^{(l-1)}$ | Core BP equation |
| 10 | With momentum | $\Delta w_{ij}(t+1) = \eta \delta_j a_i + \alpha \Delta w_{ij}(t)$ | Smooths oscillation |

> **Symbols Quick-Reference:** $\eta$ = learning rate, $\alpha$ = momentum, $\delta$ = local gradient (error signal), $w$ = weight, $b$ = bias, $L$ = last (output) layer, $f'$ = derivative of activation.

---

## 🧬 Why Each Step Matters — The Engineering Rationale

- **Why gradient descent?** Because the error surface $E(w)$ in weight-space is a *high-dimensional bowl*. The negative gradient $-\nabla E$ points toward the steepest descent — the fastest local route to the minimum.
- **Why chain rule?** Because the error at the output is a *composite function* of the input weights. The chain rule lets us decompose $\partial E / \partial w_{ij}$ into a product of locally computable terms — making distributed, parallel learning possible.
- **Why a non-linear activation?** Because without it (e.g., linear), stacking layers is mathematically equivalent to a single-layer network — the hidden layers contribute **zero representational power**.

---

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

BP networks are deployed wherever rule-based programming fails:

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Flight control, autopilot fault detection |
| **Finance** | Stock prediction, credit scoring, fraud detection |
| **Healthcare** | Disease diagnosis from imaging (X-ray, MRI) |
| **Manufacturing** | Quality control, predictive maintenance |
| **Telecom** | Handwritten digit recognition (postal sorting) |
| **Robotics** | Inverse kinematics, sensor fusion |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧪 Derivation 1: The Generalized Delta Rule (Output Layer)

### Starting Point
We want to minimize the instantaneous sum-squared error for one training sample:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{m} (d_k - y_k)^2$$

where $d_k$ is the desired output and $y_k$ is the actual output of the $k$-th output neuron.

### Step 1: Express the actual output
The output $y_k$ is the activation of the net input reaching neuron $k$ in the output layer $L$:

$$y_k = f(\text{net}_k^{(L)})$$

### Step 2: Express the net input
The net input is the weighted sum of activations from the previous (hidden) layer:

$$\text{net}_k^{(L)} = \sum_{j} w_{jk}^{(L)} \cdot a_j^{(L-1)} + b_k^{(L)}$$

### Step 3: Compute the gradient $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}}$
Apply the **chain rule** of partial differentiation:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial \text{net}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \text{net}_k}{\partial w_{jk}}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate each term independently

**Term A:** $\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k}$

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y_k}\left[\frac{1}{2}(d_k - y_k)^2\right] = -(d_k - y_k)$$

**Term B:** $\frac{\partial y_k}{\partial \text{net}_k} = f'(\text{net}_k^{(L)})$

(derivative of the activation function)

**Term C:** $\frac{\partial \text{net}_k}{\partial w_{jk}} = a_j^{(L-1)}$

(derivative of the linear sum with respect to a single weight)

### Step 5: Combine

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = -(d_k - y_k) \cdot f'(\text{net}_k^{(L)}) \cdot a_j^{(L-1)}$$

### Step 6: Define the output-layer local gradient
To simplify the notation, define:

$$\delta_k^{(L)} = (d_k - y_k) \cdot f'(\text{net}_k^{(L)})$$

Then:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = -\delta_k^{(L)} \cdot a_j^{(L-1)}$$

### Step 7: Apply gradient descent
Gradient descent tells us to update weights in the direction of the **negative gradient**:

$$\Delta w_{jk}^{(L)} = -\eta \cdot \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = \eta \cdot \delta_k^{(L)} \cdot a_j^{(L-1)}$$

This is the **Generalized Delta Rule** for output-layer weights. ∎

---

## 🧪 Derivation 2: Back-Propagation of Error (Hidden Layer)

### Starting Point
For a hidden-layer weight $w_{ij}$ connecting neuron $i$ (in layer $l-1$) to neuron $j$ (in layer $l$), the net input of $j$ depends on $w_{ij}$, but $j$'s output then propagates to **all neurons in the next layer**. This is what makes the derivation more complex.

### Step 1: Apply the chain rule
Because the hidden neuron's output affects the error only *indirectly* through downstream neurons:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j} \cdot \frac{\partial a_j}{\partial \text{net}_j} \cdot \frac{\partial \text{net}_j}{\partial w_{ij}}$$

### Step 2: Evaluate the simple terms

**Term A:** $\frac{\partial a_j}{\partial \text{net}_j} = f'(\text{net}_j^{(l)})$

**Term B:** $\frac{\partial \text{net}_j}{\partial w_{ij}} = a_i^{(l-1)}$

### Step 3: Expand the indirect term $\frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j}$
The error depends on $a_j$ through *every* neuron in the next layer $l+1$:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j} = \sum_{k} \frac{\partial E}{\partial \text{net}_k^{(l+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \text{net}_k^{(l+1)}}{\partial a_j}$$

### Step 4: Substitute the known quantities
Note that $\frac{\partial E}{\partial \text{net}_k^{(l+1)}} = -\delta_k^{(l+1)}$ and $\frac{\partial \text{net}_k^{(l+1)}}{\partial a_j} = w_{jk}^{(l+1)}$:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial a_j} = -\sum_{k} \delta_k^{(l+1)} \cdot w_{jk}^{(l+1)}$$

### Step 5: Combine all terms

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}} = -f'(\text{net}_j^{(l)}) \cdot a_i^{(l-1)} \cdot \sum_{k} \delta_k^{(l+1)} \cdot w_{jk}^{(l+1)}$$

### Step 6: Define the hidden-layer local gradient

$$\delta_j^{(l)} = f'(\text{net}_j^{(l)}) \cdot \sum_{k} w_{jk}^{(l+1)} \cdot \delta_k^{(l+1)}$$

This is the **error back-propagation equation** — the error signal $\delta$ "flows backward" from the output layer to the hidden layer. ∎

---

## 🧪 Worked Numerical Example: 2-2-1 XOR Network

### Network Setup
- 2 inputs ($x_1, x_2$), 2 hidden neurons ($h_1, h_2$), 1 output ($y$).
- Sigmoid activation $f(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$.
- Learning rate $\eta = 0.5$.
- Training sample: $x_1 = 1, x_2 = 0, d = 1$ (XOR(1,0) = 1).

### Step 1: Initial Weights (assumed)
| Connection | Initial Weight |
|---|---|
| $w_{11} = 0.4$ | $w_{21} = 0.3$ |
| $w_{12} = 0.5$ | $w_{22} = 0.2$ |
| $w_{31} = 0.6$ | $w_{32} = 0.7$ |

### Step 2: Forward Pass
**Hidden net inputs:**

$$\text{net}_{h_1} = w_{11}x_1 + w_{21}x_2 = (0.4)(1) + (0.3)(0) = 0.4$$

$$\text{net}_{h_2} = w_{12}x_1 + w_{22}x_2 = (0.5)(1) + (0.2)(0) = 0.5$$

**Hidden activations (sigmoid):**

$$a_{h_1} = f(0.4) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.4}} \approx 0.5987$$

$$a_{h_2} = f(0.5) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.5}} \approx 0.6225$$

**Output net input:**

$$\text{net}_y = w_{31}a_{h_1} + w_{32}a_{h_2} = (0.6)(0.5987) + (0.7)(0.6225) \approx 0.3592 + 0.4357 = 0.7949$$

**Output activation:**

$$y = f(0.7949) \approx 0.6892$$

### Step 3: Compute Error
$$E = \frac{1}{2}(d - y)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.6892)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.3108)^2 \approx 0.0483$$

### Step 4: Backward Pass — Output Layer Gradient
Using $f'(z) = f(z)(1-f(z)) = y(1-y)$:

$$\delta_y = (d - y) \cdot y(1-y) = (1 - 0.6892)(0.6892)(0.3108) \approx 0.0662$$

### Step 5: Update Output Weights
$$\Delta w_{31} = \eta \cdot \delta_y \cdot a_{h_1} = 0.5 \times 0.0662 \times 0.5987 \approx 0.0198$$

$$w_{31}^{(new)} = 0.6 + 0.0198 = 0.6198$$

$$\Delta w_{32} = \eta \cdot \delta_y \cdot a_{h_2} = 0.5 \times 0.0662 \times 0.6225 \approx 0.0206$$

$$w_{32}^{(new)} = 0.7 + 0.0206 = 0.7206$$

### Step 6: Backward Pass — Hidden Layer Gradients
$$\delta_{h_1} = f'(\text{net}_{h_1}) \cdot w_{31} \cdot \delta_y = (0.5987)(0.4013)(0.6198)(0.0662) \approx 0.00985$$

$$\delta_{h_2} = f'(\text{net}_{h_2}) \cdot w_{32} \cdot \delta_y = (0.6225)(0.3775)(0.7206)(0.0662) \approx 0.01120$$

### Step 7: Update Hidden Weights
$$\Delta w_{11} = \eta \cdot \delta_{h_1} \cdot x_1 = 0.5 \times 0.00985 \times 1 \approx 0.00493$$

$$w_{11}^{(new)} = 0.4 + 0.00493 = 0.4049$$

$$\Delta w_{12} = \eta \cdot \delta_{h_2} \cdot x_1 = 0.5 \times 0.01120 \times 1 \approx 0.00560$$

$$w_{12}^{(new)} = 0.5 + 0.00560 = 0.5056$$

### ✅ Result After One Epoch
The error $E$ has decreased from $0.0483$ (well, this is just the contribution of this sample, the total epoch error is the sum across samples) — the network has learned slightly. Repeating for many epochs drives $E \to 0$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🧬 Diagram 1: Multi-Layer BP Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT LAYER L0"]
        x1["X1"]
        x2["X2"]
        x3["Xn"]
    end

    subgraph HIDDEN_LAYER["HIDDEN LAYER L1"]
        h1["H1: f(sum w*X + b)"]
        h2["H2: f(sum w*X + b)"]
        h3["Hp: f(sum w*X + b)"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER L2"]
        y1["Y1: f(sum w*H + b)"]
        y2["Y2: f(sum w*H + b)"]
    end

    x1 --> h1
    x1 --> h2
    x1 --> h3
    x2 --> h1
    x2 --> h2
    x2 --> h3
    x3 --> h1
    x3 --> h2
    x3 --> h3

    h1 --> y1
    h1 --> y2
    h2 --> y1
    h2 --> y2
    h3 --> y1
    h3 --> y2

    style INPUT_LAYER fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,stroke-width:2px
    style HIDDEN_LAYER fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:2px
    style OUTPUT_LAYER fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 🔁 Diagram 2: BP Algorithm Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Initialize Weights w and Bias b Randomly"] --> B["Set Learning Rate Eta and Momentum Alpha"]
    B --> C["FOR EACH Training Sample X with Desired Output D"]
    C --> D["PHASE 1 FORWARD PASS: Compute Net Input and Activation for Each Layer"]
    D --> E["Compute Output Y at Final Layer"]
    E --> F["PHASE 2 ERROR COMPUTATION: E equals 0.5 times sum of D minus Y squared"]
    F --> G["Compute Output Layer Delta: delta_L equals D minus Y times f prime of net_L"]
    G --> H{"More Hidden Layers?"}
    H -->|YES| I["PHASE 3 BACKWARD PASS: Compute Hidden Delta delta_l equals f prime of net_l times sum of w times delta of next layer"]
    I --> J["Update Hidden Weights: w_new equals w_old plus eta times delta times activation of previous layer"]
    J --> H
    H -->|NO| K["Update Output Weights: w_new equals w_old plus eta times delta_L times a_L minus 1"]
    K --> L{"All Samples Processed? End of Epoch"}
    L -->|NO| C
    L -->|YES| M{"Error E below Threshold OR Max Epochs Reached?"}
    M -->|NO| C
    M -->|YES| N["END: Network Trained, Weights Converged"]

    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style F fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style G fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

---

## 📈 Diagram 3: Error vs Epoch Convergence Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Epoch 0: E equals 0.4800 High Error"] --> P1["Epoch 100: E equals 0.2500"]
    P1 --> P2["Epoch 500: E equals 0.0800"]
    P2 --> P3["Epoch 1000: E equals 0.0200"]
    P3 --> P4["Epoch 2000: E equals 0.0050 Converged"]

    P0 -.->|Large weight updates| P1
    P1 -.->|Smaller updates| P2
    P2 -.->|Fine tuning| P3
    P3 -.->|Near minimum| P4

    style P0 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style P1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style P2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style P3 fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e
    style P4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

---

## 🧠 Diagram 4: Information Flow During a Single BP Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORWARD["FORWARD DIRECTION"]
        F1["Input Pattern X"] --> F2["Hidden Net Computation"]
        F2 --> F3["Hidden Activation f of net"]
        F3 --> F4["Output Computation"]
        F4 --> F5["Predicted Output Y"]
    end

    F5 --> CMP["Compare Y with Desired D"]
    CMP --> ERR["Error Signal E equals D minus Y"]

    subgraph BACKWARD["BACKWARD DIRECTION"]
        ERR --> B1["Output Delta delta_L"]
        B1 --> B2["Back Propagate to Hidden: delta_l"]
        B2 --> B3["Update All Weights using Delta Rule"]
    end

    style FORWARD fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    style BACKWARD fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
    style ERR fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px
```
