<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Defuzzification. List any four commonly used defuzzification methods.**

**Model Answer:**

**Defuzzification** is the process of converting the aggregated fuzzy output set produced by the inference engine of a fuzzy logic system into a single crisp numerical value $z^*$ that can be used by real-world physical systems or controllers.

**Four commonly used methods:**

1. **Centroid Method (Center of Gravity)** — uses the center of mass of the aggregated fuzzy set.
2. **Bisector of Area Method** — finds the vertical line that bisects the area under the membership function.
3. **Mean of Maximum Method** — averages all values at which the membership function attains its peak.
4. **Weighted Average Method** — computes a weighted mean of the centers of singleton output membership functions.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Compare the Centroid Method and the Mean of Maximum Method of defuzzification.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Centroid Method** | **Mean of Maximum Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Center of mass of the entire fuzzy set | Average of the peak values |
| Formula | $z^* = \dfrac{\int z \cdot \mu_C(z)\, dz}{\int \mu_C(z)\, dz}$ | $z^* = \dfrac{\sum_{z \in Z'} z}{\vert Z' \vert}$ |
| Uses full shape of fuzzy set | Yes | No (only peak region) |
| Computational cost | High (requires integration) | Low (simple averaging) |
| Accuracy | Highest | Moderate |
| Robustness to noise | Lower | Higher |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Centroid Method of defuzzification. Derive the formula for $z^*$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An aggregated fuzzy output is defined as:
$$
\mu_C(z) = \{0.2,\ 0.5,\ 0.8,\ \mathbf{1.0},\ 0.8,\ 0.5,\ 0.2\}
$$
over $Z = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$. Compute the crisp output using the **Centroid Method**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Centroid Method Theory (7 Marks)

The **Centroid Method** (also known as the **Center of Gravity** or **Center of Area** method) finds the crisp output $z^*$ as the **center of mass** of the area enclosed by the aggregated fuzzy set $\mu_C(z)$ and the horizontal axis.

**Derivation:**

Consider the aggregated fuzzy set with continuous membership function $\mu_C(z)$ over the universe $Z = [a, b]$.

**Step 1:** The **area** under the curve (analogous to "mass") is given by:

$$
A = \int_{a}^{b} \mu_C(z)\, dz
$$

**Step 2:** The **first moment of area** about the origin is:

$$
M = \int_{a}^{b} z \cdot \mu_C(z)\, dz
$$

**Step 3:** By the physics definition of center of mass, the crisp output is:

$$
z^* = \frac{M}{A} = \frac{\int_{a}^{b} z \cdot \mu_C(z)\, dz}{\int_{a}^{b} \mu_C(z)\, dz}
$$

**Step 4:** For the **discrete** (sampled) case, replace integrals with sums:

$$
z^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i \cdot \mu_C(z_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_C(z_i)}
$$

**Diagram (Textual):**

```
    muC(z)
     1.0 |       ____
         |      /    \
     0.8 |     /      \
         |    /        \
     0.5 |   /          \
         |  /            \
     0.2 | /              \
         |/________________\________
            1   2   3   4   5   6   7   --> z
                          ^
                          |
                       z* = 4.0 (Centroid)
```

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** List $z_i$ and $\mu_C(z_i)$.

| $z_i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu_C(z_i)$ | 0.2 | 0.5 | 0.8 | 1.0 | 0.8 | 0.5 | 0.2 |

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator $\sum z_i \cdot \mu_C(z_i)$.

$$
= (1)(0.2) + (2)(0.5) + (3)(0.8) + (4)(1.0) + (5)(0.8) + (6)(0.5) + (7)(0.2)
$$

$$
= 0.2 + 1.0 + 2.4 + 4.0 + 4.0 + 3.0 + 1.4 = 16.0
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator $\sum \mu_C(z_i)$.

$$
= 0.2 + 0.5 + 0.8 + 1.0 + 0.8 + 0.5 + 0.2 = 4.0
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the centroid.

$$
z^*_{COG} = \frac{16.0}{4.0} = 4.0
$$

**Result:** The crisp output is $z^* = \mathbf{4.0}$

---

#### **Question B2 — Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Mean of Maximum** and **Bisector of Area** methods of defuzzification with neat diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The aggregated output fuzzy set of a fuzzy controller is given below. Compute the crisp value using **Bisector of Area** and **Mean of Maximum** methods. **(7 Marks)**

$$
\mu_C(z) = \{0.0,\ 0.3,\ 0.7,\ 1.0,\ 1.0,\ 0.6,\ 0.2\}
$$
over $Z = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$.

---

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks)

**Mean of Maximum (MOM):**

The MOM method computes the average of all $z$-values where $\mu_C(z)$ attains its **maximum value** $\mu_{max}$. If a plateau of maximum exists, all points within the plateau contribute equally.

$$
z^*_{MOM} = \frac{\int_{Z'} z\, dz}{\int_{Z'} dz}, \quad Z' = \{z \mid \mu_C(z) = \mu_{max}\}
$$

```
    muC(z)
     1.0 |       ____
         |      |    |    <-- Plateau Z prime
         |      |    |
         |      |    |
         |______|____|___________
              a     b    --> z
                    ^
                    |--> z asterisk equals a plus b divided by 2
```

**Bisector of Area (BOA):**

The BOA finds a vertical line $z^*$ such that the **area to its left** equals the **area to its right**.

$$
\int_{a}^{z^*} \mu_C(z)\, dz = \int_{z^*}^{b} \mu_C(z)\, dz
$$

```
    muC(z)
         |       ___
         |      /   \
         |     /     \
         |    /       \
         |   /         \
         |__/___________\________
            a   z*      b       --> z
                ^
                |--- Bisector Line
```

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Bisector of Area:**

**Step 1:** Compute cumulative sum (area from left).

$$
A(z) = \{0.0,\ 0.3,\ 1.0,\ 2.0,\ 3.0,\ 3.6,\ 3.8\}
$$

**Step 2:** Total area = $3.8$. Half area = $\mathbf{1.9}$.

**Step 3:** Find $z$ where $A(z) \geq 1.9$.

- $A(3) = 2.0 \geq 1.9$ ✓

**Step 4:** Interpolate.

$$
z^*_{BOA} = 3 + \frac{1.9 - 1.0}{2.0 - 1.0} \cdot (4 - 3) = 3 + \frac{0.9}{1.0} = 3.9
$$

**Mean of Maximum:**

**Step 1:** Find $\mu_{max} = 1.0$.

**Step 2:** Identify the plateau $Z' = \{3, 4\}$ (both have $\mu_C(z) = 1.0$).

**Step 3:** Compute the mean.

$$
z^*_{MOM} = \frac{3 + 4}{2} = 3.5
$$

**Results:**

$$
z^*_{BOA} = 3.9 \qquad z^*_{MOM} = 3.5
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Defuzzification Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in numerator**: Forgetting to multiply $z_i$ by $\mu_C(z_i)$ in the centroid formula. Many students only sum the memberships.
> 2. **Confusing BOA with MOM**: BOA uses *cumulative area*, MOM uses *peak plateau*. They are NOT the same.
> 3. **Plateau oversight in MOM/SOM/LOM**: When the maximum is at multiple points, students must average ALL of them — not just pick one.
> 4. **Using COG on singletons incorrectly**: For singleton outputs, use **Weighted Average** (faster and standard), not COG with zero-width integrals.
> 5. **Unit mismatch**: Forgetting that $z^*$ must be expressed in the same engineering units as $Z$.
> 6. **Formula truncation**: Writing only the numerator and forgetting the denominator in COG.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for Defuzzification Methods**

- **Definition**: Defuzzification converts aggregated fuzzy output into a single crisp value $z^*$.

- **7 Methods** (must memorize all formulas):
  - **COG** — center of mass (most accurate, slowest)
  - **COS** — sum of areas (faster alternative to COG)
  - **BOA** — equal area bisector
  - **MOM** — average of peaks
  - **SOM** — smallest peak (leftmost)
  - **LOM** — largest peak (rightmost)
  - **WAM** — weighted average for singletons (fastest, used in real-time systems)

- **Key Formula (COG)**:
$$
z^* = \frac{\int z \cdot \mu_C(z)\, dz}{\int \mu_C(z)\, dz}
$$

- **Discrete COG Formula**:
$$
z^* = \frac{\sum z_i \cdot \mu_C(z_i)}{\sum \mu_C(z_i)}
$$

- **Plateau Rule**: If the maximum is at a unique point, MOM = SOM = LOM. They diverge only on plateaus.

- **Symmetry Rule**: For symmetric fuzzy sets, COG ≈ MOM.

- **Application Mapping**:
  - Industrial process control → COG
  - Real-time microcontrollers → WAM
  - Pattern recognition → MOM
  - Medical diagnosis → BOA

- **Engineering Units**: $z^*$ must use the same units as the output universe $Z$.

- **Required KTU Keywords** for full marks: "crisp value", "aggregated fuzzy set", "membership function", "universe of discourse", "center of gravity".

- **Pitfalls to Avoid**: Mistaking BOA for MOM, ignoring plateau averaging, and using COG where WAM is intended for singletons.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET614 - HEURISTIC SOLUTION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What Are "Applications" in Heuristic Solution Techniques? In the context of **OEIET614 – Heuristic Solution Techniques (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4)**, the topic *Applications* refers to the deployment of heuristic and metaheuristic algorithms to solve **NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems** that arise in industry, logistics, manufacturing, and engineering design. These problems are computationally intractable for exact methods beyond small instances, making heuristics the de facto standard for real-world deployment. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4 – Applications):** > *"Application of heuristic techniques to classical NP-hard problems — Travelling Salesman Problem, Vehicle Routing, Scheduling (Flow Shop / Job Shop), Knapsack, Graph Coloring, Network Design, and Engineering Design Optimization."* --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shortcut Navigator" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition: Heuristics Are "Good Enough" Shortcuts, Not Perfect Maps** Imagine you are a delivery driver in Kochi who must visit **50 customers** spread across the city before sunset. Calculating the mathematically perfect route would take a computer **years**, but you cannot wait. A heuristic acts like a **driver's intuition**: *"Always go to the nearest unvisited customer next, and avoid crossing the same bridge twice."* This rule-of-thumb may not be mathematically optimal, but it produces a usable solution in **seconds**. | Concept | Real-World Analogy | Algorithmic Counterpart | |---|---|---| | Problem | 50-customer delivery in Kochi | Travelling Salesman Problem | | Optimal Route | Shortest mathematically proved path | Exact branch-and-bound (intractable) | | Heuristic Rule | "Visit nearest unvisited customer" | Nearest-Neighbour Heuristic | | Metaheuristic | "Try many intuitions and remember the best" | Tabu Search / Simulated Annealing | --- ### 1.3 Classification of Heuristic Applications The applications covered in this module can be grouped into **four broad families**: 1. **Routing Problems** — TSP, Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP), Capacitated VRP (CVRP). 2. **Scheduling Problems** — Flow Shop Scheduling, Job Shop Scheduling, Open Shop. 3. **Packing & Selection Problems** — 0/1 Knapsack, Bin Packing, Set Covering. 4. **Engineering & Design Problems** — Network Design, Graph Coloring, Structural Optimization. > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "apply heuristic to problem X", always (i) state the mathematical formulation, (ii) describe the heuristic rule, (iii) trace the algorithm step-by-step, and (iv) report the final solution cost. --- ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following metrics recur throughout Module 4: - **Tour Cost (TSP/VRP):** total Euclidean or matrix-based edge weight, measured in **km** or cost units. - **Makespan (C_max):** total completion time of all jobs, measured in **hours**. - **Tardiness (T_i):** delay beyond due date, $T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$, in **time units**. - **Knapsack Profit (Z):** total value of selected items, measured in **currency units**. - **Chromatic Number (χ(G)):** minimum colors needed for valid graph coloring, dimensionless. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Application 1 — Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) **Mathematical Formulation:** Given a complete weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with $n$ cities and distance matrix $D = [d_{ij}]$, find a Hamiltonian cycle of **minimum total cost**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Minimize} \quad & Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} d_{ij} \cdot x_{ij} \\ \text{Subject to} \quad & \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall i \in V \\ & \sum_{i=1, i \neq j}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall j \in V \\ & \sum_{i \in S, j \notin S} x_{ij} \geq 1, \quad \forall S \subset V, S \neq \emptyset \\ & x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\} \end{aligned} $$ **Why heuristics are needed:** - Brute-force enumeration: $(n-1)!/2$ tours. - For $n = 20$, that is $\approx 6 \times 10^{16}$ — infeasible. - Heuristics (Nearest Neighbour, 2-opt, Lin–Kernighan) deliver near-optimal tours in **polynomial time**. --- ### 2.2 Application 2 — Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) **Mathematical Formulation:** Given a depot, $n$ customers with demands $q_i$, and $m$ identical vehicles of capacity $Q$, assign each customer to exactly one vehicle and find the minimum-cost routes starting and ending at the depot. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Minimize} \quad & Z = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \sum_{(i,j)} d_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}^k \\ \text{Subject to} \quad & \sum_{k=1}^{m} \sum_{j \in V} x_{ij}^k = 1, \quad \forall i \in V \setminus \{0\} \\ & \sum_{i \in V} q_i \cdot y_{ik} \leq Q, \quad \forall k \\ & \sum_{j \in V} x_{ij}^k = \sum_{j \in V} x_{ji}^k = y_{ik} \\ & x_{ij}^k, y_{ik} \in \{0, 1\} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Amazon, Flipkart, BlueDart, and Kerala State Road Transport use VRP variants for last-mile delivery. --- ### 2.3 Application 3 — Scheduling (Makespan Minimization) For $n$ jobs on $m$ machines, let $p_{ij}$ denote processing time of job $i$ on machine $j$. $$ C_{\max} = \max_i \{C_i\} $$ where $C_i$ is the **completion time** of job $i$. Heuristics such as **Johnson's Rule**, **NEH (Nawaz–Enscore–Ham)**, and **Palmer's Heuristic** are commonly used. --- ### 2.4 Application 4 — 0/1 Knapsack Problem $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Maximize} \quad & Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot x_i \\ \text{Subject to} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i \leq W \\ & x_i \in \{0, 1\} \end{aligned} $$ **Greedy heuristic:** sort by value-to-weight ratio $r_i = v_i / w_i$ in descending order and pick greedily until capacity is exhausted. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Reference Table (Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE)** | # | Problem Domain | Key Formula | Units / Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | TSP Tour Cost | $Z = \sum_{(i,j) \in T} d_{ij}$ | km or cost units | | 2 | TSP Complexity | $(n-1)! \,/\, 2$ tours | counts | | 3 | Nearest-Neighbour Rule | $\text{Pick} \, j^* = \arg\min_{j \notin V'} d_{ij}$ | index | | 4 | VRP Capacity | $\sum_{i \in R_k} q_i \leq Q$ | demand units | | 5 | Makespan (Flow Shop) | $C_{\max} = \max_i C_i$ | hours | | 6 | Johnson's Rule Sort | $\min(p_{i1}, p_{i2})$ ascending | hours | | 7 | NEH Sort Key | $\sum_j p_{ij}$ descending | hours | | 8 | Tardiness | $T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$ | hours | | 9 | Knapsack Profit | $Z = \sum v_i x_i$ | currency | | 10 | Knapsack Ratio | $r_i = v_i \,/\, w_i$ | ratio (value/weight) | | 11 | 2-opt Gain | $\Delta = d_{ij} + d_{kl} - d_{ik} - d_{jl}$ | cost units | | 12 | Chromatic Number | $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$ | integer | | 13 | Graph Density | $\rho = 2 \vert E \vert \,/\, [n(n-1)]$ | dimensionless | | 14 | Simulated Annealing | $P = \exp(-\Delta E \,/\, T)$ | probability | | 15 | Cooling Schedule | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k, \ 0.8 \leq \alpha \leq 0.99$ | temperature | --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **TSP → VLSI Circuit Drilling:** Minimize drill head travel on PCB boards. - **VRP → Logistics & E-commerce Delivery:** Flipkart, Amazon India routing. - **Scheduling → Kerala Petrochemical Refineries:** Crude oil processing order. - **Knapsack → Project Capital Budgeting:** Select maximum-value IT projects. - **Graph Coloring → Frequency Assignment:** Mobile tower channel allocation. - **Engineering Design → Aerospace Component Sizing:** Minimize weight subject to strength constraints. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Travelling Salesman Problem (Nearest-Neighbour Heuristic) **Problem Statement:** Solve the TSP for 4 cities A, B, C, D using the Nearest-Neighbour (NN) Heuristic starting from city A. Distance matrix: $$ D = \begin{bmatrix} - & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 10 & - & 35 & 25 \\ 15 & 35 & - & 30 \\ 20 & 25 & 30 & - \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1 — Start at city A.** Available: {B, C, D}. - $d(A,B) = 10$, $d(A,C) = 15$, $d(A,D) = 20$. - Minimum = **10** → Go to **B**. **Step 2 — From B.** Available: {C, D}. - $d(B,C) = 35$, $d(B,D) = 25$. - Minimum = **25** → Go to **D**. **Step 3 — From D.** Available: {C}. - Only option: **C**. **Step 4 — Return to start.** - $d(C,A) = 15$. **Step 5 — Compute total tour cost.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Tour: } & A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow C \rightarrow A \\ \text{Total Cost } Z &= d(A,B) + d(B,D) + d(D,C) + d(C,A) \\ &= 10 + 25 + 30 + 15 \\ &= 80 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification via Brute Force (KTU Valuation Insight):** > The three other tours are $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow A = 10+35+30+20 = 95$; $A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow A = 15+35+25+20 = 95$; $A \rightarrow D \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow A = 20+25+35+15 = 95$. The NN heuristic found the **optimal tour cost = 80**. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — 0/1 Knapsack with Greedy Heuristic **Problem Statement:** A KTU final-year student has a project backpack with capacity $W = 10$ kg. Five project tools are available: | Item | Weight $w_i$ (kg) | Value $v_i$ (₹) | |---|---|---| | Laptop | 6 | 50000 | | Tablet | 4 | 30000 | | Notebook | 3 | 8000 | | Calculator | 2 | 6000 | | Toolkit | 5 | 12000 | **Step 1 — Compute value-to-weight ratio $r_i = v_i / w_i$:** $$ \begin{aligned} r_{\text{Laptop}} &= 50000 \,/\, 6 \approx 8333.33 \\ r_{\text{Tablet}} &= 30000 \,/\, 4 = 7500 \\ r_{\text{Notebook}} &= 8000 \,/\, 3 \approx 2666.67 \\ r_{\text{Calculator}} &= 6000 \,/\, 2 = 3000 \\ r_{\text{Toolkit}} &= 12000 \,/\, 5 = 2400 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Sort descending by ratio:** Laptop (8333) → Tablet (7500) → Calculator (3000) → Notebook (2667) → Toolkit (2400). **Step 3 — Greedy selection with capacity check:** | Step | Item | Weight | Cumulative | Feasible? | Action | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Laptop | 6 | 6 | Yes | Add | | 2 | Tablet | 4 | 10 | Yes (= capacity) | Add | | 3 | Calculator | 2 | 12 | No (exceeds 10) | Skip | | 4 | Notebook | 3 | 13 | No | Skip | | 5 | Toolkit | 5 | 15 | No | Skip | **Step 4 — Final solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Selected items: } & \{\text{Laptop, Tablet}\} \\ \text{Total Weight: } & 6 + 4 = 10 \, \text{kg} \leq W \\ \text{Total Value: } & Z = 50000 + 30000 = 80000 \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Greedy knapsack is **not always optimal**. The optimal solution here is the same (since Calculator alone gives ₹6000/kg but cannot fit), but always verify by checking fractional relaxation in the board exam. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Job Shop Scheduling (Johnson's Rule for 2-Machine Flow Shop) **Problem Statement:** Schedule 4 jobs on 2 machines (M1, M2) to minimize makespan $C_{\max}$. Each job must visit M1 then M2. | Job | $p_{i1}$ (M1) | $p_{i2}$ (M2) | |---|---|---| | J1 | 5 | 2 | | J2 | 1 | 6 | | J3 | 9 | 7 | | J4 | 3 | 8 | **Step 1 — For each job compute $\min(p_{i1}, p_{i2})$:** - J1: 2 (M2) - J2: 1 (M1) - J3: 7 (M2) - J4: 3 (M1) **Step 2 — Sort ascending by the minimum:** J2(1) → J4(3) → J1(2) → J3(7) → reorder as J2, J1, J4, J3 (since J4 has min at M1 placed later in M1-position queue): > [!NOTE] > **Johnson's Two-List Algorithm:** > - **List 1** (min on M1): place jobs in ascending order of $p_{i1}$ at the **front** of the sequence. > - **List 2** (min on M2): place jobs in ascending order of $p_{i2}$ at the **back** of the sequence. - M1-min jobs (sorted by $p_{i1}$): J2(1), J4(3). - M2-min jobs (sorted by $p_{i2}$): J1(2), J3(7). **Johnson's Sequence: J2 → J4 → J1 → J3** **Step 3 — Compute completion times on both machines:** | Job | Start M1 | Finish M1 | Start M2 | Finish M2 | |---|---|---|---|---| | J2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 7 | | J4 | 1 | 4 | 7 | 15 | | J1 | 4 | 9 | 15 | 17 | | J3 | 9 | 18 | 18 | 25 | **Step 4 — Makespan:** $$ C_{\max} = 25 $$ **Verification of Idle time on M2:** M2 idle from $t=0$ to $t=1$ (waiting for J2). Otherwise continuous. Total idle = **1 hour**. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Heuristic × Application Mapping | Application | Classical Heuristic | Metaheuristic | Time Complexity | Solution Quality | |---|---|---|---|---| | TSP | Nearest-Neighbour, 2-opt | SA, GA, ACO | $O(n^2)$ | Within 5–10% of optimal | | VRP | Clarke–Wright Savings | Tabu Search, ALNS | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | Within 3–8% of optimal | | Flow Shop | Johnson, NEH, Palmer | GA, SA | $O(n \log n)$ | Within 5% of optimal | | Job Shop | Priority Dispatch (SPT, LPT) | GA, ACO | $O(n^2 m)$ | Within 10% of optimal | | 0/1 Knapsack | Greedy by ratio | GA, BSO | $O(n \log n)$ | Sub-optimal | | Graph Coloring | DSATUR, Welsh–Powell | GA, SA | $O(n^2)$ | Near-minimum colors | | Network Design | Add/Drop heuristic | SA | $O(E \log V)$ | Within 10% of LP bound | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Heuristic Applications Classification ```mermaid graph TD A[Heuristic Applications]:::root A --> B[Routing Problems]:::cat A --> C[Scheduling Problems]:::cat A --> D[Packing and Selection]:::cat A --> E[Engineering Design]:::cat B --> B1[TSP]:::node B --> B2[VRP]:::node B --> B3[CVRP]:::node B1 --> B1a[Nearest Neighbour]:::leaf B1 --> B1b[2-opt]:::leaf B1 --> B1c[Lin-Kernighan]:::leaf B2 --> B2a[Clarke-Wright]:::leaf B2 --> B2b[Sweep Algorithm]:::leaf C --> C1[Flow Shop]:::node C --> C2[Job Shop]:::node C --> C3[Open Shop]:::node C1 --> C1a[Johnson Rule]:::leaf C1 --> C1b[NEH Heuristic]:::leaf C1 --> C1c[Palmer Slope]:::leaf C2 --> C2a[Priority Dispatch]:::leaf C2 --> C2b[Shifting Bottleneck]:::leaf D --> D1[0/1 Knapsack]:::node D --> D2[Bin Packing]:::node D --> D3[Set Covering]:::node D1 --> D1a[Greedy by Ratio]:::leaf D1 --> D1b[Greedy by Value]:::leaf D2 --> D2a[First Fit Decreasing]:::leaf E --> E1[Graph Coloring]:::node E --> E2[Network Design]:::node E --> E3[Structural Opt]:::node E1 --> E1a[Welsh-Powell]:::leaf E1 --> E1b[DSATUR]:::leaf E2 --> E2a[Greedy Add]:::leaf classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef cat fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef node fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Heuristic Solver ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Problem Input
Distance Matrix OR Job Times]:::in FD[Formulation Module
Mathematical Model]:::proc HV[Heuristic Engine
NN / Johnson / Greedy]:::core META[Metaheuristic Layer
SA / Tabu / GA]:::meta EV[Evaluation Module
Cost Function Cmax]:::ev FB[Feedback Loop
Acceptance Criterion]:::fb OUT[Output Best Solution
Tour OR Schedule]:::out P --> FD FD --> HV HV --> EV EV -->|Improvement Found| META META --> HV EV -->|Termination Met| FB FB --> OUT classDef in fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef core fill:#f6b26b,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef meta fill:#e69138,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef ev fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef fb fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef out fill:#38761d,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Nearest-Neighbour TSP Algorithm Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start at city 1]:::start C[Current city = last visited]:::proc NM[Find unvisited city with minimum distance]:::proc ADD[Add city to tour, mark visited]:::proc CHK{All cities visited?}:::dec RET[Return to start city]:::proc COMP[Compute total tour cost]:::out E[End]:::end S --> C C --> NM NM --> ADD ADD --> CHK CHK -->|No| C CHK -->|Yes| RET RET --> COMP COMP --> E classDef start fill:#76a5af,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef proc fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef dec fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef out fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef end fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *State any three real-world applications of the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP).* **Model Answer:** 1. **VLSI Circuit Board Drilling:** Sequencing drill holes to minimize head travel time. 2. **Logistics & Delivery:** Route planning for e-commerce companies like Flipkart / Amazon India. 3. **Genome Sequencing in Bioinformatics:** Reconstructing DNA fragments in correct order. (Each application ≈ 1 mark. Total: 3 marks.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *What is the makespan in job shop scheduling? Why is minimizing it NP-hard?* **Model Answer:** - **Makespan** $C_{\max}$ is the **maximum completion time** across all jobs — the total time required to finish the entire schedule. - $C_{\max} = \max_i \{C_i\}$ where $C_i$ is the completion time of job $i$. - It is **NP-hard** because with $n$ jobs and $m$ machines there are $(n!)^m$ possible schedules, and no polynomial-time exact algorithm is known for $n \geq 20$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Routing Focus** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Solve the following Travelling Salesman Problem instance using the **Nearest-Neighbour Heuristic** starting from city 1. Distance matrix: $$ D = \begin{bmatrix} - & 2 & 9 & 10 \\ 1 & - & 6 & 4 \\ 15 & 7 & - & 8 \\ 6 & 12 & 3 & - \end{bmatrix} $$ **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Start at city 1.** Unvisited = {2, 3, 4}. - $d(1,2) = 2, \ d(1,3) = 9, \ d(1,4) = 10$. - Minimum = **2** → Move to **city 2**. **Step 2 — From city 2.** Unvisited = {3, 4}. - $d(2,3) = 6, \ d(2,4) = 4$. - Minimum = **4** → Move to **city 4**. **Step 3 — From city 4.** Unvisited = {3}. - Move to **city 3**. **Step 4 — Return to start.** - $d(3,1) = 15$. **Step 5 — Total cost:** $$ Z = d(1,2) + d(2,4) + d(4,3) + d(3,1) = 2 + 4 + 3 + 15 = \mathbf{24} $$ **Tour:** $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 1$. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Apply the **Clarke–Wright Savings Algorithm** to a VRP with one depot (0), three customers (A, B, C), and vehicle capacity $Q = 50$. Demands and distances: | Pair | Distance | Demand | |---|---|---| | 0–A | 4 | 20 | | 0–B | 6 | 15 | | 0–C | 8 | 30 | | A–B | 5 | — | | B–C | 7 | — | | A–C | 9 | — | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute savings $S_{ij} = d(0,i) + d(0,j) - d(i,j)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{AB} &= 4 + 6 - 5 = 5 \\ S_{BC} &= 6 + 8 - 7 = 7 \\ S_{AC} &= 4 + 8 - 9 = 3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Sort savings descending:** $S_{BC} = 7$ → $S_{AB} = 5$ → $S_{AC} = 3$. **Step 3 — Initial routes (depot-only):** Route 1: 0–A–0, Route 2: 0–B–0, Route 3: 0–C–0. **Step 4 — Merge starting from highest savings:** | Merge | Feasibility Check | Result | |---|---|---| | Merge B & C → route 0–B–C–0 | Demand = 15 + 30 = 45 ≤ 50 ✓ | **Accept** | | Merge A with route B–C → 0–A–B–C–0 | Demand = 20 + 15 + 30 = 65 > 50 ✗ | **Reject** | | Merge A & B (but B is in merged route) → N/A | Skip | — | | Merge A & C (but C is in merged route) → N/A | Skip | — | **Step 5 — Final routes:** - Route 1: 0–B–C–0 (cost = 6 + 7 + 8 = 21) - Route 2: 0–A–0 (cost = 4 + 4 = 8) **Total VRP cost** = 21 + 8 = **29**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Scheduling Focus** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Apply **Johnson's Rule** to the following 2-machine, 4-job flow shop problem. Each job visits M1 then M2. | Job | $p_{i1}$ | $p_{i2}$ | |---|---|---| | A | 7 | 4 | | B | 3 | 6 | | C | 5 | 2 | | D | 8 | 9 | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — For each job, compute $\min(p_{i1}, p_{i2})$ and identify machine:** - A: $\min = 4$ (M2) - B: $\min = 3$ (M1) - C: $\min = 2$ (M2) - D: $\min = 8$ (M1) **Step 2 — Partition into two lists:** - **List 1 (M1-min, sorted ascending by $p_{i1}$):** B(3), D(8). - **List 2 (M2-min, sorted ascending by $p_{i2}$):** C(2), A(4). **Step 3 — Johnson's sequence:** B → D → C → A. **Step 4 — Compute completion times:** | Job | Start M1 | End M1 | Start M2 | End M2 | |---|---|---|---|---| | B | 0 | 3 | 3 | 9 | | D | 3 | 11 | 11 | 20 | | C | 11 | 16 | 20 | 22 | | A | 16 | 23 | 23 | 27 | **Makespan:** $C_{\max} = \mathbf{27}$. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **NEH Heuristic** for the **$m$-machine flow shop** problem. How does it differ from Johnson's Rule? **Model Answer:** **NEH Algorithm (Nawaz–Enscore–Ham, 1983):** 1. **Step 1:** Compute the total processing time $T_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} p_{ij}$ for each job. 2. **Step 2:** Sort jobs in **decreasing** order of $T_i$. 3. **Step 3:** Take the first two jobs and find the better of the two possible sequences (forward or reverse). This is the partial sequence. 4. **Step 4:** For each remaining job (in decreasing $T_i$ order), insert it into all $k+1$ positions of the current partial sequence, where $k$ is the length of the partial sequence. Choose the position that yields the **minimum makespan**. 5. **Step 5:** Repeat until all jobs are inserted. **Difference from Johnson's Rule:** | Feature | Johnson's Rule | NEH Heuristic | |---|---|---| | Machines | Only **2 machines** | **$m$ machines** (any) | | Optimality | Optimal for 2-machine | Heuristic (near-optimal) | | Complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2 m)$ | | Insertion | Fixed order | Evaluates all positions | | Best for | Small 2-machine instances | General $m$-machine flow shop | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE Valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting the return edge** in TSP — students often compute $n-1$ edges instead of $n$ edges (loss of 2 marks). > 2. **Wrong partition in Johnson's Rule** — placing M1-min jobs in M2-list and vice versa → invalid sequence (loss of 3–4 marks). > 3. **Ignoring vehicle capacity constraints in VRP** — Clarke–Wright merges can violate capacity; always verify $\sum q_i \leq Q$ before accepting a merge (loss of 2 marks). > 4. **Forgetting idle-time computation in scheduling** — examiners award 1–2 marks for explicitly showing idle intervals on machines. > 5. **Not stating the heuristic rule before tracing the algorithm** — KTU valuators expect a one-line definition (e.g., *"Nearest-Neighbour: pick the closest unvisited city"*) before the step-by-step table. > 6. **Mixing up $C_i$ (completion of job $i$) with $C_{\max}$ (overall makespan)** — common 1-mark loss. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Applications (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision)** - **TSP:** Heuristics = Nearest-Neighbour, 2-opt. Tour cost = sum of $n$ edges (including return). NN is **fast but not optimal**; always brute-force-verify for $n \leq 6$. - **VRP:** Clarke–Wright **savings** $S_{ij} = d(0,i) + d(0,j) - d(i,j)$; merge highest savings first; respect capacity $Q$. - **Flow Shop Scheduling:** Johnson for 2-machines (optimal); NEH for $m$-machines (heuristic). Compute idle times explicitly. - **Job Shop Scheduling:** Priority dispatch rules (SPT, LPT, EDD); Shifting Bottleneck for complex cases. - **0/1 Knapsack:** Greedy by $v_i/w_i$ ratio. **Greedy is NOT always optimal** — verify with dynamic programming for small $n$. - **Bin Packing:** First Fit Decreasing (FFD) gives $\leq 11/9 \cdot OPT + 6/9$ theoretical bound. - **Graph Coloring:** Welsh–Powell (sort descending by degree, color greedily). Lower bound: $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$ (clique number). - **Simulated Annealing:** Accept worse moves with probability $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$. Cooling: $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$. - **Tabu Search:** Maintains a tabu list to avoid cycling. Aspiration criterion overrides tabu status if a new best is found. - **GA Crossover:** Single-point, two-point, uniform. Mutation probability $p_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$. - **Always:** state problem formulation → state heuristic rule → trace step-by-step → report final cost with units.
